topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, networked system of collaborating computational entities (cyber) that are tightly integrated with and control physical processes (physical), where the embedded computers and networks monitor, coordinate, and integrate the computation with the physical dynamics in a continuous, feedback-driven loop.

Formally, a CPS can be defined as a tuple:

$$
\text{CPS} = \langle P, C, S, A, F \rangle
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Set of **physical processes** (sensors, actuators, environment)
- $C$ = Set of **cyber components** (controllers, processors, software)
- $S$ = Set of **sensing/actuation signals**
- $A$ = Set of **communication/actuation actions**
- $F$ = Set of **feedback functions** $f: S \rightarrow A$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining characteristic of a CPS is the **bi-directional coupling** between the cyber and physical domains — computation influences physical behavior, and physical state influences computation.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Privacy in CPS

**Privacy in CPS** is the property of a cyber-physical system that ensures sensitive information about users, physical states, system operations, and contextual data — collected, transmitted, and processed by the cyber components — is protected from unauthorized observation, inference, and disclosure throughout the system's lifecycle.

Mathematically, privacy preservation in CPS requires that for any adversary $\mathcal{A}$ observing system outputs $\mathcal{O}$, the posterior knowledge about a sensitive attribute $X$ is bounded:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{O}) = x] \leq \epsilon \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\text{no info}) = x]
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (a small positive constant).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Home Thermostat**
>
> Imagine a **smart home** where a thermostat (physical) talks to your phone app (cyber). The thermostat measures the room temperature, sends data to the cloud, and receives commands back to adjust the heat. Now, this is a **CPS** — the physical (room) and the cyber (app, cloud) are linked.
>
> But here's the privacy issue: by analyzing the temperature patterns over time, an attacker (or even the utility company) can deduce **when you are home, when you sleep, when you go on vacation**. This is privacy leakage — the *physical signals* themselves become a window into your personal life.
>
> **Privacy in CPS** is about ensuring that while the system works correctly (your house stays warm), it does not unintentionally **broadcast your private life** through its observable behavior.

## 1.4 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Sensor** | Physical device that measures a real-world quantity | Temperature sensor, ECG electrode |
| **Actuator** | Physical device that performs an action on the real world | Motor, valve, brake |
| **Controller** | Cyber component that processes sensor data and issues commands | Microcontroller, PLC |
| **Physical Dynamics** | Time-evolution of the real-world process governed by physics | $\dot{x} = f(x, u, t)$ |
| **Privacy Leakage** | Unintended inference of sensitive data from observable system behavior | Inferring illness from wearable data |
| **Side-Channel** | An unintended information channel (timing, power, output) that leaks private data | CPU timing attack on a smart meter |

## 1.5 Why Privacy is Fundamentally Different in CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike traditional IT systems, CPS privacy concerns extend beyond **data-at-rest** to include:
> 1. **Physical state privacy** — the physical process itself is sensitive
> 2. **Contextual privacy** — sensor streams reveal behavior patterns
> 3. **Actuation privacy** — control commands leak information about system goals
> 4. **Cross-layer privacy** — combining cyber logs with physical data amplifies leakage
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Conceptual Stack of CPS Privacy

A CPS can be decomposed into five interacting layers, each of which presents distinct privacy challenges:

1. **Physical Layer (Sensors & Actuators)** — Raw measurements originate here. Privacy concern: sensor noise, calibration data, and physical signals directly encode private attributes (e.g., a smart meter reveals household occupancy).

2. **Communication Layer (Network)** — Data is transmitted between field devices and controllers. Privacy concern: eavesdropping, traffic analysis, and inference from packet timing.

3. **Computation Layer (Controllers/Cloud)** — Data is processed, fused, and stored. Privacy concern: aggregation attacks, model inversion, membership inference on trained ML models.

4. **Decision Layer (Actuation)** — Control commands are issued. Privacy concern: command patterns reveal system objectives, control policy, or operator intent.

5. **Feedback Coupling Layer** — The bi-directional loop itself. Privacy concern: closed-loop dynamics amplify small leaks; perturbations in actuation can be detected by external observers (stealth attacks).

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations of Privacy

### 2.2.1 Information-Theoretic Privacy

The mutual information between the sensitive variable $X$ and the observable output $O$ quantifies privacy leakage:

$$
I(X; O) = H(X) - H(X \mid O)
$$

- $H(X)$ = Shannon entropy of the sensitive attribute
- $H(X \mid O)$ = Conditional entropy given the observation
- A **perfectly private** system satisfies $I(X; O) = 0$, meaning $O$ reveals no information about $X$.

### 2.2.2 Differential Privacy (DP)

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for all neighboring datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record, and for all output sets $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

### 2.2.3 k-Anonymity

A dataset is $k$-anonymous if each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ other records with respect to quasi-identifiers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Parameter | Privacy Implication |
|---------|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Mutual Information Leakage** | $I(X;O) = H(X) - H(X \mid O)$ | Bits | Higher = worse privacy |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{x} p(x) \log_2 p(x)$ | Bits | Measures uncertainty |
| **Differential Privacy** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\epsilon$ (privacy budget) | Lower $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy |
| **k-Anonymity** | $\vert Q\text{-ident}(D) \vert \geq k$ | $k$ (group size) | Higher $k$ = stronger privacy |
| **CPS State Dynamics** | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t), w(t))$ | State $x$, input $u$, noise $w$ | Defines physical evolution |
| **Observation Model** | $y(t) = h(x(t)) + v(t)$ | Observation $y$, noise $v$ | What the adversary sees |
| **Adversary Uncertainty** | $H(X \mid Y=y) \geq \delta$ | $\delta$ (entropy floor) | Privacy threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in KTU-expected problems involving *privacy-preserving sensor design*, *smart grid privacy*, *wearable health data*, and *autonomous vehicle data leakage analysis*.

## 2.4 Why This Matters: Engineering Applications

- **Smart Grids:** Differential privacy on smart meter readings prevents utility companies from inferring consumer behavior.
- **Healthcare CPS:** Wearable ECG devices must protect both raw signals and inferred health conditions.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** LiDAR/camera data must be obfuscated when shared for V2X communication.
- **Industrial IoT:** Sensor fusion must not leak proprietary process information to competitors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Privacy Leakage from CPS Observations

Consider a CPS where the physical state $X$ evolves as:

$$
X_{t+1} = a X_t + b U_t + W_t
$$

and the observable output $Y_t$ is:

$$
Y_t = c X_t + V_t
$$

where $W_t, V_t$ are independent Gaussian noise terms with variances $\sigma_W^2, \sigma_V^2$.

**Step 1:** Compute the prior entropy of the sensitive state. Assuming $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X^2)$:

$$
H(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(2\pi e \, \sigma_X^2)
$$

This is the standard differential entropy of a Gaussian random variable.

**Step 2:** Compute the posterior entropy after observing $Y = y$. For a linear Gaussian system, the optimal estimator is the Kalman filter, with posterior variance:

$$
\sigma_{X \mid Y}^2 = \left( \frac{1}{\sigma_X^2} + \frac{c^2}{\sigma_V^2} \right)^{-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the posterior entropy:

$$
H(X \mid Y) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left( 2\pi e \, \sigma_{X \mid Y}^2 \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the privacy leakage (mutual information):

$$
I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)
$$

Substituting:

$$
I(X; Y) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left( \frac{\sigma_X^2}{\sigma_{X \mid Y}^2} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using the closed-form posterior variance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X; Y) &= \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{c^2 \sigma_X^2}{\sigma_V^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The privacy leakage grows with the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** $\frac{c^2 \sigma_X^2}{\sigma_V^2}$. Higher sensor sensitivity ($c \uparrow$) or lower measurement noise ($V \downarrow$) **increases leakage** — a fundamental CPS privacy trade-off.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Privacy vs. Utility Trade-off

| Design Choice | Effect on Privacy | Effect on Utility (Performance) |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| Increase sensor noise $\sigma_V^2$ | ↑ Privacy (lower leakage) | ↓ Utility (less accurate control) |
| Add DP noise to output $Y$ | ↑ Privacy (bounded leakage) | ↓ Utility (noisy control) |
| Reduce sensor resolution | ↑ Privacy (less information) | ↓ Utility (coarse control) |
| Aggregate data over time | ↑ Privacy (hides individuals) | ↓ Utility (delayed response) |
| Use homomorphic encryption | ↑ Privacy (cryptographic) | ↓ Utility (compute overhead) |
| Differential privacy on statistics | ↑ Privacy (statistical) | ↓ Utility (perturbed estimates) |

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A wearable health CPS measures heart rate $X$ with prior variance $\sigma_X^2 = 100$ (bpm)$^2$. The sensor has gain $c = 1$ and measurement noise variance $\sigma_V^2 = 25$. Compute the privacy leakage in bits.

**Step 1 — Compute SNR:**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{c^2 \sigma_X^2}{\sigma_V^2} = \frac{1 \cdot 100}{25} = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Compute leakage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X; Y) &= \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + 4) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \log_2(5) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 2.3219 \\
&\approx 1.161 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The wearable leaks approximately **1.16 bits** of information per measurement about the user's heart rate. To reduce this below **0.5 bits**, one would need to increase $\sigma_V^2$ such that:

$$
0.5 = \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{100}{\sigma_V^2} \right) \Rightarrow \sigma_V^2 \geq \frac{100}{2^1 - 1} = 100
$$

i.e., the sensor noise must be **at least equal** to the prior variance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level CPS Architecture with Privacy Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PhysEnv[Physical Environment] -->|Sensors| PhysLayer
    PhysLayer[Physical Layer: Sensors Actuators] -->|Raw Signal| CommLayer
    CommLayer[Communication Layer: Network Bus] -->|Encrypted Packet| CompLayer
    CompLayer[Computation Layer: Controller Cloud] -->|Control Command| ActLayer
    ActLayer[Decision Layer: Actuation] -->|Physical Action| PhysEnv
    CompLayer -->|Telemetry| PrivacyMod[Privacy Module: DP Filter Anonymizer]
    PrivacyMod -->|Sanitized Data| UserApp[User Application / Auditor]
    Adversary[Adversary Eavesdropper] -.->|Taps| CommLayer
    Adversary -.->|Infers State| PhysEnv
    classDef privacy fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef adversary fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class PrivacyMod,UserApp privacy
    class Adversary adversary
```

## 4.2 Information Flow and Privacy Leakage Paths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Sensitive Physical State X] -->|Encoded| Y[Observable Output Y]
    Y -->|Observed by| Adv[Adversary A]
    Adv -->|Inference| Xhat[Estimated X-hat]
    X -.->|Leakage Channel| Xhat
    DP[Differential Privacy Mechanism M] -->|Perturbs| Y
    X --> DP
    classDef sensitive fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef threat fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    class X,Xhat,Y sensitive
    class Adv threat
```

## 4.3 Privacy Threat Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PrivacyThreats[Privacy Threats in CPS] --> IdentityThreats[Identity Threats]
    PrivacyThreats --> BehaviorThreats[Behavior Inference Threats]
    PrivacyThreats --> ContextThreats[Contextual Disclosure Threats]
    IdentityThreats --> Id1[Re-identification via Sensor Data]
    IdentityThreats --> Id2[Linkage Attack across CPS Streams]
    BehaviorThreats --> Be1[Lifestyle Inference Smart Meter]
    BehaviorThreats --> Be2[Health Inference Wearable]
    BehaviorThreats --> Be3[Mobility Tracking V2X]
    ContextThreats --> Co1[Location Leakage GPS]
    ContextThreats --> Co2[Temporal Pattern Mining]
    ContextThreats --> Co3[Social Graph Reconstruction]
    classDef root fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px
    classDef leaf fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    class PrivacyThreats root
    class Id1,Id2,Be1,Be2,Be3,Co1,Co2,Co3 leaf
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Privacy-Preserving CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        SensorA[Sensor A Physical]
        SensorB[Sensor B Physical]
    end
    subgraph PrivacyStage[Privacy Processing Stage]
        NoiseGen[Noise Generator]
        DPFilter[Differentially Private Filter]
        CryptoEnc[Homomorphic Encryptor]
    end
    subgraph DecisionStage[Decision Stage]
        Controller[Secure Controller MPC]
        Policy[Privacy Policy Engine]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output Stage]
        Actuator[Actuator]
        AuditLog[Privacy Audit Log]
    end
    SensorA --> DPFilter
    SensorB --> CryptoEnc
    NoiseGen --> DPFilter
    DPFilter --> Controller
    CryptoEnc --> Controller
    Policy --> Controller
    Controller --> Actuator
    Controller --> AuditLog
    classDef privacy fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secure fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    class NoiseGen,DPFilter,CryptoEnc,Policy privacy
    class Controller,AuditLog secure
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and list any four distinguishing features of CPS over traditional embedded systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A Cyber-Physical System (CPS) is an integration of computation, networking, and physical processes, where embedded computers and networks monitor, control, and feedback to coordinate with the physical world in real time.

**Four distinguishing features:**
1. **Bi-directional coupling** between cyber and physical components (closed feedback loop).
2. **Real-time constraints** with strict timing requirements for sensing, computation, and actuation.
3. **Heterogeneity** — integration of physical dynamics, software, communication, and human interaction.
4. **Distributed and networked architecture** — multiple interacting components across spatial scales.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Privacy" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Differentiate between data privacy and contextual privacy.**

**Model Answer:**
**Privacy in CPS** is the property that ensures sensitive information about users, system states, and physical processes is protected from unauthorized inference, observation, and disclosure throughout the system's lifecycle.

| Aspect | Data Privacy | Contextual Privacy |
|--------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Focus** | Protects the raw data values | Protects the meaning derived from data |
| **Example** | Encrypting an ECG waveform | Inferring a patient's disease from ECG patterns |
| **Threat** | Unauthorized access to data | Inference attacks from data |
| **Solution** | Encryption, access control | Noise injection, DP, anonymization |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In definition-based Part B questions, students often write *generic definitions* without referencing the **bi-directional coupling**, **feedback loop**, or **privacy-specific CPS threats**. Examiners expect at least **2 marks** for a context-aware definition, **2 marks** for a real-world example, and the remaining marks for the requested elaboration. Avoid one-line answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Define Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a CPS and identify the privacy-sensitive components at each layer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A CPS is a system where physical processes are monitored, coordinated, and controlled by embedded computational elements through a continuous feedback loop. The cyber and physical components are tightly coupled such that each influences the other's behavior.

**Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**

The CPS architecture consists of four layers — **Sensing Layer, Communication Layer, Computation Layer, and Actuation Layer** — with a continuous feedback loop.

```
[Sensors] → [Communication] → [Controller] → [Actuators]
   ↑                                              ↓
   └──────────── Physical Environment ────────────┘
```

**Layer-wise privacy-sensitive components (3 marks):**

| Layer | Privacy-Sensitive Component | Privacy Risk |
|-------|------------------------------|--------------|
| **Sensing Layer** | Raw sensor measurements (e.g., ECG, smart meter) | Direct exposure of personal physical state |
| **Communication Layer** | Network packets, MAC addresses, timing | Eavesdropping, traffic analysis |
| **Computation Layer** | Aggregated data, ML model parameters | Membership inference, model inversion |
| **Actuation Layer** | Control commands | Reveals system goals and operator intent |

---

**(b) Define privacy in CPS. Derive the information-theoretic privacy leakage for a linear Gaussian CPS observation model. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Privacy in CPS refers to the protection of sensitive information — including user identity, physical state, behavior patterns, and contextual attributes — from being inferred by an adversary observing the system's inputs, outputs, and side-channels.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a sensitive state $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_X, \sigma_X^2)$ observed via:

$$
Y = cX + V, \quad V \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_V^2)
$$

**Step 1:** Prior differential entropy:

$$
H(X) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(2\pi e \, \sigma_X^2)
$$

**Step 2:** Posterior variance (linear Gaussian):

$$
\sigma_{X \mid Y}^2 = \frac{\sigma_X^2 \sigma_V^2}{c^2 \sigma_X^2 + \sigma_V^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Posterior entropy:

$$
H(X \mid Y) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2\left( 2\pi e \, \sigma_{X \mid Y}^2 \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Privacy leakage (mutual information):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(X; Y) &= H(X) - H(X \mid Y) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \log_2\left( \frac{\sigma_X^2}{\sigma_{X \mid Y}^2} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{c^2 \sigma_X^2}{\sigma_V^2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The leakage increases with **SNR**. Stronger sensors → higher privacy loss. Privacy-preserving CPS design must inject noise or coarsen observations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Define the term "Privacy" in CPS. Explain the various categories of privacy threats with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Privacy in CPS is the guarantee that sensitive information about the system's users, physical states, and operational context remains confidential against unauthorized observers and inference attacks, despite the system's openness to sensing, communication, and control.

**Privacy Threat Categories (5 marks):**

1. **Identity Threats** — An adversary identifies an individual from anonymized CPS data.
   - *Example:* Re-identifying a patient in a published smart-hospital dataset using sensor fingerprints.

2. **Behavior Inference Threats** — Patterns of life are inferred from sensor streams.
   - *Example:* Smart meter data revealing sleep schedules, meal times, occupancy.

3. **Contextual Disclosure Threats** — Side information (location, time) amplifies leakage.
   - *Example:* Combining GPS with traffic data to identify daily commute routes.

4. **Actuation Privacy Threats** — Control commands reveal system policy.
   - *Example:* Inferring a power grid's load-shedding strategy from switch actuation timing.

5. **Cross-Layer Threats** — Combining cyber logs with physical signals amplifies leakage.
   - *Example:* Correlating network traffic logs with sensor noise to identify device usage.

**Conclusion (0 mark reserved, included above):** CPS privacy is multi-dimensional; defenses must address all five categories.

---

**(b) With a block diagram, explain the architecture of a privacy-preserving CPS. Discuss the role of differential privacy in such systems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The privacy-preserving CPS architecture integrates a **Privacy Module** between the **Sensor/Computation layer** and the **External Observer/Auditor**, performing **noise injection**, **DP filtering**, and **policy enforcement**.

**Role of Differential Privacy (4 marks):**

Differential Privacy (DP) is a mathematical framework that bounds the influence of any single record (or sensor sample) on the system's output.

**Mechanism:** For neighboring inputs $D, D'$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Advantages in CPS:**
1. **Quantifiable privacy guarantee** — the parameter $\epsilon$ is a tunable privacy budget.
2. **Composition properties** — privacy degrades predictably under repeated queries.
3. **Robustness** — DP holds against arbitrary auxiliary information attacks.
4. **Implementation ease** — Laplace or Gaussian noise can be added to sensor outputs.

**CPS-Specific considerations:** Adding DP noise must be balanced against the control performance; over-noising destabilizes the closed-loop system. Adaptive DP mechanisms tune $\epsilon$ based on the system's current operating regime.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Definition" (Module 1):**
>
> - **CPS Definition:** Integration of computation, communication, and physical processes with **bi-directional feedback coupling**.
> - **Privacy in CPS Definition:** Protection of sensitive information (user, state, behavior, context) from unauthorized observation and inference.
> - **Three Defining Pillars of CPS:** Cyber, Physical, Feedback Coupling.
> - **Four Key Privacy Threat Categories:** Identity, Behavior Inference, Contextual Disclosure, Actuation Privacy (plus Cross-Layer).
> - **Information-Theoretic Leakage Formula:** $I(X;Y) = \frac{1}{2} \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ for linear Gaussian systems.
> - **Differential Privacy:** $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$.
> - **Privacy vs. Utility Trade-off:** Higher privacy (noise) → lower control accuracy.
> - **Real-world examples to memorize:** Smart meter, wearable ECG, autonomous vehicle (V2X), smart grid, industrial IoT.
> - **Layered CPS architecture:** Sensing → Communication → Computation → Actuation → Feedback.
> - **Adversary Model:** An observer with access to outputs, side-channels, and possibly auxiliary information.
> - **Key Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs):** DP, k-anonymity, homomorphic encryption, secure MPC, federated learning.
> - **Examiner's Keyword Triggers:** Always mention "feedback loop", "bi-directional coupling", "side-channel", and "privacy budget" in CPS definition answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that seamlessly integrate sensing, computation, communication, and physical actuation through a tight, bidirectional coupling between the **cyber** (computational/communication) and **physical** (sensing/actuation) domains. The **Characteristics of CPS** are the distinguishing design, operational, and structural attributes that collectively differentiate CPS from traditional embedded systems, IoT, and distributed information systems.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT743 - Privacy in CPS)** curriculum, the characteristic set is foundational to understanding **where**, **how**, and **why** privacy violations emerge in CPS — because each characteristic directly defines a *privacy attack surface*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT743 - Module 1):** "Characteristics of Cyber-Physical Systems" is the gateway topic. Every privacy-preserving mechanism studied in later modules (homomorphic encryption, differential privacy, secure MPC, federated learning) exists *only because* one or more of these characteristics creates a vulnerability.

## 1.2 The Canonical CPS Characteristic Set

The peer-reviewed consensus on CPS characteristics (drawn from foundational works by Lee, Seshia, Alur, and the NIST CPS Framework) yields the following **eight core characteristics** relevant to PECPT743:

| # | Characteristic | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyber-Physical Integration** | Tight, bidirectional feedback between computation and physical processes |
| 2 | **Real-Time Operation** | Bounded, deterministic response to physical events |
| 3 | **Heterogeneity** | Mix of hardware, software, communication protocols, and physical substrates |
| 4 | **Distributed & Networked Architecture** | Geographically dispersed nodes communicating over shared channels |
| 5 | **Safety & Mission Criticality** | Failure can cause loss of life, property, or mission |
| 6 | **Autonomy & Adaptivity** | Self-governing control loops with closed-loop adaptation |
| 7 | **Continuous Sensing & Data Generation** | Persistent, high-volume, fine-grained data streams |
| 8 | **Long Lifecycle & Evolution** | Decades-long operation with iterative updates |

> [!NOTE]
> These eight characteristics are **NOT mutually exclusive** — they form a **coupled, mutually reinforcing matrix**. For example, *Real-Time Operation* (2) is impossible without *Cyber-Physical Integration* (1), and *Continuous Sensing* (7) is what enables *Autonomy* (6).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Healthcare Ward

To make these characteristics instantly memorable, consider a **modern hospital ICU ward** as a CPS:

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Smart ICU Ward**
> - **Cyber-Physical Integration** = The heart monitor (physical sensor) triggers an alarm (cyber) → the ventilator adjusts flow rate (physical actuator) automatically. The pulse oximeter value flows into the electronic health record (EHR) for ML-based sepsis prediction. A *bidirectional, closed loop* of cyber ↔ physical.
> - **Real-Time Operation** = The ventilator **must** deliver the next breath within a few milliseconds of the patient's inhale trigger. A 500 ms delay is not "slow software" — it is **patient death**.
> - **Heterogeneity** = The ward contains pulse oximeters (BLE), infusion pumps (Wi-Fi/MQTT), ventilators (proprietary CAN-bus), nurse badges (RFID), and a central server (Ethernet) — all on one network.
> - **Distributed & Networked** = A bedside monitor in Room 12 communicates with the nurses' station, the cloud ML model, and the pharmacy inventory — across multiple subnets.
> - **Safety-Criticality** = A single missed alarm or wrong drug dose is fatal.
> - **Autonomy & Adaptivity** = The infusion pump titrates insulin delivery *automatically* based on continuous glucose readings — no human in the loop.
> - **Continuous Sensing & Data Generation** = Each patient generates ~**130 MB/day** of waveform data, vitals, and metadata — streamed 24/7.
> - **Long Lifecycle** = Hospital equipment is amortized over **10–15 years**, far longer than typical IT refresh cycles of 3–5 years.

Now ask the privacy question: **Can an attacker correlate Room 12's medication timing with anonymous bed assignments, nurse badge swipe times, and visitor smartphone Wi-Fi probes to re-identify the patient — even after "de-identification"?** *Every answer in PECPT743 Module 2+ is built on this question.*

## 1.4 Why Characteristics Matter for Privacy (The Privacy Bridge)

The PECPT743 course teaches privacy **in the CPS context** — not generic data privacy. The bridge between *characteristics* and *privacy challenges* is direct:

$$\text{Privacy Risk} = f(\text{CPS Characteristic Set}) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} w_i \cdot \text{Exposure}_i(\text{Characteristic}_i)$$

Where each characteristic contributes a weighted **privacy exposure vector** $w_i$. For example:
- *Continuous Sensing* (Characteristic 7) → **$w_7$ is the highest weight** because persistent, fine-grained sensor streams enable re-identification, behavior inference, and location tracking.
- *Distributed & Networked* (Characteristic 4) → exposes data **in transit** across multiple trust boundaries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A common 3-mark question asks: *"List any four characteristics of CPS."* A 14-mark question asks: *"Explain how CPS characteristics create unique privacy challenges compared to traditional IT systems."* — answering the second requires **mapping each characteristic to a specific privacy threat**, not just listing the characteristics.

---

## 1.5 KTU-Style Mnemonic: **"CHAS-DALS"**

For exam recall, the eight characteristics can be memorized as:

**CHAS** — **DALS**
- **C**yber-Physical Integration
- **H**eterogeneity
- **A**utonomy & Adaptivity
- **S**afety-Criticality
- **D**istributed & Networked
- **A**lways-on (Continuous Sensing)
- **L**ong Lifecycle
- **S**peed (Real-Time)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why and How" Behind Each Characteristic

Below is a structured **Why/How** breakdown of each characteristic, with the **privacy implication** explicitly linked — this is the KTU high-yield content.

### 2.1.1 Cyber-Physical Integration

- **Why it exists:** To enable *physical processes to be observed, reasoned about, and manipulated* by software at a fidelity impossible with humans alone.
- **How it works:** Sensors convert physical phenomena → signals → digital data → cyber computation → control decisions → actuator commands → physical effect → sensor reads new state → loop repeats.
- **Privacy implication:** The same tight loop that enables control also enables **side-channel inference**. An attacker observing actuator commands (e.g., smart-meter power consumption) can infer private behaviors (occupancy, sleep patterns).

### 2.1.2 Real-Time Operation

- **Why it exists:** Many physical processes (braking, insulin delivery, power-grid frequency) cannot tolerate variable latency.
- **How it works:** Hard real-time CPS enforce **bounded Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** using RTOS, scheduling theory, and dedicated hardware.
- **Privacy implication:** Real-time constraints often **foreclose computationally heavy privacy mechanisms** (e.g., fully homomorphic encryption, zk-SNARKs) — creating a **privacy-vs-latency trade-off** that is central to PECPT743 design problems.

$$\text{Latency Budget: } T_{\text{budget}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{communicate}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$$

$$T_{\text{privacy}} \le T_{\text{budget}} \quad \text{(constraint for any privacy mechanism)}$$

### 2.1.3 Heterogeneity

- **Why it exists:** CPS span multiple physical domains (mechanical, electrical, chemical, biological) and multiple vendor ecosystems.
- **How it works:** Different components use different OS, communication protocols, data formats, and security postures — connected via *gateways* or *middleware*.
- **Privacy implication:** A **weakest-link problem** — the sensor with the weakest access control leaks data from the entire system. Heterogeneity also complicates **end-to-end encryption** deployment.

### 2.1.4 Distributed & Networked Architecture

- **Why it exists:** Physical processes are inherently spatially distributed (a smart grid spans continents; a vehicle has 70+ ECUs).
- **How it works:** Nodes communicate over CAN, LIN, EtherCAT, Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, etc. Often **multi-hop** with intermediate aggregators.
- **Privacy implication:** Data traverses **multiple trust boundaries**. Each hop is an *eavesdropping* or *tampering* opportunity. **Differential privacy** at the aggregator is a common mitigation.

### 2.1.5 Safety & Mission Criticality

- **Why it exists:** CPS failures can be catastrophic (airbag deploys at wrong time, nuclear reactor control).
- **How it works:** Redundancy, fail-safe design, formal verification, safety standards (ISO 26262, IEC 61508, IEC 62443).
- **Privacy implication:** Safety overrides can force **disclosure** (e.g., emergency health data broadcast) — creating tension with confidentiality.

### 2.1.6 Autonomy & Adaptivity

- **Why it exists:** Closed-loop control without humans is essential for scale (smart grid, autonomous vehicles, large-scale IoT).
- **How it works:** ML/AI models ingest sensor data → produce control actions → adapt online.
- **Privacy implication:** **Model inversion attacks** can reconstruct training data (which may be private sensor recordings) from the autonomous controller itself.

### 2.1.7 Continuous Sensing & Data Generation

- **Why it exists:** CPS must maintain situational awareness of the physical state to control it.
- **How it works:** Sensors sample at fixed rates (e.g., ECG at 250 Hz, smart meter every 15 min, autonomous vehicle LiDAR at 10 Hz).
- **Privacy implication:** **The most important characteristic for privacy.** Persistent fine-grained data enables *re-identification*, *behavioral profiling*, *inference of sensitive attributes* (e.g., health from keystroke dynamics).

**Data generation rate of a CPS:**

$$R_{\text{data}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_s} f_{s,j} \cdot b_j \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$

where $f_{s,j}$ is the sampling frequency and $b_j$ is the bits per sample for sensor $j$.

### 2.1.8 Long Lifecycle & Evolution

- **Why it exists:** Physical infrastructure (power plants, vehicles, medical devices) lasts decades; software must be updatable.
- **How it works:** OTA (Over-The-Air) updates, firmware patching, cryptographic key rotation.
- **Privacy implication:** Old cryptographic primitives become **broken** (e.g., SHA-1 collision attacks). Devices deployed with privacy-vulnerable firmware may be **impossible to patch** (recall cost, embedded constraints). **Forward secrecy** and **crypto-agility** become mandatory.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready mathematical toolkit** for any numerical or analytical question on CPS characteristics.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPS data generation rate | $R_{\text{data}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_s} f_{s,j} \cdot b_j$ | Total data volume produced per second | bits/s |
| 2 | Real-time latency budget | $T_{\text{budget}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$ | Maximum end-to-end response time | s (or ms) |
| 3 | Privacy mechanism feasibility | $T_{\text{privacy}} \le T_{\text{budget}}$ | Privacy algorithm must fit inside deadline | s |
| 4 | k-anonymity (privacy metric) | $\forall r: \vert C(r) \vert \ge k$ | Every record indistinguishable from $k-1$ others | — |
| 5 | Differential privacy (Laplace) | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Noise scale proportional to sensitivity / privacy budget | — |
| 6 | Privacy budget composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \varepsilon_i$ | Total privacy loss over $m$ queries | — |
| 7 | Re-identification upper bound (Münchner) | $P(\text{re-id}) \le \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{N}{U}$ | $N$ population, $U$ unique signatures | — |
| 8 | Cryptographic key retirement | $t_{\text{key}} \le t_{\text{device}} - t_{\text{overlap}}$ | Key must be rotated before quantum threat window | years |
| 9 | Heterogeneity index | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{p} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon entropy of protocol/device mix | bits |
| 10 | Network trust boundary count | $B = E - V + 1$ (graph) | Number of distinct trust zones ($E$ edges, $V$ nodes) | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These characteristics and their mathematical formulations directly inform engineering decisions in:

- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Safety-criticality + real-time → drives *deterministic Ethernet* and *ASIL-D* partitioning.
- **Healthcare (FDA Cyber Medical Devices):** Continuous sensing + long lifecycle → drives *post-market cybersecurity monitoring* mandates.
- **Smart Grid (IEC 61850, NIST IR 7628):** Distributed + heterogeneous → drives *substation gateway privacy* designs.
- **Industrial IoT (IEC 62443):** Autonomy + heterogeneity → drives *zone-and-conduit* security architectures.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** All 8 characteristics simultaneously → most demanding CPS design space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The eight characteristics are not an academic taxonomy — they are a **design constraint set**. Every CPS architect begins with a *characteristic profile* of the target system before choosing hardware, protocols, and security/privacy mechanisms.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivations** of the key formulas introduced in Section 2 — the type of work the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark analytical question.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CPS Data Generation Rate $R_{\text{data}}$

**Problem Setup:** A modern autonomous vehicle is equipped with:
- 8 cameras, each producing 2 MP frames at 30 fps, RGB color, 8 bits/channel
- 4 radar sensors, each producing 200 KB/s
- 1 LiDAR unit producing 60 MB/s
- 12 ultrasonic sensors, each producing 5 KB/s
- 1 GPS/IMU producing 100 Hz × 64 bytes per sample

**Step 1: List every sensor and its contribution.**

| Sensor | Count $n$ | Sampling $f_s$ | Bits/sample $b$ | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera | 8 | 30 fps | $2{,}000{,}000 \times 3 \times 8 = 4.8 \times 10^7$ | $8 \times 30 \times 4.8 \times 10^7$ |
| Radar | 4 | — | — | $4 \times 2 \times 10^5$ |
| LiDAR | 1 | — | — | $1 \times 6 \times 10^7$ |
| Ultrasonic | 12 | — | — | $12 \times 5 \times 10^3$ |
| GPS/IMU | 1 | 100 Hz | 512 bits | $1 \times 100 \times 512$ |

**Step 2: Evaluate each subtotal in bits/s.**

Camera subtotal:
$$R_{\text{cam}} = 8 \times 30 \times 4.8 \times 10^7 = 1.152 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits/s} = 11.52 \text{ Gbps}$$

Radar subtotal:
$$R_{\text{radar}} = 4 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ bits/s} = 0.8 \text{ Mbps}$$

LiDAR subtotal:
$$R_{\text{lidar}} = 1 \times 6 \times 10^7 = 6 \times 10^7 \text{ bits/s} = 60 \text{ Mbps}$$

Ultrasonic subtotal:
$$R_{\text{US}} = 12 \times 5 \times 10^3 = 6 \times 10^4 \text{ bits/s} = 60 \text{ kbps}$$

GPS/IMU subtotal:
$$R_{\text{GPS}} = 100 \times 512 = 5.12 \times 10^4 \text{ bits/s} = 51.2 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3: Sum all subtotals using the canonical formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{data}} &= \sum_{j=1}^{n_s} f_{s,j} \cdot b_j \\
&= R_{\text{cam}} + R_{\text{radar}} + R_{\text{lidar}} + R_{\text{US}} + R_{\text{GPS}} \\
&= 1.152 \times 10^{10} + 8 \times 10^5 + 6 \times 10^7 + 6 \times 10^4 + 5.12 \times 10^4 \\
&= 1.152 \times 10^{10} + 0.008 \times 10^9 + 0.060 \times 10^9 + \text{negligible} \\
&\approx 1.159 \times 10^{10} \text{ bits/s} \\
&\approx 11.59 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpret for privacy.**

This is **~1.45 GB/s** of *raw, often plaintext* sensor data. The privacy implication: this volume of data, if exfiltrated, allows full reconstruction of a vehicle's route, behavior, occupants' faces (camera), and even passenger heart rate (ballistocardiography from座椅 pressure sensors). **This is why Module 2+ of PECPT743 studies on-device privacy mechanisms** — the data rate is too high to upload to a trusted cloud in plaintext.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Privacy Mechanism Latency Feasibility

**Problem Setup:** A smart insulin pump must respond to a continuous glucose monitor (CGM) reading within **$T_{\text{budget}} = 250$ ms**. Three privacy mechanisms are proposed:

| Mechanism | $T_{\text{privacy}}$ | Passes Constraint? |
|---|---|---|
| M1: TLS 1.3 encryption | 1.8 ms | ✅ |
| M2: AES-256-GCM + HMAC | 0.4 ms | ✅ |
| M3: Fully Homomorphic Encryption (CKKS scheme) | 180 ms | ❌ |
| M4: Differential privacy (Laplace noise addition) | 0.05 ms | ✅ |
| M5: Secure Multi-Party Computation (3-party) | 120 ms | ✅ (marginal) |

**Step 1: Apply the constraint $T_{\text{privacy}} \le T_{\text{budget}}$.**

$$T_{\text{privacy}}^{(i)} \le 250 \text{ ms} \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate each candidate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{privacy}}^{(1)} &= 1.8 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; 1.8 \le 250 \;\; \checkmark \\
T_{\text{privacy}}^{(2)} &= 0.4 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.4 \le 250 \;\; \checkmark \\
T_{\text{privacy}}^{(3)} &= 180 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; 180 \le 250 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (tight)} \\
T_{\text{privacy}}^{(4)} &= 0.05 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; 0.05 \le 250 \;\; \checkmark \\
T_{\text{privacy}}^{(5)} &= 120 \text{ ms} \;\Rightarrow\; 120 \le 250 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (very tight)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Design recommendation.**

A **layered approach** combining M2 (confidentiality) + M4 (statistical privacy) is feasible and provides defense-in-depth. FHE (M3) is **not deployable** in this CPS without hardware acceleration (FPGA/ASIC), illustrating the **privacy-vs-real-time trade-off** central to CPS design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Differential Privacy Budget Composition

**Problem Setup:** A smart grid releases aggregated household consumption data to researchers. Each query is answered with $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. The utility runs $m = 100$ queries over a billing cycle. What is the total privacy loss?

**Step 1: State the basic sequential composition theorem.**

For a sequence of mechanisms $\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{M}_m$ each providing $\varepsilon_i$-differential privacy, the composed mechanism provides:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step 2: Assume uniform privacy budget allocation (typical KTU assumption).**

Each query: $\varepsilon_i = 0.1$. Total queries: $m = 100$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{100} 0.1 \\
&= 100 \times 0.1 \\
&= 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Interpret.**

An $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 10$ is **catastrophically weak** for privacy — it provides almost no protection. Real systems cap $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \le 1$ (strong) to $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \le 5$ (moderate).

**Step 4: Recompute under safe allocation.**

To keep $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \le 1$ over 100 queries:

$$\varepsilon_i \le \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{total}}}{m} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01$$

This illustrates the **continuous sensing characteristic** (7) — a CPS can issue thousands of queries, rapidly exhausting any finite privacy budget.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_1 \cdot \varepsilon_2$ (multiplication) for sequential composition. This is **wrong**. Sequential composition is **additive** under the basic theorem. (Advanced theorems like Rényi DP allow tighter bounds, but the KTU syllabus uses the basic additive form.)

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — k-Anonymity Threshold

**Problem Setup:** A hospital releases a de-identified patient dataset. The quasi-identifier set is {age, ZIP, gender}. For $k$-anonymity, every combination of QI values must appear in at least $k$ rows. The smallest equivalence class size in the released data is found to be 3.

**Step 1: Apply the k-anonymity constraint.**

$$\forall r: \vert C(r) \vert \ge k$$

where $C(r)$ is the equivalence class containing record $r$.

**Step 2: Identify the minimum class size.**

$$\min_r \vert C(r) \vert = 3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = 3$$

**Step 3: Privacy interpretation.**

An attacker can narrow a target down to **1 out of 3 individuals** — clearly insufficient for most privacy regulations. Standard medical privacy guidelines require $k \ge 5$ (HIPAA Safe Harbor) or $k \ge 11$ (EU EDPB guidance for strong anonymization).

**Step 4: Recommended action — generalize or suppress QI values.**

If $k = 5$ is required, ZIP must be truncated to the first 3 digits, and age to a 10-year band. The privacy-vs-utility trade-off is explicit:

$$\text{Utility}(k) = \frac{1}{k} \quad \text{(information content decreases as } k \text{ increases)}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Characteristics × Privacy Threat Mapping

This is the **single most exam-relevant analytical artifact** in PECPT743 Module 1. It must be memorized for any 14-mark question.

| CPS Characteristic | Primary Privacy Threat | Example Attack | Standard Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cyber-Physical Integration** | Side-channel inference from actuator outputs | Smart-meter load monitoring reveals TV watching habits | Load signature randomization, differential privacy |
| **Real-Time Operation** | Privacy mechanism cannot be deployed | E.g., FHE exceeds deadline → developer disables encryption | Hardware acceleration, lightweight crypto (ASCON) |
| **Heterogeneity** | Weakest-link exploitation | Unsecured BLE medical device leaks patient vitals | Unified identity management, security gateways |
| **Distributed & Networked** | Eavesdropping in transit, man-in-the-middle | Attacker on shared CAN bus injects false messages | TLS/DTLS, mutual authentication, network segmentation |
| **Safety-Criticality** | Emergency disclosure overrides privacy | eCall system transmits GPS location unencrypted | Selective disclosure, conditional anonymity |
| **Autonomy & Adaptivity** | Model inversion, membership inference | Reconstruct training data from autonomous controller | Federated learning, on-device training, DP-SGD |
| **Continuous Sensing & Data Generation** | Re-identification, behavior profiling, inference | 15-min smart meter data infers household occupancy, sleep, religion | k-anonymity, DP, on-device aggregation |
| **Long Lifecycle & Evolution** | Cryptographic obsolescence, unpatched vulnerabilities | SHA-1-broken medical device, 15 years old | Crypto-agility, post-quantum crypto planning |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question will often ask: *"Pick any FOUR characteristics and explain how they create unique privacy challenges in CPS compared to traditional IT systems."* This matrix gives you the **complete answer template** — choose four rows and expand each to ~3.5 marks of explanation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The 3C Architecture of CPS (Cyber-Physical-Cognitive Loop)

This block diagram shows how the eight characteristics emerge from a single fundamental feedback loop. **Mermaid-compliant node IDs and clean label text** are used throughout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PHYSICAL WORLD\nSensors Actuators\nEnvironment"] -->|"Analog Signal\nContinuous Sensing"| node2
    node2["CYBER LAYER\nComputation\nCommunication\nRTOS + ML"] -->|"Control Command\nReal Time\nAutonomy"| node3
    node3["ACTUATOR OUTPUT\nPhysical Effect\nSide Channel\nInference"] -->|"Process Change"| node1
    node4["HETEROGENEOUS\nDEVICES\nBLE WiFi CAN\nLIN EtherCAT"] -.-> node2
    node5["DISTRIBUTED\nNODES\nMulti Hop\nNetwork"] -.-> node2
    node6["SAFETY\nCRITICALITY\nISO 26262\nIEC 62443"] -.-> node2
    node7["LONG LIFECYCLE\nOTA UPDATES\nCrypto Agility"] -.-> node2
    node8["PRIVACY\nATTACK SURFACE\nRe Identification\nBehavior Inference"] -.-> node3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The central triangle (node1 → node2 → node3 → node1) is the **canonical CPS feedback loop**. The four dashed arrows (node4–node7) are **architectural characteristics** that shape the cyber layer. The privacy attack surface (node8) emerges wherever data leaves the loop — typically at sensor transmission or actuator output.

    end

    A --> T1 --> M1

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Privacy-vs-Real-Time Trade-off Surface

This schematic illustrates the fundamental engineering trade-off in CPS privacy design: **stronger privacy mechanisms consume more computation time**, threatening real-time deadlines.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PRIVACY STRENGTH\nWeak to Strong\nNo Protection to FHE"] --> nodeB
    nodeB["COMPUTATION\nLATENCY\nT privacy\nmilliseconds"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["REAL TIME\nDEADLINE\nT budget\n250 ms example"] --> nodeD
    nodeD["DESIGN\nOUTCOME\nFeasible or\nInfeasible"]
    nodeA -.->|"Trade off"| nodeC
    nodeE["LIGHTWEIGHT CRYPTO\nAES GCM HMAC\n0.4 ms"] -.->|"Region 1\nInfeasible"| nodeD
    nodeF["DP LAPLACE NOISE\n0.05 ms"] -.->|"Region 2\nFeasible"| nodeD
    nodeG["SECURE MPC\n120 ms"] -.->|"Region 3\nMarginal"| nodeD
    nodeH["FULLY HOMOMORPHIC\nFHE 180 ms"] -.->|"Region 4\nInfeasible\nNeeds HW"| nodeD
```

**Interpretation:** The privacy engineer must select a mechanism whose $T_{\text{privacy}}$ falls **strictly below** the system $T_{\text{budget}}$. For high-privacy mechanisms (FHE, MPC) that exceed the deadline, **hardware acceleration** (FPGA, ASIC, secure enclaves like ARM TrustZone / Intel SGX) becomes mandatory.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Lifecycle Privacy Degradation Curve

This diagram shows how a CPS's privacy posture **degrades over its operational lifetime** due to Characteristic 8 (Long Lifecycle) — a uniquely CPS phenomenon absent in traditional IT.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeY0["YEAR 0\nDeployment\nCrypto Fresh\nPatched"] --> nodeY3["YEAR 3\nIT Refresh\nComparable"]
    nodeY3 --> nodeY7["YEAR 7\nCrypto Aging\nPatching Slow"]
    nodeY7 --> nodeY12["YEAR 12\nVulnerable\nUnpatched"]
    nodeY12 --> nodeY15["YEAR 15\nEOL\nNo Updates"]
    nodeY0 -.->|"Privacy Level\nHigh"| nodeP1["STRONG"]
    nodeY3 -.->|"Privacy Level\nGood"| nodeP2["ACCEPTABLE"]
    nodeY7 -.->|"Privacy Level\nDegrading"| nodeP3["WEAK"]
    nodeY12 -.->|"Privacy Level\nCritical Risk"| nodeP4["BROKEN"]
    nodeY15 -.->|"Privacy Level\nAbsent"| nodeP5["NONE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why PECPT743 Module 3+ studies **forward-secret** and **post-quantum cryptographic** mechanisms. A CPS deployed in 2024 may still be operational in 2044 — long after RSA-2048 and ECDSA become breakable by quantum adversaries.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

> **"List any THREE characteristics of Cyber-Physical Systems and state one privacy implication for each."**

**Model Answer (Structured for 3 marks):**

| # | Characteristic | Privacy Implication (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Continuous Sensing & Data Generation** | Persistent, fine-grained sensor data enables re-identification and behavior profiling even after de-identification. |
| 2 | **Real-Time Operation** | Real-time deadlines prevent deployment of computationally heavy privacy mechanisms (e.g., FHE), forcing weaker privacy in safety-critical CPS. |
| 3 | **Distributed & Networked Architecture** | Data traverses multiple trust boundaries, increasing eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attack surface. |

*(Alternative valid characteristics: Cyber-Physical Integration, Heterogeneity, Safety-Criticality, Autonomy, Long Lifecycle.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]`

> **"Differentiate between a Cyber-Physical System and a traditional embedded system based on characteristics."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A traditional embedded system is a **single-device, fixed-function** computational unit (e.g., a microwave controller). A CPS, in contrast, is characterized by:

1. **Networked and Distributed** — CPS nodes communicate over shared networks (CAN, 5G, Wi-Fi) with other CPS and the cloud; embedded systems are typically standalone. *(1 mark)*
2. **Cyber-Physical Coupling with Real-Time Control** — CPS have a tight, closed feedback loop with the physical process and bounded latency; embedded systems may run non-real-time firmware with no physical actuation loop. *(1 mark)*
3. **Continuous Data Generation and Long Lifecycle** — CPS produce high-volume sensor streams over decades; embedded systems have bounded, low-volume I/O and shorter firmware refresh cycles. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> **"With suitable examples, explain in detail the EIGHT characteristics of Cyber-Physical Systems and discuss how EACH characteristic creates unique privacy challenges that are not present in traditional IT systems."**

#### Part A(a) — The Eight Characteristics (7 Marks)

| # | Characteristic | Definition + Example (≈0.9 marks each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyber-Physical Integration** | Tight bidirectional coupling between cyber and physical layers. *Example: Autonomous vehicle LiDAR → perception stack → braking actuator in 50 ms.* |
| 2 | **Real-Time Operation** | Deterministic, bounded response time. *Example: Insulin pump must deliver dose within 250 ms of CGM reading.* |
| 3 | **Heterogeneity** | Multiple OS, protocols, hardware. *Example: Smart factory with PROFINET, OPC-UA, Modbus, MQTT.* |
| 4 | **Distributed & Networked** | Geographically dispersed nodes. *Example: Smart grid spans substations across a country, communicating via IEC 61850.* |
| 5 | **Safety & Mission Criticality** | Failure causes loss of life/property. *Example: Aircraft fly-by-wire system, nuclear reactor control.* |
| 6 | **Autonomy & Adaptivity** | Self-governing closed-loop control with ML. *Example: Tesla FSD adapting to new road signs via OTA-learned models.* |
| 7 | **Continuous Sensing & Data Generation** | Persistent fine-grained data streams. *Example: ICU patient generating 130 MB/day of vitals.* |
| 8 | **Long Lifecycle & Evolution** | Decades of operation with updates. *Example: Industrial PLC deployed 2005, still operational 2025.* |

#### Part A(b) — Unique Privacy Challenges (7 Marks)

For each characteristic, a *unique* privacy challenge — one that does **not** exist in traditional IT — must be articulated:

1. **Cyber-Physical Integration → Side-Channel Privacy Leak:** Smart-meter power consumption traces (an actuator output) reveal appliance usage, sleep schedules, and number of occupants — a *physical-domain inference* with no IT-system analog.
2. **Real-Time Operation → Forced Privacy Degradation:** Real-time deadlines exclude strong but slow privacy primitives. CPS developers often *disable* encryption to meet deadlines, a trade-off that does not exist in batch IT systems.
3. **Heterogeneity → Cross-Domain Re-identification:** Data from heterogeneous sources (e.g., wearable ECG + smart speaker audio + room light sensor) can be cross-correlated to re-identify an anonymous user, even when each source alone is "anonymized."
4. **Distributed & Networked → Trust Boundary Multiplication:** Each network hop is a new eavesdropping point. In an industrial CPS, an attacker on a single compromised PLC can leak data from the entire production line.
5. **Safety-Criticality → Coerced Disclosure:** Emergency systems (eCall, medical alert) may be legally required to broadcast identifying data, conflicting with confidentiality. The *safety-vs-privacy* trade-off has no IT counterpart.
6. **Autonomy & Adaptivity → Model Inversion & Membership Inference:** An attacker who steals the autonomous controller's ML model can reconstruct private training sensor data (e.g., a patient's ECG traces used to train an arrhythmia detector).
7. **Continuous Sensing → Persistent Re-identification Risk:** Unlike IT systems where data is event-driven, CPS data is *always on*, generating enough temporal granularity to defeat k-anonymity and DP unless carefully engineered.
8. **Long Lifecycle → Privacy Decay Over Time:** CPS deployed today must remain private-secure for 15+ years. Cryptographic obsolescence, unpatched vulnerabilities, and outdated privacy regulations cause *time-dependent* privacy degradation — unique to CPS.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write a *generic* answer about IT privacy (e.g., "data breaches," "unauthorized access"). This will score at most 4–5 marks out of 7 in part (b). The examiner awards full marks **only** when the privacy challenge is shown to be *uniquely enabled* by a CPS characteristic. Use phrasing such as: *"Unlike traditional IT, this characteristic causes …"*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Model]`

> **"For a Smart Healthcare CPS (e.g., a connected ICU ward), describe its architecture and explain how the characteristics of CPS jointly create privacy challenges. Propose a layered privacy-preserving design that addresses at least FOUR characteristics."**

#### Part B(a) — Smart ICU Architecture (7 Marks)

A Smart ICU CPS architecture consists of:

- **Sensing Layer:** Bedside monitors (ECG, SpO₂, BP), infusion pumps, ventilators, RFID nurse badges, BLE wearables, ambient cameras.
- **Communication Layer:** Wi-Fi (HL7/FHIR), ZigBee (vitals), BLE (wearables), CAN (ventilator internal), wired Ethernet to EHR server.
- **Cyber Layer:** Edge gateway running real-time analytics, ML-based sepsis prediction, drug-interaction checking.
- **Physical/Actuation Layer:** Automated drug delivery, ventilator adjustments, alert dispatch to nurses.

The **eight CPS characteristics manifest** in this system as follows:
- *Integration:* Vitals → ML model → drug pump adjustment loop.
- *Real-Time:* Ventilator breath delivery within tens of ms.
- *Heterogeneity:* Mixed protocols (HL7, BLE, CAN).
- *Distributed:* 20+ ICU rooms, central nursing station, cloud ML.
- *Safety-Critical:* Any failure risks patient death.
- *Autonomy:* Closed-loop insulin titration.
- *Continuous Sensing:* ~130 MB/day per patient, 24/7.
- *Long Lifecycle:* ICU equipment amortized over 12+ years.

#### Part B(b) — Layered Privacy Design (7 Marks)

A privacy-by-design solution addressing at least four characteristics:

| Layer | Addresses Characteristic(s) | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **1. On-Device Encryption** | Distributed & Networked (4), Long Lifecycle (8) | AES-256-GCM at sensor; TLS 1.3 in transit; crypto-agility for PQC migration |
| **2. Edge Differential Privacy** | Continuous Sensing (7), Autonomy (6) | Add Laplace noise ($\varepsilon = 0.5$) to aggregated vitals before cloud upload; preserves statistical utility while preventing re-identification |
| **3. Federated Learning at Edge** | Autonomy (6), Distributed (4) | ML model trained across ICU rooms without centralizing raw patient data; DP-SGD for additional protection |
| **4. Real-Time-Compatible Lightweight Crypto** | Real-Time Operation (2) | Use ASCON (NIST LWC standard) — ~0.4 ms latency — instead of slow RSA/AES chains |
| **5. Heterogeneous Gateway with Unified IAM** | Heterogeneity (3) | Single identity broker translates between BLE, Wi-Fi, CAN identities; enforces least-privilege per device type |
| **6. k-Anonymized Release** | Continuous Sensing (7) | Patient discharge summaries generalized to k = 5 before research release |

**Justification:** The design respects the **privacy-vs-real-time trade-off** (Characteristic 2) by selecting only those mechanisms whose $T_{\text{privacy}} \le T_{\text{budget}}$ (see Section 3.2 derivation). The layered structure ensures **defense-in-depth** — no single mechanism failure leaks patient data.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must know for KTU exam)**

- ✅ **Eight canonical CPS characteristics:** Cyber-Physical Integration, Real-Time, Heterogeneity, Distributed, Safety-Critical, Autonomy, Continuous Sensing, Long Lifecycle. (Mnemonic: **CHAS-DALS**)
- ✅ **Continuous Sensing is the #1 privacy-critical characteristic** — it is what makes CPS privacy fundamentally harder than IT privacy.
- ✅ **Privacy is NOT a single mechanism** — it is a *layered* property arising from the joint interaction of all eight characteristics.
- ✅ **Real-time constraint formula:** $T_{\text{privacy}} \le T_{\text{budget}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$
- ✅ **Sequential DP composition is ADDITIVE:** $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \varepsilon_i$ — NOT multiplicative.
- ✅ **k-Anonymity threshold:** $\forall r: \vert C(r) \vert \ge k$ — typical $k \ge 5$ for medical, $k \ge 11$ for strong anonymization.
- ✅ **Privacy decay over lifecycle:** CPS deployed today must remain private for 15+ years → demand *forward secrecy* and *post-quantum crypto agility*.
- ✅ **Re-identification paradox:** De-identification alone is insufficient in CPS because **continuous, multi-modal sensor data** enables cross-source correlation attacks.
- ✅ **Standard mappings to remember for exam:**
  - Continuous Sensing → Re-identification → k-anonymity / DP
  - Distributed Networked → Eavesdropping → TLS / DTLS
  - Autonomy → Model inversion → Federated learning / DP-SGD
  - Long Lifecycle → Crypto obsolescence → PQC / Crypto-agility
- ✅ **Always link characteristic → threat → mitigation** in 14-mark answers; the examiner's key is structured around this triad.
- ✅ **Reference standards to cite (if asked):** ISO 26262 (automotive safety), IEC 62443 (industrial security), NIST IR 7628 (smart grid), FDA premarket cybersecurity guidance (medical), NIST CPS Framework.

> **End of PECPT743 — Module 1 — Characteristics Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Architecture of CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components and Architecture of CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered, networked system-of-systems that integrates **computation, networking, and physical processes** through a continuous, bidirectional feedback loop. Embedded computers and networks monitor (sense) and control (actuate) the physical environment, typically with real-time, safety-critical, and reliability constraints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A CPS is a mechanism that is controlled or monitored by computer-based algorithms, tightly integrated with the Internet and its users. In the context of privacy, CPS includes physical infrastructures (sensors, actuators, embedded devices) that collect, transmit, and process sensitive user data, creating a unique attack surface that combines cyber vulnerabilities with physical-world consequences.

The key characteristics of a CPS are:

- **Cyber-Physical Coupling:** The behavior of the physical system affects the software and vice versa, in real time.
- **Heterogeneity:** Composed of hardware, software, networking, and physical components.
- **Temporal Constraints:** Operates under strict timing (real-time) requirements.
- **Safety-Critical:** Failures can cause physical harm, environmental damage, or privacy breaches.
- **Networked & Distributed:** Components are spread across geographical and logical boundaries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

To intuitively understand a CPS, consider the **human body**:

| CPS Component | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors** | Eyes, ears, skin, nerves | Collect raw data from the physical world |
| **Communication Network** | Nervous system | Transmits signals to the brain |
| **Computation / Controller** | Brain | Processes data, makes decisions |
| **Actuators** | Muscles, glands | Performs actions on the physical world |
| **Feedback Loop** | Reflexes / homeostasis | Closes the loop for adaptive control |
| **Physical Process** | Body organs / environment | The world being sensed and controlled |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy Implication:** Just as the human body's sensory data (heart rate, location, speech) is highly private, the data collected by CPS sensors is intimately tied to user identity, behavior, and location — making privacy a first-class design concern in PECPT743.

## 1.3 CPS vs. IoT vs. Embedded Systems

It is important to distinguish CPS from related paradigms:

- **Embedded System:** A single device with a microcontroller running dedicated firmware (e.g., a washing machine controller). It may not be networked or have feedback with the physical world.
- **IoT (Internet of Things):** A network of physical objects ("things") with sensors, software, and connectivity. Focuses primarily on *connectivity and data exchange*.
- **CPS:** Builds on IoT and embedded systems, but adds the **closed-loop control of physical processes** as a first-class citizen. The "cyber" part actively *changes* the physical state.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** "CPS = Sense → Compute → Actuate → Repeat, in real time, with feedback." If a system merely *monitors* without changing the physical state, it is closer to IoT. If it *controls* the physical process, it is a true CPS.

---

## 1.4 Why CPS Architecture Matters for Privacy

In PECPT743, understanding the **architecture** of CPS is the foundation for understanding *where* privacy can be breached:

1. **Sensor layer** → sensitive raw data is captured (e.g., camera, microphone, GPS).
2. **Communication layer** → data is transmitted (vulnerable to eavesdropping).
3. **Computation/Storage layer** → data is processed and stored (vulnerable to inference attacks).
4. **Actuation layer** → side effects of actuation can leak information (e.g., smart meter load signatures).
5. **Application layer** → user-facing services expose data to third parties.

Each architectural layer is a **privacy threat surface**, and the rest of this course will study mitigation techniques at each layer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Components of a CPS

A CPS, at its lowest level of abstraction, is composed of **five foundational components**. Each component plays a specific role and contributes to the overall privacy posture of the system.

### 2.1.1 Sensors (The Physical-to-Cyber Bridge)

Sensors are transducers that convert **physical phenomena** (temperature, pressure, light, motion, sound, chemical concentration) into **electrical signals** that can be processed by digital systems.

**Categories:**
- **Active sensors:** Emit energy (e.g., LiDAR, radar) and measure the reflection.
- **Passive sensors:** Measure ambient energy (e.g., thermistor, camera, microphone).
- **Scalar sensors:** Output a single value (e.g., temperature).
- **Vector sensors:** Output multi-dimensional data (e.g., IMU — accelerometer + gyroscope + magnetometer).

**Key Specification Metrics (Bold for Memorization):**
- **Sampling Rate (Hz):** Number of samples per second.
- **Resolution (bits):** Quantization depth of the ADC.
- **Dynamic Range (dB):** Ratio between largest and smallest detectable signal.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR, in dB).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy Insight:** A higher sampling rate and resolution means *more granular* (and therefore more invasive) data. A microphone sampling at 44.1 kHz vs. 8 kHz captures dramatically more private information (voice, background speech). Sensor specifications are directly a **privacy parameter**.

### 2.1.2 Actuators (The Cyber-to-Physical Bridge)

Actuators perform the inverse operation of sensors: they convert **electrical control signals** into **physical actions** (movement, heating, opening a valve, displaying information).

**Common Actuator Types:**
- **Electrical:** Motors, solenoids, relays, LEDs.
- **Mechanical:** Hydraulic / pneumatic cylinders, robotic arms.
- **Thermal:** Heaters, Peltier coolers.
- **Chemical:** Dosing pumps, gas release valves.

**Key Specification Metrics:**
- **Response time (ms):** Delay from command to physical change.
- **Precision and accuracy.**
- **Power consumption (W).**

### 2.1.3 Communication Subsystem (The Nervous System)

The communication subsystem is responsible for **data transfer** between sensors, controllers, actuators, and external systems (cloud, other CPS nodes, users).

**Communication paradigms in CPS:**

| Paradigm | Range | Example Protocols | CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wired Fieldbus** | < 100 m | CAN, Modbus, Profibus | Industrial CPS, automotive |
| **PAN / LAN** | < 100 m | Bluetooth, ZigBee, Wi-Fi | Smart home, wearables |
| **LPWAN** | 1–10 km | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Sigfox | Smart city, agriculture |
| **Cellular** | Wide | 4G/5G, LTE-M | V2X, telemedicine |
| **Wired Backhaul** | Wide | Ethernet, Fiber | Data center CPS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy Impact:** Communication channels are the **eavesdropping frontier**. Protocols with weak or no encryption (e.g., legacy ZigBee, plain MQTT) leak sensor data in transit. The choice of protocol directly determines the attack surface.

### 2.1.4 Computation Subsystem (The Brain)

The computation subsystem consists of **embedded processors, microcontrollers, FPGAs, edge servers, or cloud platforms** that execute the control logic, data analytics, and decision-making algorithms.

**Tiers of Computation in CPS:**

1. **Sensor-level / Edge tier:** TinyML on microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+).
2. **Gateway / Fog tier:** ARM Cortex-A class, Raspberry Pi, edge servers.
3. **Cloud tier:** Hyperscale data centers for ML, big-data analytics, coordination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy-Utility Trade-off:** Performing computation closer to the sensor (edge) reduces data in transit (better privacy) but is computationally limited. Cloud computation offers powerful analytics but requires sharing raw data (worse privacy). **Federated learning and differential privacy** are emerging solutions.

### 2.1.5 Physical Process (The Plant)

In control theory, the "plant" is the **physical system being controlled** — the engine, the chemical reactor, the room, the patient, the power grid. The plant has its own **dynamics** governed by physical laws (Newton's laws, thermodynamics, fluid dynamics, etc.).

The plant is described by its **state variables** $x(t)$, which evolve according to a **state-space model**:

$$\dot{x}(t) = f(x(t), u(t), w(t))$$

$$y(t) = h(x(t), v(t))$$

where $u(t)$ is the control input, $y(t)$ is the measured output, and $w(t), v(t)$ are process and measurement noise.

## 2.2 The Closed-Loop Feedback Control Paradigm

The defining architectural pattern of CPS is the **closed-loop feedback controller**. Unlike open-loop systems (where the control action is predetermined), a closed-loop system continuously:

1. **Sense** the current state of the plant.
2. **Compare** the sensed state to a desired reference.
3. **Compute** a control action using a control law (e.g., PID, MPC, RL).
4. **Actuate** the physical system.
5. **Repeat** at the next sampling instant.

**Generic Closed-Loop Block Diagram (in words):**

$$\text{Reference} \, r(t) \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow u(t) \rightarrow \text{Plant} \rightarrow y(t) \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Comparator} \rightarrow e(t) \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \ldots$$

The **error signal** is $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following table. It is high-yield for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| State-space model | $\dot{x}(t) = f(x, u, w)$ | Continuous-time plant dynamics |
| Output equation | $y(t) = h(x, v)$ | Sensor model |
| PID control law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are gains |
| Sampling period | $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz) |
| Nyquist criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency |
| Latency budget | $T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$ | Must be $< T_{\text{deadline}}$ |
| SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\max}}{V_{\min}}\right)$ | dB, voltage ratio |
| Data rate (sensor) | $R = f_s \cdot b \cdot c$ | $b$ = bits/sample, $c$ = channels |
| Energy per bit (comm) | $E_b = P_{\text{TX}} / R$ | Joules/bit, key for IoT CPS |
| Actuator response | $\tau_a$ | Time constant in seconds |

**Boundary Conditions and Real-World Constraints:**

- $T_{\text{latency}} \le T_{\text{deadline}}$ — otherwise the system is **unsafe** (hard real-time failure).
- $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$ — otherwise **aliasing** corrupts sensor data.
- $\text{SNR} > 0 \text{ dB}$ — required for useful sensing; usually SNR $> 40$ dB is desired.
- $K_p, K_i, K_d \ge 0$ — negative gains in a basic feedback loop cause instability.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility and Engineering Domains

CPS architectures underpin every modern safety-critical and data-intensive system:

- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS / SCADA):** Power plants, water treatment, oil & gas.
- **Automotive:** ADAS, autonomous driving, V2X.
- **Healthcare:** Implantable devices, robotic surgery, remote patient monitoring.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic control, smart grids, environmental monitoring.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** UAVs, satellite control, missile guidance.
- **Robotics:** Manufacturing, surgical, service, and collaborative robots.
- **Smart Homes & Wearables:** The consumer IoT frontier, where PECPT743's privacy focus is paramount.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time Deadline Constraint for a CPS Control Loop

**Problem Setup (KTU-style problem):**
A CPS is composed of: a sensor with sampling rate $f_s$, a communication channel with propagation delay $T_{\text{comm}}$, a controller with worst-case execution time (WCET) $T_{\text{compute}}$, and an actuator with mechanical response time $\tau_a$. The overall deadline for closing the control loop is $T_{\text{deadline}}$. Derive the condition that guarantees the system meets its real-time requirement.

### Step 1: Identify the components of total loop latency

The total time elapsed between the physical phenomenon occurring and the actuator's response can be decomposed as:

$$T_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}}$$

**Explanation of each term:**

- $T_{\text{sense}}$ = time for the sensor to acquire and digitize a sample. This is bounded below by the **analog front-end settling time** and the **ADC conversion time**.
- $T_{\text{comm}}$ = sum of transmission, propagation, queuing, and reception delays across all network hops.
- $T_{\text{compute}}$ = the **WCET** of the control algorithm on the chosen hardware.
- $T_{\text{actuate}}$ = the actuator's mechanical/electrical response time constant.

### Step 2: Express each term in measurable engineering quantities

$$T_{\text{sense}} = \frac{1}{f_s} = T_s$$

$$T_{\text{comm}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{\text{prop},i} + T_{\text{tx},i} + T_{\text{queue},i} \right)$$

$$T_{\text{compute}} = \text{WCET}_{\text{controller}}$$

$$T_{\text{actuate}} = \tau_a$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the total latency expression

$$T_{\text{latency}} = T_s + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{\text{prop},i} + T_{\text{tx},i} + T_{\text{queue},i} \right) + \text{WCET}_{\text{controller}} + \tau_a$$

### Step 4: State the real-time deadline condition

For the CPS to be **safe and correct** under hard real-time constraints, the total latency must be strictly less than the deadline:

$$T_s + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{\text{prop},i} + T_{\text{tx},i} + T_{\text{queue},i} \right) + \text{WCET}_{\text{controller}} + \tau_a \;\le\; T_{\text{deadline}}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to expose a design constraint on the sampling period

Solving for $T_s$ (the only term under the designer's direct control via sensor selection):

$$T_s \;\le\; T_{\text{deadline}} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{\text{prop},i} + T_{\text{tx},i} + T_{\text{queue},i} \right) - \text{WCET}_{\text{controller}} - \tau_a$$

**Interpretation:** A larger $T_{\text{deadline}}$ permits slower sampling. Tighter deadlines require faster sensors, leaner control code, or lower-latency networks.

### Step 6: Apply the Nyquist sampling bound

To avoid aliasing, the sampling rate $f_s = 1/T_s$ must satisfy:

$$f_s \;\ge\; 2 \cdot f_{\max}$$

where $f_{\max}$ is the highest frequency component in the plant's relevant dynamics.

### Step 7: Combine the real-time and Nyquist constraints

The feasible design space for $T_s$ is the intersection:

$$\max\!\left( \frac{1}{2 f_{\max}}, \, 0 \right) \;\le\; T_s \;\le\; T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{comm}} - \text{WCET}_{\text{controller}} - \tau_a$$

If the upper bound is **less than** the lower bound, the system is **unrealizable** with the chosen components — the CPS architect must replace a component (e.g., use a faster network, a more powerful controller, or a faster actuator).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensor Data Rate and Privacy Cost

**Problem Setup:**
A wearable CPS contains $c$ sensor channels, each sampling at $f_s$ Hz with a quantization depth of $b$ bits. Derive the raw data rate $R$ and the **privacy cost** in terms of bits per second that the user is "exposing."

### Step 1: Samples per second per channel

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_s} \quad \text{[Hz, samples/sec]}$$

### Step 2: Bits per sample per channel

$$b \quad \text{[bits/sample]}$$

### Step 3: Bits per second for a single channel

$$R_1 = f_s \cdot b$$

### Step 4: Bits per second for $c$ channels

$$R = c \cdot f_s \cdot b$$

### Step 5: Convert to bytes and bits

$$R_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{c \cdot f_s \cdot b}{8} \quad \text{[bytes/sec]}$$

$$R_{\text{bits}} = c \cdot f_s \cdot b \quad \text{[bits/sec, or bps]}$$

### Step 6: Privacy interpretation

Define the **Privacy Exposure Rate** $P_e$ as the volume of identifiable raw data leaving the device:

$$P_e = R_{\text{bits}} = c \cdot f_s \cdot b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
