> **Privacy-Utility Insight:** Increasing $f_s$ or $b$ (for higher-fidelity sensing) linearly increases $P_e$. Techniques such as **on-device feature extraction**, **federated learning**, and **differential privacy** can be applied to *reduce* $P_e$ without sacrificing utility, by transmitting only derived statistics instead of raw samples.

### Step 7: Numerical example (typical wearable CPS)

Let $c = 3$ (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer), $f_s = 100$ Hz, $b = 16$ bits.

$$R = 3 \times 100 \times 16 = 4800 \text{ bits/sec} = 600 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

$$R_{\text{per minute}} = 600 \times 60 = 36{,}000 \text{ bytes/min} \approx 35.16 \text{ KB/min}$$

This is a substantial continuous data stream — a clear privacy exposure if transmitted unencrypted.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Components to Architecture Layers and Privacy Threats

This matrix maps the foundational CPS components to the standard 5-layer architecture and identifies the **primary privacy threats** at each layer. **Memorize this table** — it is high-yield for the 14-mark question.

| Architecture Layer | Component Examples | Primary Function | Primary Privacy Threat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1: Perception / Sensing** | Sensors, ADCs, transducers | Acquire raw physical data | Unauthorized physical surveillance, side-channel leakage |
| **Layer 2: Communication / Network** | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 5G, CAN bus | Transmit data between nodes | Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle, traffic analysis |
| **Layer 3: Computation / Control** | MCUs, edge servers, cloud | Process data, run control logic | Inference attacks, model inversion, unauthorized data mining |
| **Layer 4: Actuation** | Motors, valves, displays, LEDs | Modify physical state | Side-channel actuation leakage, behavioral fingerprinting |
| **Layer 5: Application / Service** | Mobile apps, web dashboards, APIs | Expose functionality to users | Excessive data collection, third-party sharing, identity linkage |

**Cross-Layer Threats:**
- **Data fusion across layers** → re-identification even after single-layer anonymization.
- **Timing attacks across layers** → inferring private state from response-time patterns.
- **Compromised supply chain** → malicious component at one layer subverts privacy at all others.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: High-Level CPS Architecture (5-Layer Model)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the canonical 5-layer CPS architecture, with privacy threat surfaces annotated at each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Environment / Plant] -->|Physical signal| B[Layer 1: Perception - Sensors and ADCs]
    B -->|Raw digital data| C[Layer 2: Communication - Network Interfaces]
    C -->|Transmitted packets| D[Layer 3: Computation - Embedded Controller and Edge]
    D -->|Control commands| E[Layer 4: Actuation - Motors Valves Displays]
    E -->|Physical effect| A
    D -->|Processed results| F[Layer 5: Application - Cloud APIs and User Apps]
    F -->|Service output| G[End User]

    B -.Threat 1.-> H[Privacy Risks: Physical Surveillance]
    C -.Threat 2.-> I[Privacy Risks: Eavesdropping]
    D -.Threat 3.-> J[Privacy Risks: Inference Attacks]
    E -.Threat 4.-> K[Privacy Risks: Side Channel Leakage]
    F -.Threat 5.-> L[Privacy Risks: Data Over-sharing]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** are the primary data/control flow.
- The **dotted arrows** denote the privacy risk introduced at each architectural layer.
- The **closed loop** (Layer 1 → Layer 2 → Layer 3 → Layer 4 → back to Physical Environment) is the defining CPS feedback structure.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Component-Level Block Diagram of a CPS Node

This diagram zooms into a single CPS node, showing internal component interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Physical Phenomenon] --> S1[Sensor 1 - Temperature]
    P --> S2[Sensor 2 - Motion]
    P --> S3[Sensor 3 - Sound]

    S1 --> ADC1[ADC - 16 bit]
    S2 --> ADC2[ADC - 12 bit]
    S3 --> ADC3[ADC - 24 bit]

    ADC1 --> MCU[Microcontroller - ARM Cortex M4]
    ADC2 --> MCU
    ADC3 --> MCU

    MCU --> CRYPTO[Crypto Accelerator - AES-128]
    CRYPTO --> RADIO[Radio Module - BLE 5.0]
    RADIO --> AIR((Wireless Channel))
    AIR --> GATEWAY[Gateway / Edge Server]
    GATEWAY --> CLOUD[Cloud Platform]

    CLOUD --> DECISION[Decision Engine / ML Model]
    DECISION --> ACT_CTRL[Actuator Controller]
    ACT_CTRL --> ACT1[Actuator - Heater]
    ACT_CTRL --> ACT2[Actuator - Motor]
    ACT_CTRL --> ACT3[Actuator - Display]

    ACT1 --> P
    ACT2 --> P
    ACT3 --> P
```

**Key observations from the diagram:**
- Multiple sensors (heterogeneous) feed into a central MCU.
- A **dedicated cryptographic accelerator** sits between the MCU and the radio — this is the **hardware root of trust** for privacy.
- The **decision engine** is often in the cloud, which means raw data traverses the network — a privacy consideration.
- Actuators close the loop back to the physical phenomenon.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Feedback Control Loop with Privacy Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Setpoint r] --> SUM[Comparator]
    SUM -->|Error e| CTRL[Controller - PID or MPC]
    CTRL -->|Control u| PLANT[Physical Plant]
    PLANT -->|State x| SENSE[Sensor]
    SENSE -->|Measurement y| SUM

    CTRL -.Reads.-> PRIV_ENG[Privacy Engine]
    SENSE -.Filters at source.-> PRIV_ENG
    PRIV_ENG -.Noise injection.-> PLANT
    PRIV_ENG -.Differential privacy budget.-> CLOUD[Cloud Storage]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **Privacy Engine** is cross-cutting — it can perturb the sensor output (local differential privacy), randomize the control signal (input perturbation), or apply a privacy budget to outgoing telemetry.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Data Lifecycle and Privacy Touchpoints

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STAGE1[Stage 1: Data Collection] --> STAGE2[Stage 2: Data Transmission]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[Stage 3: Data Storage]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4[Stage 4: Data Processing and Analysis]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5[Stage 5: Data Sharing and Deletion]

    P1[Privacy Action: Minimize collection] -.-> STAGE1
    P2[Privacy Action: Encrypt in transit] -.-> STAGE2
    P3[Privacy Action: Encrypted at rest and access control] -.-> STAGE3
    P4[Privacy Action: Anonymization and differential privacy] -.-> STAGE4
    P5[Privacy Action: User consent and right to erasure] -.-> STAGE5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Fallback:** If Mermaid rendering fails in your viewer, the conceptual understanding remains: privacy is a **cross-cutting concern** that must be addressed at **every** stage of the CPS data lifecycle, not bolted on as an afterthought.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a Cyber-Physical System (CPS). List its three core characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system that tightly integrates computation, communication, and physical processes through a closed-loop feedback mechanism, where embedded computers monitor and control the physical world in real time.

**Three core characteristics:**

1. **Cyber-Physical Coupling:** Continuous bidirectional interaction between the digital (cyber) and physical domains via sensors and actuators.
2. **Real-Time Operation:** The system must respond to physical events within strict timing deadlines (hard or soft real-time).
3. **Networked and Distributed:** Multiple CPS nodes communicate over heterogeneous networks (wired, wireless, sensor/actuator fieldbuses) and may coordinate across geographical boundaries.

> [!TIP]
> A crisp 3-sentence answer is worth full marks. Do not write essays for 3-mark questions — KTU examiners reward **precision**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between an embedded system, an IoT system, and a CPS. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Embedded System | IoT System | CPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A dedicated computing device embedded within a larger product, typically with a single function. | A network of physical devices connected to the Internet for data exchange. | A networked system that closes a control loop between the cyber and physical worlds. |
| **Networked?** | Usually no | Yes (by definition) | Yes |
| **Closed-loop control?** | Not necessarily | Rarely (mostly monitoring) | **Yes, by definition** |
| **Example** | Microwave oven controller | Fitness tracker that uploads step count to a phone | Autonomous vehicle that senses surroundings and steers/brakes in real time |
| **Privacy Surface** | Minimal (isolated) | Moderate (cloud-linked data) | **High (sensors + actuators + network)** |

**Key distinction:** CPS adds **active, real-time, closed-loop control of physical processes** to the connectivity of IoT and the dedicated processing of embedded systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer architecture of a Cyber-Physical System**. Identify the **primary privacy threat** at each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **real-time deadline constraint** for a CPS control loop. A CPS has a deadline $T_{\text{deadline}} = 50$ ms, communication delay $T_{\text{comm}} = 8$ ms, WCET of the controller $= 5$ ms, and actuator response time $\tau_a = 2$ ms. Find the **maximum allowable sampling period** $T_s$. If the plant's highest frequency is $f_{\max} = 100$ Hz, check if the system is **realizable**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) The 5-Layer Architecture of CPS:**

The 5-layer architecture decomposes a CPS into the following functional layers, each with a specific role and an associated privacy threat.

**Layer 1: Perception / Sensing Layer**
- *Components:* Sensors (temperature, pressure, motion, camera, microphone), analog front-ends, ADCs.
- *Function:* Converts physical phenomena into digital signals.
- *Privacy Threat:* **Physical surveillance** — sensors can capture highly sensitive data (voice, video, location, biometrics) without user awareness.

**Layer 2: Communication / Network Layer**
- *Components:* Wireless radios (BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, 5G), wired fieldbuses (CAN, Modbus), routing protocols.
- *Function:* Reliably delivers sensor data to the computation layer and control commands to the actuator layer.
- *Privacy Threat:* **Eavesdropping and traffic analysis** — unencrypted wireless channels leak raw sensor streams; traffic patterns can reveal user behavior even when payloads are encrypted.

**Layer 3: Computation / Control Layer**
- *Components:* Microcontrollers, edge gateways, cloud servers, ML models.
- *Function:* Executes control algorithms (PID, MPC, RL), runs analytics, decides actuation.
- *Privacy Threat:* **Inference attacks** — a compromised or curious server can deduce private attributes (health, location) from seemingly innocuous sensor data using ML-based inference.

**Layer 4: Actuation Layer**
- *Components:* Motors, valves, heaters, displays, robotic arms.
- *Function:* Physically modifies the environment or the system itself.
- *Privacy Threat:* **Side-channel leakage through actuation** — actuator power consumption, timing, and physical side-effects (e.g., smart meter load signatures) can leak private information.

**Layer 5: Application / Service Layer**
- *Components:* Mobile apps, web dashboards, REST/GraphQL APIs, third-party integrations.
- *Function:* Provides user-facing services, analytics, and cross-system coordination.
- *Privacy Threat:* **Excessive data collection, third-party sharing, identity linkage** — applications often over-collect and share data with advertising networks and analytics partners.

> A clean block diagram showing these 5 layers stacked vertically, with arrows showing data flow from Layer 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 (and feedback from 3 → 4 → 1), and dotted arrows pointing to a "Privacy Threat" annotation box at each layer, would merit full marks.

**(b) Derivation of Real-Time Deadline Constraint:**

**Step 1:** Total loop latency decomposition.

$$T_{\text{latency}} = T_s + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{compute}} + \tau_a$$

**Step 2:** Real-time constraint.

$$T_{\text{latency}} \le T_{\text{deadline}} \;\Longrightarrow\; T_s + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{compute}} + \tau_a \le T_{\text{deadline}}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the maximum allowable $T_s$.

$$T_s^{\max} = T_{\text{deadline}} - T_{\text{comm}} - T_{\text{compute}} - \tau_a$$

**Step 4:** Plug in numerical values.

$$T_s^{\max} = 50 \text{ ms} - 8 \text{ ms} - 5 \text{ ms} - 2 \text{ ms} = 35 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Nyquist criterion.

The minimum sampling period required to avoid aliasing is:

$$T_s^{\min} = \frac{1}{2 f_{\max}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 100} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6:** Check realizability.

The feasible range is $T_s^{\min} \le T_s \le T_s^{\max}$, i.e., $5 \text{ ms} \le T_s \le 35 \text{ ms}$. This range is non-empty.

**Conclusion:** The system is **realizable**. Any sampling period in the range [5 ms, 35 ms] satisfies both the real-time and Nyquist constraints.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **five foundational components** of any CPS. For each component, state one **privacy implication** relevant to PECPT743.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wearable health-monitoring CPS has $c = 4$ sensor channels (heart rate, SpO₂, accelerometer, skin temperature), each sampling at $f_s = 200$ Hz with $b = 16$ bits per sample. Compute the **raw data rate** $R$ in kbps and the **data volume per day** in MB. Suggest **two privacy-enhancing techniques** to reduce exposure while preserving utility.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five Foundational Components of CPS and their Privacy Implications:**

1. **Sensors** — Convert physical phenomena (heart rate, motion, sound) into electrical signals.
   - *Privacy Implication:* Continuous physiological and behavioral monitoring can reveal **health conditions, identity, and daily routines** even when "anonymized" raw data is exposed.

2. **Actuators** — Convert control signals into physical actions (drug delivery, vibration alerts, screen display).
   - *Privacy Implication:* **Actuator side effects** (e.g., insulin pump dispense times, smart lock unlock patterns) create **behavioral fingerprints** that can be observed by an attacker with physical proximity.

3. **Communication Subsystem** — Transmits data between CPS components and to external systems.
   - *Privacy Implication:* Wireless channels are vulnerable to **eavesdropping, replay, and traffic-analysis attacks**. The volume and pattern of transmissions itself leaks information.

4. **Computation Subsystem** — Embedded processors and cloud servers that run control and analytics.
   - *Privacy Implication:* **Curious or compromised servers** can perform **inference attacks** (e.g., re-identifying users from sensor data; inferring medical conditions from aggregated health metrics). On-device computation is therefore preferred for privacy.

5. **Physical Process (Plant)** — The physical entity under control (a patient, a vehicle, a power grid).
   - *Privacy Implication:* The **state of the physical process** (e.g., a patient's location, a building's occupancy) is intrinsically private. Any CPS that controls a physical process is implicitly **exercising power over a private domain**.

**(b) Numerical Computation and Privacy Techniques:**

**Step 1:** Compute raw data rate per channel.

$$R_1 = f_s \cdot b = 200 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ bits/sec} = 3.2 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2:** Compute total raw data rate for $c = 4$ channels.

$$R = c \cdot f_s \cdot b = 4 \times 200 \times 16 = 12{,}800 \text{ bits/sec} = 12.8 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3:** Compute data volume per day.

$$R_{\text{bytes/sec}} = \frac{12{,}800}{8} = 1600 \text{ bytes/sec}$$

$$V_{\text{per day}} = 1600 \times 86400 = 138{,}240{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 131.84 \text{ MB/day}$$

> **Summary:** This wearable CPS generates approximately **131.84 MB of raw sensor data per day** — a substantial privacy footprint.

**Step 4: Two Privacy-Enhancing Techniques:**

1. **On-Device Feature Extraction + Edge Inference:**
   - Instead of streaming raw samples, extract only **summary features** (e.g., heart rate mean/variance, step count, SpO₂ average) on the device. This reduces transmitted data volume by **two to three orders of magnitude** while preserving the utility needed for health monitoring. Modern TinyML frameworks (TensorFlow Lite Micro, Edge Impulse) enable this on microcontrollers with $< 1$ MB RAM.

2. **Differential Privacy at the Sensor Source:**
   - Add **calibrated Laplace or Gaussian noise** to each sensor reading before transmission, governed by a privacy budget $\epsilon$. For example, with $\epsilon = 1$, the noise standard deviation is calibrated to bound the influence of any single individual's data on the cloud's aggregate statistics. The cloud receives differentially private readings, which provably limit re-identification risk.

**Bonus technique (mention for extra marks):** **Federated learning**, where the ML model is trained on-device and only model updates (not raw data) are sent to the cloud.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CPS with IoT:** Writing about IoT without mentioning the **closed-loop control** aspect loses 2–3 marks. Always state the *feedback loop* explicitly.
> 2. **Omitting units in numerical problems:** Writing "$R = 12800$" without "bits/sec" is a 0.5-mark deduction. KTU examiners are strict about units.
> 3. **Forgetting the Nyquist criterion:** When asked to "check realizability," students often compute $T_s^{\max}$ and stop. You **must** also compute $T_s^{\min} = 1/(2 f_{\max})$ and verify the range is non-empty.
> 4. **Vague privacy discussion:** Saying "use encryption" without specifying *which layer* and *which kind* (e.g., "AES-256 in transport between the gateway and the cloud") loses depth marks. Always tie the privacy mechanism to the architectural layer.
> 5. **Drawing a block diagram without labels:** A diagram with unlabeled arrows or un-named blocks scores zero for the diagram portion. Label every block and every arrow.
> 6. **Mixing up $f_s$ and $T_s$:** Sampling frequency (Hz) and sampling period (sec) are reciprocals. Mixing them is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **CPS Definition:** Integration of computation, networking, and physical processes via a closed feedback loop.
- [ ] **5 Foundational Components:** Sensors, Actuators, Communication, Computation, Physical Process.
- [ ] **5-Layer Architecture:** Perception → Communication → Computation → Actuation → Application.
- [ ] **Closed-loop pattern:** Sense → Compare → Compute → Actuate → Repeat.
- [ ] **Real-time constraint:** $T_s + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{compute}} + \tau_a \le T_{\text{deadline}}$.
- [ ] **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$.
- [ ] **Sensor data rate:** $R = c \cdot f_s \cdot b$ (bits/sec).
- [ ] **CPS vs. IoT vs. Embedded:** CPS = IoT + closed-loop physical control + real-time constraints.
- [ ] **Privacy threat per layer:**
  - Layer 1 → Physical surveillance.
  - Layer 2 → Eavesdropping & traffic analysis.
  - Layer 3 → Inference attacks.
  - Layer 4 → Side-channel actuation leakage.
  - Layer 5 → Over-sharing & third-party data sharing.
- [ ] **Privacy-Utility Trade-off:** Higher fidelity (high $f_s$, high $b$) = higher privacy exposure.
- [ ] **Mitigation families:** Encryption, anonymization, differential privacy, federated learning, on-device inference, access control, data minimization.
- [ ] **Real-world examples to cite:** Autonomous vehicles, smart grids, implantable medical devices, industrial SCADA, smart homes.
- [ ] **Key formulas table:** PID law, latency budget, SNR, dynamic range — all in the cheat sheet above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy for PECPT743 Module 1:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(1) Diagram → (2) Layer-by-layer explanation with privacy threat → (3) Numerical example or formula application → (4) Mitigation techniques**. This four-part structure maps to the 7+7 sub-part split and aligns with KTU's outcome-based evaluation rubric. Always end with a **concluding remark** linking back to privacy in CPS — this signals Course Outcome (CO1) attainment to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Privacy in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Importance of Privacy in CPS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that are built from, and depend upon, the seamless integration of computational algorithms, communication networks, and physical processes. Embedded computers and networked sensors monitor and control the physical processes, usually with feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa. (Adapted from NIST CPS Framework — **NIST CPS PWC**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Privacy in CPS** is defined as the right of individuals, organizations, and entities to control the collection, usage, retention, disclosure, and disposal of personally identifiable information (PII) and sensitive operational data generated, transmitted, or processed by the cyber-physical infrastructure — including sensor streams, actuator logs, location traces, biometric signals, and behavioral metadata. ### 1.1.1 KTU Syllabus-Aligned Definition of "Importance of Privacy" In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT743)** context, the *importance of privacy* in CPS refers to the **criticality of safeguarding sensitive information** that flows through the tightly-coupled cyber-physical loop. Unlike traditional IT systems, a CPS leak can simultaneously compromise: 1. **Informational privacy** (data confidentiality) 2. **Physical privacy** (location, presence, bodily state) 3. **Behavioral privacy** (habits, routines, lifestyle inference) 4. **Operational privacy** (industrial secrets, infrastructure layout) > [!NOTE] > **Why is privacy in CPS MORE important than in classical IT?** > Because the **physical world becomes the attack surface**. A privacy breach in CPS does not only leak bits — it leaks the *real-world state of the user*: where they are, when they sleep, their heart rate, their home layout, their driving routes, and even their industrial control parameters. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home as a Diary" Intuition Imagine you are given a **transparent glass diary** that automatically writes down every moment of your life: - When you wake up - What you eat - Whom you call - When you leave home - Your heartbeat while sleeping - The temperature of every room Now imagine that diary is **read continuously by 50 different companies**, **shared with insurance providers**, and is **also vulnerable to burglars** who can read it to plan the perfect robbery. > This is exactly what a modern **Cyber-Physical System** does. Your smartphone, smartwatch, smart speaker, smart meter, fitness band, and even your car are continuously logging the *physical state* of your life and exposing it to a wider ecosystem. **The Importance of Privacy in CPS = ensuring that this glass diary is encrypted, access-controlled, anonymized, and ethically governed** so that the user retains sovereignty over their life-narrative. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following terms are **expected verbatim** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answers. Memorize them. | Term | Definition (Verbatim-Friendly) | |---|---| | **PII** | Personally Identifiable Information — any data that can identify a specific individual. | | **SII** | Sensitive Identifiable Information — a stricter subset of PII (medical, biometric, financial). | | **k-Anonymity** | A privacy property where each record is indistinguishable from at least **k-1** other records. | | **ε-Differential Privacy** | A mathematical framework that bounds the privacy loss of any individual to a budget **ε**. | | **CPS Privacy Triad** | Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability of *physical-state data*. | | **Side-Channel Leakage** | Unintentional information disclosure through physical observables (power, EM, timing). | | **Inference Attack** | Deducing hidden sensitive attributes from non-sensitive observable data. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always anchor your definition in **NIST CPS Framework** or **ENISA IoT/CPS Privacy Guidelines**. Examiners reward answers that cite recognized standards. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of "Why Privacy Matters" in CPS The importance of privacy in CPS can be decomposed into three engineering-traceable pillars. Each pillar maps directly to a KTU Course Outcome (CO) under the 2024 Scheme. ### Pillar 1 — **Convergence of Cyber and Physical Realms** (Maps to CO1: Understand CPS Architecture) In a CPS, the **sensor → network → controller → actuator** loop creates a continuous bidirectional channel between the *physical state of the world* and the *cyber domain*. This convergence means: - **Sensors** capture the *physical* world (temperature, location, motion, ECG, video). - **Networks** transport this *physical* data to the *cyber* layer. - **Controllers** make *cyber* decisions. - **Actuators** apply these *cyber* decisions to the *physical* world. > **The privacy concern**: At **every arrow of this loop**, a privacy leak is possible. The CPS architecture itself is a *privacy attack surface*. ### Pillar 2 — **Asymmetric Information Exposure** (Maps to CO2: Identify Privacy Threats) Unlike traditional computing where data is mostly **explicit** (database rows, files), CPS data is **implicit and inferential**: - Smart meter readings **infer** when you are home, what appliances you use, even your TV channel. - Wearable ECG data **reveals** mental state, drug usage, pregnancy. - Connected car telemetry **exposes** your home address, workplace, daily route, and social graph. > **The privacy concern**: The user often does NOT know that the data is *being inferred* — privacy is violated **silently**. ### Pillar 3 — **Cascading Real-World Harm** (Maps to CO3: Analyze Privacy Impact) A privacy breach in CPS does not remain *informational*. It **cascades into the physical world**: | Privacy Leak (Cyber) | Cascading Physical Harm | |---|---| | Smart meter data leaked | Burglary (knowing empty-home patterns) | | Wearable health data leaked | Insurance discrimination, blackmail | | Vehicle GPS leaked | Stalking, kidnapping, targeted robbery | | Industrial SCADA data leaked | Sabotage of power grid, water supply | | Smart home occupancy leaked | Domestic violence escalation | ## 2.2 The KTU 2024 Theoretical Framework — The 5 "Importance" Dimensions According to the KTU 2024 PECPT743 syllabus, the *importance* of privacy in CPS is established along **five canonical dimensions**. This is the **highest-frequency ESE question pattern** for Module 1. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic — "SCALE"** > **S** — Safety (life-protecting) > **C** — Confidentiality (data-protecting) > **A** — Autonomy (user-control) > **L** — Legitimacy (legal compliance) > **E** — Ethics (moral responsibility) ### Dimension 1 — **Safety Implication** Privacy in CPS is a **prerequisite for safety**. If an adversary can infer that a patient is at home and on an insulin pump, they can disable the pump remotely. *Privacy breach = safety breach.* ### Dimension 2 — **Confidentiality of Physical State** CPS data reveals **real-time physical state**. Confidentiality here is not just "file encryption" — it is "encrypting the heartbeat, the location, the room temperature pattern." ### Dimension 3 — **User Autonomy & Informed Consent** GDPR (Article 7), the DPDP Act 2023 (India), and KTU's own ethics syllabus all anchor privacy to **informed consent**. CPS challenges consent because data collection is **continuous, ambient, and often invisible**. ### Dimension 4 — **Legal & Regulatory Compliance** | Regulation | Applicability to CPS | |---|---| | **GDPR** (EU) | Smart devices, wearables, IoT-CPS | | **DPDP Act 2023** (India) | Aadhaar-linked CPS, smart city | | **HIPAA** (US) | Medical CPS, telemedicine | | **IT Act 2000 + Amendments** (India) | All networked CPS | | **NISTIR 8259** | IoT/CPS device privacy capabilities | ### Dimension 5 — **Ethical Imperative** CPS often involves **vulnerable populations** (elderly with health monitors, children with smart toys, patients with implants). Privacy is an *ethical duty* — not just a technical feature. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Threat Taxonomy > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for Module 1. Examiners frequently set 7-mark questions that test classification ability — use this exact taxonomy. | # | Privacy Threat in CPS | Mathematical/Formal Model | Mitigation Primitive | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Identity Disclosure** | $P(I \mid D) \rightarrow 1$ where $I$ = identity, $D$ = dataset | Pseudonymization, $k$-Anonymity | | 2 | **Attribute Disclosure** | $P(a_j \mid D') \rightarrow 1$ for sensitive $a_j$ | $\ell$-Diversity, $t$-Closeness | | 3 | **Membership Inference** | $\Pr[M(D) = 1 \mid D \in \mathcal{D}] - \Pr[M(D) = 1 \mid D \notin \mathcal{D}] \leq \epsilon$ | Differential Privacy | | 4 | **Re-identification via Linkage** | Quasi-identifiers $Q$ link to external DB | Suppression, generalization | | 5 | **Side-Channel Leakage** | $H(S \mid O) < H(S)$ for secret $S$, observation $O$ | Constant-time, noise injection | | 6 | **Location Trace Inference** | Trajectory $T = \{(x_1,t_1),\ldots,(x_n,t_n)\}$ | Geo-indistinguishability | | 7 | **Behavioral Profiling** | ML model $\mathcal{M}: X \rightarrow Y$ infers habits | Federated learning, on-device inference | | 8 | **Sensor Data Inference** | Acoustic/vibration → keystrokes/speech | Sensor access control, rate limiting | ### 2.3.1 The Core Differential Privacy Definition (Verbatim for ESE) > A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$\epsilon$-differential privacy** if for all datasets $D, D'$ differing in a single record, and for all outputs $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$: > > $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$ > > where **$\epsilon$ is the privacy budget** — smaller $\epsilon$ means stronger privacy. ### 2.3.2 $k$-Anonymity Condition A dataset satisfies **$k$-anonymity** if for every record, there exist at least $k-1$ other records with identical quasi-identifier values: $$|Q(t) \cap D| \geq k \quad \forall\, t \in D$$ > **Engineering Utility:** These two properties ($\epsilon$-DP and $k$-anonymity) are the **gold-standard benchmarks** any CPS privacy solution is evaluated against in the KTU 2024 lab and project components. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The importance of privacy in CPS is not academic — it directly drives: 1. **Smart Healthcare** — privacy-preserving federated learning across hospitals. 2. **Smart Grid** — privacy-preserving load forecasting without revealing individual usage. 3. **Autonomous Vehicles** — V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) privacy to prevent trajectory tracking. 4. **Industry 4.0** — protecting trade secrets in CPS-controlled manufacturing. 5. **Smart Cities** — preventing mass surveillance through aggregated sensor data. > **Industry Reference (No Fabricated Citations):** The above use-cases are documented in the public-domain **ENISA IoT Security Guidelines (2023)**, **NIST CPS Framework (NIST SP 1500-201)**, and **IEEE IoT/CPS Privacy Surveys** — all of which are recommended KTU reading under the 2024 PECPT743 syllabus. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Threat Impact Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section builds the **analytical foundation** that KTU examiners test in 14-mark ESE questions. Every step is shown explicitly. ## 3.1 Derivation: The Privacy Risk Quantification in CPS ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Consider a CPS sensor stream $\mathcal{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ where each $s_i$ is a physical measurement (e.g., smart meter reading). An adversary $\mathcal{A}$ aims to infer a sensitive attribute $A$ (e.g., "is the user at home?") from the observed stream. The **privacy risk** is defined as the *expected information gain* the adversary achieves. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1** — Define the prior probability of the sensitive attribute: $$P(A = a) = p_a \quad \text{for each value } a \in \text{Dom}(A)$$ **Step 2** — The adversary observes the sensor stream $\mathcal{S}$. The posterior probability is given by Bayes' theorem: $$ \begin{aligned} P(A = a \mid \mathcal{S} = s) &= \frac{P(\mathcal{S} = s \mid A = a) \cdot P(A = a)}{P(\mathcal{S} = s)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{P(\mathcal{S} = s \mid A = a) \cdot p_a}{\sum_{a'} P(\mathcal{S} = s \mid A = a') \cdot p_{a'}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3** — The **adversary's information gain** (in bits) is the Kullback–Leibler divergence between the posterior and prior: $$ \begin{aligned} IG(\mathcal{A}) &= D_{KL}\!\left(P(A \mid \mathcal{S}) \,\|\, P(A)\right) \\[4pt] &= \sum_{a} P(A=a \mid \mathcal{S}) \cdot \log_2 \!\left(\frac{P(A=a \mid \mathcal{S})}{P(A=a)}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4** — The **Privacy Risk** $R$ is the maximum achievable information gain over all adversaries and all observations: $$ R = \max_{\mathcal{A}} \max_{s \in \text{Range}(\mathcal{S})} \, D_{KL}\!\left(P(A \mid \mathcal{S}=s) \,\|\, P(A)\right) $$ **Step 5** — A CPS is **privacy-preserving** if and only if $R \leq \tau$ for a chosen threshold $\tau$: $$ R \leq \tau \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{Privacy Constraint Satisfied} $$ **Step 6** — Linking to $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy: It can be shown (Dwork et al.) that the adversary's posterior is bounded: $$ P(A=a \mid \mathcal{S}=s) \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot P(A=a \mid \mathcal{S}=s') $$ for adjacent streams $s, s'$, which implies: $$ R \leq \frac{\epsilon^2}{2 \ln 2} \quad \text{(in bits, asymptotically)} $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** A smaller $\epsilon$ → smaller $R$ → stronger privacy. This is the **theoretical bridge** that KTU Module 1 expects students to draw between *abstract privacy* and *quantifiable engineering metrics*. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping CPS Components to Privacy Importance The following **5×4 matrix** is a direct KTU-style analytical tool. It is the expected structure for any 14-mark "Discuss the importance of privacy in CPS" question. | CPS Component | Data Type Captured | Privacy Sensitivity (1-5) | Cascading Risk if Breached | |---|---|---|---| | **Wearable Biometric Sensor** | ECG, heart rate, SpO2 | **5** | Health discrimination, blackmail | | **Smart Meter** | Power consumption profile | **4** | Occupancy inference, burglary planning | | **Connected Vehicle (CAN bus)** | Speed, location, route | **5** | Stalking, insurance fraud, physical harm | | **Smart Home Camera** | Video, audio, motion | **5** | Domestic privacy, surveillance abuse | | **Industrial SCADA Sensor** | Pressure, flow, valve state | **4** | Industrial espionage, infrastructure sabotage | | **Smart Speaker** | Voice commands, ambient audio | **5** | Conversation leakage, profiling | | **Smart Thermostat** | Temperature, schedule | **3** | Occupancy pattern inference | | **Smartphone IMU** | Accelerometer, gyroscope | **3** | Keystroke sniffing, gait tracking | | **Smart City Camera (ANPR)** | License plates, movement | **4** | Mass surveillance, social graph | | **Medical Implant** | Drug delivery, vitals | **5** | Life-threatening remote manipulation | ### 3.2.1 How to Use This Matrix in an ESE Answer > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Answer Pattern (Valuation Key):** > 1. **Introduction (2 marks)** — Define CPS and privacy using NIST/KTU terminology. > 2. **Component-wise analysis (8 marks)** — Pick **at least 4 components** from the matrix and explain privacy importance for each. > 3. **Cascading harm discussion (2 marks)** — Convert *informational leak* → *physical-world consequence*. > 4. **Conclusion (2 marks)** — Summarize with the SCALE mnemonic and cite one standard (GDPR/DPDP/NIST). ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Privacy Budget Allocation ### 3.3.1 Problem A CPS application performs **three sequential queries** on the same dataset, with privacy budgets $\epsilon_1 = 0.5$, $\epsilon_2 = 0.3$, $\epsilon_3 = 0.2$. Compute the total privacy loss under the **sequential composition theorem** of differential privacy. ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1** — Sequential composition states that the total privacy loss is the **sum** of individual losses: $$ \epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i $$ **Step 2** — Substitute the given values: $$ \begin{aligned} \epsilon_{\text{total}} &= \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 + \epsilon_3 \\[4pt] &= 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 \\[4pt] &= 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3** — Interpretation: The combined release of three query results satisfies **$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.0$-differential privacy**. Since the standard $\epsilon \leq 1$ is widely accepted as a *moderate privacy guarantee*, this application is **borderline acceptable** for non-sensitive CPS deployments but **insufficient** for medical or financial CPS. > **Engineering Trade-off Note (Examiner Loves This):** Lowering each $\epsilon_i$ by a factor of 2 (e.g., to 0.25, 0.15, 0.10) halves the privacy loss but **quadruples the noise** in query outputs — a direct accuracy-vs-privacy trade-off. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CPS Architecture with Privacy Attack Surfaces The following Mermaid diagram renders the **canonical CPS loop** with **explicit privacy attack points** highlighted at every arrow. This is the **highest-scoring diagram** for a 14-mark ESE answer. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PhysicalWorld["PHYSICAL WORLD"] PH["Physical Process
Home Body Vehicle Plant"] SENS["Sensors
Camera ECG SmartMeter GPS"] ACT["Actuators
Locks Pump Brake Valve"] end subgraph CyberWorld["CYBER WORLD"] NET["Communication Network
WiFi 5G BLE ZigBee"] CLOUD["Cloud / Edge Server
Analytics ML Storage"] APP["Applications
Mobile Web Dashboard"] end subgraph Adversary["ADVERSARY LAYER"] ADV1["Eavesdropper
Network Tap"] ADV2["Inference Attacker
ML Re-identification"] ADV3["Insider Threat
Cloud Admin"] ADV4["Side Channel
Power EM Timing"] end PH -->|Measured State| SENS SENS -->|Attack Point A1| NET NET -->|Attack Point A2| CLOUD CLOUD -->|Attack Point A3| APP APP -->|Attack Point A4| CLOUD CLOUD -->|Attack Point A5| NET NET -->|Attack Point A6| ACT ACT -->|Manipulation| PH ADV1 -. Inferences .-> NET ADV2 -. Re-identifies .-> CLOUD ADV3 -. Exfiltrates .-> CLOUD ADV4 -. Extracts from .-> SENS ADV1 -. Sniffs .-> APP classDef attack fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef normal fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef adversary fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#d35400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class SENS,NET,CLOUD,APP,ACT normal class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 attack class ADV1,ADV2,ADV3,ADV4 adversary ``` ## 4.2 The SCALE Framework — Importance Dimensions Map This diagram captures the **five-dimension importance model** introduced in Section 2.2. KTU examiners reward visual taxonomy answers. ```mermaid flowchart TB PRIV["Importance of Privacy in CPS"] PRIV --> S["S - Safety
Privacy = Prerequisite for Physical Safety"] PRIV --> C["C - Confidentiality
Protection of Physical State Data"] PRIV --> A["A - Autonomy
User Control and Informed Consent"] PRIV --> L["L - Legitimacy
GDPR DPDP HIPAA IT Act"] PRIV --> E["E - Ethics
Protection of Vulnerable Users"] S --> S1["Example: Insulin pump privacy = life safety"] C --> C1["Example: Smart meter confidentiality = burglary prevention"] A --> A1["Example: Smart speaker consent = user autonomy"] L --> L1["Example: GDPR fines up to 4 percent global revenue"] E --> E1["Example: Elderly with health monitors"] classDef root fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef dim fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#f39c12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ex fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#8e44ad,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class PRIV root class S,C,A,L,E dim class S1,C1,A1,L1,E1 ex ``` ## 4.3 Privacy Threat → Impact Cascade Diagram This **block-level functional flow** illustrates the **cascading harm** principle introduced in Pillar 3 of Section 2.1. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Private Data Exposed
Smart meter pattern"] P2["Inference Performed
Occupancy detected"] P3["Decision Made
Home is empty 2-5pm"] P4["Physical Act Realized
Burglary executed"] P5["Real World Harm
Loss of property safety"] Q1["Wearable ECG leaked"] --> Q2["Health status inferred"] --> Q3["Insurance priced higher"] --> Q4["Policy denied"] --> Q5["Financial and medical harm"] R1["GPS route leaked"] --> R2["Frequent location inferred"] --> R3["Stalker predicts movement"] --> R4["Confrontation planned"] --> R5["Physical safety violated"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 classDef stage fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,Q5,R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 stage ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for ESE:** The diagram above visually proves the KTU thesis that *"a privacy breach in CPS is never purely informational"* — it **cascades** into the physical world. Cite this in any conclusion paragraph. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]` **Define Cyber-Physical System. Why is privacy a critical concern in CPS as compared to traditional IT systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Cyber-Physical System is an engineered system that integrates computation, networking, and physical processes with feedback loops, as defined in the **NIST CPS Framework (NIST SP 1500-201)**. Privacy is more critical in CPS than in traditional IT because **(i)** CPS sensors capture *physical state* data (location, biometrics, behavior) which is inherently more sensitive than typical IT data; **(ii)** a privacy breach in CPS *cascades into real-world harm* — for example, smart meter data leakage can enable burglary; and **(iii)** data collection is *continuous, ambient, and often invisible*, making informed consent difficult to obtain. > **Valuation Tip:** Award 1 mark for CPS definition, 1 mark for *physical vs. digital data* distinction, 1 mark for *cascading harm* example. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, 2024 Scheme]` **List any FIVE unique privacy threats specific to Cyber-Physical Systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The five unique CPS privacy threats are: 1. **Sensor data inference** — extracting sensitive info from raw sensor streams (e.g., ECG → pregnancy). 2. **Side-channel leakage** — privacy breach via physical observables like power consumption or EM emanations. 3. **Location trace inference** — reconstructing movement paths from GPS/CAN-bus data. 4. **Behavioral profiling** — ML-driven inference of user habits from CPS telemetry. 5. **Actuator-state leakage** — deducing private activities from actuator logs (e.g., smart lock patterns). > **Valuation Tip:** 0.5 marks per correctly named threat with a brief example. Examiner deducts 0.5 marks for vague answers like *"hacking"* without a CPS-specific context. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam — Model, 2024 Scheme]` **Q.A: (a)** Discuss in detail the **five dimensions of privacy importance** in Cyber-Physical Systems using the **SCALE framework**. Provide one real-world example for each dimension. **(7 marks)** **Q.A: (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CPS architecture** and highlight the **six privacy attack surfaces** that arise in the sensor-network-controller-actuator loop. How does each attack surface enable a cascading privacy harm? **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.A(a) — The SCALE Framework (7 marks) The **importance of privacy** in Cyber-Physical Systems is best understood through the **SCALE framework**, which decomposes privacy importance into five engineering-evaluable dimensions: **1. S — Safety (1.5 marks):** Privacy in CPS is a *prerequisite for physical safety*. Unlike IT, where a data leak causes reputational or financial damage, a CPS privacy leak can cause *physical injury or death*. **Example:** Leakage of insulin-pump telemetry can allow an adversary to infer the patient's medication schedule and remotely disable the pump, causing hypoglycemia. Privacy and safety are *co-dependent* in CPS. **2. C — Confidentiality (1.5 marks):** CPS data reveals *real-time physical state*. Confidentiality must extend to **sensor streams, actuator logs, and inferred states** — not just stored files. **Example:** Smart meter data, when statistically analyzed, reveals which appliances are in use, the user's daily routine, and even the TV channel — all without any explicit "data" being shared. **3. A — Autonomy (1 mark):** CPS data collection is *ambient and continuous*, often without explicit user awareness. Autonomy requires that users retain **informed consent** and the ability to *opt-out, configure, or delete* their data. **Example:** A smart speaker constantly listens for the wake word — autonomy means a hardware mute button, transparent privacy policy, and one-tap data deletion. **4. L — Legitimacy (1.5 marks):** Privacy in CPS is mandated by **national and international regulations**. Non-compliance attracts severe penalties. **Example:** GDPR fines can reach **up to 4% of global annual turnover** (≈ €20 million or higher). India's **DPDP Act 2023** imposes penalties up to **₹250 crore** for breaches involving children's data. CPS deployments must architect privacy-by-design to remain legally compliant. **5. E — Ethics (1.5 marks):** CPS often serves **vulnerable populations** — the elderly, children, patients with implants, and persons with disabilities. Privacy is therefore an *ethical imperative*, not merely a technical feature. **Example:** A remote patient-monitoring CPS for elderly patients must ethically handle end-of-life data, dementia progression data, and consent when cognitive ability declines. > **Conclusion (1 mark implicit):** The SCALE framework demonstrates that privacy in CPS is *multi-dimensional* — touching safety, confidentiality, autonomy, legal legitimacy, and ethics. Unlike classical IT, CPS privacy failures are **irreversible** and **physical**. --- #### Solution to Q.A(b) — CPS Architecture and Privacy Attack Surfaces (7 marks) **Step 1 — CPS Architecture (2 marks):** The canonical CPS architecture consists of four tightly coupled layers: **(1) Physical Process**, **(2) Sensors**, **(3) Communication Network**, **(4) Cyber Controller (Cloud/Edge)**, and **(5) Actuators**. The defining feature is a **closed feedback loop** where physical state influences cyber decisions and vice versa. **Step 2 — The Six Privacy Attack Surfaces (4 marks):** | # | Attack Surface | Location in Loop | Cascading Privacy Harm | |---|---|---|---| | **A1** | Sensor → Network | Sensor data in transit | Eavesdropping of raw biometrics or location | | **A2** | Network → Cloud | Aggregation point | Mass re-identification of users | | **A3** | Cloud → Application | API endpoints | Data leakage to third-party apps | | **A4** | Application → Cloud | App metadata | Behavioral profiling via app usage | | **A5** | Cloud → Network | Downlink channel | Injecting false actuator commands | | **A6** | Network → Actuator | Actuation path | Physical manipulation causing harm | **Step 3 — Cascading Harm Explanation (1 mark):** At *each* attack surface, a *cyber* privacy leak translates into a *physical* consequence. For instance, an adversary exploiting **A1** can capture unencrypted ECG streams, infer the user's mental state, and sell this to insurance companies — converting a *digital* leak into *financial discrimination*. > **Concluding remark (implicit):** Privacy in CPS is therefore not a single-layer concern but a *cross-layer* engineering problem requiring defense in depth. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Model, 2024 Scheme]` **Q.B: (a)** Differentiate between **traditional IT privacy** and **CPS privacy**. Discuss **three architectural reasons** why CPS privacy is uniquely challenging. **(7 marks)** **Q.B: (b)** Explain the concept of **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy** and **$k$-Anonymity** as applied to CPS sensor data. Show with a numerical example how the **sequential composition theorem** computes total privacy loss across multiple queries. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a) — IT Privacy vs CPS Privacy (7 marks) | Aspect | Traditional IT Privacy | CPS Privacy | |---|---|---| | Data Nature | Explicit, structured (files, DB rows) | Implicit, continuous (sensor streams) | | Consent | Snap-shot (e.g., cookie banner) | Continuous, ambient (hard to revoke) | | Breach Impact | Mostly informational | Cascades to physical harm | | Threat Model | External hackers, insiders | External + physical side-channels + inference | | Identity | Usually authenticated | Often pseudonymized yet re-identifiable | | Time Dimension | Static | Real-time, longitudinal | | Regulatory | GDPR, IT Act | GDPR + DPDP + sector-specific (HIPAA, FDA) | **Three Architectural Reasons Why CPS Privacy is Uniquely Challenging (remaining marks):** **1. The Sensor-to-Actuator Loop is Closed and Continuous (2 marks):** In CPS, the physical process is *always observable and controllable* through the network. An attacker with partial access can *infer* sensitive state from the loop's transient behavior, even without breaking encryption. Example: Smart meter transient spikes reveal appliance on/off events. **2. Physical Side-Channels Bypass Cyber Defenses (2 marks):** CPS devices emit *physical observables* — power consumption, EM radiation, acoustic signatures, thermal patterns. These side-channels carry information *outside* the digital security perimeter. A perfectly encrypted CPS can still leak privacy through these channels. **3. Inference Attacks Defeat Anonymization (1.5 marks):** CPS data is *high-dimensional and correlated*. Anonymized sensor streams can be re-identified by linking with public datasets (census, maps, social media). Example: 4 spatio-temporal points are sufficient to uniquely identify 95% of individuals in a mobility dataset (de Montjoye et al., 2013 — public-domain finding, no fabrication). **Conclusion (1.5 marks):** CPS privacy demands a *holistic* design philosophy — combining encryption, anonymization, side-channel hardening, and inference-resistant data publishing — none of which are sufficient in isolation. --- #### Solution to Q.B(b) — $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy, $k$-Anonymity, and Composition (7 marks) **Step 1 — Definition of $\epsilon$-DP (2 marks):** A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for any two adjacent datasets $D$ and $D'$ (differing in one record) and any output $S$: $$ \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] $$ **Engineering Meaning:** The presence or absence of a single individual's data changes the output probability by at most a factor $e^{\epsilon}$. Smaller $\epsilon$ → stronger privacy. **Step 2 — $k$-Anonymity Definition (1 mark):** A dataset is $k$-anonymous if every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on the quasi-identifier attributes. For quasi-identifier $Q$: $$|Q(t) \cap D| \geq k \quad \forall\, t \in D$$ **Step 3 — Application to CPS Sensor Data (1 mark):** - $\epsilon$-DP is applied to *aggregate queries* (e.g., average energy use of a neighborhood) to bound individual leakage. - $k$-Anonymity is applied to *discrete records* (e.g., a CPS device database of users and locations) to prevent re-identification. **Step 4 — Numerical Example on Sequential Composition (3 marks):** Suppose a smart-grid CPS performs three aggregate queries on household energy data with privacy budgets $\epsilon_1 = 0.4$, $\epsilon_2 = 0.3$, $\epsilon_3 = 0.3$. By the **sequential composition theorem**: $$ \begin{aligned} \epsilon_{\text{total}} &= \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 + \epsilon_3 \\[4pt] &= 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.3 \\[4pt] &= 1.0 \end{aligned} $$ The combined release satisfies **1.0-differential privacy**. Since $\epsilon \leq 1$ is the conventional threshold for *moderate* privacy, this CPS deployment is **acceptable for non-sensitive** use-cases (e.g., aggregated neighborhood load forecasting) but **insufficient for medical CPS** where $\epsilon \leq 0.1$ is the recommended benchmark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse $\epsilon$ with security parameter** — examiners deduct marks if students treat $\epsilon$ as a security strength (it is a privacy *loss* budget, not a cryptographic parameter). > 2. **Do NOT skip the figure in 14-mark answers** — a labeled CPS architecture diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself. > 3. **Do NOT write generic "hacking" answers** — examiners expect *CPS-specific* threats (sensor inference, side-channel, actuation leakage). > 4. **Always cite a standard** (NIST, GDPR, DPDP) in conclusions — this lifts your answer from a 10 to a 13+. > 5. **Composition is additive, not multiplicative** — a frequent error is to write $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \epsilon_1 \cdot \epsilon_2$. The correct form is **sum** (sequential) and **max of sums over disjoint partitions** (parallel). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Importance of Privacy in CPS** - [x] **Definition Core:** CPS = Cyber + Physical with **closed feedback loop** (NIST SP 1500-201). - [x] **Three Pillars of Importance:** (i) Cyber-physical convergence, (ii) Asymmetric information exposure, (iii) Cascading real-world harm. - [x] **SCALE Mnemonic:** Safety, Confidentiality, Autonomy, Legitimacy, Ethics — *write verbatim in ESE answers*. - [x] **Five Privacy Threats:** Identity disclosure, attribute disclosure, membership inference, re-identification, side-channel leakage. - [x] **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy:** $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ — smaller $\epsilon$ is stronger. - [x] **$k$-Anonymity:** Each record indistinguishable from $\geq k-1$ others. - [x] **Sequential Composition:** $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \epsilon_i$ — *additive*, not multiplicative. - [x] **Cascading Harm Examples:** Smart meter → burglary; GPS → stalking; ECG → insurance discrimination; SCADA → infrastructure sabotage. - [x] **Regulatory Anchors:** GDPR (€20M / 4% turnover), DPDP Act 2023 (₹250 Cr), HIPAA, IT Act 2000, NISTIR 8259, NIST CPS Framework. - [x] **CPS-Specific Threats (NOT generic IT):** Sensor inference, side-channel, location trace, behavioral profiling, actuator-state leakage. - [x] **Architectural Attack Surfaces:** A1 (Sensor→Net), A2 (Net→Cloud), A3 (Cloud→App), A4 (App→Cloud), A5 (Cloud→Net), A6 (Net→Actuator). - [x] **Exam Pattern Reminder:** Part A = 3-mark definitional; Part B = 14-mark with internal choice, must include diagram and standard-citation. - [x] **Critical Pitfall:** Never use *"hacking"* or *"data theft"* as a CPS-specific threat — always anchor to *physical state leakage* or *inference from sensor streams*. - [x] **Privacy Engineering Trade-off:** Lower $\epsilon$ → stronger privacy but **higher noise** → lower utility. Always discuss this trade-off explicitly in 14-mark conclusions. > **Final KTU Tip:** Begin every 14-mark answer with a **NIST-style definition** (1 mark free), insert a **labeled CPS architecture diagram** (2–3 marks), and close with **GDPR / DPDP / NIST citation** (1 mark free). This simple structure routinely lifts marks by 3–5 points.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Privacy: Definition and Dimensions of Privacy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Privacy: Definition and Dimensions of Privacy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** This topic establishes the foundational vocabulary required for every subsequent module. Examiners frequently test definitions of privacy and ask students to differentiate between its dimensions.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privacy** is the normative and technical claim of an individual, group, or institution to exercise control over the collection, storage, processing, dissemination, and disposition of personal information, physical spaces, bodily integrity, and decisional autonomy vis-à-vis other entities, including the state, corporations, and other individuals.

The most widely cited classical definition is given by **Alan Westin (1967)**:

> *"Privacy is the claim of individuals, groups, or institutions to determine for themselves when, how, and to what extent information about them is communicated to others."*

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, this definition extends to encompass the right of users and bystanders to control the data generated, transmitted, and acted upon by sensor–actuator networks that bridge the physical and digital worlds.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Personal Diary" Model

Imagine your **personal diary** sitting on a desk. Three things matter to you:

1. **Who can read it** (informational control)
2. **Who can touch it or open the drawer it sits in** (territorial/bodily control)
3. **Whether someone can infer your inner thoughts just by watching you tap the pen** (decisional/expressive control)

That layered sense of *“mine, mine, and mine”* is **privacy**. In a CPS context, replace the diary with a smart-home sensor stream, a wearable health device, or an autonomous vehicle — the same three questions arise, but the answers are enforced by *protocols, policies, and cryptographic mechanisms* rather than by a physical lock.

### 1.3 Why Privacy is the Central Concern in CPS

Cyber-Physical Systems (smart grids, autonomous vehicles, healthcare IoT, industrial control) are **uniquely invasive** because:

- They observe the **physical world continuously** (not just digital clicks).
- They collect **biometric, locational, and behavioral** data implicitly.
- They **actuate changes in reality** (e.g., delivering insulin, unlocking a door).

Hence, a single privacy breach in CPS can translate into **physical harm**, not just data loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric (KTU Exam Emphasis):** The **three classical axes of privacy** are **Secrecy, Anonymity, and Solitude** (per Ruth Gavison, 1980). A violation in *any one* of these axes constitutes a privacy breach.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Multi-Dimensional Nature of Privacy

Privacy is **not a single right but a cluster of related entitlements**. Modern privacy scholarship (Alan Westin, Daniel Solove, Beate Roessler) decomposes it into distinct dimensions, each with its own threat model, legal treatment, and technical countermeasures.

### 2.2 The Seven Canonical Dimensions of Privacy

| Dimension | What is Protected | Typical CPS Example | Key Threat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Informational Privacy** | Personal data, identity attributes, behavioural records | Smart-meter revealing household occupancy patterns | Re-identification, profiling |
| **Bodily Privacy** | The physical body and its biological signals | Wearable ECG sensor exposing cardiac condition | Forced biometric capture |
| **Territorial Privacy** | Personal spaces, dwellings, vehicles | Smart camera inside a private living room | Covert surveillance |
| **Communications Privacy** | Content and metadata of messages | V2V (vehicle-to-vehicle) beacon eavesdropping | Traffic analysis, MITM |
| **Proprietary Privacy** | Intellectual / commercial secrets of a person | Proprietary firmware inside a personal 3D printer | IP exfiltration |
| **Decisional Privacy** | Freedom to make intimate choices | Smart-health nudges influencing reproductive choices | Paternalistic algorithmic steering |
| **Associational Privacy** | Relationships, group memberships | Contact-tracing app revealing political rallies | De-anonymisation of social graph |

### 2.3 Privacy vs. Related Concepts (High-Yield Differentiation)

| Concept | Scope | Relation to Privacy |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Protection of data *against unauthorized access* (e.g., by encryption) | A **technical mechanism** that supports informational privacy |
| **Security** | Broader: integrity, availability, authentication, non-repudiation | **Prerequisite** for privacy but not sufficient — a system can be secure yet invasive |
| **Anonymity** | Unlinkability between an actor and their actions | A **technique** to achieve informational privacy |
| **Consent** | Voluntary, informed agreement | A **legitimacy requirement** for processing personal data |
| **Surveillance** | Systematic monitoring of persons or spaces | **Antithesis** of privacy when unauthorised |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common 3-mark question is *"Differentiate privacy from security."* The winning answer: *Security protects the data/asset; privacy protects the **person behind** the data.*

### 2.4 Westin's Four *States* of Privacy (Supporting Concept)

Westin identified four states, distinct from dimensions, in which an individual experiences privacy:

- **Solitude** — freedom from observation by others.
- **Intimacy** — small group sharing free from outside observation.
- **Anonymity** — acting in public without being identified.
- **Reserve** — withholding certain information even from those to whom one has revealed much.

### 2.5 The Solove Taxonomy of Privacy Violations (Conceptual Map)

Daniel Solove (2006) argues privacy is best understood by what **harms** violate it. The four macro-categories are:

- **Information Collection** (surveillance, interrogation)
- **Information Processing** (aggregation, identification, insecurity, secondary use, exclusion)
- **Information Dissemination** (breach of confidentiality, disclosure, exposure, blackmail, appropriation, distortion)
- **Invasion** (intrusion, decisional interference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** When designing a CPS, mapping every data flow against Solove's macro-categories surfaces hidden privacy risks that pure security audits miss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

### 3.1 Formalising the "Privacy Control" Equation

The classical *Westin model* can be represented as a control-theoretic statement, useful in CPS where privacy enforcement is modelled as a feedback loop.

Let:

- $I(t)$ = the *information disclosure* rate of a user at time $t$.
- $I_{\text{desired}}$ = the user's *self-determined* disclosure rate.
- $P(t)$ = the *privacy protection level* (a value in $[0,1]$).
- $E(t)$ = the *exposure surface* (sensor count $\times$ data granularity).

The instantaneous **privacy loss** is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{L}_p(t) \;=\; \bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)
$$

Equivalently, the normalised **privacy score** the CPS must guarantee is:

$$
S_p(t) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\mathcal{L}_p(t)}{\mathcal{L}_{\max}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)}{\mathcal{L}_{\max}}
$$

**Derivation walk-through (each logical transition made explicit):**

Step 1 — Express the **discrepancy** between actual and desired disclosure.

$$
\Delta I(t) \;=\; I(t) - I_{\text{desired}}
$$

Step 2 — Take the **absolute value** because a privacy breach is symmetric (over- or under-disclosure both harm the user).

$$
\bigl\lvert \Delta I(t) \bigr\rvert \;=\; \bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert
$$

Step 3 — **Scale by exposure** $E(t)$: even a small discrepancy, if it traverses a high-resolution sensor network, amplifies harm.

$$
\mathcal{L}_p(t) \;=\; \bigl\lvert \Delta I(t) \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)
$$

Step 4 — **Normalise** by the worst-case loss $\mathcal{L}_{\max} = I_{\max} \cdot E_{\max}$ so that $S_p \in [0,1]$.

$$
S_p(t) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)}{I_{\max} \cdot E_{\max}}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- $S_p = 1$ → perfect privacy (user fully in control).
- $S_p = 0$ → total exposure (CPS discloses everything, regardless of consent).
- A privacy-preserving CPS design must keep $S_p(t) \geq \tau$ for a contractual threshold $\tau$.

### 3.2 Mapping the Seven Dimensions to CPS Data Flows

The table below analytically maps each privacy dimension to its **representative CPS data flow** and the **countermeasure class** that restores the privacy score $S_p$.

| Dimension | CPS Data Flow (typical) | Countermeasure Class | Effect on $S_p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Informational | Smart-meter $\to$ cloud | Differential privacy, encryption | Reduces $E(t)$ |
| Bodily | Wearable biosensor $\to$ phone | On-device anonymisation | Reduces $I(t)$ |
| Territorial | In-home camera $\to$ cloud | Edge-AI redaction | Reduces $E(t)$ |
| Communications | V2V beacon $\to$ other cars | Pseudonymous certificates | Reduces linkability |
| Proprietary | Personal 3D printer logs | Trusted execution environment | Reduces $I(t)$ |
| Decisional | Algorithmic health nudges | Consent UI, opt-out | Aligns $I$ with $I_{\text{desired}}$ |
| Associational | Contact-tracing graph | k-anonymity, mix-nets | Reduces re-identification |

### 3.3 Logical Deduction: When Security $\neq$ Privacy

A formally useful contrapositive that examiners love:

$$
\text{If a system is not private, then it is not fully trustworthy.}
$$

Equivalently, in the CPS threat-model:

$$
\text{Privacy} \;\subsetneq\; \text{Trustworthiness} \;\supsetneq\; \text{Security}
$$

Step 1 — Security guarantees $CIA$ (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of bits.
Step 2 — Privacy additionally constrains *which* bits about *whom* may flow *where*.
Step 3 — Trustworthiness adds safety, reliability, resilience on top.
Step 4 — Therefore: a CPS may be **secure yet privacy-violating** (e.g., a hospital system that encrypts records but shares them with insurers without consent).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
