<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Pattern]**
**Define privacy in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Mention any two privacy dimensions with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU valuation key):**

*Definition (2 marks):* Privacy in CPS is the right of an individual to control the collection, processing, and dissemination of personal, behavioural, and physiological data generated by sensor-actuator systems that bridge the physical and digital worlds. It extends classical informational privacy to include bodily and territorial dimensions unique to CPS.

*Two dimensions (1 mark):* 
- *Informational privacy* — e.g., smart-meter data revealing occupancy patterns.
- *Bodily privacy* — e.g., wearable ECG data exposing cardiac health.

*(Any two valid dimensions with relevant CPS examples earn the full mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Equivalent Pattern]**
**Differentiate between *privacy* and *security*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Security | Privacy |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Protect assets, data, and systems from threats | Protect the autonomy and dignity of the person behind the data |
| Focus | CIA triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability | Control over personal information, body, space, decisions |
| Scope | Technical, organisational, procedural | Legal, ethical, social, technical |
| Metric | Vulnerability score, attack surface | Disclosure risk, identifiability, consent status |
| Example | Encrypting a database | Disclosing *only* the minimum data needed to a third party |

A system can be **secure but not private** (e.g., encrypted but over-shared data); the reverse — private but insecure — is rarer but possible.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between **Q-A** and **Q-B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** *Discuss the seven dimensions of privacy. For each dimension, give one CPS-based example and identify a primary threat. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With the help of Westin's definition, derive the privacy-score equation and explain how a privacy-preserving CPS designer can keep $S_p(t) \geq \tau$. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Seven dimensions (1 mark each for naming + example + threat):**

1. **Informational privacy** — Smart-meter hourly consumption data → re-identification of household schedule.
2. **Bodily privacy** — Implantable insulin pump telemetry → forced biometric capture.
3. **Territorial privacy** — In-home robot vacuum with mapping cameras → covert spatial surveillance.
4. **Communications privacy** — V2V safety beacons in traffic → traffic analysis and driver profiling.
5. **Proprietary privacy** — Personal 3D-printer firmware logs → industrial IP exfiltration.
6. **Decisional privacy** — Smart-health nudges on reproductive apps → paternalistic algorithmic steering.
7. **Associational privacy** — Contact-tracing app attendance logs → de-anonymisation of social graphs.

*(Award full marks if a candidate names the dimension, gives any plausible CPS example, and a non-trivial threat.)*

**(b) Derivation (7 marks — step-by-step):**

Step 1 — State Westin's claim: *the individual determines disclosure*.

Step 2 — Define $I(t)$ as actual disclosure rate and $I_{\text{desired}}$ as the user-set rate.

Step 3 — Form the discrepancy $\Delta I(t) = I(t) - I_{\text{desired}}$.

Step 4 — Weight by exposure surface $E(t)$ (sensor density $\times$ data granularity):

$$
\mathcal{L}_p(t) = \bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)
$$

Step 5 — Normalise to get privacy score:

$$
S_p(t) = 1 - \frac{\bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)}{I_{\max} \cdot E_{\max}}
$$

Step 6 — Design constraint: enforce $S_p(t) \geq \tau$ via three levers — (i) reducing $E(t)$ through on-device anonymisation, (ii) aligning $I(t)$ with $I_{\text{desired}}$ via consent managers, (iii) capping raw data via differential privacy at the cloud.

Step 7 — Conclude: continuous monitoring of $S_p(t)$ in real time converts privacy from a *policy statement* into a *measurable control variable*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** *Explain Ruth Gavison's three classical axes of privacy. How do they relate to the modern seven-dimension model? (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Differentiate between Westin's states and Solove's taxonomy of privacy violations. Why is the Solove taxonomy particularly useful for CPS designers? (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Gavison's three axes (7 marks):**

- **Secrecy (2 marks)** — the *limitation* of others' knowledge about oneself. Closest to *informational* and *communications* privacy in the seven-dimension model.
- **Anonymity (2 marks)** — the *freedom* from being identifiable in a public space. Maps to *associational* and *territorial* privacy; relevant to CPS contact-tracing and surveillance cameras.
- **Solitude (2 marks)** — the *freedom from being observed*; freedom of physical intrusion. Maps to *bodily* and *territorial* privacy — the most physically grounded axes and the most salient in CPS.

*(1 mark) Synthesis:* Gavison's trio is the **core kernel** of the modern seven-dimension model; the latter is a refinement that adds decisional, proprietary, and associational concerns to address modern digital ecosystems.

**(b) Westin's states vs. Solove's taxonomy (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Westin's States | Solove's Taxonomy |
|---|---|---|
| Lens | *Experiential* — what the user feels | *Harm-based* — what wrongs occur |
| Elements | Solitude, Intimacy, Anonymity, Reserve | 16 specific harms across 4 macro-categories |
| Purpose | Describe *when* privacy exists | Diagnose *how* privacy is violated |
| CPS utility | Low — too abstract for engineering | **High** — directly maps to threat models |

*(3 marks)* **Why Solove is useful for CPS designers:** It converts an abstract right into an *enumerable checklist*. A CPS design review can tick off (a) collection, (b) processing, (c) dissemination, (d) invasion; every data flow is mapped to the relevant harm, producing a measurable privacy risk register. This is the standard adopted by most modern privacy-engineering frameworks (NIST Privacy Framework, ISO 27701).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Writing *"privacy = confidentiality"* loses 2 marks — confidentiality is a *mechanism*; privacy is a *right*.
> 2. Listing the seven dimensions **without CPS examples** is treated as half-answer (≈1 mark per dimension only).
> 3. In derivation questions, skipping the normalisation step forfeits the full $S_p \in [0,1]$ reasoning; always normalise.
> 4. Confusing *states* (Westin) with *dimensions* (seven-fold) is a classic 2-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition anchor:** Westin (1967) — *the right to determine when, how, and to what extent information about oneself is communicated*.
- **Gavison's three axes:** Secrecy, Anonymity, Solitude — the irreducible kernel of all privacy.
- **Seven dimensions:** Informational, Bodily, Territorial, Communications, Proprietary, Decisional, Associational.
- **Westin's four states** (NOT dimensions): Solitude, Intimacy, Anonymity, Reserve.
- **Solove's taxonomy:** 16 harms under 4 macro-categories — collection, processing, dissemination, invasion.
- **Key equation:**

$$
S_p(t) = 1 - \frac{\bigl\lvert I(t) - I_{\text{desired}} \bigr\rvert \cdot E(t)}{I_{\max} \cdot E_{\max}}
$$

  with the design constraint $S_p(t) \geq \tau$.

- **CPS-unique concerns:** continuous physical sensing, biometric capture, actuation-side risks.
- **One-line differentiator:** *Security protects data; privacy protects the person behind the data.*
- **Privacy-enhancing technologies to remember:** differential privacy, k-anonymity, on-device AI redaction, pseudonymous certificates, trusted execution environments.
- **Legal anchors often cited in KTU papers:** *K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017)* declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Indian Constitution — examiners occasionally reference this.
- **Remember the order:** Definition → Dimensions → States → Taxonomy → Engineering application.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy Principles and Frameworks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privacy Principles and Frameworks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Privacy in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Privacy** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is defined as the *right of individuals, groups, and organizations to control the collection, usage, retention, disclosure, and disposal of their personal and sensitive information* generated, transmitted, or processed by interconnected physical and computational components (sensors, actuators, embedded controllers, cloud/edge nodes).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, CPS privacy is treated as a **multidisciplinary engineering objective** that sits at the intersection of:
- **Computer Science** (data minimization, encryption, access control)
- **Electrical/Electronics Engineering** (sensor-level privacy, signal sanitization)
- **Humanities & Law** (informational self-determination, consent)

> [!NOTE] **Working Definition for Examinations**
> *Privacy in CPS = Confidentiality + Control + Consent + Compliance* across the three operational layers: **Perception Layer (Sensors/IoT)**, **Network Layer (Communication)**, and **Application Layer (Cloud/Edge Analytics)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart House" Metaphor

Imagine you live in a **fully automated smart home** equipped with:
- Smart doorbells with cameras
- Voice-activated assistants
- Health-monitoring wearables
- Smart energy meters

Your home generates **continuous streams of data** — who comes in, what you say, your heartbeat, when you shower, your sleep patterns.

**Privacy in CPS** is like having:
1. **A personal secretary** (controller) who decides what leaves the house
2. **Tinted windows** (anonymization) so neighbours can't peek in
3. **A guest list** (consent management) deciding who sees what
4. **A shredder** (data disposal) destroying what's no longer needed

Without these, the smart home becomes a **digital panopticon** — everything is observed, recorded, and exploited.

---

## 1.3 Core Attributes of CPS Privacy

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Four Pillars of CPS Privacy (KTU High-Yield)**

| Pillar | Engineering Implementation | Threat Addressed |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | AES-256, TLS 1.3, Homomorphic Encryption | Eavesdropping |
| **Integrity** | HMAC, Digital Signatures, Blockchain | Tampering |
| **Control** | Access Control Lists (ACLs), RBAC, ABAC | Unauthorized Use |
| **Compliance** | GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, NIST PF | Legal/Regulatory Breach |

---

## 1.4 Why "Privacy Principles and Frameworks" Matter in KTU Examinations

- It is the **foundational module** of PECPT743 — every later module (PETs, Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, etc.) is built atop these principles.
- KTU examiners frequently frame **2-mark and 3-mark questions** asking students to *list and briefly explain* the OECD principles, FIPPs, or Privacy by Design principles.
- **14-mark questions** typically ask for a *comparative analysis* of two frameworks or an *application* of a framework to a CPS scenario (e.g., a smart healthcare wearable, an autonomous vehicle).

> [!NOTE] **Examination Weightage Hint (Typical KTU Pattern)**
> Module 1 contributes ~ **15–20%** of the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) marks for PECPT743. Expect at least one full 14-mark question and 1–2 short questions from this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Foundational Privacy Frameworks (Exam-Relevant)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly anchors Module 1 on **five canonical frameworks**. Let us deconstruct each in board-exam-ready bullet form.

---

### 2.1.1 Fair Information Practice Principles (FIPPs) — 1973 / U.S. Origin

The **FIPPs** originated from the U.S. Department of Health, Education, and Welfare (HEW) report (1973) and later became the bedrock of the **Privacy Act of 1974** and the **OECD Guidelines**.

**The 5 FIPPs Principles:**
1. **Notice / Awareness** — Data subjects must be informed *what* data is collected and *why*.
2. **Choice / Consent** — Opt-in/opt-out mechanisms must be provided.
3. **Access / Participation** — Subjects can view and correct their data.
4. **Integrity / Security** — Data must be accurate and protected from misuse.
5. **Enforcement / Redress** — There must be a mechanism to resolve violations.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Memory Hook:** *"N-C-A-I-E → No Child Admits Inadequate Enforcement"* (Notice, Choice, Access, Integrity, Enforcement).

---

### 2.1.2 OECD Privacy Guidelines (1980, Revised 2013)

The **Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)** issued the first *internationally binding* privacy framework. The 2013 revision aligned it with modern challenges (cloud, big data, IoT).

**The 8 OECD Principles:**

| # | Principle | CPS Engineering Mapping |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Collection Limitation** | Sensor sampling rate caps, edge filtering |
| 2 | **Data Quality** | Noise injection, calibration protocols |
| 3 | **Purpose Specification** | Tagging data with intended use (metadata) |
| 4 | **Use Limitation** | Access Control Lists (ACLs), purpose-binding APIs |
| 5 | **Security Safeguards** | Encryption, TPM, Secure Elements |
| 6 | **Openness** | Transparent privacy policies (machine-readable) |
| 7 | **Individual Participation** | User dashboards, consent interfaces |
| 8 | **Accountability** | Data Protection Officers (DPOs), audit logs |

> [!NOTE] The 2013 revision added two **expansive concepts**: **Privacy Management Programme** and **Risk-Based Approach**.

---

### 2.1.3 Privacy by Design (PbD) — Ann Cavoukian, 1995

Ann Cavoukian (former Information & Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, Canada) proposed **7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design**. KTU 2024 Module 1 lists these as **mandatory**.

**The 7 PbD Principles:**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** — Anticipate privacy invasions *before* they occur.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — No opt-in required; max privacy out-of-the-box.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** — Not bolted on as an afterthought.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** — Privacy ≠ trade-off with functionality.
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection** — From data birth to secure destruction.
6. **Visibility and Transparency** — Open to verification by users and regulators.
7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric**

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Pro-Default-Embed-Full-Vis-User"* (7 P's of PbD).

---

### 2.1.4 General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) — EU, 2018

While not strictly a "framework" in the OECD sense, GDPR is the **de facto global benchmark** for privacy law and is examined under KTU 2024.

**The 7 GDPR Principles (Article 5):**

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency**
2. **Purpose Limitation**
3. **Data Minimization**
4. **Accuracy**
5. **Storage Limitation**
6. **Integrity and Confidentiality**
7. **Accountability**

> [!NOTE] GDPR also introduces **Rights of Data Subjects**: Right to Access, Right to Rectification, Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten), Right to Data Portability, Right to Object.

---

### 2.1.5 NIST Privacy Framework (v1.0, 2020)

The U.S. **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** released the Privacy Framework to complement its Cybersecurity Framework. It is built around **5 Functions**:

1. **Identify-P** — Establish privacy governance
2. **Govern-P** — Define roles, policies, processes
3. **Control-P** — Implement data management controls
4. **Communicate-P** — Transparency to data subjects
5. **Protect-P** — Apply security controls to privacy

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 frequently tests: *"Map GDPR's accountability principle to NIST's Govern-P function."* This is a **2-mark application question**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examination Cheat Code**
> The following table consolidates every "exam-defining" principle across the 5 frameworks.

| Framework | # of Principles | Origin | Key Strength | Key Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FIPPs** | 5 | USA, 1973 | Foundational clarity | No tech-specific guidance |
| **OECD Guidelines** | 8 | OECD, 1980/2013 | International consensus | Non-binding |
| **Privacy by Design** | 7 | Canada, 1995 | Engineering-friendly | Self-regulatory |
| **GDPR** | 7 (Art. 5) | EU, 2018 | Legally enforceable | Heavy compliance cost |
| **NIST PF** | 5 Functions | USA, 2020 | Risk-based, modular | Voluntary |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Framework Most Applied | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Healthcare Wearables** | GDPR + PbD | Health data is "special category" |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | OECD + NIST PF | Cross-border data flows |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | PbD + FIPPs | Default-privacy for sensors |
| **Smart Cities** | OECD + GDPR | Citizen surveillance risks |
| **Smart Agriculture** | FIPPs + PbD | Low-risk, farmer consent focus |

---

## 2.4 Cross-Framework Mapping (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE] **KTU 14-Mark Favourite Question**
> *"Compare and contrast OECD Privacy Guidelines and GDPR."* — Use the matrix below as a template.

| Dimension | OECD (2013) | GDPR (2018) |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Force** | Non-binding recommendation | Legally binding regulation |
| **Scope** | All personal data | All personal data of EU residents |
| **Consent** | Implicit (with safeguards) | Explicit, freely given, specific |
| **Breach Notification** | Recommended | Mandatory within 72 hours |
| **Sanctions** | Reputational | Up to €20M or 4% of global turnover |
| **DPO Mandate** | Encouraged | Mandatory in many cases |
| **Data Portability** | Not specified | Explicitly granted |
| **Right to Erasure** | Not specified | Explicitly granted |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of a Privacy Risk Score for a CPS Component

> [!NOTE] The following is a **synthesized analytical model** that KTU examiners love to test as a 14-mark applied question. It combines OECD, GDPR, and PbD principles into a single scoring function.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a CPS node (e.g., a wearable ECG sensor) that collects data $D$ from a user $U$. The privacy risk $R$ is a function of:
- **Sensitivity** of the data ($S$)
- **Volume** of data collected ($V$)
- **Duration** of retention ($\tau$)
- **Number of third parties** with access ($N$)
- **Strength of security controls** ($\sigma$)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Base Privacy Risk Function**

We start with the principle of *Collection Limitation* (OECD) and *Data Minimization* (GDPR). The risk grows with sensitivity and volume:

$$R_{\text{base}} = S \cdot V$$

where:
- $S$ = sensitivity coefficient, normalized to $S \in [0, 1]$
- $V$ = volume of data records, normalized to $V \in [0, 1]$

**Step 2 — Apply the Retention Multiplier (Storage Limitation Principle)**

Per GDPR Article 5(1)(e), data must not be kept longer than necessary. The retention factor introduces **exponential decay** in safety over time:

$$R_{\tau} = R_{\text{base}} \cdot e^{\lambda \tau}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = exposure growth rate, $\lambda > 0$ (e.g., $\lambda = 0.01$ per day)
- $\tau$ = retention period (in days)

This models the fact that **older stored data is more vulnerable** due to evolving attack vectors.

**Step 3 — Apply the Dissemination Multiplier (Use Limitation Principle)**

Each third-party access point compounds the risk. We use a *logarithmic* model because doubling the access surface does not double the risk (diminishing marginal exposure):

$$R_N = R_{\tau} \cdot \log_2(N + 1)$$

where:
- $N$ = number of authorized third parties
- The $+1$ avoids the singularity at $N = 0$

**Step 4 — Apply the Security Discount (Integrity & Confidentiality Principle)**

Strong security controls reduce risk. The *security discount factor* is:

$$D_{\sigma} = e^{-\sigma}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = security strength, $\sigma \in [0, 5]$ (with $\sigma = 5$ representing military-grade encryption)

**Step 5 — Combine All Factors into the Final Privacy Risk Equation**

$$R_{\text{privacy}} = S \cdot V \cdot e^{\lambda \tau} \cdot \log_2(N + 1) \cdot e^{-\sigma}$$

Simplifying the two exponentials:

$$R_{\text{privacy}} = S \cdot V \cdot \log_2(N + 1) \cdot e^{(\lambda \tau - \sigma)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Interpretation**
> A CPS architect should minimize $R_{\text{privacy}}$ by:
> - Lowering $S$ (anonymization, pseudonymization)
> - Lowering $V$ (data minimization at the edge)
> - Lowering $\tau$ (auto-deletion policies)
> - Lowering $N$ (strict purpose binding)
> - Increasing $\sigma$ (encryption, secure enclaves)

### 3.1.3 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

Suppose:
- $S = 0.9$ (highly sensitive: ECG data)
- $V = 0.6$ (moderate volume)
- $\tau = 365$ days (1 year retention)
- $\lambda = 0.005$ per day
- $N = 3$ third parties
- $\sigma = 2$ (AES-128 equivalent)

**Calculation:**

$$\lambda \tau = 0.005 \times 365 = 1.825$$

$$\lambda \tau - \sigma = 1.825 - 2 = -0.175$$

$$e^{-0.175} \approx 0.839$$

$$\log_2(3 + 1) = \log_2(4) = 2$$

$$R_{\text{privacy}} = 0.9 \times 0.6 \times 2 \times 0.839$$

$$R_{\text{privacy}} = 1.08 \times 0.839$$

$$R_{\text{privacy}} \approx 0.906$$

A risk score of $\approx 0.91$ on a $0$–$1$ normalized scale indicates **high residual privacy risk** — the system needs remediation (e.g., increase $\sigma$ to 4, reduce $\tau$ to 90 days).

---

## 3.2 Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) — Stepwise Workflow

> [!NOTE] **KTU 3-Mark Favourite**
> *"List the steps of a Privacy Impact Assessment."* Memorize these in order.

The PIA is a **systematic process** mandated by GDPR Article 35 and aligned with OECD's Risk-Based Approach.

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Need** — Determine whether a PIA is required (e.g., processing sensitive data, large-scale monitoring, vulnerable subjects).
2. **Step 2 — Describe the Processing** — Document nature, scope, context, and purposes of data processing.
3. **Step 3 — Assess Necessity & Proportionality** — Is the collection *necessary*? Is it *proportionate* to the purpose? (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) — Data Minimization).
4. **Step 4 — Identify and Assess Risks** — To data subjects' rights and freedoms.
5. **Step 5 — Identify Mitigations** — Technical (encryption, anonymization) and organizational (policies, training).
6. **Step 6 — Document and Review** — Produce the PIA report; review periodically.
7. **Step 7 — Consult Stakeholders** — DPO, data subjects, regulators (where mandated).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Mapping: GDPR Principles → PbD Principles

This is a **highly KTU-relevant** derivation (often tested as a 7-mark sub-question).

| GDPR Principle (Art. 5) | Closest PbD Principle | Mapping Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency | Visibility and Transparency | Both demand openness |
| Purpose Limitation | Privacy as Default | Data is locked to specified use |
| Data Minimization | Privacy Embedded into Design | Architecturally minimized at design time |
| Accuracy | Full Functionality (Positive-Sum) | Accurate data ≠ loss of utility |
| Storage Limitation | End-to-End Security (Lifecycle) | Data lifecycle ends with secure disposal |
| Integrity & Confidentiality | End-to-End Security | Security is a PbD pillar |
| Accountability | Respect for User Privacy | User-centric governance |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Scheme Part A comprises short-answer questions testing definitions, lists, and brief explanations. Each carries **3 marks**.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**List and briefly explain the 7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design (PbD).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design, proposed by **Ann Cavoukian (1995)**, are:

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** — Anticipate privacy-invasive events before they occur, rather than reacting post-breach.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — The maximum degree of privacy is automatically ensured; users need not take any action.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** — Privacy is an integral component of the system architecture, not a bolted-on add-on.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** — Privacy and functionality co-exist; it rejects the false trade-off.
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection** — Secure data from collection to destruction across the entire lifecycle.
6. **Visibility and Transparency** — All privacy-related components are open to inspection by users and regulators.
7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric** — The system is designed around user interests, with strong consent mechanisms.

*(1 mark for correct listing, 1 mark for brief explanation of 3–4 principles, 1 mark for naming the originator/context.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between FIPPs and OECD Privacy Guidelines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FIPPs (1973) | OECD (1980/2013) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | U.S. HEW Report | Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development |
| **Scope** | National (USA) | International (38 member countries) |
| **Principles** | 5 | 8 |
| **Key Addition** | — | Includes **Accountability** and **Openness** as distinct principles |
| **Legal Force** | Soft law basis | Soft law, but adopted by many national laws |

The FIPPs can be viewed as a **subset of the OECD framework**, with the OECD adding more rigorous cross-border data flow guidance and an explicit accountability principle (added in 2013 revision).

*(1 mark for tabular comparison, 1 mark for FIPPs, 1 mark for OECD specifics.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with **module internal choice**. Each sub-part typically carries **7 marks**. The model answers below are exam-board ready.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**"Privacy by Design is the most engineering-relevant framework for Cyber-Physical Systems." Critically evaluate this statement with reference to PbD's 7 principles and at least one alternative framework.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the 7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design in detail, with a CPS-specific example for each.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Compare PbD with GDPR (or NIST PF), highlighting engineering implications and limitations.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]

The 7 Foundational Principles of **Privacy by Design (PbD)**, articulated by **Ann Cavoukian in 1995**, are designed to embed privacy into the very fabric of engineering systems — making them highly suitable for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), where physical sensors, network communications, and application analytics create a complex privacy surface.

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial [1 mark]**
   In a CPS context (e.g., a smart healthcare wearable), this principle translates to **threat modelling at the design phase** itself. Engineers must conduct **Privacy Threat Assessments (PTA)** before deployment, identifying risks such as re-identification, side-channel inference, and data leakage — and designing countermeasures in advance (e.g., differential privacy noise injection).

2. **Privacy as the Default Setting [1 mark]**
   Consider a smart speaker: it should ship with **microphone disabled by default**, requiring explicit user activation. Similarly, an autonomous vehicle should default to **on-device processing** rather than cloud upload. The principle ensures that the *path of least resistance* for the user is also the *most private* configuration.

3. **Privacy Embedded into Design [1 mark]**
   In Industrial IoT, this means that **sensor data is filtered, anonymized, and aggregated at the edge** before transmission. The architecture itself enforces privacy — not a separate privacy module that can be disabled.

4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum [1 mark]**
   A smart grid can use **homomorphic encryption** to perform billing analytics on encrypted consumption data — achieving both privacy *and* operational functionality. PbD rejects the myth that privacy must compromise utility.

5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection [1 mark]**
   A CPS device must implement **cryptographic data destruction** at end-of-life (NIST SP 800-88 media sanitization). The data lifecycle includes collection → storage → processing → sharing → archival → disposal, and each stage must be privacy-preserving.

6. **Visibility and Transparency [1 mark]**
   Users must have access to **machine-readable privacy policies** (e.g., P3P, MICE — Machine-readable Icons for Privacy Expressions). Smart devices should display *what data they collect, where it goes, and how long it is kept* — auditable by both user and regulator.

7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric [0.5 mark]**
   Consent dashboards, granular permission controls, and right-to-erasure interfaces must be first-class UI features, not buried in settings menus.

8. **Synthesizing remark** [0.5 mark]: Together, these 7 principles make PbD the *only* major framework explicitly crafted for engineering implementation, distinguishing it from policy-only frameworks like OECD or legal frameworks like GDPR.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]

| Dimension | Privacy by Design (PbD) | GDPR (2018) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Engineering philosophy / design framework | Legal regulation |
| **Binding Force** | Voluntary / self-regulation | Legally enforceable across the EU |
| **Granularity** | 7 foundational principles | 7 processing principles + 6 data subject rights |
| **CPS Engineering Hooks** | Default privacy, embedded design, lifecycle security | Lawful basis, data minimization, DPO appointment |
| **Strengths** | Highly actionable for engineers; technology-agnostic | Strong enforcement, global extraterritorial reach |
| **Weaknesses** | No penalty mechanism; relies on organizational will | Heavy compliance overhead; not natively engineering-oriented |

**Critical Evaluation [3 marks]:**

The statement that *PbD is the most engineering-relevant framework* is **largely true** but with caveats:
- PbD's principles map directly to **engineering decisions** (architecture, defaults, lifecycle) — making it ideal for CPS developers.
- However, GDPR complements PbD by providing the **legal teeth** that PbD lacks. In practice, leading CPS companies (e.g., Apple, Siemens) follow a *hybrid* model: GDPR for compliance + PbD for engineering culture.
- A pure PbD approach without regulatory backing risks being **ignored under commercial pressure** (e.g., ad-driven business models). GDPR's **€20M / 4% turnover fine** acts as a powerful deterrent.
- **Conclusion:** PbD is *indispensable* as a design philosophy, but for *enforceable* privacy in CPS deployments, GDPR (or equivalent national laws) must be the operational floor, with PbD as the engineering ceiling.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**"With the proliferation of IoT-enabled Cyber-Physical Systems, traditional privacy frameworks are inadequate." Discuss this statement by comparing the OECD Privacy Guidelines (2013 revision) with the NIST Privacy Framework (2020), and explain how each addresses CPS-specific challenges.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:** Detail the 8 OECD Privacy Guidelines and the 5 Functions of the NIST Privacy Framework, with specific CPS applications.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:** Critically compare both frameworks on the dimensions of scope, enforceability, CPS-relevance, and adaptability, concluding with a recommended integrated approach.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]

**The 8 OECD Privacy Guidelines (1980, revised 2013):**

1. **Collection Limitation** [0.5 mark] — In CPS, this translates to **minimum-necessary sensor sampling** (e.g., a smart thermostat need not sample every second if the user only needs hourly averages).
2. **Data Quality** [0.5 mark] — Sensor calibration, anomaly detection, and noise reduction ensure that the personal inferences drawn from CPS data are accurate.
3. **Purpose Specification** [0.5 mark] — Each data stream in a CPS must carry a **machine-readable purpose tag** (e.g., W3C Data Privacy Vocabulary).
4. **Use Limitation** [0.5 mark] — Access Control Lists (ACLs) and **purpose-binding APIs** prevent secondary use of CPS data.
5. **Security Safeguards** [0.5 mark] — Hardware root-of-trust (TPM, Secure Elements), encryption at rest and in transit.
6. **Openness** [0.5 mark] — Privacy policies for CPS must be **publicly auditable** and machine-readable.
7. **Individual Participation** [0.5 mark] — User dashboards showing what data is collected, by whom, and for how long.
8. **Accountability** [0.5 mark] — Data Protection Officers (DPOs) for large-scale CPS deployments, with audit logs.

**The 5 NIST Privacy Framework Functions:**

1. **Identify-P** [0.4 mark] — Inventory all CPS data assets, classify by sensitivity, map to legal obligations.
2. **Govern-P** [0.4 mark] — Establish cross-functional privacy governance: legal, engineering, product, compliance.
3. **Control-P** [0.4 mark] — Implement data management controls: minimization, de-identification, retention policies.
4. **Communicate-P** [0.4 mark] — Transparency mechanisms: privacy notices, user dashboards, breach disclosures.
5. **Protect-P** [0.4 mark] — Apply security controls (encryption, access control, network segmentation) to privacy data.

**CPS-Specific Applications [1.5 marks]:**
- **Smart Healthcare (e.g., continuous glucose monitors):** OECD's Purpose Specification ensures data is used only for diabetes management; NIST's Control-P enforces on-device processing.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** OECD's Use Limitation prevents location data from being shared with advertisers; NIST's Protect-P enforces encrypted V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication.
- **Smart Cities:** OECD's Collection Limitation caps video surveillance retention; NIST's Govern-P mandates a city-level privacy governance board.

---

#### Model Solution — Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparative Analysis [5 marks]:**

| Dimension | OECD (2013) | NIST PF (2020) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | All personal data, cross-border | All personal data, US-centric but modular | OECD more global; NIST more flexible |
| **Enforceability** | Non-binding (soft law) | Voluntary framework | Both lack legal force; NIST more adaptable |
| **CPS-Relevance** | General; CPS addressed in 2013 revision's risk-based approach | Explicitly engineered for IoT/IIoT/AI | NIST has an edge |
| **Adaptability** | Slow to update (33-year gap between major revisions) | Modular; easy to extend with profiles | NIST is more agile |
| **Engineering Depth** | Policy-level guidance | Process-level and risk-level | NIST is more actionable |
| **Global Adoption** | 38 OECD members + many non-members | US Federal mandate; gaining global traction | OECD wider; NIST deeper in tech sectors |

**Critical Evaluation [1.5 marks]:**

The claim that *traditional privacy frameworks are inadequate for CPS* is **partially valid**:
- **Valid aspects:** Both OECD and NIST were not originally designed for the **scale, velocity, and heterogeneity** of CPS data. Sensor fusion, real-time processing, and AI inference create privacy risks (e.g., re-identification from aggregated sensor data) that pre-2013 frameworks did not anticipate.
- **Counter-argument:** The 2013 OECD revision and NIST PF 2020 both incorporate **risk-based approaches** that are reasonably adaptive. The frameworks' *principles* are timeless; only the *implementation guidance* needs periodic updates.

**Recommended Integrated Approach [0.5 mark]:**
A *layered hybrid model* is recommended:
- **Foundation:** OECD's internationally recognized 8 principles for global harmonization.
- **Operational Layer:** NIST PF's 5 Functions for engineering and risk management.
- **Compliance Layer:** GDPR (or local equivalent) for legal enforceability.
- **Design Culture:** Privacy by Design for engineering culture.

This **OECD + NIST + GDPR + PbD** stack provides a **defence-in-depth** privacy architecture for CPS — addressing policy, process, law, and engineering simultaneously.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PECPT743 Module 1**

1. **Confusing FIPPs with OECD** — They are related but distinct. FIPPs (5 principles) preceded OECD (8 principles). Examiners penalize students who mix them up.
2. **Forgetting the originator of PbD** — Ann Cavoukian (1995) is *not optional*; quoting her earns an extra mark.
3. **Listing principles without CPS context** — PECPT743 is *Privacy in CPS*, not generic privacy law. Always tie your answer to a CPS scenario (smart healthcare, autonomous vehicles, IIoT, smart cities).
4. **Not mentioning the 2013 OECD revision** — The 2013 revision added **Privacy Management Programme** and **Risk-Based Approach**; missing these is a 2-mark loss.
5. **Ignoring the NIST PF's 2020 release year** — Examiners specifically test whether you know NIST PF is *newer* than GDPR.
6. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — A well-labelled diagram or comparison table can fetch 2–3 bonus marks and demonstrates structured thinking.
7. **Failing to mention DPO** — For GDPR-based questions, the *Data Protection Officer* role is a frequent sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- [ ] **FIPPs** = **5 principles** (Notice, Choice, Access, Integrity, Enforcement) — origin: **USA, 1973**.
- [ ] **OECD Guidelines** = **8 principles** — origin: **1980**, revised **2013** (added risk-based approach).
- [ ] **Privacy by Design** = **7 principles** — originator: **Ann Cavoukian, 1995**.
- [ ] **GDPR** = **Article 5** has **7 principles** + **6 data subject rights** — enforceable from **May 25, 2018**.
- [ ] **NIST Privacy Framework** = **5 Functions** (Identify-P, Govern-P, Control-P, Communicate-P, Protect-P) — released **January 2020**.
- [ ] **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** has **7 standard steps** (memorize in order).
- [ ] **GDPR maximum penalty** = **€20 million OR 4% of annual global turnover**, whichever is higher.
- [ ] **GDPR breach notification** must occur within **72 hours** of awareness.
- [ ] **CPS Privacy Risk Equation:** $R_{\text{privacy}} = S \cdot V \cdot \log_2(N+1) \cdot e^{(\lambda \tau - \sigma)}$
- [ ] **PbD's core engineering dictum:** *"Privacy is the default; it is embedded; it spans the full lifecycle."*
- [ ] **OECD's key CPS addition (2013):** Risk-based approach + Privacy Management Programme.
- [ ] **NIST PF's key CPS addition (2020):** Explicitly models AI/ML privacy risks.
- [ ] **Most KTU 14-mark question pattern:** *"Compare Framework X with Framework Y, applied to a CPS scenario."* — Always include a **comparison table** + **CPS example**.
- [ ] **The Five Pillars of CPS Privacy:** *Confidentiality, Integrity, Control, Compliance* (and **Consent** as the fifth).
- [ ] **Three Layers of CPS where privacy must be enforced:** *Perception, Network, Application*.
- [ ] **PbD memory hook:** *"Pro-Default-Embed-Full-Vis-User"* (7 P's).
- [ ] **OECD memory hook:** *"C-Q-P-U-S-O-I-A"* (Collection, Quality, Purpose, Use, Security, Openness, Individual, Accountability).
- [ ] **GDPR memory hook:** *"L-P-D-A-S-I-A"* (Lawfulness, Purpose, Data-min, Accuracy, Storage, Integrity, Accountability).
- [ ] **NIST PF memory hook:** *"I-G-C-C-P"* (Identify, Govern, Control, Communicate, Protect).
- [ ] **Three Rights of Data Subjects under GDPR frequently tested:** *Right to Erasure, Right to Data Portability, Right to Object*.
- [ ] **Privacy by Design's most novel principle (KTU favourite):** *Positive-Sum, Full Functionality* — privacy does not require sacrificing functionality.
- [ ] **Cross-framework insight:** GDPR's *Accountability* ↔ OECD's *Accountability* ↔ NIST's *Govern-P* ↔ PbD's *Visibility & Transparency* — all converge on the same concept of demonstrable, auditable privacy practice.

> [!NOTE] **Final Examiner Tip**
> In every answer, end with a **synthesis line** like: *"Hence, an effective CPS privacy posture requires a hybrid approach combining [Framework A]'s [strength] with [Framework B]'s [strength], as exemplified by the [CPS scenario] use case."* This single sentence often decides between a 12 and a 14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Legal and Ethical Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Legal and Ethical Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *Legal and Ethical Considerations* refer to the structured set of jurisdictional statutes, regulatory mandates, professional codes of conduct, and moral principles that govern the design, deployment, data-handling, and lifecycle operation of systems where computational algorithms tightly interact with physical processes and human users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Legal considerations deal with *codified, enforceable* rules (Acts, Directives, Regulations, Court precedents). Ethical considerations deal with *non-codified but normative* principles (fairness, autonomy, dignity, accountability) that guide decision-making when the law is silent, ambiguous, or technologically outdated.

The two are **complementary and non-substitutable**:
- **Law** = the *minimum* acceptable behavior (compliance baseline).
- **Ethics** = the *aspirational* standard of behavior (good practice ceiling).

Together they form the **Privacy Governance Stack** of any CPS deployment.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Hospital"

Imagine a futuristic **Smart Hospital** (a CPS) where ICU beds automatically monitor vitals, AI prescribes dosages, and robots dispense medicine.

| Layer | Real-World Analogy in Smart Hospital | Maps To |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Rules** | Hospital must follow the *Clinical Establishments Act* and *Drugs & Cosmetics Act* | GDPR, IT Act, HIPAA |
| **Ethical Rules** | Doctor still takes *informed consent* even if AI suggests a treatment | Autonomy, Beneficence |
| **CPS Component** | The bed sensor, the AI engine, the robot arm | Sensors, Actuators, Networks |
| **Privacy Risk** | Patient's heartbeat data is a *sensitive personal datum* | Data minimization, Purpose limitation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** A CPS engineer who follows only the law but ignores ethics is like a doctor who follows the prescription rules perfectly but never asks *“Does the patient actually want this treatment?”* The system is legally compliant but morally hollow.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam High-Yield)

- **Data Subject:** The identifiable individual whose data a CPS collects (e.g., the patient, the driver, the homeowner).
- **Data Controller:** The entity that determines the *purpose and means* of processing (e.g., the smart-grid operator, the OEM).
- **Data Processor:** The entity that processes data *on behalf of* the controller (e.g., a cloud analytics vendor).
- **Privacy by Design (PbD):** Embedding privacy controls into the *architecture* of a CPS from day one — not as a retrofit.
- **Privacy by Default:** The *default settings* of a CPS must be the most privacy-protective option.
- **Informed Consent:** Permission that is *specific, informed, free, and unambiguous*.
- **Algorithmic Accountability:** The obligation to *explain and justify* decisions made by automated CPS subsystems.
- **Right to be Forgotten (RTBF):** The right of a data subject to demand *erasure* of personal data under specific conditions.
- **Data Minimization:** Collecting *only* the data strictly necessary for the stated purpose.
- **Purpose Limitation:** Using data *only* for the purpose it was collected for.
- **Cross-Border Data Flow:** Transfer of personal data across national jurisdictions — a major legal flashpoint in CPS (e.g., IoT devices shipping EU user data to US servers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — KTU):** The *intersection* of law and ethics is where the most difficult CPS decisions are made. An autonomous vehicle may be *legally* allowed to log passenger location (because the user clicked "I Agree"), but *ethically* questionable if the user did not truly understand the consent form.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in CPS

A CPS is uniquely risky from a privacy standpoint because it:
1. Operates **continuously and invisibly** (sensors always on).
2. Generates **high-velocity, high-volume** personal data.
3. Performs **physical actions** (braking, dosing, locking) where errors can cause bodily harm.
4. **Blurs accountability** between the manufacturer, software vendor, network operator, and user.
5. Creates **secondary use** risks (a fitness tracker’s heart data being sold to insurers).

For these reasons, *legal* compliance alone is insufficient — the system needs *ethical* safeguards baked in.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars — Laws vs. Ethics in CPS Privacy

The privacy governance of a CPS rests on two distinct but interlocking pillars.

#### Pillar A — Legal Considerations (Codified, Enforceable)

These are the *black-letter laws* a CPS must obey. They vary by jurisdiction, but the most influential ones for KTU-aligned study are:

**1. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) — European Union, 2018**
- Applies extraterritorially to any CPS that processes EU residents' data.
- 7 core principles: *Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity & Confidentiality, Accountability.*
- Mandates *Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)* for high-risk processing.
- Fines up to **4% of global annual turnover or €20 million**, whichever is higher.

**2. California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) / CPRA — USA, 2020/2023**
- Grants *Right to Know, Right to Delete, Right to Opt-Out of Sale*.
- Particularly relevant for CPS vendors selling into the US market.

**3. Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008) — India**
- Section 43A: Compensation for negligence in handling *sensitive personal data*.
- Section 72A: Punishment for disclosure of personal information in breach of contract.
- SPDI Rules, 2011: Define *sensitive personal data* and mandate consent.

**4. Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) — USA, 1996**
- Governs *Protected Health Information (PHI)*.
- Critical for medical CPS (wearables, remote surgery robots, smart implants).

**5. Personal Data Protection Bill (India, proposed) & Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023**
- India's evolving framework introducing *Data Fiduciary*, *Data Principal* rights, and *Consent Managers*.

**6. Sector-Specific Regulations**
- **Automotive:** UNECE WP.29, ISO/SAE 21434 (cybersecurity for vehicles).
- **Medical Devices:** FDA 21 CFR Part 820, EU MDR 2017/745.
- **Industrial IoT:** NISTIR 8259, IEC 62443.

#### Pillar B — Ethical Considerations (Non-Codified, Normative)

These are the *moral compasses* that govern decisions where the law is silent, where multiple legal options exist, or where technology outpaces regulation.

**1. Principlism (Beauchamp & Childress — Bioethics Framework)**
Four pillars, easily mapped to CPS:
- **Autonomy:** Respect the user's right to self-decision (consent, opt-out, override).
- **Beneficence:** Do good (the CPS should improve safety, health, efficiency).
- **Non-Maleficence:** Do no harm (prevent algorithmic bias, data leaks, physical harm).
- **Justice:** Fair distribution of benefits and risks (no discriminatory CPS profiling).

**2. The IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD) Framework**
- Eight general principles: *Human Rights, Well-Being, Data Agency, Effectiveness, Transparency, Accountability, Awareness of Misuse, Competence.*
- Industry-grade guidance specifically written for *autonomous and intelligent systems* (i.e., CPS).

**3. The Asilomar AI Principles & OECD AI Principles**
- Stress *human-centered values, transparency, robustness, accountability*.

**4. Consequentialist / Deontological / Virtue-Ethics Views**
- **Consequentialism (Utilitarianism):** The right action is the one that maximizes overall well-being. *(CPS: optimize for aggregate road safety, even if it logs individual driving data.)*
- **Deontology (Kantian):** The right action follows a moral rule, regardless of outcome. *(CPS: never collect a datum unless explicit consent is given, even if doing so would save lives.)*
- **Virtue Ethics:** The right action is what a *virtuous engineer* would do. *(CPS: design with humility, foresight, and care for the user.)*

---

### 2.2 The "Legal-Ethical Gap" — Where Most CPS Failures Occur

A CPS is most vulnerable in the **Legal-Ethical Gap** — the space where:
- The law permits an action but ethics forbids it.
- The law forbids an action but ethics permits it.
- The law is silent and ethics provides the only guidance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Concept:** The *Legal-Ethical Gap* is the most-tested area in Module 1 questions. Students should be able to *identify* a real-world CPS scenario and classify it into the gap.

**Examples:**
- A smart-speaker recording private conversations *legally* under broad Terms of Service but *unethically* because users did not understand.
- An employer tracking delivery drivers' biometrics *legally* under workplace law but *unethically* if used for punitive scoring.
- A medical CPS refusing to share anonymized outbreak data *legally* permitted, but *unethically* withholding public-health benefit.

---

### 2.3 KTU Reference Sheet — Key Frameworks at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a *high-yield cheat sheet* for KTU ESE preparation. Memorize the columns and the act → principle mapping.

| Framework / Law | Jurisdiction | Type | Year | CPS Relevance | Core Principle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | EU | Law | 2018 | Universal (extraterritorial) | Lawful, fair, transparent processing |
| **CCPA / CPRA** | California, USA | Law | 2020 / 2023 | Consumer CPS, wearables | Right to know, delete, opt-out |
| **IT Act + SPDI Rules** | India | Law | 2000 / 2008 / 2011 | All Indian CPS deployments | Sensitive personal data consent |
| **DPDPA** | India | Law | 2023 | Modern Indian CPS | Data fiduciary obligations |
| **HIPAA** | USA | Law | 1996 | Medical CPS, wearables | PHI confidentiality |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | EU | Law | 2017 | Medical-device CPS | Safety + post-market surveillance |
| **ISO/SAE 21434** | Global | Standard | 2021 | Automotive CPS | Cybersecurity engineering |
| **NIST Privacy Framework** | USA | Standard | 2020 | All CPS | Identify-Protect-Control-Inform-Respond |
| **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** | Global | Ethics | 2016/2019 | Autonomous CPS | 8 principles incl. data agency |
| **Principlism (Bioethics)** | Academic | Ethics | 1979 | Medical & human CPS | Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice |
| **OECD AI Principles** | International | Ethics | 2019 | AI-driven CPS | Human-centered, transparent, robust |
| **UNECE WP.29** | UN | Law | 2020 | Connected vehicles | Cyber Security Management System |
| **Right to be Forgotten** | EU / India | Law | 2018 / 2023 | All CPS with user data | Erasure on request |
| **Privacy by Design (PbD)** | Global | Ethics + Law | Ann Cavoukian, 1995 | All CPS | Embed privacy at architecture stage |

---

### 2.4 The 7 Foundational Principles of GDPR (Map These to Every CPS Question)

The GDPR is the global gold standard. Almost every KTU question on legal considerations will pivot around these 7 principles. Master them:

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency** — Process data legally, fairly, and transparently.
2. **Purpose Limitation** — Collect for specified, explicit, legitimate purposes only.
3. **Data Minimization** — Adequate, relevant, *limited* to what is necessary.
4. **Accuracy** — Keep data accurate and up to date.
5. **Storage Limitation** — Keep data only as long as necessary.
6. **Integrity and Confidentiality** — Ensure appropriate security.
7. **Accountability** — The controller must *demonstrate* compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** When designing a CPS sensor, ask — *“Which of these 7 principles is my sensor collection strategy violating?”* If the answer is "none," you have a privacy-respecting design. This single question is a KTU-favorite 7-mark prompt.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Where Legal-Ethical Considerations Apply |
|---|---|
| **Smart Grid (Energy CPS)** | Smart-meter data reveals household occupancy patterns → privacy laws on energy-consumption data. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Dashcam + lidar data may capture bystanders → consent issues, data-sharing with insurers. |
| **Medical CPS (Implants, Wearables)** | Continuous health telemetry = PHI → HIPAA, GDPR Art. 9 (special categories). |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Worker-tracking wearables raise surveillance and dignity issues. |
| **Smart Cities** | CCTV + traffic + pollution sensors = mass surveillance concerns. |
| **Drones (UAVs)** | Aerial imaging crosses property lines → trespass, voyeurism laws. |
| **Robotic Surgery** | Data sovereignty when the robot is operated remotely across borders. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Applying the Framework to a CPS Case

Since this topic is conceptual and not equation-based, the “derivation” equivalent is a **step-by-step Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** — the structured method a CPS engineer uses to identify and mitigate legal-ethical risks. We will apply it to a **Smart Insulin Pump** (a medical CPS).

---

### 3.1 The Case System — Smart Insulin Pump (SIP)

A wearable medical CPS that:
- Continuously monitors blood glucose via a subcutaneous sensor.
- Automatically injects insulin via a micro-pump.
- Uploads dosing history, meal logs, location, and activity to a cloud server.
- Allows the patient's endocrinologist to adjust dosage remotely.
- Sends marketing-style "wellness tips" based on the collected data.

The patient is a 14-year-old Type-1 diabetic. Their parents co-manage the device via a mobile app.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Skill:** A PIA is the single most testable structured procedure in this module. Memorize the 6 stages below.

**Stage 1 — Describe the CPS and Its Data Flows**
- *Identify* the data: glucose readings, insulin doses, timestamps, GPS location, meal photos, app usage logs.
- *Identify* the actors: patient, parent, endocrinologist, device manufacturer, cloud provider, insurance partner (if data is sold).
- *Identify* the legal hooks: This is medical CPS in India (DPDPA 2023) and possibly EU (GDPR Art. 9 — health data is a *special category*).

**Stage 2 — Identify the Lawful Basis for Processing**
- For the *medical* purpose → patient/guardian **explicit consent** (GDPR Art. 9(2)(a)).
- For the *wellness-tips marketing* → **separate, granular consent** is required. Pre-ticked boxes are *invalid* under GDPR.
- For the *insurer data sharing* → **explicit opt-in**, not bundled consent.

**Stage 3 — Apply the 7 GDPR Principles**
- *Purpose limitation:* Marketing is a *different purpose* from medical dosing. Either re-consent or anonymize.
- *Data minimization:* Does the pump need GPS to function? **No.** Strip it.
- *Storage limitation:* 7 years of medical data is justified, but 7 years of meal-photo metadata is not.
- *Integrity & confidentiality:* End-to-end encryption; access logs; no plaintext cloud storage.

**Stage 4 — Conduct an Ethical Review (Beyond the Law)**
- *Autonomy:* A 14-year-old has developing autonomy. Is the device giving the teen a voice, or is the parent fully in control? Ethical design suggests a *graduated consent* model.
- *Non-maleficence:* Could the data be used to *deny insurance* to the child later? Pre-empt this with contractual use-limits.
- *Justice:* Is the device affordable across socio-economic strata, or does it create a *two-tiered* diabetic population?

**Stage 5 — Identify and Mitigate Risks**
- *Risk:* Cloud breach exposing thousands of diabetic profiles.
- *Mitigation:* On-device differential privacy, pseudonymization, zero-trust cloud architecture.
- *Risk:* Algorithmic bias — the dose-calibration AI was trained on adults; may under-dose teens.
- *Mitigation:* Pediatric training data, periodic fairness audits, human-in-the-loop override.

**Stage 6 — Document, Sign Off, and Monitor**
- Maintain a *Record of Processing Activities (ROPA)* as required by GDPR Art. 30.
- Schedule annual *Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)*.
- Establish a *Data Protection Officer (DPO)* if processing is large-scale or high-risk.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Mapping the Legal-Ethical Gap for the Smart Insulin Pump

$$
\text{Gap Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \big( \text{Lawful?} \oplus \text{Ethical?} \big)_i
$$

Where $\oplus$ is the XOR (logical exclusive-or) operation. A score $> 0$ means there is a *legal-ethical misalignment*.

Let us tabulate the SIP features:

| Feature | Lawful? | Ethical? | Gap? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose sensing for dosing | Yes (consent) | Yes | No | Continue |
| Wellness-tip marketing | Only if separate consent | Questionable (uses health data) | **Yes** | Re-design with opt-in |
| Sharing with insurance | Only with explicit opt-in | Unethical without consent | **Yes** | Block by default |
| Continuous GPS tracking | Not necessary | Surveillance over-reach | **Yes** | **Remove GPS** |
| Remote dosage by doctor | Yes (clinical) | Yes | No | Audit trail required |
| Data sold to advertisers | No (GDPR/DPDPA) | No | No | **Strictly prohibited** |
| Parental full override | Yes (guardian) | Ethically grey (teen autonomy) | Borderline | Add teen consent mode |

$$\text{Gap Score}_{\text{SIP}} = 3 \;\; (\text{marketing}, \text{GPS}, \text{insurance-share})$$

**Engineering Decision:** The CPS team must redesign the SIP to reduce Gap Score to 0 before deployment. This is the *Legal-Ethical Compliance Function (LECF)* a privacy-aware CPS engineer optimizes.

---

### 3.4 Quick Algorithm (Pseudo-Flow) for KTU Writing

While we **do not** use code in non-CS subjects, the following *logical* procedure is what KTU expects in 14-mark answers:

1. **Identify** the CPS, its data, and its stakeholders.
2. **Map** applicable laws (jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction).
3. **Apply** the relevant ethical framework (Principlism / IEEE EAD).
4. **Compute** the Legal-Ethical Gap.
5. **Recommend** Privacy-by-Design controls.
6. **Document** the PIA / DPIA.

> [!NOTE]
> In a 14-mark KTU answer, these 6 steps form the natural 6-paragraph structure. Each paragraph is roughly 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — The Privacy Governance Stack for CPS

This diagram shows the layered relationship between laws, ethics, standards, and engineering controls in a CPS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layerA["LAW LAYER"]:::law
    layerB["ETHICS LAYER"]:::ethics
    layerC["STANDARDS LAYER"]:::std
    layerD["ENGINEERING CONTROLS LAYER"]:::ctrl
    layerE["CPS SYSTEM UNDER DESIGN"]:::cps

    g1["GDPR - EU"]:::law
    g2["DPDPA 2023 - India"]:::law
    g3["HIPAA - USA Health"]:::law
    g4["IT Act 2000 - India"]:::law
    g5["CCPA - California"]:::law

    e1["Principlism Autonomy Beneficence Non Maleficence Justice"]:::ethics
    e2["IEEE Ethically Aligned Design"]:::ethics
    e3["OECD AI Principles"]:::ethics
    e4["Asilomar Principles"]:::ethics

    s1["ISO SAE 21434 Automotive"]:::std
    s2["NIST Privacy Framework"]:::std
    s3["IEC 62443 Industrial"]:::std
    s4["EU MDR 2017 745 Medical"]:::std

    c1["Privacy by Design"]:::ctrl
    c2["Data Minimization"]:::ctrl
    c3["Encryption and Pseudonymization"]:::ctrl
    c4["Consent Management"]:::ctrl
    c5["DPIA and Audit Trail"]:::ctrl

    cps1["Smart Medical Device"]:::cps
    cps2["Autonomous Vehicle"]:::cps
    cps3["Industrial IoT"]:::cps
    cps4["Smart Grid"]:::cps

    layerA --- g1
    layerA --- g2
    layerA --- g3
    layerA --- g4
    layerA --- g5

    layerB --- e1
    layerB --- e2
    layerB --- e3
    layerB --- e4

    layerC --- s1
    layerC --- s2
    layerC --- s3
    layerC --- s4

    layerD --- c1
    layerD --- c2
    layerD --- c3
    layerD --- c4
    layerD --- c5

    layerE --- cps1
    layerE --- cps2
    layerE --- cps3
    layerE --- cps4

    layerA --> layerB
    layerB --> layerC
    layerC --> layerD
    layerD --> layerE

    classDef law fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ethics fill:#e5f2ff,stroke:#2c3e9e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef std fill:#e8f8e8,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cps fill:#f0e6ff,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows indicate a *funneling hierarchy*. The CPS at the bottom must simultaneously satisfy *all four* layers. A failure at any layer breaks the privacy posture. This is a frequent KTU diagram question.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Legal-Ethical Gap Identification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["STEP A Identify CPS Use Case"]:::step
    stepB["STEP B List Data Collected"]:::step
    stepC["STEP C Identify Stakeholders"]:::step
    stepD["STEP D Map Applicable Laws"]:::step
    stepE["STEP E Apply Ethical Principles"]:::step
    stepF["STEP F Compute Legal Ethical Gap"]:::step
    stepG["STEP G Design PbD Controls"]:::step
    stepH["STEP H Document DPIA Sign Off"]:::step
    stepI["DEPLOY CPS PRIVACY COMPLIANT"]:::endnode

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI

    classDef step fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Stakeholder Responsibility Matrix (RACI-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stk1["CPS MANUFACTURER"]:::manuf
    stk2["DATA CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    stk3["DATA PROCESSOR CLOUD VENDOR"]:::proc
    stk4["END USER DATA SUBJECT"]:::user
    stk5["REGULATOR GOVT BODY"]:::reg
    stk6["ETHICS REVIEW BOARD"]:::eth

    r1["Designs PbD and Security"]:::resp
    r2["Determines Purpose of Processing"]:::resp
    r3["Stores and Processes Data"]:::resp
    r4["Provides Informed Consent and Exercises Rights"]:::resp
    r5["Enforces Law Audits Fines"]:::resp
    r6["Reviews Ethical Risks and Approves Deployment"]:::resp

    stk1 --- r1
    stk2 --- r2
    stk3 --- r3
    stk4 --- r4
    stk5 --- r5
    stk6 --- r6

    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> r4
    r4 --> r5
    r5 --> r6

    classDef manuf fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#af601a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eth fill:#f2f4f4,stroke:#34495e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resp fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the *separation of duties*. The manufacturer *designs*, the controller *decides the purpose*, the processor *stores*, the user *consents*, the regulator *audits*, and the ethics board *reviews*. This separation is the cornerstone of modern CPS privacy governance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE patterns. Mark distribution is **3 marks for Part A short questions** and **14 marks for Part B long questions** (with sub-parts of 7+7).

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Distinguish between "Legal Considerations" and "Ethical Considerations" in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Give one example of each from a CPS domain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Legal Consideration | Ethical Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Codified, enforceable statutes | Normative, non-codified principles |
| **Enforcement** | Courts, regulators, fines | Professional bodies, peer review, public opinion |
| **Flexibility** | Slow to update; jurisdiction-bound | Adaptive; can pre-empt future laws |
| **Example** | GDPR mandates explicit consent for processing biometric data from a fitness CPS. | Ethically, a smart workplace should not monitor bathroom breaks even though it is technically feasible and not explicitly illegal. |
| **Failure Consequence** | Penalty, prosecution, injunction | Loss of trust, brand damage, employee revolt |

*(Full-marks answer: provide 2 such rows + 1 line summary stating they are complementary and both necessary.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*What is "Privacy by Design (PbD)"? List any FOUR of its foundational principles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Privacy by Design (PbD)** is a framework developed by Dr. Ann Cavoukian (1995) that calls for privacy to be *embedded into the design, architecture, and operations* of technologies and business practices **from the very inception** of a CPS — rather than being retrofitted as an afterthought.

**Four Foundational Principles of PbD:**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** — Anticipate privacy risks before they materialize.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — The CPS must ship with maximum-privacy defaults; no opt-out of baseline protection.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** — Privacy is a *core functional requirement*, not an add-on module.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** — Design rejects the false trade-off that privacy must be sacrificed for functionality.
5. *(Optional)* End-to-End Security — Full lifecycle protection.
6. *(Optional)* Visibility and Transparency — Users must be able to see what is being collected.
7. *(Optional)* Respect for User Privacy — Keep the user interests central.

*(Any 4 well-explained = full marks.)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In 14-mark answers, students often write *generic* answers about "privacy is important" or "GDPR is a law". **This will score ≤ 4 / 14.** The board examiner wants *domain-specific* mapping — pick a CPS (medical / vehicle / grid / IIoT) and *apply* the framework. Generic definitions get partial credit; case-study application gets full credit.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
*(a)* Explain the **seven core principles of GDPR** and discuss how each applies to a **Smart Healthcare CPS** (e.g., a remote patient-monitoring wearable). *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With a suitable example, define the **"Legal-Ethical Gap"** in CPS design. Discuss how an engineer can use the **Principlism framework** to resolve two distinct gaps. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**The 7 GDPR Principles Applied to a Smart Healthcare CPS:**

Let the CPS be a **continuous remote cardiac monitor (wearable patch)** that streams ECG data, location, and activity to a hospital cloud.

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency** — The hospital must have a *lawful basis* (vital interests or explicit consent) and must publish a transparent notice listing what is collected. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* The patch's companion app must display a layered privacy notice, not a 40-page EULA.

2. **Purpose Limitation** — Data collected for *cardiac monitoring* cannot be repurposed for *insurance underwriting* without fresh consent. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* The hospital's research arm must re-consent before using the data for a cardiac-AI study.

3. **Data Minimization** — The patch should not collect *GPS* unless the cardiology use case strictly requires it. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* Strip the GPS module if location is not medically needed. This is a 0.5-mark engineering decision.

4. **Accuracy** — Faulty ECG readings must be flagged and corrected. The patient must have a way to dispute. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* Implement a patient-facing dashboard to view and correct records.

5. **Storage Limitation** — Retain raw ECG for the clinically necessary period (typically 10 years for cardiac data, per medical norms); auto-delete older records. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* Automated retention policy on the hospital cloud.

6. **Integrity and Confidentiality** — End-to-end encryption, role-based access, audit logs. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, multi-factor auth for clinicians.

7. **Accountability** — The hospital must *demonstrate* compliance via a Record of Processing Activities (ROPA), DPIA, and a Data Protection Officer. *(0.5 mark)*
   → *CPS application:* The hospital DPO signs off on the patch deployment; annual DPIA refresh.

> *(3.5 marks for explaining the 7 principles + 3.5 marks for the CPS application mapping. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Definition of Legal-Ethical Gap (2 marks):**
The *Legal-Ethical Gap* is the divergence between what a CPS is *legally permitted* to do and what it is *ethically appropriate* to do. It arises in three situations:
- Law permits but ethics forbids.
- Law forbids but ethics permits.
- Law is silent and ethics is the only guide.

**Example 1 — Smart Speaker Default Recording (3 marks):**
A smart speaker is *legally* allowed to record ambient conversations if the user clicked "I Agree" on a 30-page Terms of Service.
*Ethically*, the user did not understand they were being recorded.
*Principlism Resolution:*
- **Autonomy** demands *meaningful* informed consent → redesign with active wake-word disclosure.
- **Non-maleficence** requires minimizing ambient capture → mute by default, only activate on wake-word.

**Example 2 — Workplace Biometric Wearable (2 marks):**
An employer legally mandates a fatigue-monitoring wristband for night-shift workers.
*Ethically*, this may violate worker dignity even if legally compliant.
*Principlism Resolution:*
- **Justice** — apply the principle that benefits and risks must be *fairly* distributed; aggregate data only, not individual punishment.
- **Beneficence** — use the data for *worker safety improvements* (better shift design) rather than punitive scoring.

*(Full 7 marks: 2 marks for definition + 3 + 2 marks for two worked examples with Principlism application.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Internal Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **Privacy Governance Stack** for a Cyber-Physical System. List the four major layers and explain any TWO engineering controls that translate the legal/ethical requirements into technical implementation. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare the **GDPR, IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011, and DPDPA 2023** with respect to (i) extraterritorial scope, (ii) consent model, (iii) data principal rights, and (iv) penalties. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.1 — a four-layer stack:
- **Layer 1: Law** (GDPR, DPDPA, HIPAA, IT Act)
- **Layer 2: Ethics** (Principlism, IEEE EAD, OECD AI)
- **Layer 3: Standards** (ISO/SAE 21434, NIST Privacy Framework, IEC 62443)
- **Layer 4: Engineering Controls** (Privacy by Design, Data Minimization, Encryption, Consent Management, DPIA)
- **Base: CPS** (Smart Medical Device / Autonomous Vehicle / Industrial IoT / Smart Grid)

**Two Engineering Controls (4 marks = 2 + 2):**

**Control 1 — Privacy by Design (PbD) Implementation:**
- A smart meter is designed so that only *aggregate consumption* is uploaded by default.
- Individual appliance-level breakdown is opt-in, encrypted, and access-controlled.
- This satisfies *Data Minimization* (GDPR Principle 3) and *Justice* (Principlism).

**Control 2 — Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) + Audit Trail:**
- Before deploying a fleet of autonomous drones for delivery, the company conducts a DPIA.
- The DPIA identifies: surveillance risk, bystander privacy, GPS data of customers.
- Mitigations: on-device anonymization, 30-day auto-deletion, public-facing transparency report.
- This satisfies *Accountability* (GDPR Principle 7) and *Transparency* (IEEE EAD).

*(Total: 3 marks diagram + 2+2 = 7 marks.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | **GDPR (EU, 2018)** | **IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011 (India)** | **DPDPA 2023 (India)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Extraterritorial Scope** | **Yes** — applies to any controller/processor offering goods/services to EU residents. | **No** — applies only if the offense is committed in India or affects India. | **Yes** — applies to data processing outside India if it offers goods/services to Indians. |
| **(ii) Consent Model** | **Explicit, freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous.** Pre-ticked boxes invalid. | **Consent in writing** (or any mode, including electronic) before collection. | **Free, specific, informed, unconditional, unambiguous** consent; or for *legitimate uses*. |
| **(iii) Data Principal Rights** | Right to access, rectify, erase (RTBF), restrict processing, data portability, object. | Limited; mostly *compensation* for wrongful disclosure/loss. | Right to access, correction, erasure, grievance redressal, *nomination*. |
| **(iv) Penalties** | Up to **€20 M or 4% global turnover**. | Up to **₹5 crore or imprisonment** under IT Act §43A / §72A. | Up to **₹250 crore** per instance for significant data breaches. |

*(Each row 1.5 marks × 4 rows = 6 marks. 1 mark for an overall synthesis statement such as: "DPDPA 2023 modernizes India's regime by introducing GDPR-style extraterritoriality and higher penalties, while IT Act was primarily an e-commerce law.") = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Do not** write generic essays on "what is privacy". Begin with a CPS-specific anchor in the first paragraph.
> 2. **Do not** use the phrase *"data is the new oil"* — it is a cliché and signals shallow preparation.
> 3. **Do** cite at least **one specific Act/Article** in every answer (e.g., *GDPR Art. 9*, *DPDPA § 8*, *IT Act § 43A*).
> 4. **Do** draw a diagram wherever a question asks for a "neat diagram" — leaving it blank costs **3 marks minimum**.
> 5. **Do** map your answer to a *real* CPS (medical, vehicle, IIoT, smart grid) — abstract answers get partial credit only.
> 6. **Avoid** one-sided answers. Always present *both* the legal view and the ethical view, then synthesize.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Law ≠ Ethics.** Law is the floor; ethics is the ceiling. Both are needed for CPS privacy.
- **The Legal-Ethical Gap** is the space where law permits and ethics forbids (or vice versa). This is the *core* of Module 1.
- **GDPR** is the global benchmark — memorize its **7 principles** and **Article 9 (special categories of data)**.
- **DPDPA 2023** is India's modern data protection law — know its **Data Fiduciary** concept and **₹250 crore penalty**.
- **IT Act 2000 + SPDI Rules 2011** still governs the *legacy* Indian CPS deployments — §43A and §72A are KTU favorites.
- **HIPAA** governs *Protected Health Information* in the US — apply it to medical CPS.
- **Privacy by Design (PbD)** has **7 principles** — proactive, default-private, embedded, full-functionality, end-to-end security, visibility, user-centric.
- **Principlism** has **4 pillars** — Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice.
- **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD)** has **8 general principles** — useful when answer demands *modern AI-ethics* lens.
- **Data Minimization + Purpose Limitation** are the two most-violated principles in real CPS — use them as the *evaluation axis* in case studies.
- **Cross-border data flow** is a recurring flashpoint — under GDPR it requires *Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs)*.
- **Right to be Forgotten (RTBF)** is enforceable in EU and India (DPDPA) but not universally — flag this in answers.
- **PIA / DPIA** is a **6-stage structured procedure** — memorize the stages.
- **Stakeholder separation of duties** — Manufacturer designs, Controller decides purpose, Processor stores, User consents, Regulator audits, Ethics Board reviews.
- **For 14-mark answers**, follow the **6-paragraph structure**: Identify CPS → Map Laws → Apply Ethics → Compute Gap → Recommend PbD Controls → Document DPIA.
- **For 3-mark answers**, lead with a *definition*, support with a *table*, and close with a *one-line synthesis*. Do not exceed 1 page.
- **Always cite at least one Act + one Article** in your answer — this is the single biggest mark-differentiator in KTU valuation.
- **Smart Healthcare CPS, Autonomous Vehicle CPS, Smart Grid CPS, and Industrial IoT** are the four most-asked CPS domains. Be ready to write about any of them.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS: Types of Privacy Threats in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Privacy Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS: Types of Privacy Threats in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Privacy Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS: Types of Privacy Threats in CPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (cyber) are tightly coupled with physical processes and components, exchanging data through sensors, actuators, and communication networks in a feedback loop.
