Examples include smart grids, autonomous vehicles, industrial IoT, smart healthcare, and intelligent transportation systems.

A **Privacy Threat in CPS** is any circumstance, event, or adversarial action that has the potential to violate the confidentiality of personally identifiable information (PII), behavioral patterns, location, identity, or contextual data generated, transmitted, stored, or processed by the cyber or physical components of a CPS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Privacy threats in CPS refer to the potential risks and malicious actions that can lead to unauthorized access, disclosure, misuse, or inference of sensitive personal, behavioral, locational, or operational data across the cyber-physical interaction layer.*

A **Vulnerability** is a weakness or flaw in the system's design, implementation, configuration, or operation that can be exploited by a threat actor to compromise privacy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart Home Telemetry" Analogy**
>
> Imagine your house is fitted with smart meters, motion sensors, voice assistants, and a smart lock. Individually, each device seems harmless. But if an attacker (or even the electricity board) collects all this data together, they can deduce:
> - **When you wake up** (motion sensor patterns)
> - **When you are away from home** (energy usage drops + door lock status)
> - **What you watch on TV** (smart TV network packets)
> - **Your health condition** (sleep tracker + bathroom humidity)
>
> Each device is a "physical sensor" generating "cyber data." The CPS bridges both worlds. Privacy threats exist at every bridge point — at the **sensor (perception)**, the **network (transmission)**, the **cloud (storage)**, and the **actuator (feedback)**.

### 1.3 Key CPS Properties That Exacerbate Privacy Risks

| Property | Description | Privacy Implication |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Heterogeneity** | Diverse hardware, protocols, vendors | Inconsistent security postures create leak points |
| **Real-time operation** | Strict latency deadlines | Encryption overhead may be skipped |
| **Resource constraints** | Limited CPU/memory/energy on edge devices | Lightweight (often weaker) crypto |
| **Physical exposure** | Sensors in public spaces | Direct capture of sensitive contexts |
| **Long lifecycle** | Devices deployed for 10–20 years | Outdated firmware, unpatched vulnerabilities |
| **Closed-loop control** | Sensor → Controller → Actuator | A poisoned reading can manipulate physical outcomes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU-highlighted):**
> - **CIA Triad for Privacy** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability, extended to **Contextual Integrity** (Helen Nissenbaum's framework).
> - **k-Anonymity ≥ 5**, **l-Diversity ≥ 3**, **t-Closeness ≤ 0.2** are typical industry thresholds for de-identified datasets.
> - **Differential Privacy budget (ε) ≤ 1.0** is considered "strong" privacy; **ε = 0.1** is "very strong."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Threat–Vulnerability–Risk Chain in CPS

The privacy compromise in CPS follows a structured chain that KTU expects you to articulate:

1. **Asset Identification** → Personal data, location, behavior, identity.
2. **Threat Identification** → Adversary with capability + intent.
3. **Vulnerability Mapping** → Weak link in perception / transmission / processing / actuation.
4. **Attack Vector Selection** → Passive, active, side-channel, inference.
5. **Impact Quantification** → Re-identification, profiling, discrimination, physical harm.
6. **Countermeasure Deployment** → Anonymization, encryption, differential privacy, access control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this chain matters in KTU exams:** Questions often test the student's ability to map a *given attack* (e.g., "smart meter load monitoring") to the correct *stage* in the chain. Memorize this six-step pipeline.

## 2.2 Classification of Privacy Threats in CPS

### A. Based on the **Layer of Compromise**

| Layer | Threat Type | Example |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Perception Layer** | Sensor-based privacy threats | Camera surveillance, acoustic sensing, motion profiling |
| **Communication Layer** | Network-based privacy threats | Eavesdropping, traffic analysis, replay attacks |
| **Computation Layer** | Data-processing privacy threats | Inference from aggregated data, re-identification |
| **Actuation Layer** | Action-based privacy threats | Manipulating outputs to coerce user behavior |
| **Storage Layer** | Persistence-based privacy threats | Database breach, cloud storage exposure |

### B. Based on the **Type of Information Disclosed**

| Threat Type | Description | CPS Example |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Identity Privacy** | Revealing who the user is | RFID tag cloning in toll systems |
| **Location Privacy** | Revealing where the user is/has been | GPS tracking in autonomous vehicles |
| **Behavior Privacy** | Revealing what the user is doing | Smart-home occupancy patterns |
| **Body Privacy** | Revealing physiological/bio-metric data | Wearable health monitors |
| **Social Privacy** | Revealing social graph/relationships | V2V communication in connected cars |
| **Usage Privacy** | Revealing how a device/service is used | Smart TV viewing habits |
| **Temporal Privacy** | Revealing when an action occurred | Power consumption timing in smart meters |
| **Inferential Privacy** | Deducing hidden sensitive attributes | Inferring disease from medication schedule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** *Inferential Privacy* is a high-yield concept. It is the most sophisticated threat because the raw data may be "non-sensitive," yet combinations and statistical correlations can expose deeply private facts.

### C. Based on **Adversary Capability**

| Adversary Class | Description |
|-----------------|-------------|
| **Passive (Eavesdropper)** | Listens to data in transit; does not modify |
| **Active (Man-in-the-Middle)** | Intercepts, alters, or injects messages |
| **Honest-but-Curious (HbC)** | Follows protocol but mines data (e.g., cloud provider) |
| **Malicious Insider** | Authorized user abusing privilege |
| **Compromised Node** | A legitimate device hijacked by attacker |
| **Global Eavesdropper** | Observes all network traffic (ISP, nation-state) |

## 2.3 Common Vulnerabilities in CPS That Enable Privacy Threats

1. **Plaintext communication** between sensors and gateways (e.g., unencrypted MQTT, HTTP).
2. **Default credentials and hard-coded keys** in IoT firmware.
3. **Lack of authentication** at the perception layer (passive RFID, BLE beacons).
4. **Centralized cloud architecture** — single point of data aggregation.
5. **Insufficient anonymization** — releasing "anonymized" datasets that are re-identifiable.
6. **Side-channel leakage** — power, EM, timing, acoustic emanations.
7. **Over-privileged third-party apps** in smart ecosystems.
8. **Insecure firmware update mechanisms** (no signature verification).
9. **Untrusted supply chain** — counterfeit hardware with backdoors.
10. **Long-term data retention** beyond functional necessity.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for ESE**

| # | Concept | Definition / Formula | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Threat** | Potential cause of privacy breach | 2 |
| 2 | **Vulnerability** | Weakness that enables a threat | 2 |
| 3 | **Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | 3 |
| 4 | **k-Anonymity** | Each record indistinguishable from $k-1$ others | 3 |
| 5 | **Differential Privacy (ε)** | Adding noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ such that $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ | 5 |
| 6 | **Re-identification** | Linking "anonymous" record to identity | 4 |
| 7 | **Membership Inference** | Determining if $x \in D$ | 4 |
| 8 | **Contextual Integrity** | Privacy as appropriate information flow within a social context | 3 |
| 9 | **Side-channel** | Leakage via physical observables (power, EM) | 2 |
| 10 | **TRA / STRIDE** | Threat modeling frameworks used in CPS design | 3 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid:** Differential privacy applied to consumption data prevents utility-side profiling.
- **Healthcare CPS:** Federated learning + homomorphic encryption protects patient data while training ML models.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** V2X pseudonym certificates prevent long-term location tracking.
- **Industrial IoT:** Hardware Security Modules (HSMs) and TPMs safeguard sensor identities.
- **Smart Cities:** Privacy-preserving crowd analytics using edge AI avoids transmitting raw video.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Threat Modeling Walkthrough

Since the syllabus is conceptual, the "derivations" below are **analytical frameworks and risk-quantification workflows** that KTU examiners reward with full marks.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Mapping a CPS to its Privacy Threat Surface

### Step 1 — Decompose the CPS into Functional Blocks

Take a **Smart Health Monitoring Wearable** (e.g., a smartwatch with ECG, SpO₂, GPS, accelerometer).

$$\text{CPS} = \{\text{Sensors}, \text{Pre-processor}, \text{Communication}, \text{Cloud AI}, \text{Actuator (vibration/alert)}\}$$

### Step 2 — Identify Assets at Each Block

$$\text{Assets} = \{\text{ECG waveform}, \text{GPS trace}, \text{Accelerometer profile}, \text{user identity}, \text{alert history}\}$$

### Step 3 — Enumerate Threats per Asset

For each asset $a_i$, list the threat $T_i$ and adversary class $A_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{ECG re-identification} \Rightarrow \text{Inference attack by HbC cloud} \\
T_2 &: \text{GPS trace leakage} \Rightarrow \text{Passive eavesdropper on BLE} \\
T_3 &: \text{Behavioral profiling} \Rightarrow \text{Global adversary} \\
T_4 &: \text{Identity theft} \Rightarrow \text{Active MITM on unencrypted channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Map Vulnerabilities

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &: \text{Raw ECG transmitted in plaintext} \\
V_2 &: \text{BLE pairing uses default PIN} \\
V_3 &: \text{Cloud stores un-anonymized GPS logs} \\
V_4 &: \text{No certificate pinning in mobile app}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Risk Score

Using the standard $\text{Risk} = L \times I$ matrix (Likelihood × Impact), each in $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(T_1) &= 4 \times 5 = 20 \quad \text{(High)} \\
R(T_2) &= 5 \times 4 = 20 \quad \text{(High)} \\
R(T_3) &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \quad \text{(Medium)} \\
R(T_4) &= 2 \times 5 = 10 \quad \text{(Medium)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always show this Likelihood × Impact table in 14-mark answers; examiners award 2 marks for it.

### Step 6 — Propose Countermeasures

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &\Rightarrow \text{Differential Privacy on ECG features} \\
T_2 &\Rightarrow \text{Pseudonymous BLE addresses + ECC} \\
T_3 &\Rightarrow \text{On-device edge AI, no raw data offload} \\
T_4 &\Rightarrow \text{Mutual TLS + certificate pinning}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Differential Privacy Budget Composition

Differential privacy is widely cited in KTU 2024 module answers. The **sequential composition theorem** states that if mechanisms $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_k$ each satisfy $\varepsilon_i$-DP, the combined release satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP where:

$$\varepsilon = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Parallel composition** (on disjoint subsets) gives:

$$\varepsilon = \max_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step-by-step example:**

A smart meter releases hourly consumption (mechanism $M_1$, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.1$) and daily aggregate (mechanism $M_2$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.1$). If released on the **same dataset**:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.2
$$

This still satisfies "very strong privacy" since $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$.

**Advanced composition** (for $k$ mechanisms, each $\varepsilon$-DP, with failure probability $\delta$):

$$
\varepsilon' = \sqrt{2k \ln(1/\delta)} \cdot \varepsilon + k \varepsilon (e^{\varepsilon} - 1)
$$

For $k = 10$, $\varepsilon = 0.1$, $\delta = 10^{-5}$:

$$
\varepsilon' = \sqrt{2 \cdot 10 \cdot 11.5} \cdot 0.1 + 10 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (e^{0.1} - 1)
$$

$$
\varepsilon' = \sqrt{230} \cdot 0.1 + 1 \cdot 0.105 = 1.517 + 0.105 \approx 1.62
$$

> This exceeds 1.0, so **basic sequential composition is tighter** in this regime. KTU exam questions often test the distinction.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Vulnerability → Countermeasure (TVC Matrix)

This is the **single most important table** for 14-mark KTU answers.

| # | Privacy Threat | Layer | Vulnerability | Impact | Countermeasure |
|---|---------------|-------|---------------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Location tracking (GPS) | Communication | Unencrypted BLE | High | Pseudonym rotation, mix-zones |
| 2 | Behavioral inference (energy use) | Computation | Low sampling rate, raw data | High | Differential privacy, edge aggregation |
| 3 | Identity disclosure (RFID) | Perception | Static tag ID | High | Randomized hash, blocker tags |
| 4 | Eavesdropping (smart speaker) | Communication | Plaintext voice stream | Critical | End-to-end encryption (E2EE) |
| 5 | Re-identification (health data) | Storage | Anonymized ≠ de-identified | High | k-anonymity + l-diversity |
| 6 | Membership inference (ML model) | Computation | Over-fit model | Medium | DP-SGD, regularization |
| 7 | Side-channel (power analysis) | Physical | Direct chip access | Critical | Shielding, constant-power logic |
| 8 | Insider abuse (cloud admin) | Storage | Excessive privilege | High | Attribute-based access control (ABAC) |
| 9 | Trajectory profiling (V2X) | Communication | Long-lived pseudonym | Medium | Short-lived pseudonyms + mix-zones |
| 10 | Temporal correlation (login times) | Computation | Granular timestamps | Medium | Time-bucket aggregation |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step STRIDE Application to a CPS (Examiner's Favorite)

**STRIDE** is Microsoft's threat-modeling taxonomy, mapped to CPS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{S} &: \text{ Spoofing identity} \\
\text{T} &: \text{ Tampering with data} \\
\text{R} &: \text{ Repudiation of actions} \\
\text{I} &: \text{ Information disclosure} \\
\text{D} &: \text{ Denial of service} \\
\text{E} &: \text{ Elevation of privilege}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each maps to a **privacy threat** in CPS:

- **S → Identity privacy** (cloning a smart-meter ID)
- **T → Behavioral privacy** (altering sensor readings to skew ML inference)
- **R → Audit privacy** (erasing logs to hide surveillance)
- **I → All data privacy** (the core disclosure)
- **D → Availability → indirectly privacy** (forcing fallback to insecure mode)
- **E → Authorization bypass** (accessing another user's data)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Reference Architecture with Privacy Threat Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Physical World\nUsers, Vehicles, Equipment] --> nodeB[Perception Layer\nSensors, RFID, Cameras, GPS]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Edge / Fog Layer\nPre-processing, Local AI]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Communication Layer\n5G, WiFi, BLE, Zigbee]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Cloud Layer\nBig Data, ML, Storage]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Application Layer\nDashboards, Alerts, Control]
    F2[Decision Loop]:::ctrl --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeC
    classDef ctrl fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

**Threat Surfaces Annotated:**

| Layer | Privacy Threats | Vulnerability Hotspot |
|-------|----------------|----------------------|
| Perception | Video/audio capture, RFID cloning | Unprotected sensors |
| Edge | Re-identification from feature vectors | Weak on-device crypto |
| Communication | Eavesdropping, MITM | Unencrypted protocols |
| Cloud | Aggregation inference, insider abuse | Centralized PII store |
| Application | Excessive data display, log leakage | Poor UI/UX privacy |

---

## 4.2 Taxonomy of CPS Privacy Threats

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Privacy Threats in CPS]
    root --> node1[Identity Threats]
    root --> node2[Location Threats]
    root --> node3[Behavior Threats]
    root --> node4[Data Content Threats]
    root --> node5[Temporal Threats]
    root --> node6[Inference Threats]
    node1 --> node1a[Impersonation]
    node1 --> node1b[Re-identification]
    node2 --> node2a[Tracking]
    node2 --> node2b[Profiling]
    node3 --> node3a[Habit mining]
    node3 --> node3b[Activity recognition]
    node4 --> node4a[Direct disclosure]
    node4 --> node4b[Side-channel leakage]
    node5 --> node5a[Timing attacks]
    node5 --> node5b[Sequencing attacks]
    node6 --> node6a[Membership inference]
    node6 --> node6b[Attribute inference]
    node6 --> node6c[Linkage attacks]
```

---

## 4.3 Attack Flow Diagram: Smart Meter Privacy Breach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Step 1: Install\nSmart Meter] --> s2[Step 2: Adversary\nTaps Wireless Link]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Collect\nLoad Signatures]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Apply NILM\nNon-Intrusive Load Monitoring]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: Infer Appliance\nUsage Patterns]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6: Profile\nOccupancy, Lifestyle]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7: Privacy Breach:\nBehavior + Identity]
    classDef threat fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    class s2,s3,s4,s5,s6,s7 threat
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The smart-meter NILM (Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring) attack is the **classic KTU Module 1 case study**. Memorize the seven-step attack chain above — it appears in past university exam papers.

---

## 4.4 Threat–Vulnerability–Countermeasure Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Asset] --> p2[Identify Threat]
    p2 --> p3[Find Vulnerability]
    p3 --> p4[Assess Risk]
    p4 --> p5{Acceptable?}
    p5 -->|No| p6[Apply Countermeasure]
    p6 --> p7[Re-assess Residual Risk]
    p7 --> p5
    p5 -->|Yes| p8[Document & Monitor]
    classDef assess fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px
    class p2,p3,p4,p7 assess
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three types of privacy threats specific to Cyber-Physical Systems and give one real-world example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Location Privacy Threat** — Continuous GPS broadcasting in connected vehicles allows an adversary to reconstruct the full trajectory of a user. *Example:* Insurance telematics tracking driving routes.
2. **Behavioral Privacy Threat** — Smart meters expose appliance-level usage patterns through NILM, leaking when occupants are home, sleeping, or on vacation. *Example:* UK smart-meter rollout controversy.
3. **Identity Privacy Threat** — RFID-based access cards broadcast a static unique identifier that can be cloned. *Example:* Contactless transit card cloning.

*(Each point: 1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a privacy threat and a privacy vulnerability in CPS with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Privacy Threat | Privacy Vulnerability |
|--------|----------------|------------------------|
| Definition | A potential cause of an unwanted privacy breach | A weakness that allows the threat to materialize |
| Nature | External / adversary-driven | Internal / system-driven |
| Example | An eavesdropper listening to BLE traffic | The BLE link not using encryption |

A **threat** is the *who/what*; a **vulnerability** is the *how*; **impact** is the *so what*. *(1 mark for distinction + 1 mark each for example.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> **Note:** Answer **ANY ONE** full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A(a) [7 marks]:**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a Cyber-Physical System. For each layer, identify at least one specific privacy threat and its corresponding vulnerability.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) CPS Layered Architecture (3 marks):**

The CPS architecture is conventionally divided into **five layers**: Perception, Network (Communication), Middleware (Edge/Fog), Application, and Business (or equivalently Perception → Communication → Computation → Actuation → Storage). A diagram (drawn in exam) should show:

$$\text{Physical Process} \to \text{Perception} \to \text{Network} \to \text{Computation/Cloud} \to \text{Application} \to \text{Feedback to Physical}$$

**(ii) Layer-wise Privacy Threats & Vulnerabilities (4 marks):**

| Layer | Privacy Threat | Vulnerability |
|-------|----------------|---------------|
| Perception | Video/audio capture by compromised camera | Hard-coded default password |
| Network | Eavesdropping on MQTT traffic | No TLS configured |
| Edge | Local data extraction if device stolen | No full-disk encryption |
| Cloud | Insider data mining by employees | Excessive admin privilege |
| Application | Logged PII visible in dashboards | Insufficient RBAC |

**(Conclusion — 1 extra mark for closure):**
A defense-in-depth strategy — encryption + access control + anonymization — must be applied across all layers.

---

**A(b) [7 marks]:**
*Discuss in detail the non-intrusive load monitoring (NILM) attack on smart meters. What privacy information can be inferred, and what countermeasures can be deployed?*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) NILM Attack Description (3 marks):**
NILM is a privacy attack where an adversary observes the **aggregate power-consumption signal** at the smart meter and disaggregates it into individual appliance signatures using signal-processing and ML techniques (e.g., hidden Markov models, deep neural networks). The signal is sampled at 1 Hz or higher and contains unique current/voltage patterns for each appliance.

**(ii) Privacy Information Inferred (2 marks):**

- Occupancy pattern (when the house is empty)
- Sleep schedule (bedroom lights, AC cycles)
- Appliance ownership (wealth indicators)
- Health condition (e.g., medical equipment usage)
- Religious/cultural practices (e.g., cooking patterns)

**(iii) Countermeasures (2 marks):**

1. **Battery-based load smoothing** — masks appliance transients.
2. **Differential privacy noise injection** on smart-meter readings.
3. **Homomorphic encryption** for aggregation in the cloud.
4. **Tarrif/regulatory policy** — limit sampling frequency.
5. **Federated NILM** — on-device, no raw data transmission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B(a) [7 marks]:**
*Explain the STRIDE threat-modeling framework and apply it to a connected autonomous vehicle (CAV) to identify all six categories of privacy threats.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) STRIDE Overview (2 marks):**
STRIDE = **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation disclosure, **D**enial of service, **E**levation of privilege. It is a mnemonic for categorizing threats during the design phase of any system, including CPS.

**(ii) Application to Connected Autonomous Vehicle (5 marks):**

| STRIDE | Threat in CAV | Privacy Impact |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **S**poofing | Forged V2X message claiming to be an ambulance | False prioritization, location fraud |
| **T**ampering | Modifying GPS coordinates in CAN bus | Misleading navigation logs |
| **R**epudiation | Driver denies enabling geo-fence breach | Audit trail compromised |
| **I**nformation Disclosure | V2V beacon reveals real-time location | Continuous trajectory tracking |
| **D**enial of Service | Jamming V2X → fallback to cellular unencrypted | Privacy exposed via fallback channel |
| **E**levation of Privilege | Compromise of infotainment OS | Access to in-cabin camera/mic |

**(Conclusion — bonus 1 mark):**
STRIDE produces a structured threat register; combined with DREAD scoring, it prioritizes mitigations during CAV design.

---

**B(b) [7 marks]:**
*Describe three privacy-preserving techniques suitable for CPS, and compare their trade-offs in terms of computational overhead, communication cost, and level of privacy guarantee.*

**Model Answer:**

| Technique | Mechanism | Privacy Guarantee | Computation | Communication |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Differential Privacy** | Adds Laplace / Gaussian noise to aggregates | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | Low–Medium | Low (same payload + noise) |
| **Homomorphic Encryption** | Computes on ciphertext | Information-theoretic | High (10×–100× slower) | High (ciphertext expansion) |
| **Federated Learning** | Trains locally, shares gradients | Partial (gradient leakage possible) | Medium (per device) | Medium (gradients sent) |
| **k-Anonymity** | Suppresses / generalizes quasi-identifiers | $k$-level indistinguishability | Low | Low |
| **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** | Distributes trust across nodes | Cryptographic | High | High |

**(2 marks for description of three, 3 marks for trade-off table, 2 marks for conclusion.)**

**Conclusion:** The choice depends on the CPS's **latency budget**, **device capability**, and **threat model**. For example, wearable healthcare CPS often use **DP + on-device learning** because homomorphic encryption is too slow for real-time vitals.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing "threat" with "attack"** — A *threat* is the potential; an *attack* is the realization. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 2. **Writing only IT-style threats** — KTU specifically tests CPS-specific threats (e.g., NILM, RFID tracking, V2X pseudonymity). Generic "phishing" answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions** — A neat labeled block diagram is worth 2 marks minimum.
> 4. **Skipping the countermeasure** — Every threat answer must end with at least one corresponding defense; an unbalanced answer loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring physical-layer threats** — CPS has *physical* attack surfaces (side-channel, tampering). Pure-cyber answers are incomplete.
> 6. **Not specifying the threat model** — "Differential privacy" without stating $\varepsilon$ value is half-answered.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CPS** = Cyber (computation/network) + Physical (sensors/actuators) — privacy threats exist in **both** domains and their **interaction**.
- **Eight core threat types**: Identity, Location, Behavior, Body, Social, Usage, Temporal, Inferential.
- **Vulnerabilities are the enablers** — every threat exploits at least one weakness in perception, communication, computation, or storage.
- **STRIDE + DREAD** is the KTU-blessed threat-modeling framework — memorize both.
- **Differential privacy** budget $\varepsilon \leq 1$ is "strong"; basic composition: $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.
- **k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness** are de-identification pillars; cite numbers (k ≥ 5) for marks.
- **Inference attacks** (membership, attribute, linkage) are the most subtle — they exploit correlations, not raw data.
- **Side-channel threats** (power, EM, timing) are physical-layer leaks unique to CPS.
- **Case studies to memorize**: Smart-meter NILM, V2X tracking, wearable health re-identification, RFID cloning.
- **Always end an answer with a countermeasure** mapped to a specific threat — never leave the answer attack-only.
- **Defense-in-depth** is the KTU-recommended architectural principle: encrypt + authenticate + anonymize + audit.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Privacy Threats and Vulnerabilities in CPS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Attack Vectors and Vulnerabilities
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Attack Vectors and Vulnerabilities

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** and their privacy guarantees, an **Attack Vector** is defined as the *pathway, mechanism, or sequence of steps* by which an adversary gains unauthorized access, manipulates data, or causes a privacy breach within a system. It represents the *entry point* and the *operational route* the attacker uses to compromise confidentiality, integrity, availability, or privacy (CIAP) properties.

A **Vulnerability**, on the other hand, is a *weakness, flaw, or gap* in the system's design, implementation, configuration, or operational procedures that can be *exploited* by one or more attack vectors to compromise the system's security or privacy posture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> The relationship is fundamental — *Vulnerabilities are the doors; Attack Vectors are the keys.* Every successful privacy breach in a CPS requires the alignment of **three elements**: a motivated **Threat Agent**, an accessible **Vulnerability**, and a feasible **Attack Vector**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home as a Cyber-Physical System:

- **Vulnerability** = An unlocked back window, a weak door lock, a smarthome hub running outdated firmware, or a poorly trained family member who opens emails from strangers.
- **Attack Vector** = The *specific method* the burglar uses: climbing through the window, picking the lock, phishing the homeowner for the Wi-Fi password, or jamming the alarm signal.
- **Attack Surface** = The *sum total* of all doors, windows, vents, smart devices, and even human interactions that an attacker could potentially exploit.

In CPS, your "home" is a connected system of **sensors** (cameras, temperature sensors), **actuators** (motors, valves, smart locks), **controllers** (PLCs, microcontrollers), and **communication networks** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, 5G, industrial buses). Every sensor reading, every wireless packet, and every firmware update is a potential window for an attacker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy Implication in CPS:** Unlike traditional IT security, a privacy breach in CPS can have *physical-world consequences* — a compromised medical device can leak patient health data AND alter drug dosages, while a breached smart meter can reveal when you're home (occupancy privacy).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU 2024 Required Vocabulary)

- **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):** Industry-standard **0.0 to 10.0** scale for rating vulnerability severity.
- **CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures):** Unique identifier format `CVE-YYYY-NNNNN` for publicly known vulnerabilities.
- **Attack Surface:** Quantitative measure of all possible entry points.
- **Zero-Day:** A previously unknown vulnerability with no available patch (severity often rated **9.0–10.0** on CVSS).
- **STRIDE:** Microsoft's threat classification model (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege).
- **MITRE ATT&CK:** A curated knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques based on real-world observations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Attack Vectors in CPS

Attack vectors are classified along the **CPS architectural layers** they target:

### Layer 1: Perception/Sensor Layer Attack Vectors
- **Sensor Spoofing:** Injecting false sensor readings (e.g., GPS spoofing in autonomous vehicles).
- **Sensor Jamming:** Overwhelming RF/optical sensors with noise.
- **Side-Channel Attacks:** Extracting cryptographic keys by analyzing power consumption, EM emissions, or timing.

### Layer 2: Communication/Network Layer Attack Vectors
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM):** Intercepting and altering communication between CPS components.
- **Eavesdropping/Sniffing:** Passive monitoring of unencrypted wireless traffic (privacy breach).
- **Replay Attacks:** Capturing and retransmitting valid commands to trick the system.
- **Denial of Service (DoS) / Distributed DoS (DDoS):** Flooding the network to disrupt CPS operation.
- **Routing Attacks (in WSN/IoT):** Sinkhole, wormhole, blackhole, and selective forwarding.

### Layer 3: Control/Processing Layer Attack Vectors
- **False Data Injection (FDI):** Compromising the **integrity** of measurements fed to state estimators.
- **Code Injection / Malware:** Buffer overflow, SQL injection, or firmware backdoors.
- **Logic Bomb / Time Bomb:** Malicious code activated by triggers.
- **Privilege Escalation:** Gaining unauthorized root/admin access to controllers (PLCs, RTUs).

### Layer 4: Application/Service Layer Attack Vectors
- **API Exploitation:** Breaching REST/GraphQL interfaces of cloud CPS services.
- **Web Application Attacks:** XSS, CSRF targeting CPS dashboards.
- **Cloud Misconfiguration:** Exposing S3 buckets containing telemetry data.

### Layer 5: Cross-Cutting / Human-Centered Vectors
- **Social Engineering:** Phishing, pretexting, baiting targeting CPS operators.
- **Insider Threats:** Malicious or negligent employees with legitimate access.
- **Supply Chain Compromise:** Tampering with hardware/firmware before deployment (e.g., rogue chips, trojaned updates).

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Vulnerabilities in CPS

| **Vulnerability Class** | **Description** | **CPS-Specific Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Vulnerabilities** | Design flaws in physical components | Unprotected JTAG/SWD debug ports on industrial PLCs |
| **Software Vulnerabilities** | Bugs in firmware, OS, or applications | Buffer overflow in SCADA HMI software |
| **Network/Protocol Vulnerabilities** | Weaknesses in communication protocols | Default credentials in Modbus/TCP devices |
| **Cryptographic Vulnerabilities** | Weak/deprecated encryption | Hardcoded AES-128 keys in IoT firmware |
| **Configuration Vulnerabilities** | Insecure default settings | Unnecessary open ports on smart meters |
| **Human/Procedural Vulnerabilities** | Weak operational practices | Shared admin passwords, no security training |
| **Privacy Vulnerabilities** | Excessive data collection/transmission | Smart speaker always-on audio streaming |
| **Supply Chain Vulnerabilities** | Third-party component compromise | Backdoored library in device firmware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy-Specific Vulnerabilities** are a unique concern in CPS because they often involve *inferential disclosure* — even if raw data is encrypted, patterns in actuator behavior (e.g., HVAC usage) can reveal sensitive information (e.g., when a home is occupied).

## 2.3 The Attack Tree Model (Schneier's Formalism)

An **attack tree** is a hierarchical, AND/OR-structured decomposition of an attacker's goal into progressively smaller sub-goals.

- **OR nodes:** The attacker succeeds if *any* child node succeeds.
- **AND nodes:** The attacker must succeed at *all* child nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Attack Trees Matter for KTU:** They are the foundational tool for *systematic threat modeling* in CPS privacy engineering and frequently appear in 14-mark questions asking students to "model the threats to a given CPS scenario."

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Framework Sheet

| **Framework / Metric** | **Formula / Structure** | **Application in CPS Privacy** |
|---|---|---|
| **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{Score} = f(\text{Impact}, \text{Exploitability})$ | Prioritizing patch management in industrial CPS |
| **Attack Feasibility** | $F = P_{\text{success}} \times C_{\text{cost}}^{-1}$ | Where $P_{\text{success}}$ is probability, $C_{\text{cost}}$ is attacker cost |
| **Risk Score** | $R = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Standard risk assessment for privacy breaches |
| **Attack Surface Size** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot e_i$ | Where $w_i$ is weight, $e_i$ is exposure level of entry point $i$ |
| **Information Leakage** | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ | Mutual information between private state $X$ and observable $Y$ |
| **STRIDE Categories** | $\{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$ | Microsoft threat classification — *very high-yield* for KTU |
| **CIA Triad + P** | $\{C, I, A, P\}$ | Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, **Privacy** |
| **OWASP IoT Top 10** | 10 ranked categories | Industry baseline for IoT/CPS vulnerability classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Privacy Extension to CIA:** Traditional IT security uses the CIA triad. KTU 2024 emphasizes the addition of **Privacy (P)** as a first-class concern in CPS — recognizing that data minimization, purpose limitation, and consent are non-negotiable privacy properties distinct from confidentiality.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, KTU graduates working in CPS design teams use this knowledge to:

1. **Conduct Security & Privacy Threat Modeling** during the design phase (shift-left security).
2. **Prioritize Penetration Testing** efforts using CVSS scores.
3. **Design Privacy-by-Default** architectures with minimal attack surfaces.
4. **Comply with regulations** like India's **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**, EU GDPR, and sector-specific standards (e.g., IEC 62443 for industrial automation).
5. **Incident Response Triage** when CPS devices are compromised in operational technology (OT) environments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Risk Score Calculation for a CPS Privacy Breach

The **Risk Score** quantifies the severity of a privacy threat in a CPS. It is computed as a multiplicative composite of three factors.

### Step 1: Define the Three Component Variables

- $T$ = **Threat Likelihood** (rated 1–5, where **5** = almost certain occurrence)
- $V$ = **Vulnerability Severity** (rated 1–5, where **5** = trivial to exploit)
- $I$ = **Privacy Impact** (rated 1–5, where **5** = catastrophic, e.g., exposure of medical/health data)

### Step 2: Apply the Multiplicative Risk Formula

$$
R = T \times V \times I
$$

**Textual logic:** Risk scales with all three dimensions because even a high-impact threat (e.g., data leak of millions of records) has zero realized risk if no vulnerability exists, and even an easy vulnerability causes no harm without an active threat actor.

### Step 3: Compute Numerical Examples Across CPS Scenarios

**Example A — Smart Home Voice Assistant:**

$$
R_{\text{voice}} = 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64
$$

Threat is high (voice data constantly transmitted), vulnerability is high (often weak cloud APIs), impact is moderate-to-high (private conversations leaked).

**Example B — Industrial PLC in Power Grid:**

$$
R_{\text{PLC}} = 3 \times 3 \times 5 = 45
$$

Threat is moderate (targeted, not opportunistic), vulnerability is moderate (often air-gapped but legacy), impact is critical (cascading grid failure).

**Example C — Implanted Medical Device:**

$$
R_{\text{medical}} = 2 \times 2 \times 5 = 20
$$

Threat is lower (highly targeted), vulnerability is moderate (some have wireless updates), impact is catastrophic (life-threatening).

### Step 4: Map the Risk Score to a Risk Treatment Tier

$$
\text{Tier}(R) = \begin{cases} \text{Critical} & R \geq 75 \\ \text{High} & 50 \leq R < 75 \\ \text{Medium} & 25 \leq R < 50 \\ \text{Low} & R < 25 \end{cases}
$$

**Textual logic:** This mapping aligns with the **NIST SP 800-30** risk severity bands and provides a defensible threshold for budget allocation of privacy countermeasures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: CVSS v3.1 Base Score

The **CVSS Base Score** is the industry-standard 0.0–10.0 metric. The 2024 KTU syllabus requires familiarity with its construction.

### Step 1: Identify the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - \left[ (1 - C_I) \times (1 - C_C) \times (1 - C_A) \right]
$$

Where:
- $C_I$ = Confidentiality Impact (None=0, Low=0.22, High=0.56)
- $C_C$ = Integrity Impact (None=0, Low=0.22, High=0.56)
- $C_A$ = Availability Impact (None=0, Low=0.22, High=0.56)

### Step 2: Compute Impact

$$
\text{Impact} = \begin{cases} 6.42 \times \text{ISS} & \text{if Scope Unchanged} \\ 7.52 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (\text{ISS} - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if Scope Changed} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Exploitability

$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

Where:
- $AV$ = Attack Vector (Network=0.85, Adjacent=0.62, Local=0.55, Physical=0.20)
- $AC$ = Attack Complexity (Low=0.77, High=0.44)
- $PR$ = Privileges Required (None=0.85, Low=0.62, High=0.27)
- $UI$ = User Interaction (None=0.85, Required=0.62)

### Step 4: Compute the Final Base Score

$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}\left( \min\left( \text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10.0 \right) \right)
$$

**Textual logic:** The `roundup` ensures a single lowest-impact vector cannot push the score to zero — there is always a residual risk acknowledged.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping STRIDE to CPS Privacy Concerns

| **STRIDE Letter** | **Threat Category** | **CPS Privacy Manifestation** | **Example in CPS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Forged sensor identities leak false telemetry | Fake temperature sensor reporting "normal" in a cold-chain pharma CPS |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Altered actuator commands violate data integrity | Modified insulin pump dosage in medical CPS |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | User denies performing an action with privacy consequences | Smart lock operator denies unlocking without audit trail |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Direct privacy breach via unauthorized data exposure | Hacked smart meter revealing household occupancy patterns |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Disruption prevents privacy-preserving controls from operating | DDoS on home security system disables video recording |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Attacker gains root access, exposing all data | Admin compromise in smart city traffic control |

**Textual logic for the matrix:** Each STRIDE category maps to *at least one* CPS privacy harm. KTU examiners use this matrix as the canonical answer to "categorize the threats" type questions.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Building an Attack Tree for a Smart Home CPS

### Goal (Root Node - OR)
**G:** Compromise privacy of the smart home occupant

### Level 1 Decomposition (OR node — any path succeeds)

1. **Sub-Goal A:** Intercept in-home sensor data
2. **Sub-Goal B:** Compromise the smart hub
3. **Sub-Goal C:** Exploit cloud API
4. **Sub-Goal D:** Social engineer the occupant

### Level 2 Decomposition (AND nodes — both must succeed)

For **Sub-Goal A** to succeed, the attacker needs:
- **A1.1:** Wi-Fi network access (AND)
- **A1.2:** Unencrypted sensor protocol (e.g., Zigbee without APS encryption) (AND)

For **Sub-Goal C** to succeed, the attacker needs:
- **C.1:** Valid authentication token (AND)
- **C.2:** API endpoint with excessive data exposure (AND)

### Level 3 Leaf Nodes (Primitive Attack Vectors)

- $A1.1.1$ = Crack weak Wi-Fi password (WPA2-PSK with short passphrase)
- $A1.2.1$ = Exploit Zigbee Touchlink commissioning flaw
- $C.1.1$ = Phish occupant for cloud credentials
- $C.2.1$ = Exploit IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) in REST API
- $D.1$ = Vishing call impersonating utility company

**Textual logic:** The attack tree enumerates *feasible* paths. Privacy engineers then assign cost/detection ratings to each leaf and compute the *minimum-cost attack path* — the most critical path to defend.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS Attack Vector Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Adversary] --> B[Attack Vector Selection]
    B --> C{CPS Attack Surface}
    C --> D[Sensor Layer]
    C --> E[Network Layer]
    C --> F[Control Layer]
    C --> G[Application Layer]
    C --> H[Human Layer]

    D --> D1[Sensor Spoofing]
    D --> D2[Side Channel]
    D --> D3[Jamming]

    E --> E1[Man in the Middle]
    E --> E2[Eavesdropping]
    E --> E3[Replay Attack]
    E --> E4[DoS or DDoS]

    F --> F1[False Data Injection]
    F --> F2[Malware]
    F --> F3[Privilege Escalation]

    G --> G1[API Exploit]
    G --> G2[Cloud Misconfig]
    G --> G3[Web Attack]

    H --> H1[Phishing]
    H --> H2[Insider Threat]
    H --> H3[Supply Chain]

    D1 --> I[Vulnerability Exploited]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    E4 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J[Privacy Breach in CPS]
    J --> J1[Data Exfiltration]
    J --> J2[Identity Disclosure]
    J --> J3[Behavioral Inference]
    J --> J4[Physical Harm]
```

**Textual explanation of the flow:** The diagram traces an attacker's journey from *motivation* → *attack vector selection* → *CPS layer targeted* → *specific attack technique* → *underlying vulnerability* → *resulting privacy breach*. Each path represents a distinct family of threats relevant to KTU 2024 assessment.

---

## 4.2 Attack Tree Schematic for Smart Home CPS Privacy Compromise

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G[Compromise Smart Home Privacy]:::goal

    G --> A[Intercept Sensor Data]
    G --> B[Compromise Smart Hub]
    G --> C[Exploit Cloud API]
    G --> D[Social Engineer Occupant]

    A --> A1[WiFi Network Access]:::andNode
    A --> A2[Unencrypted Protocol]:::andNode
    A1 --> A1a[Crack Weak WPA2 PSK]:::leaf
    A2 --> A2a[Zigbee Touchlink Flaw]:::leaf

    B --> B1[Default Credentials]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Firmware Backdoor]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Phish for Cloud Token]:::leaf
    C --> C2[IDOR in REST API]:::leaf

    D --> D1[Vishing Call]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Pretext as Utility]:::leaf

    classDef goal fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef andNode fill:#5f1f3a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#3a5f1f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

**Textual explanation:** Goal node (navy), AND child nodes (maroon) require all sub-conditions; leaf nodes (green) are atomic attack vectors. The tree guides defense prioritization — defenders should focus on the *cheapest-to-defeat leaf node per AND branch* to break the attack.

---

## 4.3 STRIDE-to-CPS Privacy Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[S - Spoofing]:::s --> P1[Fake Sensor Identity]
    T[T - Tampering]:::t --> P2[Modified Actuator Command]
    R[R - Repudiation]:::r --> P3[No Audit Trail]
    I[I - Info Disclosure]:::i --> P4[Direct Data Leak]
    D[D - Denial of Service]:::d --> P5[Disabled Privacy Control]
    E[E - Elevation of Privilege]:::e --> P6[Root Access to All Data]

    P1 --> OUT[CPS Privacy Harm]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
    P6 --> OUT

    classDef s fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef t fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef i fill:#69db7c,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef e fill:#b197fc,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Textual explanation:** Each STRIDE letter (color-coded) flows into a specific CPS privacy manifestation, all converging to a unified privacy harm outcome. This diagram is the **go-to visual answer** for KTU questions on "categorize threats using STRIDE."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Differentiate between an attack vector and a vulnerability in the context of a Cyber-Physical System. Provide one example of each from a smart healthcare CPS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Vulnerability:** A *weakness or flaw* in the system that can be exploited. It exists passively regardless of whether an attacker is present.
  - *Example:* A Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) pacemaker programmer using a hardcoded 4-digit PIN for pairing.
- **Attack Vector:** The *pathway or method* used by an adversary to exploit a vulnerability and reach the target.
  - *Example:* A nearby attacker using a software-defined radio (SDR) to brute-force the BLE PIN and issue rogue pacing commands.
- **Key distinction:** A vulnerability is a *property of the system*; an attack vector is a *property of the adversary's strategy*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]
**List and briefly explain the six categories of the STRIDE threat modeling framework as applied to CPS privacy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **S – Spoofing:** Forging sensor/actuator identity, e.g., fake GPS signal in autonomous vehicle.
2. **T – Tampering:** Modifying data in transit, e.g., altered insulin pump dosage.
3. **R – Repudiation:** User denies actions due to missing logs, e.g., unauthorized smart-lock access.
4. **I – Information Disclosure:** Unauthorized data exposure, e.g., hacked smart meter leaking occupancy.
5. **D – Denial of Service:** Disrupting privacy controls, e.g., DDoS disabling CCTV.
6. **E – Elevation of Privilege:** Gaining higher access rights, e.g., root compromise of industrial controller.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the layered architecture of a Cyber-Physical System and identify the attack vectors specific to **each layer**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **complete attack tree** for the goal *"Compromise the privacy of an autonomous vehicle's passenger data"* showing at least three OR-children and two AND-branches. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**CPS Architecture (5 layers, 1 mark for diagram + 6 marks for layer-wise vectors):**

1. **Perception Layer:** Sensors (LIDAR, cameras, GPS, IMU, ultrasonic). *Attack vectors:* sensor spoofing (GPS spoofing, LIDAR jamming), adversarial patches on road signs fooling cameras. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Communication Layer:** CAN bus, V2X, 5G, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth. *Attack vectors:* CAN bus injection, V2X message forgery, MitM on OTA updates, replay attacks. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Control/Processing Layer:** ECU, ADAS processors, AI inference engines. *Attack vectors:* false data injection to object detection, malware via diagnostic port, model inversion attacks on neural networks. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Application/Service Layer:** Infotainment OS, mobile companion apps, cloud telematics. *Attack vectors:* malicious app permissions, API token theft, cloud database breach. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Human/Physical Layer:** Driver, passengers, mechanics, key fobs. *Attack vectors:* relay attack on keyless entry, social engineering of service center staff. *(1 mark)*

**Conclusion (optional, 0.5 marks):** Each layer's attack vectors must be mitigated by layer-appropriate controls (sensor authentication, bus encryption, secure boot, app sandboxing, multi-factor auth).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Attack Tree Construction (1 mark for tree structure + 6 marks for content):**

**Root Goal:** Compromise passenger data privacy in autonomous vehicle.

**Level 1 — OR decomposition (3 children, 2 marks):**
- A: Intercept in-cabin sensors
- B: Compromise infotainment system
- C: Breach OEM cloud backend

**Level 2 — AND decomposition (2 branches, 3 marks):**

- **Branch A (AND):**
  - A1: Exploit microphone always-on (no mute hardware)
  - A2: Exploit unencrypted in-vehicle network (CAN/CAN-FD without MAC)
  - *Leaf vectors:* A1.1 — malware injection via USB; A2.1 — CAN sniffer hardware.
- **Branch C (AND):**
  - C1: Obtain valid OAuth token
  - C2: Exploit GraphQL introspection exposing other users' data
  - *Leaf vectors:* C1.1 — phishing via in-vehicle email client; C2.1 — IDOR in `/user/{id}/trips`.

**Level 3 — Leaf attack vectors (1 mark for atomic leaves).**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Minimum-cost path analysis reveals that **Branch C.2 (GraphQL introspection)** is the cheapest exploit (no physical access needed) and should be the *highest-priority* defensive investment.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **CVSS v3.1 framework** with its key metric groups. Compute the **Base Score** for a vulnerability with: *Attack Vector = Network, Attack Complexity = Low, Privileges Required = None, User Interaction = None, Confidentiality Impact = High, Integrity Impact = High, Availability Impact = None, Scope = Unchanged.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart city's traffic management CPS uses 50,000 IoT sensors. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the **Risk Score** for the privacy breach *"Real-time vehicle location tracking leak"* given: Threat Likelihood = 4, Vulnerability Severity = 5, Privacy Impact = 5. Map the score to a risk treatment tier.
- (ii) Identify **three privacy-specific vulnerabilities** unique to CPS that do not exist in traditional IT systems. Justify each in one sentence.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**CVSS Framework Explanation (3 marks):**

CVSS v3.1 has **three metric groups**:
1. **Base Metrics** (intrinsic vulnerability properties): AV, AC, PR, UI, C, I, A, Scope.
2. **Temporal Metrics** (change over time): Exploit Code Maturity, Remediation Level, Report Confidence.
3. **Environmental Metrics** (organization-specific): Confidentiality/Integrity/Availability Requirements, Modified Base metrics.

**Computation Steps (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute Impact Sub-Score (ISS):
$$
\text{ISS} = 1 - [(1-0.56)(1-0.56)(1-0)] = 1 - [0.44 \times 0.44 \times 1.0] = 1 - 0.1936 = 0.8064
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Impact (Scope Unchanged):
$$
\text{Impact} = 6.42 \times 0.8064 = 5.177
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Exploitability:
$$
\text{Exploitability} = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 = 8.22 \times 0.4737 = 3.894
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Base Score:
$$
\text{BaseScore} = \text{roundup}(\min(5.177 + 3.894,\ 10.0)) = \text{roundup}(9.071) = 9.1
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a **CRITICAL** severity vulnerability (CVSS ≥ 9.0), demanding immediate remediation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Risk Score Calculation (3.5 marks):**
$$
R = T \times V \times I = 4 \times 5 \times 5 = 100
$$

Since $R = 100 \geq 75$, the risk treatment tier is **CRITICAL**. The organization must implement immediate privacy controls: end-to-end encryption of location telemetry, differential privacy noise injection, and immediate access logging with anomaly detection.

**(ii) Three CPS-Unique Privacy Vulnerabilities (3.5 marks — ~1.16 marks each):**

1. **Physical-World Inference Attacks:** CPS actuators produce *observable physical effects* (HVAC, lighting, water usage) from which an attacker can infer private states (e.g., sleep patterns, occupancy) even when raw sensor data is encrypted. *(Justification: Traditional IT systems have no physical actuation side-channel.)*
2. **Cross-Domain Data Correlation:** CPS data streams (medical device telemetry + smart home sensor data + vehicle GPS) when correlated across silos enable re-identification of individuals even after de-identification of individual streams. *(Justification: IT systems rarely have such rich multi-domain sensor fusion.)*
3. **Inseparability of Data and Safety:** Privacy-preserving mechanisms (e.g., introducing noise for differential privacy) can *degrade control-loop performance*, creating a unique trade-off absent in traditional IT — refusing to share data may *itself* become a safety hazard. *(Justification: IT data is rarely safety-critical.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse "Vulnerability" with "Threat." A *threat* is the actor; a *vulnerability* is the weakness; an *attack vector* is the path. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Do not** state CVSS scores without showing the calculation steps. Partial marks are awarded for the *intermediate ISS, Impact, and Exploitability* values.
> 3. **Attack trees** must show explicit **AND/OR** node distinction. Drawing a flat list of vectors without tree structure earns **at most 3 of 7 marks**.
> 4. **Privacy ≠ Confidentiality.** Always cite *inferential disclosure* or *occupancy inference* to demonstrate understanding of the CPS-specific privacy concept.
> 5. **Memorize STRIDE order:** *S-T-R-I-D-E* (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation). Writing it in the wrong order costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Attack Vectors and Vulnerabilities**

- [ ] **Definition Anchor:** Vulnerability = *system weakness*; Attack Vector = *adversary's path*.
- [ ] **Five CPS Layers:** Perception, Communication, Control, Application, Human — each has unique attack vectors.
- [ ] **STRIDE Order:** **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nfo Disclosure, **D**enial of Service, **E**levation of Privilege.
- [ ] **CVSS Range:** 0.0 (None) to 10.0 (Critical). Score ≥ 9.0 = Critical; 7.0–8.9 = High.
- [ ] **Risk Formula:** $R = T \times V \times I$, with tier thresholds at **25, 50, 75**.
- [ ] **Privacy in CPS ≠ Confidentiality.** Include inferential disclosure, occupancy detection, and cross-domain correlation.
- [ ] **Attack Trees:** OR children (any path succeeds) vs. AND children (all required). Always show node types explicitly.
- [ ] **MITRE ATT&CK** is the industry-standard knowledge base for adversary techniques — refer to it in any threat-modeling answer.
- [ ] **Real-time KTU-relevant regulations:** India's **DPDPA 2023**, EU **GDPR**, **IEC 62443** (industrial CPS), **NIST CSF 2.0**.
- [ ] **High-frequency exam keywords:** *attack surface, zero-day, side-channel, false data injection, replay attack, sinkhole, wormhole, differential privacy, privacy-by-design.*
- [ ] **One-line exam mnemonic:** *"Vectors are paths, Vulnerabilities are doors, Threats are actors."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of Privacy Breaches in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Studies of Privacy Breaches in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (cyber components) are tightly coupled with physical processes (sensors, actuators, and mechanical/electrical hardware). The system is monitored, coordinated, and controlled through a continuous feedback loop between the cyber and physical domains.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology for PECPT743, a CPS is defined as a network of embedded computing devices (sensors, controllers, edge devices) that interact with the physical world through input–output mechanisms, while simultaneously generating, transmitting, and storing sensitive data about users, processes, and infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS = Cyber (computation + communication) + Physical (sensing + actuation + control)**
> Examples: Smart Grids, Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA), Autonomous Vehicles, Smart Healthcare (IoMT), Smart Cities, Industrial IoT (IIoT).

## 1.2 Privacy in the CPS Context

**Privacy in CPS** is the right of individuals, organizations, and critical infrastructure operators to control the collection, transmission, storage, and inference drawn from the data generated by their physical processes and embedded devices. Unlike traditional IT privacy (which concerns only data), CPS privacy is unique because the **physical state itself becomes a privacy vector** — temperature readings, power consumption patterns, water flow rates, vehicle telematics, and even building occupancy can leak personally identifiable or operationally sensitive information through **side-channel inference**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart House That Talks Too Much"

Imagine a smart home (a CPS) where the thermostat reports the indoor temperature every 30 seconds to a cloud server for "efficiency optimization." While the user thinks they are sharing a harmless number, an attacker can reconstruct the **occupancy schedule** of the home — when the family wakes up, when they leave for work, when they sleep — purely from temperature data.

This is *exactly* what happened in the **research attack on smart meters** and was the privacy concern in the **Target HVAC breach (2013)**: the building's physical state itself leaked private behavioral data. Case studies of privacy breaches in CPS are essentially **forensic case files** of when these silent conversations were hijacked, leaked, or weaponized.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examinations

KTU examiners, under the **2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, expect students to map theoretical privacy principles (CIA triad, GDPR, data minimization, differential privacy) to **real-world breaches**. Module 1 sets the foundation by exposing the **what-could-go-wrong** scenarios that the rest of the course theory aims to prevent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Case studies demonstrate the *real-world manifestation* of CPS vulnerabilities. Students must be able to (a) identify the attack vector, (b) classify the privacy dimension violated, and (c) recommend the corresponding countermeasure.

## 1.5 Classification of CPS Privacy Breaches

Privacy breaches in CPS are broadly classified into the following categories, which recur across every case study:

| Breach Class | Description | Example CPS Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Exfiltration** | Theft of operational/personal data from CPS sensors | Smart meters, wearables |
| **Behavioral Inference** | Reconstructing user behavior from sensor metadata | Smart home, IoT |
| **Physical Surveillance Leakage** | Unauthorised access to camera/actuator streams | Verkada cameras, baby monitors |
| **Lateral Movement Pivots** | CPS used as a stepping stone into enterprise IT | Target HVAC → POS network |
| **Safety-Critical Manipulation** | Privacy breach combined with physical harm | Oldsmar water plant, Triton/TRISIS |
| **Critical Infrastructure Disruption** | Mass-scale breach affecting societal privacy (electricity, water) | Ukraine 2015, Colonial Pipeline |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a CPS Privacy Breach — A Five-Stage Model

Every case study in this module can be deconstructed into a recurring five-stage pattern. Memorizing this model allows KTU students to answer descriptive 14-mark questions with structured diagrams and crisp bullet points.

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance (Cyber-Physical Mapping)**
- Attacker profiles the CPS: device types, firmware versions, communication protocols (Modbus, DNP3, MQTT, Zigbee, Z-Wave, OPC-UA).
- Side-channel data (EM emissions, timing, power traces) is collected passively.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access (Pivot Point Exploitation)**
- Entry is gained through the **weakest, often non-obvious, edge device** — a vendor laptop, an HVAC controller, an IP camera, a remote vendor VPN credential.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement & Data Aggregation**
- Attacker traverses from the cyber layer into the physical layer, or vice versa.
- Sensitive telemetry (occupancy, telemetry, video, biometric) is aggregated for inference.

**Stage 4 — Privacy Impact Realization**
- Damage manifests as: identity disclosure, behavioral profiling, safety compromise, regulatory violation (GDPR, HIPAA, NERC-CIP), reputational loss.

**Stage 5 — Exfiltration or Disruption**
- Data leaves the CPS via covert channels (DNS tunneling, HTTPS abuse, steganography) OR physical actuation is altered (Stuxnet centrifuges, Oldsmar sodium hydroxide).

## 2.2 Theoretical Frameworks for Privacy in CPS

### 2.2.1 The Three Pillars of CPS Privacy

1. **Data Privacy** — confidentiality of raw sensor data
2. **Behavioral Privacy** — preventing inference about user activity/routine
3. **Contextual Privacy** — preventing correlation of physical events with identities

### 2.2.2 The Privacy–Security–Safety Triangle

In CPS, these three properties are **mutually constraining**:

$$
\text{Safety} \neq \text{Security} \neq \text{Privacy}
$$

$$
\text{A breach of any one weakens the other two.}
$$

For example, a ransomware attack on a hospital (security breach) directly threatens patient privacy (records exposure) and patient safety (treatment delays).

### 2.2.3 Risk Quantification

The classical risk equation used by KTU examiners and industrial standards (ISO 31000, NIST SP 800-30) is:

$$
R = P \times I
$$

Where:
- $R$ = **Risk magnitude**
- $P$ = **Probability** of privacy breach occurrence (0 to 1)
- $I$ = **Impact** (financial, reputational, safety, regulatory)

For multi-vector attacks, the total risk is the union of individual risks:

$$
R_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)
$$

### 2.2.4 Information Leakage Metric (Mutual Information)

For a sensor reading $S$ and private attribute $X$ (e.g., presence at home), the privacy leakage is measured as **mutual information**:

$$
I(S; X) = \sum_{s \in S} \sum_{x \in X} P(s,x) \log_2 \frac{P(s,x)}{P(s)\,P(x)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $I(S;X) = 0$, the sensor reveals no information about the private attribute (perfect privacy). Most CPS sensors have $I(S;X) \gg 0$, making them privacy-risky.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Cheat Table

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-examination relevant for Module 1 case-study analysis. Units and applicability are noted explicitly.

| Concept | Formula / Notation | Unit / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk magnitude | $R = P \times I$ | dimensionless [0,1] | Risk prioritization in CPS design |
| Composite risk (n vectors) | $R_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | dimensionless [0,1] | Aggregated CPS threat modelling |
| Information leakage | $I(S;X) = \sum P(s,x) \log_2 \frac{P(s,x)}{P(s)\,P(x)}$ | bits | Quantifying sensor-side privacy loss |
| Differential privacy budget | $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) | real number (lower = stronger) | Tuning noise in CPS telemetry |
| Mean Time to Breach | $MTB = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours/days | Reliability-privacy trade-off |
| Cost of a breach | $C = C_{direct} + C_{regulatory} + C_{reputation}$ | USD (typically **millions–billions**) | Economic justification for privacy controls |
| Network propagation | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}$ | nodes | Modelling lateral spread (e.g., Mirai, Stuxnet) |
| Attack surface cardinality | $A_S = \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert$ where $V$ = vulnerabilities, $E$ = exposed endpoints | integer | Quantifying CPS exposure |

> [!CAUTION]
> The $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation above is **mathematical cardinality / absolute value**, written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Do **not** confuse with the OR operator.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Relevance

The frameworks above are not academic luxuries. They map directly to industrial practice:

- **NIST SP 800-82** (Guide to Industrial Control Systems Security) uses $R = P \times I$ for sectoral risk scoring.
- **GDPR Article 32** mandates "appropriate technical measures" — the law uses privacy leakage $I(S;X)$ implicitly when requiring data minimization.
- **NERC-CIP** (North American Electric Reliability Corporation – Critical Infrastructure Protection) uses $R_{total}$ formulas for cascading-failure analysis.
- **IoT botnet propagation** (Mirai) is modeled using $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$ exponential growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When answering KTU 14-mark questions, always **cite the standard** (NIST, ISO 27001, IEC 62443, GDPR) alongside the formula. Examiners reward this linkage explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Detailed Case Study Walk-Throughs

This section provides an **exhaustive forensic analysis** of six landmark CPS privacy breaches. Each case is decomposed into the five-stage model and the corresponding privacy dimensions, mapped to KTU Course Outcomes **CO1** (Understanding CPS privacy fundamentals) and **CO2** (Analyzing real-world threats).

---

## 3.1 Case Study 1: Stuxnet (2010) — The Iran Natanz Nuclear Facility

### 3.1.1 Background
Stuxnet was a sophisticated, state-sponsored worm that targeted the **Siemens S7-300 PLCs** (Programmable Logic Controllers) controlling uranium-enrichment centrifuges at Iran's Natanz facility. It is widely regarded as the **first publicly-known cyberweapon targeting a CPS**.

### 3.1.2 The Five-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance**
- Intelligence agencies (allegedly U.S. NSA + Israeli Unit 8200) gathered intelligence on the **Siemens STEP 7** engineering software and the **S7-300 PLC** firmware.
- Insider knowledge of centrifuge spin frequencies (≈ 1000 Hz) and gas-centrifuge process physics was encoded into the payload.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access**
- Stuxnet entered the air-gapped Natanz facility via a **USB flash drive** (insider or contractor negligence).
- Exploited **four zero-day vulnerabilities** in Windows, including the famous **.LNK (CPLINK)** and **Printer Spooler** vulnerabilities.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement & Data Aggregation**
- The worm searched for **Siemens WinCC/STEP 7** software on the corporate LAN.
- Validated the target using a **hard-coded digital fingerprint** of specific Siemens PLCs and Frequency Converters (from Finnish manufacturer Vacon and Iranian manufacturer Fararo Paya).

**Stage 4 — Privacy / Safety Impact Realization**
- Although aimed at **safety** (physical destruction of centrifuges), Stuxnet had severe **privacy implications**:
  - It exfiltrated **operational telemetry** (centrifuge spin rates, valve positions) back to command-and-control servers — these are trade secrets of a sovereign nuclear programme.
  - It demonstrated that **air-gapped CPS are not immune** — privacy of the *physical process state* was compromised.

**Stage 5 — Exfiltration / Manipulation**
- Centrifuge rotor speeds were cycled between **1410 Hz and 2 Hz** in a 27-day attack pattern, while reporting **normal values** to the HMI.
- Roughly **1000 centrifuges** were destroyed or degraded.

### 3.1.3 Privacy Dimensions Violated
1. **Confidentiality** — operational data leaked.
2. **Integrity** — process state falsified.
3. **Availability** — physical process degraded.
4. **Contextual privacy** — sovereign state secrets exposed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Stuxnet is the canonical case showing that **air-gapping is not a privacy control** for CPS. The 2010 attack is the moment cybersecurity and CPS-privacy became inseparable disciplines.

---

## 3.2 Case Study 2: Target Data Breach (2013) — HVAC as a Privacy Pivot

### 3.2.1 Background
In late 2013, **Target Corporation (USA)** lost credit/debit card details of **~110 million customers**. The entry point was not the Point-of-Sale (POS) network — it was a **HVAC vendor's credentials**.

### 3.2.2 The Five-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance**
- Attackers sent **phishing emails to Fazio Mechanical Services**, a Pittsburgh-based HVAC contractor, stealing vendor credentials.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access**
- Using stolen credentials, attackers entered Target's **vendor portal**, which had access to the **building automation system** controlling HVAC, refrigeration, and energy management — a classic CPS.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement**
- The attackers exploited Target's network segmentation weakness: the vendor portal had **broad network access** to the corporate LAN.
- Custom **Kaptoxa (BlackPOS) malware** was installed on POS terminals.

**Stage 4 — Privacy Impact**
- **40 million credit/debit card numbers** stolen.
- **70 million records** including names, addresses, emails, phone numbers stolen.
- Direct violation of **PCI-DSS** and **state-level privacy laws**.

**Stage 5 — Exfiltration**
- Data exfiltrated via **FTP to compromised external servers** in batches.

### 3.2.3 CPS Privacy Lesson
The HVAC system (a CPS) was used as a **non-obvious pivot** to reach financial data. This case is the textbook example for the principle: **in CPS, the privacy boundary is the entire supply chain, not the immediate application**.

---

## 3.3 Case Study 3: Ukraine Power Grid Attack (December 2015)

### 3.3.1 Background
On **23 December 2015**, three Ukrainian regional electricity distribution companies (**Prykarpattyaoblenergo, Chernivtsioblenergo, Kyivoblenergo**) were attacked, causing **~225,000 customers** to lose power for 6 hours.

### 3.3.2 The Five-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance**
- Attackers spent **~6 months** inside the corporate networks studying SCADA/ICS topology.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access**
- **BlackEnergy 3** malware delivered via spear-phishing with **malicious Microsoft Word documents** exploiting **CVE-2014-4114**.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement**
- Attackers harvested **VPN credentials**, moved to the **SCADA HMI workstations** (Siemens SIMATIC WinCC).
- They used **KillDisk** to wipe disks and render HMI unrecoverable.

**Stage 4 — Privacy / Safety Impact**
- **Substation circuit breakers** were remotely opened via the HMI — a direct CPS safety breach.
- **Call-center phone lines** were flooded with a denial-of-service to prevent customers reporting outages (also a privacy of communications breach).
- **Firmware of serial-to-Ethernet gateways** was corrupted, blocking remote re-closure.

**Stage 5 — Exfiltration / Disruption**
- No data exfiltration was the goal — the attack was **purely disruptive** of the physical process.
- Yet **customer data** (addresses, account numbers, usage history) was exposed during the multi-month dwell time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Ukraine 2015 is the first confirmed **CPS-induced mass blackout**. It established the principle that **SCADA privacy = societal privacy** — outages leak behavioural data of an entire population.

---

## 3.4 Case Study 4: Mirai Botnet (October 2016) — IoT CPS as a Weapon

### 3.4.1 Background
**Mirai** was a malware strain that infected **~600,000 IoT devices** (CCTV cameras, DVRs, home routers) — all CPS — and launched a **DDoS attack** peaking at **1.2 Tbps** against Dyn DNS, taking down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit, and GitHub on 21 October 2016.

### 3.4.2 The Five-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance**
- Mirai scanned the entire IPv4 internet on **port 23 (Telnet) and port 2323**.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access**
- Default-credential brute-forcing against **61 known IoT manufacturer default usernames/passwords** (e.g., `admin:admin`, `root:xc3511`, `root:vizxv`).
- Devices with **telnet open and default credentials** were infected in seconds.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement / Aggregation**
- Infected devices (bots) reported to a **Command-and-Control (C2) server** at `hida31.org` (later `lolcathost`).
- Bot population grew via exponential network propagation:

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}
$$

where $k$ depends on scan rate and infection success probability.

**Stage 4 — Privacy / Safety Impact**
- **Privacy of device owners:** CCTV footage, baby-monitor streams, and home-network traffic were accessible to attackers.
- **Privacy of users of the targeted services (Twitter, Reddit):** service unavailability prevented legitimate communication, a privacy-of-communications breach.

**Stage 5 — Disruption**
- Coordinated DDoS using **SYN floods, UDP floods, DNS water-torture, GRE floods**.
- Source code was released publicly on HackForums, spawning **Okiru, Satori, Masuta, and Josho** variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Mirai demonstrates that **a CPS device with a weak password is a privacy breach waiting to be a privacy weapon**. IoT manufacturers shipping default credentials bear a privacy-by-design violation.

---

## 3.5 Case Study 5: Triton / TRISIS (2017) — Safety Instrumented System Attack

### 3.5.1 Background
In August 2017, attackers targeted the **Schneider Electric Triconex Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** at a Saudi Arabian petrochemical plant. The SIS is the last line of defence against physical catastrophe.

### 3.5.2 Attack Mechanics
- The malware, dubbed **TRITON (or TRISIS)**, reprogrammed the **Tricon** safety controllers to disable emergency shutdowns.
- Intended outcome: **catastrophic physical damage** (and loss of life), which would have terminated the *physical* process and destroyed all telemetry — a **total wipeout of the CPS privacy state**.
- The attack failed only because of a **bug in the malware** that triggered an automatic safe shutdown — accidentally saving the plant.

### 3.5.3 Privacy Dimension
- The intent was destruction rather than exfiltration, but it represented the **ultimate CPS privacy breach**: a complete loss of confidentiality, integrity, and availability of a safety-critical CPS, with collateral privacy violations for plant workers, neighbouring communities, and downstream supply chains.

---

## 3.6 Case Study 6: Oldsmar Water Treatment Plant (February 2021)

### 3.6.1 Background
On **5 February 2021**, an attacker gained remote access to the **SCADA system** of the Oldsmar, Florida water treatment plant and attempted to raise the **sodium hydroxide (NaOH) level from 100 ppm to 11,100 ppm** — a lethal concentration.

### 3.6.2 Five-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Reconnaissance**
- The attacker used **TeamViewer** (remote-desktop software) — a CPS connectivity tool often misconfigured.

**Stage 2 — Initial Access**
- Plant operators were using **a single shared password** on the TeamViewer instance, with **no multi-factor authentication**.
- Critical infrastructure was running on **Windows 7 (out-of-support OS)**.

**Stage 3 — Lateral Movement / Privilege Escalation**
- The attacker reached the **HMI controlling the chemical feed system**.

**Stage 4 — Privacy / Safety Impact**
- Sodium hydroxide was raised to a lethal dose — would have caused **mass poisoning of ~15,000 residents**.
- Drinking-water telemetry, plant operational data, and resident consumption patterns were exposed.

**Stage 5 — Mitigation**
- An attentive operator observed the cursor moving on his screen and immediately **reverted the dosage**.
- This case underscores the **human-in-the-loop** as the last line of CPS-privacy defence.

---

## 3.7 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is the **highest-yield visual** for KTU 14-mark answers. Memorise the structure; fill in case-specific details during the exam.

| Case | Year | CPS Domain | Attack Vector | Privacy Dimension Breached | Countermeasure Taught |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stuxnet | 2010 | Nuclear ICS | USB + 4 zero-days | Confidentiality, Integrity, Physical | Air-gap is insufficient; need behavioural monitoring |
| Target | 2013 | Retail HVAC | Phished vendor creds | Confidentiality (110M records) | Network segmentation, vendor access control |
| Ukraine | 2015 | Power Grid SCADA | Phishing + BlackEnergy | Availability + behavioural | Multi-factor, OT-IT segmentation, IEC 62443 |
| Mirai | 2016 | IoT Botnet | Default credentials | Confidentiality, availability | Unique passwords, telnet closure |
| Triton | 2017 | Petrochemical SIS | Spear-phishing + SIS | All three CIA + Safety | SIS hardening, anomaly detection |
| Oldsmar | 2021 | Water Utility SCADA | TeamViewer + weak auth | All three CIA + Safety | MFA, OS hardening, least privilege |

---

## 3.8 Quantitative Leakage Demonstration — Worked Example (Non-Programmatic)

> [!NOTE]
