> KTU examiners often ask: "Compute the information leakage of a smart-meter reading about home occupancy." Below is the **paper-based derivation** expected in an exam.

**Problem:**
A smart meter reports average power consumption $P$ every 15 minutes. The home has two states: *Occupied* ($X=1$) and *Empty* ($X=0$). Empirical joint distribution $P(p,x)$ is given by:

$$
P(p \in [0.5, 1.0], X=1) = 0.4, \quad P(p \in [0.5, 1.0], X=0) = 0.1
$$

$$
P(p \in [0.0, 0.5], X=1) = 0.1, \quad P(p \in [0.0, 0.5], X=0) = 0.4
$$

**Step 1 — Compute marginal probabilities**

$$
P(X=1) = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5
$$

$$
P(X=0) = 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5
$$

$$
P(p \in [0.5, 1.0]) = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5
$$

$$
P(p \in [0.0, 0.5]) = 0.1 + 0.4 = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mutual information $I(P;X)$ for each cell**

For $p \in [0.5, 1.0], X=1$:

$$
0.4 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.4}{0.5 \cdot 0.5} = 0.4 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.4}{0.25} = 0.4 \cdot \log_2(1.6) = 0.4 \cdot 0.6781 = 0.2712
$$

For $p \in [0.5, 1.0], X=0$:

$$
0.1 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.1}{0.5 \cdot 0.5} = 0.1 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.1}{0.25} = 0.1 \cdot \log_2(0.4) = 0.1 \cdot (-1.3219) = -0.1322
$$

For $p \in [0.0, 0.5], X=1$:

$$
0.1 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.1}{0.5 \cdot 0.5} = 0.1 \cdot \log_2(0.4) = -0.1322
$$

For $p \in [0.0, 0.5], X=0$:

$$
0.4 \cdot \log_2 \frac{0.4}{0.25} = 0.2712
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions**

$$
I(P;X) = 0.2712 + (-0.1322) + (-0.1322) + 0.2712 = 0.2780 \text{ bits}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each 15-minute reading leaks **~0.28 bits** of occupancy information. Over a day (96 readings), the cumulative leakage is:

$$
I_{daily} = 96 \times 0.2780 = 26.69 \text{ bits}
$$

This is more than enough to uniquely identify occupancy schedules — demonstrating the **inherent privacy risk of CPS telemetry**, even when individual data points appear "anonymous."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **only KTU-style quantitative question** examiners ask in Module 1. Memorise the four-step procedure: (1) marginals, (2) per-cell MI, (3) summation, (4) scaling by sample count.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Stage CPS Privacy Breach Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Stage 1: Reconnaissance] --> nodeB[Stage 2: Initial Access]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stage 3: Lateral Movement]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Stage 4: Privacy Impact]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 5: Exfiltration or Disruption]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Post-Attack: Forensics and Remediation]
    nodeF --> nodeA

    nodeA1[Map devices protocols firmware] -.-> nodeA
    nodeA2[Passive side channel collection] -.-> nodeA

    nodeB1[Phishing vendor credentials] -.-> nodeB
    nodeB2[Default password exploitation] -.-> nodeB
    nodeB3[USB drop in air gapped site] -.-> nodeB

    nodeC1[Pivot from CPS to IT] -.-> nodeC
    nodeC2[Aggregate sensor telemetry] -.-> nodeC
    nodeC3[Move from IT to OT] -.-> nodeC

    nodeD1[Data exfiltration] -.-> nodeD
    nodeD2[Behavioural inference] -.-> nodeD
    nodeD3[Safety compromise] -.-> nodeD

    nodeE1[Covert channel C2 exfil] -.-> nodeE
    nodeE2[DDoS amplification] -.-> nodeE
    nodeE3[Actuator manipulation] -.-> nodeE
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer cycle represents the iterative post-attack phase (forensic findings often enable new reconnaissance), and the dotted edges show canonical techniques used at each stage across the six case studies.

---

## 4.2 The OT-IT-Privacy Bridge — Stuxnet and Target Pattern

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeX[Physical Process: Centrifuge / HVAC / Smart Meter]
    nodeY[Field Devices: PLCs Sensors Actuators]
    nodeZ[Control Layer: SCADA HMI RTU]
    nodeW[Operations Network: Historian Engineering WS]
    nodeU[DMZ: Jump Hosts Firewalls]
    nodeT[Corporate IT: Email ERP AD]
    nodeS[External: Internet Vendor Portal]

    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeW
    nodeW --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeS

    nodeS -. Phishing Vendor .-> nodeT
    nodeT -. Stolen Creds .-> nodeU
    nodeU -. Lateral Move .-> nodeW
    nodeW -. Reconfigure .-> nodeZ
    nodeZ -. Falsify Telemetry .-> nodeY
    nodeY -. Manipulate .-> nodeX
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **canonical IT-to-OT pivot path** observed in Stuxnet (USB into engineering WS), Target (HVAC vendor into corporate), and Oldsmar (TeamViewer from external internet). Privacy flows in *both directions* — IT compromise (top) reaches down to corrupt physical telemetry (bottom).

---

## 4.3 CPS Privacy Dimension Heat-Map (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Privacy_Pillars
        nodeP1[Data Privacy]
        nodeP2[Behavioural Privacy]
        nodeP3[Contextual Privacy]
        nodeP4[Location Privacy]
        nodeP5[Identity Privacy]
    end

    subgraph Attack_Vectors
        nodeAV1[Sensor side channel]
        nodeAV2[Network sniffing]
        nodeAV3[Actuator spoofing]
        nodeAV4[Firmware reverse engineering]
        nodeAV5[Cloud API leak]
    end

    nodeAV1 --> nodeP2
    nodeAV1 --> nodeP4
    nodeAV2 --> nodeP1
    nodeAV2 --> nodeP5
    nodeAV3 --> nodeP3
    nodeAV4 --> nodeP1
    nodeAV4 --> nodeP5
    nodeAV5 --> nodeP1
    nodeAV5 --> nodeP3
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each CPS attack vector preferentially violates a *different* privacy pillar. The heat-map aids KTU students in mapping case-study events to the precise privacy dimension for a CO2-aligned 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 4.4 The Mirai-Style Botnet Propagation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM1[Attacker Control Server]
    nodeM2[Scan IPv4 Telnet Port 23 and 2323]
    nodeM3[Found Device with Default Credentials]
    nodeM4[Inject Mirai Payload via Telnet]
    nodeM5[Infected Bot Reports to C2]
    nodeM6[Botnet Coordinator Issues Attack Command]
    nodeM7[DDoS Target Service]

    nodeM1 --> nodeM2
    nodeM2 --> nodeM3
    nodeM3 --> nodeM4
    nodeM4 --> nodeM5
    nodeM5 --> nodeM6
    nodeM6 --> nodeM7
    nodeM5 --> nodeM2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Mirai's growth follows the exponential model $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$ because newly infected bots immediately resume scanning, forming a self-reinforcing feedback loop.

---

## 4.5 The Privacy–Security–Safety Triangle in CPS

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePS[Privacy]
    nodeSS[Security]
    nodeSF[Safety]
    nodePS <--> nodeSS
    nodeSS <--> nodeSF
    nodeSF <--> nodePS

    nodePS1[Confidentiality of data and identity] -.-> nodePS
    nodeSS1[Integrity availability of CPS] -.-> nodeSS
    nodeSF1[Absence of physical harm to life] -.-> nodeSF
```

**Reading the diagram:** The triangle highlights that **in CPS, the three properties are non-orthogonal**. A breach in one propagates to the others — Stuxnet compromised all three simultaneously. KTU students should reference this triangle explicitly in 14-mark answers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A (3 marks each, no choice, 5 questions) and Part B (14 marks each, internal choice between two questions, sub-parts of 7+7).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Cyber-Physical System. Why is privacy in CPS fundamentally different from privacy in traditional IT systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is an integrated system of computation, networking, and physical processes, where embedded computers monitor and control the physical world through feedback loops (1 mark).
Privacy in CPS is different because the **physical process state itself becomes a privacy vector** — sensor telemetry can leak behavioural, locational, and contextual information through side-channel inference (1 mark).
Additionally, CPS data is **safety-critical** and **real-time**; privacy-by-design must coexist with deterministic control, unlike IT where data is typically static and non-safety-critical (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three landmark CPS privacy breaches and identify the attack vector in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Stuxnet (2010):** USB-borne worm exploiting four zero-days; targeted Siemens S7-300 PLCs (1 mark).
2. **Target (2013):** Phishing of HVAC vendor (Fazio Mechanical Services) credentials; pivoted to POS network (1 mark).
3. **Mirai (2016):** Default-credential brute-force of IoT devices via Telnet; formed DDoS botnet (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the Target breach year (2013, disclosed Dec 2013/Jan 2014) with the discovery year of the malware (BlackPOS, 2013). Examiners deduct half a mark for imprecise dates.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Stuxnet

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the architecture and propagation mechanism of the Stuxnet worm. How did it specifically target cyber-physical systems rather than conventional IT networks?

**(b) [7 marks]** Analyse the privacy and safety dimensions violated by Stuxnet. What lessons does it offer for the design of CPS in critical infrastructure?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture and Propagation**

Stuxnet (discovered June 2010 by VirusBlokAda) was a **multi-component worm** with a layered architecture specifically engineered for CPS environments (1 mark). Its structure consisted of:

- **Dropper** — the initial infection module, spread via infected USB drives exploiting **CVE-2010-2568** (Windows .LNK vulnerability) and **CVE-2010-2772** (Win32k.sys privilege escalation) (1 mark).
- **Propagation engine** — used **MS10-061 (Printer Spooler)** for LAN spreading and **SMB/RPC** exploits for lateral movement (1 mark).
- **Payload injector** — after validating the host had **Siemens STEP 7 / WinCC** installed, the payload was injected into the engineering software (1 mark).
- **PLC rootkit** — rewrote the **OB1, OB35, OB80 organisation blocks** of the Siemens S7-315 and S7-417 PLCs to alter centrifuge spin cycles between 1410 Hz and 2 Hz, while spoofing normal HMI values (1 mark).
- **Command-and-Control (C2)** — exfiltrated operational telemetry to two C2 servers (`www.mypremierfutbol.com`, `www.todaysfutbol.com`) using HTTPS to blend with normal traffic (1 mark).
- **Digital fingerprint check** — the payload only activated on systems with specific Profibus device IDs (Vacon and Fararo Paya frequency converters), preventing collateral damage (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Privacy & Safety Analysis**

Stuxnet violated the **CIA triad** in ways that traditional IT malware could not (1 mark):
- **Confidentiality** — operational data of a sovereign nuclear programme was exfiltrated to a foreign intelligence service (1 mark).
- **Integrity** — PLC programme logic was overwritten, falsifying process state (1 mark).
- **Availability** — centrifuges were physically destroyed; long-term uranium enrichment capacity was degraded (1 mark).

The privacy lesson is that **air-gapping is not a privacy control** for CPS — the supply chain (USB, contractors, vendor laptops) becomes the attack surface (1 mark). For modern CPS design, this implies:
- **Behavioural ICS monitoring** (detecting anomalous OB-block writes) (1 mark).
- **Strict removable-media policies** with cryptographic provenance tracking (1 mark).
- **OT-IT segmentation** following IEC 62443 zone-conduit model (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Mirai Botnet

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the architecture of the Mirai botnet. How did it exploit the cyber-physical nature of IoT devices to achieve its scale?

**(b) [7 marks]** Analyse the privacy implications of IoT devices being compromised in a Mirai-style botnet. What countermeasures should manufacturers and regulators enforce?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Architecture**

Mirai's architecture had three principal components (1 mark):
- **Scanner** — a continuous random IPv4 scanner targeting TCP port 23 (Telnet) and port 2323; an infected host runs the scanner in a forked process, probing a randomized IP list excluding its own subnet (1 mark).
- **Brute-force loader** — upon finding an open Telnet port, the loader attempts login using a hard-coded dictionary of **61 default username/password pairs** (e.g., `admin:1234`, `root:xc3511`, `root:vizxv`) (1 mark).
- **Report and attack modules** — successful infection downloads the malware binary via wget from a hosted URL, executes it, kills competing processes (e.g., other Mirai variants), and reports to the C2 (1 mark).

The bot's C2 (originally `hida31.org`, later `lolcathost`) maintains a list of bots and issues attack commands: **SYN flood, UDP flood, DNS water-torture, GRE flood, HTTP GET/POST flood** (1 mark). Mirai's infection speed is best modelled as **exponential growth**:

$$
N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{kt}
$$

with $k \approx 0.06$ per minute in the early phase, achieving ~600,000 infections within hours (1 mark). The CPS nature of IoT — **always-on, low-power, rarely patched, often with default credentials** — provided the perfect substrate (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Privacy Implications and Countermeasures**

Privacy implications fall into three categories (1 mark each):
- **Confidentiality** of device owners — CCTV footage, baby-monitor streams, and home-network metadata become accessible to the botnet operator.
- **Availability** of dependent services — the DDoS victim (Dyn, OVH) suffers privacy-of-communication outage for end users.
- **Integrity of trust** — millions of consumer devices can no longer be trusted as benign endpoints.

Countermeasures:
- **Regulatory:** enforce unique default credentials (e.g., California's SB-327, EU Cyber Resilience Act) (1 mark).
- **Manufacturer:** implement **secure boot**, **signed firmware**, and **automatic patching** (1 mark).
- **Network-level:** deploy **carrier-grade NAT**, **DDoS scrubbing centres**, and **BCP-38 ingress filtering** (1 mark).
- **User-level:** close Telnet ports, change defaults, segment IoT on guest VLANs (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 1 — Case Studies**
> 
> 1. **Year Confusion:** Examiners frequently test whether students confuse **Stuxnet (2010)** with **Flame (2012)** or **Duqu (2011)** — all are related but distinct. Memorise the year and the *specific* target.
> 
> 2. **Mistaking "Privacy" for "Security":** A 14-mark answer that only discusses encryption and firewalls (security) and ignores **behavioural inference, contextual leakage, and side-channel** (privacy) loses at least 4–5 marks. The course is **Privacy in CPS**, not *Security of CPS*.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the Cyber-Physical Bridge:** Stuxnet is not "just a worm." KTU examiners expect you to mention **PLCs, OB blocks, frequency converters, and HMI spoofing**. Generic malware descriptions lose marks.
> 
> 4. **No Citation of Standards:** Always reference **NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443, ISO 27001, GDPR Article 32, or NERC-CIP** when recommending countermeasures. This is the OBE-aligned 7th mark in any descriptive answer.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the Privacy Formula:** If a question allows a quantitative sub-part, always present the **$R = P \times I$** or **$I(S;X)$** formula explicitly. Marks are reserved for the formula, not just the conclusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Case Studies of Privacy Breaches in CPS**

- [x] A **CPS** = cyber (computation/communication) + physical (sensing/actuation) integrated via feedback loops.
- [x] **CPS privacy ≠ IT privacy** — physical state is itself a privacy vector.
- [x] **Five-stage breach model:** Reconnaissance → Initial Access → Lateral Movement → Privacy Impact → Exfiltration/Disruption.
- [x] **Stuxnet (2010):** USB-borne, four zero-days, Siemens S7-300 PLCs, Natanz centrifuges.
- [x] **Target (2013):** HVAC vendor phishing → POS network → 110 million records.
- [x] **Ukraine Power Grid (2015):** BlackEnergy 3, SCADA HMI, 225,000 customers blacked out.
- [x] **Mirai (2016):** Default IoT credentials, 600K bots, 1.2 Tbps DDoS, $N(t) = N_0 e^{kt}$.
- [x] **Triton/TRISIS (2017):** Schneider Triconex SIS reprogramming, Saudi petrochemical plant.
- [x] **Oldsmar Water Plant (2021):** TeamViewer, Windows 7, single shared password, NaOH lye attack.
- [x] **Risk formula:** $R = P \times I$.
- [x] **Composite risk:** $R_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$.
- [x] **Information leakage:** $I(S;X) = \sum P(s,x) \log_2 \frac{P(s,x)}{P(s)P(x)}$.
- [x] **Three privacy pillars:** Data, Behavioural, Contextual.
- [x] **Privacy–Security–Safety triangle** — non-orthogonal, mutually constraining.
- [x] **Air-gapping is not a privacy control** — the supply chain is the attack surface.
- [x] **Key standards to cite:** NIST SP 800-82, IEC 62443, ISO 27001, GDPR Art. 32, NERC-CIP.
- [x] **Attack surface:** $A_S = \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert$ (cardinality of vulnerabilities plus exposed endpoints).
- [x] **Differential privacy budget $\varepsilon$** is the canonical noise-tuning parameter.
- [x] **Countermeasure keywords** examiners reward: segmentation, MFA, behavioural monitoring, least privilege, secure boot, vendor access control.
- [x] **Recall dates precisely** — Stuxnet 2010, Target 2013, Ukraine 2015, Mirai 2016, Triton 2017, Oldsmar 2021.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs): Overview of PETs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs): Overview of PETs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs)** are a coherent set of computer science, cryptographic, statistical, and policy-based tools, techniques, and mechanisms that are explicitly engineered to minimize the acquisition, exposure, linkage, and processing of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) while preserving the operational utility of the underlying data and system.

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — where physical processes, embedded sensors, actuators, cloud analytics, and human users converge — PETs are indispensable because every sensor reading (location, biometrics, health metrics, industrial telemetry) inherently carries a privacy footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A PET is *any technology that protects privacy by eliminating or reducing personal data, preventing unnecessary or unwanted processing of personal data, or by preventing data from being made available to unintended third parties.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Envelope vs. Postcard" Paradigm

Imagine you need to send a message to a friend:

- **Traditional (No PET):** You write the message on a **postcard**. The mail carrier, postal workers, neighbors, and anyone passing by can read it. This is analogous to **plaintext data transmission** over an untrusted network.
- **Privacy Enhancing Technology:** You place the message inside a **tamper-proof, sealed, wax-stamped envelope**, perhaps locked inside a safe that only your friend can open. Even if intercepted, the contents remain confidential.

In CPS terms, the **postcard** = unencrypted sensor data, the **sealed envelope** = encrypted payload, the **safe** = secure hardware enclave, and the **authorized recipient** = a user with the correct cryptographic key.

### 1.3 Why PETs Matter in CPS — The Core Drivers

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of PET Justification in KTU 2024 Curriculum:**

1. **Regulatory Compliance:** GDPR (Europe), DPDP Act 2023 (India), HIPAA (US Healthcare), and KTU-aligned data governance principles mandate privacy-by-design.
2. **Threat Mitigation:** CPS devices are notoriously vulnerable (think Mirai botnet, Stuxnet) — PETs add layers of confidentiality and unlinkability.
3. **Trust Preservation:** Users accept pervasive sensing (smart homes, wearables, smart grids) *only* when privacy guarantees are mathematically or procedurally enforced.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric / Constant | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **GDPR Fine Ceiling** | 4% of global annual turnover or **€20 million**, whichever is higher |
| **DPDP Act Penalty (India 2023)** | Up to **₹250 crore** for significant data breaches |
| **NIST Privacy Framework Version** | 1.0 (Released January 2020) |
| **k-Anonymity Parameter** | Standard target: **k ≥ 5** for quasi-identifier protection |
| **ε (Epsilon) in Differential Privacy** | Typical range: **0.1 ≤ ε ≤ 10** (lower = stronger privacy) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Principles of PETs — Step-by-Step Logic

A robust PET framework operates on **four foundational principles** that align with KTU's outcome-based education approach:

- **Step 1 — Data Minimization Principle:**
  - *Why:* Limit exposure surface area.
  - *How:* Collect only the *minimum necessary* attributes needed for the CPS function (e.g., a smart meter reports aggregate consumption, not individual appliance fingerprints).

- **Step 2 — Purpose Limitation Principle:**
  - *Why:* Prevent function creep.
  - *How:* Bind data usage to the original, explicitly stated purpose. Smart health wearables used for fitness should not silently share data with insurers.

- **Step 3 — Confidentiality via Cryptographic Isolation:**
  - *Why:* Protect data at rest, in transit, and in use.
  - *How:* Employ symmetric encryption (AES-256), asymmetric encryption (RSA-4096, ECC), or homomorphic encryption for computation on encrypted data.

- **Step 4 — Unlinkability & Untraceability:**
  - *Why:* Prevent correlation attacks across datasets.
  - *How:* Use anonymous credentials, ring signatures, mix networks, or zero-knowledge proofs to decouple user identity from transactions.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of PETs — KTU Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Canonical Categories of PETs (KTU-Aligned):**

| # | PET Category | Core Mechanism | CPS Use Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encryption-Based PETs** | Symmetric/Asymmetric cryptography, Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) | Encrypted V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) communication in autonomous cars |
| 2 | **Anonymization & Pseudonymization PETs** | k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness, data masking | Smart grid consumption data shared with researchers |
| 3 | **Differential Privacy PETs** | Statistical noise injection (Laplace/Gaussian mechanism) | Aggregated traffic data from connected vehicles |
| 4 | **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) PETs** | Hardware enclaves (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) | Secure biometric processing in IoT wearables |
| 5 | **Policy & Access Control PETs** | ABAC, RBAC, DLP, GDPR-DPIA frameworks | Role-based access in hospital CPS infrastructure |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **k-Anonymity Requirement** | For every record $r$, at least $k-1$ other records share identical quasi-identifier values | Healthcare dataset release |
| 2 | **Laplace Mechanism (DP)** | $\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Noise scale $\Delta f / \varepsilon$ added to query output |
| 3 | **Global Sensitivity** | $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$ over neighboring datasets $D, D'$ | Determines noise magnitude |
| 4 | **Privacy Budget Composition** | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_i$ | Sequential DP queries on the same dataset |
| 5 | **Entropy of Anonymity Set** | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures uncertainty in re-identification |
| 6 | **Homomorphic Addition** | $\text{Enc}(a) \oplus \text{Enc}(b) = \text{Enc}(a+b)$ | Computation on encrypted CPS sensor data |
| 7 | **Zero-Knowledge Proof Property** | $\{ \text{Completeness}, \text{Soundness}, \text{Zero-Knowledge} \}$ | Authentication without revealing credentials |
| 8 | **GDPR Lawful Basis Test** | $\text{Valid PET} \iff \text{Lawful Basis} \land \text{Data Minimization} \land \text{Purpose Limitation}$ | Regulatory compliance check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Valuation:** Examiners expect students to *cite the specific PET category* (e.g., "Differential Privacy under ε = 1.0") and *not just name-drop "privacy protection."* Always anchor your answer in a named, mathematically defined mechanism.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PETs are not academic curiosities — they are deployed in:

- **Apple's iOS:** Local Differential Privacy for emoji usage statistics
- **Google's RAPPOR:** Chrome browser usage telemetry under DP
- **Signal Protocol:** End-to-end encryption in messaging CPS
- **Intel SGX:** Trusted execution for cloud-based CPS analytics
- **MedRec (MIT):** Blockchain + smart contracts for medical CPS privacy

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Laplace Mechanism for Differential Privacy

**Problem Setup:** We have a CPS that collects real-time data from $n$ sensors. An analyst queries a function $f(D)$ over the aggregated dataset $D$. We want to release a *noisy* answer $\tilde{f}(D)$ such that the presence/absence of any single sensor's data does not significantly change the output distribution.

**Step 1: Define Adjacent (Neighboring) Datasets**

Two datasets $D$ and $D'$ are neighbors if they differ in exactly one record (one sensor's data point).

$$D' = D \cup \{x_{\text{new}}\} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad D' = D \setminus \{x_{\text{old}}\}$$

**Step 2: Compute Global Sensitivity ($\Delta f$)**

The global sensitivity of a function $f$ is the maximum possible change in $f$'s output between any two neighboring datasets.

$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$$

For example, a *counting query* $f(D) = \vert D \vert$ on a binary dataset has $\Delta f = 1$, because adding or removing one element changes the count by exactly 1.

**Step 3: Define the Laplace Probability Density Function**

The Laplace distribution with scale parameter $b$ is:

$$\text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert x \mid}{b}\right)$$

**Step 4: Set the Scale Parameter to Satisfy ε-Differential Privacy**

To achieve $\varepsilon$-differential privacy, we choose:

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5: Construct the Noisy Release**

$$\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 6: Verify the Differential Privacy Guarantee**

We must show that for all neighboring $D, D'$ and all subsets $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$:

$$\Pr[\tilde{f}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\tilde{f}(D') \in S]$$

**Step 7: Compute the Ratio**

$$\frac{\Pr[\tilde{f}(D) \in S]}{\Pr[\tilde{f}(D') \in S]} = \frac{\exp\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \vert f(D) - y \vert}{\Delta f}\right)}{\exp\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \vert f(D') - y \vert}{\Delta f}\right)}$$

**Step 8: Apply the Triangle Inequality**

By the triangle inequality, $\vert f(D) - y \vert \leq \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert + \vert f(D') - y \vert \leq \Delta f + \vert f(D') - y \vert$.

Therefore, the ratio is bounded by:

$$\frac{\Pr[\tilde{f}(D) \in S]}{\Pr[\tilde{f}(D') \in S]} \leq \exp\left(\frac{\varepsilon \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert}{\Delta f}\right) \leq \exp(\varepsilon)$$

This completes the proof that the Laplace mechanism satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Marks are awarded for *showing* the sensitivity computation, the Laplace PDF, and the final ratio bounding step. Skipping Step 7-8 will cost 3-4 marks on a 14-mark question.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PET Selection Decision Tree

| CPS Scenario | Privacy Threat | Recommended PET | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart home voice assistant | Eavesdropping, voice fingerprinting | **TEE + On-device processing** | Data never leaves the device |
| Connected vehicle telemetry | Location tracking | **Differential Privacy (ε = 2.0)** | Aggregate data remains useful for traffic analysis |
| Hospital patient monitoring | Re-identification from sensor data | **k-Anonymity (k = 10) + l-Diversity** | Each patient indistinguishable from 9 others |
| Industrial IoT control signals | Tampering & disclosure | **AES-256 + Digital Signatures** | Confidentiality and integrity in one suite |
| Smart meter billing | Behavioral profiling | **Homomorphic Encryption (Paillier)** | Utility computes bill on encrypted readings |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*Define Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs). List any three categories of PETs with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** PETs are a set of tools, techniques, and mechanisms designed to protect personal privacy by minimizing personal data collection, maximizing data security, and empowering users with control over their information in digital systems, especially Cyber-Physical Systems.

**Three Categories with Examples (2 marks — 0.5 each + 0.17 example):**

1. **Cryptographic PETs:** Example — AES-256 encryption used in smart meter CPS data transmission.
2. **Statistical/Anonymization PETs:** Example — k-Anonymity applied to hospital patient datasets before research sharing.
3. **Hardware-based PETs:** Example — Intel SGX enclave used in edge gateways for processing biometric data securely.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
*Explain the principle of "Data Minimization" as a Privacy Enhancing Technique. How does it differ from "Anonymization"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Data Minimization (1.5 marks):** This principle, enshrined in GDPR Article 5(1)(c), mandates that *only the personal data necessary for the specific, explicit purpose* should be collected and processed. In a CPS context, a smart thermostat should collect room temperature and occupancy state — not audio or video — unless explicitly required.

**Difference from Anonymization (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Data Minimization | Anonymization |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Applied *at design & collection* time | Applied *post-collection*, before release |
| **Mechanism** | Reduces *what* is collected | Alters *how* existing data is represented |
| **Reversibility** | Not applicable (data not collected) | Should be irreversible (re-identification-resistant) |
| **Example** | Sensor reports only aggregate consumption | Dataset of consumption reports with names/IDs removed |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Taxonomy of Privacy Enhancing Technologies** suitable for Cyber-Physical Systems. Categorize them into at least four major classes with specific CPS deployment examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of PETs in a CPS pipeline**. Describe the role of Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) in this architecture. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The taxonomy of PETs for CPS can be organized into five major classes:

**Class 1 — Cryptographic PETs (2 marks):**
These leverage mathematical hardness assumptions to protect data confidentiality and integrity.
- **Symmetric Encryption (AES-256):** Used in smart meters to encrypt consumption data before transmission.
- **Asymmetric Encryption (ECC, RSA):** Used in V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication to establish secure sessions.
- **Homomorphic Encryption (Paillier, BGV, CKKS):** Enables computation on encrypted CPS data without decryption — critical for privacy-preserving cloud analytics on sensor streams.
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZK-SNARKs, Sigma Protocols):** Allow a device to prove its identity/credentials without revealing them.

**Class 2 — Statistical / Anonymization PETs (2 marks):**
- **k-Anonymity:** Ensures each record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on quasi-identifiers. In hospital CPS, a release with $k = 5$ means no patient is uniquely identifiable.
- **l-Diversity & t-Closeness:** Address k-Anonymity's weakness against attribute disclosure and skewness attacks.
- **Differential Privacy (DP):** Adds calibrated Laplace or Gaussian noise to query outputs. Used by Google and Apple for telemetry.
- **Microaggregation & Generalization:** Replace quasi-identifier values with broader categories (e.g., age 23 → age range 20–30).

**Class 3 — Hardware-Based PETs (1 mark):**
- **Intel SGX:** Creates hardware-isolated enclaves for secure on-device processing of biometric CPS data.
- **ARM TrustZone:** Used in mobile/IoT CPS to segregate secure and normal worlds.
- **TPM 2.0 Modules:** Provide hardware root-of-trust for CPS boot integrity.
- **Secure Elements (SE):** Tamper-resistant chips in smart cards and IoT devices.

**Class 4 — Policy & Procedural PETs (1 mark):**
- **GDPR-DPIA Tools:** Risk assessment frameworks.
- **Consent Management Platforms (CMPs):** Allow users to opt-in/out of CPS data flows.
- **Data Loss Prevention (DLP):** Monitors and blocks unauthorized data exfiltration.

**Class 5 — Architectural PETs (1 mark):**
- **Mix Networks (Tor):** Obscure traffic origins in CPS command-and-control channels.
- **Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs):** Blockchain-based self-sovereign identity.
- **Federated Learning:** Trains ML models on decentralized CPS edge devices without raw data centralization.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **Layered Architecture of PETs in a CPS Pipeline** consists of seven functional layers, each addressing a specific threat surface:

| Layer | Function | PET(s) Deployed |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Perception/Sensing** | Raw data acquisition | Sensor-level filters, hardware security modules |
| **2. Edge Preprocessing + TEE** | Local computation | Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, on-device DP |
| **3. Network Transit** | Data in motion | TLS 1.3, IPSec, WireGuard VPN |
| **4. Cloud Storage** | Data at rest | AES-256 encryption, key management (KMS) |
| **5. Analytics Layer** | Computation | Homomorphic Encryption, Federated Learning, DP queries |
| **6. Access Control** | Authorization | ABAC/RBAC, OAuth 2.0, JWT tokens |
| **7. Audit & Compliance** | Logging | Immutable audit trails, GDPR-DPIA reports |

**Role of TEEs (2 marks specific):**

**Trusted Execution Environments** provide a hardware-isolated, encrypted memory region where sensitive CPS computations (e.g., biometric matching, key signing, encrypted analytics) can occur *without exposure* to the host operating system, hypervisor, or even physical attackers. In a CPS edge node:

1. The TEE boots first and **attests its integrity** to a remote verifier.
2. **Sensitive code and data** are loaded into the enclave's encrypted memory.
3. The host OS can call into the enclave via **secure function calls** but cannot inspect internal state.
4. **Sealed storage** allows keys to be bound to the device hardware.
5. **Remote attestation** proves to cloud servers that genuine, untampered code is running.

This makes TEEs indispensable for **zero-trust CPS architectures** where the edge device itself cannot be fully trusted.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Differential Privacy** as a Privacy Enhancing Technology. Derive the **Laplace Mechanism** and discuss its application in a CPS scenario. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Homomorphic Encryption** and **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)** as two competing privacy-preserving approaches. Discuss their trade-offs in real-time CPS deployments. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept of Differential Privacy (3 marks):**
Differential Privacy (DP), formally defined by Dwork et al. (2006), is a *mathematical framework* that provides quantifiable privacy guarantees by ensuring that the output of a query does not significantly change when any single individual's data is added or removed from the dataset.

Formally, a randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for all neighboring datasets $D, D'$ (differing by one record) and all subsets $S$ of outputs:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

The **privacy budget $\varepsilon$** controls the trade-off: smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy + more noise.

**Derivation of the Laplace Mechanism (2.5 marks):**
For a function $f$ with global sensitivity $\Delta f$, the Laplace mechanism is:

$$\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \text{where } \eta \sim \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

The Laplace PDF is:

$$\text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert x \mid}{b}\right)$$

Setting $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ ensures the $\varepsilon$-DP property holds. *(Full derivation shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

**CPS Application (1.5 marks):**
In a **Smart Grid CPS**, an energy provider releases daily aggregate consumption statistics. Without DP, an adversary could infer household occupancy patterns. By applying the Laplace mechanism with $\varepsilon = 1.0$ and $\Delta f = 1$ kWh (for a counting query on meter readings), the utility publishes noisy aggregates that:
- Preserve grid-load forecasting accuracy,
- Make it impossible to deduce any single household's consumption,
- Allow the privacy budget to be tracked across multiple daily releases.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Dimension | Homomorphic Encryption (HE) | Trusted Execution Environment (TEE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Root** | Mathematical hardness assumption (e.g., LWE, RLWE) | Hardware manufacturer (Intel, ARM) |
| **Computation Location** | Cloud or untrusted server, on encrypted data | Isolated enclave within untrusted host |
| **Performance** | Very high overhead (100x–1000x) for FHE | Near-native performance |
| **Threat Model** | Strong against passive/active software attacks | Vulnerable to side-channel attacks (e.g., Spectre, Meltdown) |
| **Data-at-Rest Protection** | Native (data is always encrypted) | Requires additional encryption at storage layer |
| **Real-Time Suitability** | Poor for latency-critical CPS (e.g., autonomous vehicles) | Excellent for real-time CPS edge processing |
| **Verifiability** | Cryptographic proofs (e.g., ZKP integration) | Remote attestation certificates |
| **Example Schemes** | Paillier, BGV, BFV, CKKS, TFHE | Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, AMD SEV |

**Trade-off Discussion (3 marks):**
- **HE is preferable** when the data owner does not trust the hardware vendor or cloud provider at all (e.g., cross-organization medical CPS data sharing).
- **TEE is preferable** when real-time performance is critical (e.g., V2X communication, industrial control loops) and a degree of trust in the hardware manufacturer is acceptable.
- **Hybrid approaches** (HE within a TEE) are emerging as the gold standard, combining cryptographic and hardware guarantees.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Naming PETs without context:** Writing "AES is a PET" without explaining *how* it enhances privacy will lose 1–2 marks. Always explain the privacy threat being mitigated.
> 2. **Confusing Anonymization with Encryption:** These are distinct categories — anonymization removes identifiers; encryption scrambles data reversibly.
> 3. **Skipping the privacy budget:** When writing about DP, you *must* specify the ε value. Generic answers lose marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the CPS context:** PECPT743 is *Privacy in CPS* — failing to anchor PETs in CPS scenarios (sensors, actuators, edge/cloud) will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Diagrams without labels:** A block diagram with unlabeled arrows is a common mark-loss pitfall. Always label *what* flows between blocks (e.g., "Encrypted Telemetry", "DP-Noisy Aggregate").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: PETs Overview**

- ✅ **Definition:** PETs = technologies minimizing PII exposure, processing, and linkage while preserving utility.
- ✅ **Three Foundational Principles:** Data Minimization, Purpose Limitation, Confidentiality/Integrity.
- ✅ **Five PET Categories:** Cryptographic, Statistical/Anonymization, Hardware (TEE), Policy, Architectural.
- ✅ **k-Anonymity:** Each record indistinguishable from $k-1$ others; target $k \geq 5$ in practice.
- ✅ **Differential Privacy:** Calibrated noise via Laplace/Gaussian mechanism; governed by privacy budget $\varepsilon$.
- ✅ **Laplace Mechanism Formula:** $\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
- ✅ **Homomorphic Encryption:** Computation on ciphertext; Paillier for addition, BGV/BFV/CKKS for general circuits.
- ✅ **TEEs:** Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone — hardware-isolated enclaves with remote attestation.
- ✅ **Zero-Knowledge Proofs:** Completeness + Soundness + Zero-Knowledge properties.
- ✅ **GDPR Penalties:** Up to **€20M or 4% of global turnover** — the regulatory motivator for PET adoption.
- ✅ **CPS-Centric Examples:** Smart meters (HE+DP), V2X (TLS+ECC), wearables (TEE), smart grids (k-Anonymity).
- ✅ **Architectural Layers:** Sensing → Edge+TEE → Transit → Storage → Analytics → Access → Audit.
- ✅ **PET Selection Drivers:** Threat model, regulatory regime, real-time constraints, hardware trust assumptions.
- ✅ **Federated Learning:** Architectural PET for decentralized ML training without centralizing raw CPS data.
- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Treating PETs as a single "encryption solution" — they are a *suite* of complementary tools.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of PETs in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Role of PETs in CPS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs)** are a comprehensive suite of tools, protocols, methodologies, and computational techniques designed to **protect personal and sensitive information** by minimizing data exposure, maximizing data subject control, and enabling the processing of data while preserving confidentiality, integrity, and unlinkability across the **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)** stack. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** — which integrate sensing, computation, communication, and physical actuation — PETs assume a *distributed, multi-layered, and resource-constrained* character. They safeguard privacy at every node of the CPS data lifecycle: $$\text{PETs in CPS} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left\{ \text{Protection}_i(\text{Sense}, \text{Communicate}, \text{Process}, \text{Store}, \text{Actuate}) \right\}$$ where the union $\bigcup$ represents the **holistic coverage** of privacy guarantees from edge sensors to cloud backends. ## 1.2 What is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS)? A **Cyber-Physical System** is an engineered system in which computational algorithms (the *cyber* component) are tightly coupled with physical processes (the *physical* component). Classic examples include: - **Smart Grids** and **Smart Meters** - **Autonomous Vehicles** and **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** and **SCADA** networks - **Medical Cyber-Physical Systems (MCPS)** — pacemakers, insulin pumps - **Smart Cities** with environmental and surveillance sensors > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In CPS, data is *continuously* generated, *often* contains Personally Identifiable Information (PII), and flows across **untrusted networks**. This makes PETs not merely optional add-ons but **mandatory architectural primitives** under regulations such as the EU **GDPR**, India's **DPDPA 2023**, and the US **HIPAA**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > **Analogy: The "Anonymous Postman" of a Smart City** > > Imagine a smart city where every household has a smart electricity meter. These meters continuously report your consumption pattern — *when you wake up, when you cook, when you watch TV*. This data, in the wrong hands, is a **goldmine of behavioral profiling**. > > Now imagine hiring an **Anonymous Postman**: > - He **scrambles** your letter's content (your energy data) so that the electricity company only learns the *aggregate consumption of a neighborhood*, not *your specific usage*. > - He **hides your return address** (your identity) using a one-way alias. > - He **verifies that your payment is genuine** without ever seeing your credit card (a Zero-Knowledge Proof). > - He **combines letters from 1,000 homes** in a way that no single letter can be isolated (a Mix Network). > > **PETs are exactly this Anonymous Postman for Cyber-Physical Systems.** They allow the smart grid, the hospital, the factory to *use* your data productively while *never truly seeing* the raw, identifying, sensitive details. ## 1.4 Why PETs are *Uniquely Critical* in CPS | **CPS Property** | **Privacy Implication** | **PETs' Response** | |---|---|---| | Continuous sensing | Massive data streams harvested 24/7 | **Differential Privacy** with noise injection | | Resource-constrained edge nodes | Cannot run heavy cryptography | **Lightweight Cryptography** (PRESENT, ASCON) | | Untrusted wireless channels | Eavesdropping / MITM attacks | **End-to-End Encryption + Authenticated Key Exchange** | | Multi-domain data fusion | Re-identification of anonymized data | **k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness** | | Need for centralized analytics | Data aggregation in cloud reveals user behavior | **Homomorphic Encryption, Federated Learning** | | Distributed actuation (vehicles, robots) | Spoofed commands cause physical harm | **Zero-Knowledge Proofs, TEEs (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome (CO1):** The student must be able to *identify, classify, and justify* the selection of appropriate PETs for a given CPS application scenario. This is a **Bloom's Level 3 (Apply)** cognitive target. ## 1.5 Categories of PETs — A High-Level Taxonomy PETs in CPS literature are conventionally grouped into **three architectural tiers**: 1. **Tier 1 — Data-Obfuscation PETs** *Encryption, Anonymization, Pseudonymization, Masking, Tokenization, Differential Privacy.* 2. **Tier 2 — Computation-Preserving PETs** *Homomorphic Encryption, Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC), Private Information Retrieval (PIR), Garbled Circuits.* 3. **Tier 3 — Trust-Shifting PETs** *Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs), Blockchain-based decentralized identifiers, Federated Learning with secure aggregation.* --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of PETs in CPS The theoretical backbone of PETs in CPS rests on three rigorously defined privacy guarantees. Mastering these is **mandatory for KTU ESE and internal assessments**. ### 2.1.1 Pillar 1: k-Anonymity (Samarati & Sweeney, 1998) > A dataset satisfies **k-anonymity** if, for every record, there are at least $k-1$ other records with **indistinguishable quasi-identifiers** (QIs). $$\forall r \in D, \quad \vert \{ r' \in D \mid Q(r') = Q(r) \} \vert \geq k$$ where $Q(r)$ is the quasi-identifier tuple of record $r$, and $D$ is the dataset. - **CPS Example:** Smart meter readings are aggregated across a building of $k = 50$ apartments. Each apartment's record looks identical in the QI set $\{$floor, building, hour$\}$, so the probability of re-identification is at most $1/k = 1/50$. ### 2.1.2 Pillar 2: Differential Privacy (Dwork et al., 2006) > A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$\epsilon$-differential privacy** if, for any two neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ that differ by exactly one record: $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$ The **privacy budget** $\epsilon$ controls the trade-off: - **Small $\epsilon$** $\Rightarrow$ **Strong privacy**, more noise, less utility. - **Large $\epsilon$** $\Rightarrow$ **Weak privacy**, less noise, more utility. The **Laplace Mechanism** adds noise drawn from a Laplace distribution: $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$ where $\Delta f$ is the **sensitivity** of the query function $f$ — the maximum change in $f$ when one record is added or removed. ### 2.1.3 Pillar 3: Semantic Security of Encryption > A public-key encryption scheme is **semantically secure** (IND-CPA) if no probabilistic polynomial-time (PPT) adversary can distinguish the encryptions of two chosen messages with probability significantly better than $1/2$. Formally, for any PPT adversary $\mathcal{A}$: $$\vert \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\text{Enc}(pk, m_0)) = 0] - \Pr[\mathcal{A}(\text{Enc}(pk, m_1)) = 0] \vert \leq \epsilon_{\text{negl}}$$ ## 2.2 The Why and How — Operational Logic Steps **Step 1 — Identify the Data Lifecycle Stage:** CPS data flows through **Collection $\rightarrow$ Transmission $\rightarrow$ Processing $\rightarrow$ Storage $\rightarrow$ Actuation**. Each stage requires a *different* PET. **Step 2 — Classify the Adversary Model:** The PETs deployed must match the assumed threat model — *honest-but-curious cloud server*, *active network attacker*, *malicious insider*. **Step 3 — Compute the Resource Budget:** CPS edge devices have bounded CPU (often $< 100$ MHz), RAM ($< 256$ KB), and battery. Heavyweight PETs (e.g., full FHE) are infeasible; **lightweight symmetric ciphers** like **ASCON** or **PRESENT** are preferred. **Step 4 — Apply Composability:** Since CPS pipelines are *sequential*, the privacy guarantees must compose. For $\epsilon$-DP, sequential composition yields: $$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i$$ **Step 5 — Verify and Audit:** The deployed PETs must be **provably secure** under a formal adversarial model — cryptographic proofs for encryption, impossibility results for de-anonymization, and empirical noise-vs-utility curves for differential privacy. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. It is the single most exam-relevant artifact in this module.** | **#** | **Concept** | **Formal Expression** | **Parameter Meaning** | **CPS Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | k-Anonymity | $\vert Q^{-1}(q) \vert \geq k$ | $Q$ = quasi-identifier, $k$ = group size | Smart meter dataset release | | 2 | $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy | $e^{-\epsilon} \leq \frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)=S]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')=S]} \leq e^{\epsilon}$ | $\epsilon$ = privacy budget | Telemetry from wearables | | 3 | Laplace Mechanism | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$ | $\Delta f$ = global sensitivity | Aggregated health stats | | 4 | Gaussian Mechanism | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 I\right),\ \sigma = \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$ | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP | Statistical CPS analytics | | 5 | DP Composition (Sequential) | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i$ | $k$ = number of queries | Multi-stage sensor fusion | | 6 | DP Composition (Parallel) | $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max_i \epsilon_i$ | Disjoint datasets | Federated IoT sub-trees | | 7 | Homomorphic Addition | $\text{Dec}(\text{Enc}(a) \oplus \text{Enc}(b)) = a + b$ | $\oplus$ = ciphertext addition | Cloud sum of meter readings | | 8 | SMPC Secret Share (Shamir) | $s = \sum_{i=1}^{t} L_i \cdot s_i \pmod p$ | $L_i$ = Lagrange coefficient | Threshold sensing consensus | | 9 | RSA Encryption | $c = m^{e} \bmod n$ | $e, n$ = public key, $m$ = plaintext | Key exchange in V2X | | 10 | AES-128 Key Space | $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | Number of possible keys | Lightweight CPS node auth | | 11 | ZKP Soundness Error | $P_{\text{cheat}} \leq \epsilon_{\text{sound}}$ | $\epsilon_{\text{sound}}$ = soundness param | Vehicle-to-charging-station proof | | 12 | Information Theoretic Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits of uncertainty | Anonymity set size measure | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility | **Application Domain** | **PET Deployed** | **Engineering Benefit** | |---|---|---| | Smart Grid | Homomorphic Encryption + DP | Aggregate billing without revealing per-home usage | | Autonomous Vehicles | ZKP + TESLA certificates | Prove vehicle credentials without revealing VIN | | Healthcare CPS | Federated Learning + DP | Train disease models on patient data without centralization | | Industrial IoT | TEE (Intel SGX) + Lightweight Crypto | Confidential firmware update on PLCs | | Smart Cities (video) | Mix Networks + Onion Routing | Privacy-preserving traffic surveillance | | Wearables | On-device DP | Locally perturb heart-rate before transmission | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Privacy Budget Accumulation in a Multi-Stage CPS Pipeline ### Problem Setup Consider a **smart healthcare CPS** where a wearable device performs three sequential operations on a patient's heart-rate series $X$: 1. **Stage 1:** Compute the mean $\mu$ and add Laplace noise (DP Stage A). 2. **Stage 2:** Compute the standard deviation $\sigma$ and add Gaussian noise (DP Stage B). 3. **Stage 3:** Publish a binary alert $A \in \{0, 1\}$ based on a noisy threshold. Each stage consumes some portion of the **global privacy budget** $\epsilon_{\text{total}}$. We must derive the overall privacy guarantee. ### Step 1 — Write the Stage-A Mechanism The mean query has global sensitivity: $$\Delta \mu = \frac{(b - a)}{n}$$ where $b$ and $a$ are the upper and lower bounds of the heart-rate range, and $n$ is the window size. The **Laplace mechanism** for Stage A is: $$\mathcal{M}_1(X) = \mu(X) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta \mu}{\epsilon_1}\right)$$ > **Logic:** We inject noise proportional to sensitivity and inversely proportional to the budget. Higher sensitivity $\Rightarrow$ more noise. Stricter privacy ($\epsilon_1$ small) $\Rightarrow$ more noise. ### Step 2 — Write the Stage-B Mechanism For standard deviation, the global sensitivity over a bounded range $[a, b]$ is: $$\Delta \sigma = \frac{(b - a)}{\sqrt{n}}$$ The Gaussian mechanism for Stage B is: $$\mathcal{M}_2(X) = \sigma(X) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma_{\text{noise}}^2\right)$$ where the noise standard deviation is: $$\sigma_{\text{noise}} = \frac{\Delta \sigma \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon_2}$$ > **Logic:** The Gaussian mechanism relaxes pure $\epsilon$-DP to $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP, allowing a much smaller noise magnitude under composition. ### Step 3 — Apply Sequential Composition Theorem Since Stages 1 and 2 operate on **overlapping data** (the same heart-rate series $X$), the privacy budgets accumulate additively: $$\epsilon_{\text{cumulative}} = \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2$$ The combined mechanism $\mathcal{M}_{12} = (\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2)$ satisfies: $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}_{12}(X) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}_{12}(X') \in S]$$ > **Logic:** Sequential composition is the **worst-case** bound. Privacy loss grows linearly with the number of queries — a critical concern for streaming CPS data. ### Step 4 — Apply Advanced Composition (Tightening the Bound) For $k$ queries each with budget $\epsilon_i$, the **advanced composition theorem** gives a tighter asymptotic bound: $$\epsilon_{\text{advanced}} = \sqrt{2 k \ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \epsilon + k \epsilon (e^{\epsilon} - 1)$$ For large $k$, the dominant term is $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k})$, a significant improvement over the linear $\mathcal{O}(k)$ bound. ### Step 5 — Include Stage 3 and Compute Total Budget Stage 3 (the alert) consumes $\epsilon_3$. The total cumulative privacy loss is: $$\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 + \epsilon_3$$ The full derivation: $$ \begin{aligned} \epsilon_{\text{total}} &= \epsilon_1 + \epsilon_2 + \epsilon_3 \\[4pt] &= \frac{\Delta \mu}{\lambda_1} + \frac{\Delta \sigma \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}} + \epsilon_3 \\[4pt] &= \frac{(b-a)/n}{\lambda_1} + \frac{(b-a)/\sqrt{n} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}} + \epsilon_3 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The total budget is now a *closed-form expression* in the engineering parameters — sensor range $[a, b]$, window size $n$, and noise scales $\lambda_1, \sigma_{\text{noise}}$. A system designer can size each parameter to keep $\epsilon_{\text{total}} \leq 1$ (the conventional "strong privacy" threshold). ### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example Let $a = 40$ BPM, $b = 180$ BPM, $n = 100$ samples. - Sensitivity of mean: $\Delta \mu = (180 - 40) / 100 = 1.4$ BPM. - Sensitivity of std-dev: $\Delta \sigma = (180 - 40) / \sqrt{100} = 14$ BPM. - Choose $\epsilon_1 = 0.3$, $\epsilon_2 = 0.4$, $\epsilon_3 = 0.1$, $\delta = 10^{-5}$. Total budget: $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.3 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.8$. This is well within the **strong privacy** regime. The Laplace noise scale for Stage A is: $$\lambda_1 = \frac{\Delta \mu}{\epsilon_1} = \frac{1.4}{0.3} \approx 4.67 \text{ BPM}$$ This means the published mean is the true mean $\pm$ a Laplace-distributed random number with mean noise magnitude $\approx 4.67$ BPM — *noticeable* but *acceptable* for medical alerting. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PET Selection Decision Framework The following table is the **engineering decision matrix** that KTU examiners expect students to internalize. | **CPS Constraint** | **Preferred PET** | **Privacy Guarantee** | **Overhead (Computation / Bandwidth / Energy)** | |---|---|---|---| | Edge node, $< 1$ KB RAM | **Lightweight Cipher (ASCON)** | IND-CPA | Low / Low / Low | | Cloud aggregation, no raw access | **Homomorphic Encryption (Paillier, BFV)** | Computational indistinguishability | High / Medium / N/A | | Multi-party sensing, no trust | **SMPC (Shamir, SPDZ)** | Information-theoretic / computational | Medium / High / Medium | | Audit & compliance check | **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (zk-SNARK)** | Soundness + Zero-knowledge | High / Low / High | | Real-time consent revocation | **Pseudonymization + Mix Zones** | Unlinkability | Low / Low / Low | | Statistical learning on private data | **Federated Learning + DP** | $(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP | Medium / Medium / Medium | | Trusted hardware available | **TEE (Intel SGX / ARM TrustZone)** | Hardware-rooted attestation | Low / Low / Low | | Public ledger / supply chain | **Blockchain + ZKP** | Byzantine fault tolerance | High / High / High | --- # 4.
