KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] **Define Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs). List any three categories of PETs with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) are a class of tools, protocols, and techniques that protect personal and sensitive data by minimizing data exposure, enabling data subject control, and supporting privacy-preserving computation across the ICT stack, particularly in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). > > **Three Categories:** > 1. **Data Obfuscation PETs** — Example: Differential Privacy, which adds calibrated noise to query results. > 2. **Computation-Preserving PETs** — Example: Homomorphic Encryption, which allows computation directly on ciphertexts. > 3. **Trust-Shifting PETs** — Example: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) such as Intel SGX, which isolate sensitive computation in hardware. **(3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for categories with examples)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1] **Differentiate between k-Anonymity and $\epsilon$-Differential Privacy. State one limitation of k-Anonymity.** **Model Answer:** | **Property** | **k-Anonymity** | **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy** | |---|---|---| | Guarantee type | Syntactic | Semantic / statistical | | Adversary model | Limited (background knowledge not modeled) | Robust (any auxiliary info) | | Mechanism | Generalization, suppression | Noise injection | | Composability | Poor | Excellent (sequential & parallel) | > **Limitation of k-Anonymity:** It is *vulnerable to homogeneity and background-knowledge attacks*. If all $k$ records share the same sensitive attribute, no privacy is preserved. Moreover, k-anonymity does *not* compose — releasing two k-anonymous datasets can lead to re-identification when linked. **(3 Marks: 2 for differentiation table + 1 for limitation)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > 1. **Do NOT** write vague statements like *"PETs are used for security"* — always specify **which property** (confidentiality, unlinkability, unobservability) is preserved. > 2. **Do NOT** confuse *anonymization* with *pseudonymization*. Anonymization is **irreversible**; pseudonymization is **reversible with a key**. > 3. **Do NOT** omit the **adversary model** in differential privacy questions — examiners explicitly look for the neighboring-datasets definition. > 4. **Do NOT** present homomorphic encryption as a single primitive — specify *partially* (PHE) vs *fully* (FHE) vs *somewhat* (SHE), and the supported operations (addition, multiplication). > 5. **Always** mention the **composability** of DP — it is a guaranteed scoring point in KTU 2024 rubrics. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** **(a)** *Explain the three-tier taxonomy of Privacy Enhancing Technologies in Cyber-Physical Systems. For each tier, provide two specific PETs and a representative CPS application.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *With the aid of the Laplace mechanism, derive the noise scale required to achieve $\epsilon$-differential privacy for a smart meter query that reports total energy consumption in a building of 50 apartments. Assume each apartment's consumption lies in $[0, 10]$ kWh per hour, and the privacy budget is $\epsilon = 0.5$.* **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Three-Tier Taxonomy of PETs in CPS:** **Tier 1 — Data-Obfuscation PETs** protect the *content* of data. - *PET 1.1: Symmetric Encryption (AES-128).* Used in CPS node-to-gateway communication to prevent eavesdropping on sensor readings. - *PET 1.2: Differential Privacy.* Used in smart meter analytics to publish aggregate consumption patterns while hiding individual household usage. **Tier 2 — Computation-Preserving PETs** allow *processing without exposure*. - *PET 2.1: Homomorphic Encryption (e.g., Paillier).* Used in smart grids for cloud-side aggregation of encrypted meter readings — the grid operator computes the *sum* without ever decrypting individual readings. - *PET 2.2: Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC).* Used in federated health CPS where multiple hospitals jointly compute a model on encrypted patient data without revealing their own datasets. **Tier 3 — Trust-Shifting PETs** redistribute or *re-anchor trust* in a single party. - *PET 3.1: Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs).* Used in autonomous vehicles to prove possession of a valid license without transmitting the actual license number. - *PET 3.2: Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs, e.g., ARM TrustZone).* Used in industrial PLCs to execute control logic inside a hardware-isolated enclave, immune even to OS-level malware. > *Justification (1 mark):* The three-tier approach maps cleanly onto the **CPS data lifecycle** — Tier 1 at the *edge* (data at rest / in motion), Tier 2 at the *aggregation* layer (data in use), Tier 3 at the *decision/actuation* layer (trust in commands). **(7 Marks: 2 for taxonomy structure + 3 for 6 PETs with applications + 2 for justification)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Identify the Query Function.** The query is the total energy consumption: $$f(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i, \quad \text{where } n = 50, \quad x_i \in [0, 10] \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Global Sensitivity.** The sensitivity $\Delta f$ is the maximum change in $f$ when one record is added or removed: $$\Delta f = \max_{x, x'} \vert f(X) - f(X') \vert = \max x_i = 10 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Laplace Mechanism Formula.** The noise scale $\lambda$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} \\[4pt] &= \frac{10 \text{ kWh}}{0.5} \\[4pt] &= 20 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — State the Mechanism.** $$\mathcal{M}(X) = f(X) + \text{Lap}(20)$$ > **Interpretation:** The reported total energy consumption is the *true total* plus a random number drawn from a Laplace distribution with mean zero and scale 20 kWh. The expected absolute noise is $20$ kWh, which is large relative to the per-apartment bound — this is the *price of strong privacy* ($\epsilon = 0.5$ is strict). **Step 5 — Trade-off Discussion.** > If a weaker privacy budget $\epsilon = 2$ is acceptable, then $\lambda = 10/2 = 5$ kWh — noise is 4× smaller. The system designer must choose $\epsilon$ to balance **utility** (low noise $\Rightarrow$ accurate billing) and **privacy** (low $\epsilon \Rightarrow$ strong privacy). KTU examiners look for this *trade-off* comment. **(7 Marks: 2 for query setup + 1 for sensitivity + 1 for formula + 1 for mechanism statement + 2 for trade-off discussion)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** *Discuss the role of Differential Privacy (DP) in CPS. Explain the Laplace and Gaussian mechanisms with their respective noise addition formulas. State the advanced composition theorem for $k$ queries.* **(7 Marks)** **(b)** *Consider a fleet of 100 autonomous vehicles reporting their GPS coordinates to a central traffic management system every second. Propose a privacy-preserving architecture using TWO distinct PETs. Justify your choices and explain how they complement each other.* **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Role of DP in CPS:** Differential Privacy provides a *mathematically rigorous* guarantee that the inclusion or exclusion of any single CPS node's data does not significantly affect the output of an analytics query. This is **essential in CPS** because: - CPS devices generate *continuous* streams — DP allows *per-query* privacy budgeting. - DP is **robust to auxiliary information** — even an adversary who knows the entire dataset of other vehicles cannot infer the target vehicle's location. - DP **composes gracefully** across queries and across time, critical for streaming telemetry. **Laplace Mechanism** — for queries with **bounded sensitivity**: $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$ Achieves **pure $\epsilon$-DP**. Noise magnitude: $\mathcal{O}(1/\epsilon)$. **Gaussian Mechanism** — for queries needing **$(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP**: $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2 I\right), \quad \sigma = \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$$ Noise magnitude: $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\log(1/\delta)} / \epsilon)$. Preferred for **high-dimensional** outputs (e.g., neural network gradients in federated CPS learning). **Advanced Composition Theorem:** For $k$ adaptive queries each with budget $\epsilon$, the mechanism satisfies $(\epsilon', k\delta + \delta')$-DP where: $$\epsilon' = \sqrt{2 k \ln(1/\delta')} \cdot \epsilon + k \epsilon (e^{\epsilon} - 1)$$ For large $k$, the dominant scaling is $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k} \cdot \epsilon)$, a significant tightening over naive linear composition. **(7 Marks: 2 for role + 2 for mechanisms + 1 for advanced composition formula + 2 for interpretation)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Proposed Architecture: *Local DP + Federated Learning with Secure Aggregation.*** **PET 1: Local Differential Privacy (LDP) at the Vehicle** Each vehicle perturbs its GPS coordinate $x_i$ *locally* before transmission: $$\tilde{x}_i = x_i + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta x}{\epsilon_{\text{local}}}\right)$$ - $\Delta x$ = max GPS displacement per second (e.g., $50$ m at highway speeds). - $\epsilon_{\text{local}} = 0.5$ per vehicle. - The traffic server *never sees* the raw GPS — only the noisy version. **PET 2: Federated Learning with Secure Aggregation (SMPC)** The traffic server trains a flow-prediction model *collaboratively*: - Each vehicle computes *local model gradients* $\nabla L_i(\theta)$ on its local data. - The server uses **Secure Aggregation** (a SMPC protocol by Bonawitz et al., 2017) to compute: $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{100} \nabla L_i(\theta_t)$$ *without ever decrypting* any individual $\nabla L_i$. **Justification of Complementarity:** > [!IMPORTANT] > The two PETs are **complementary** in defense-in-depth: > - **LDP** protects *input-level* privacy — the raw GPS data is never sent in clear. > - **Secure Aggregation** protects *output-level* privacy — the server cannot see individual gradients, only their sum. > - Together, they implement **end-to-end privacy** across the CPS pipeline (sensing $\rightarrow$ communication $\rightarrow$ learning), satisfying the KTU 2024 CO1 outcome of *applying* PETs to a CPS scenario. **Trade-off:** LDP adds noise that may degrade the model's utility by 5–15%; secure aggregation adds **computational overhead** of $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ pairwise mask exchanges — a worthwhile cost for privacy. **(7 Marks: 2 for PET 1 + 2 for PET 2 + 2 for complementarity justification + 1 for trade-off)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Before KTU ESE** - **PETs Definition:** Tools, techniques, and protocols that *protect personal data* across the CPS data lifecycle. - **Three Tiers of PETs in CPS:** 1. **Data Obfuscation** — Encryption, k-Anonymity, DP, Tokenization. 2. **Computation-Preserving** — Homomorphic Encryption, SMPC, PIR, Federated Learning. 3. **Trust-Shifting** — ZKPs, TEEs, Blockchain, Mix Networks. - **k-Anonymity formula:** $\vert Q^{-1}(q) \vert \geq k$ — vulnerable to homogeneity and background-knowledge attacks. - **$\epsilon$-Differential Privacy formula:** $e^{-\epsilon} \leq \frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = S]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = S]} \leq e^{\epsilon}$ — robust to auxiliary info, composable. - **Laplace Mechanism:** $\text{Noise} = \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$ — use for **pure $\epsilon$-DP**. - **Gaussian Mechanism:** $\sigma = \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\epsilon}$ — use for **$(\epsilon, \delta)$-DP**, high-dimensional outputs. - **Sequential DP Composition:** $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \epsilon_i$ (linear, worst-case). - **Parallel DP Composition:** $\epsilon_{\text{total}} = \max \epsilon_i$ (over disjoint datasets). - **Advanced Composition:** $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{k} \cdot \epsilon)$ for $k$ queries. - **Homomorphic Encryption property:** $\text{Dec}(\text{Enc}(a) \oplus \text{Enc}(b)) = a + b$ (Paillier) — supports addition. - **SMPC (Shamir):** Secret $s$ split into $t$ shares; recovered via Lagrange interpolation: $s = \sum L_i s_i \pmod p$. - **ZKP Properties:** *Completeness* (honest prover convinces verifier), *Soundness* (cheating prover fails with high probability), *Zero-Knowledge* (verifier learns nothing beyond validity). - **TEE Examples:** Intel SGX (CPUs), ARM TrustZone (mobile/embedded), Apple Secure Enclave. - **CPS-specific challenges:** resource constraints, real-time deadlines, multi-domain data fusion, physical safety coupling. - **Regulatory drivers:** GDPR (EU), DPDPA 2023 (India), HIPAA (US healthcare), CCPA (California). - **Engineering mantra:** *"Privacy is a system property, not a feature."* PETs must be designed *into* the CPS architecture from the start. > **Golden Rule for KTU 2024:** *Always* state the **adversary model**, the **privacy budget** (for DP), and the **resource budget** (for CPS) when answering PET-related questions. Missing any of these three elements can cost you **1–2 marks per sub-question**.

topics.title: Examples of PETs in Practice
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of PETs in Practice

# Examples of PETs in Practice — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Context (PECPT743 - Privacy in CPS)**
> Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) are not just theoretical constructs — they are deployed in production systems across the world. From anonymous web browsing to privacy-preserving data analytics, this topic surveys *concrete, working* PETs and examines how they map onto Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) architectures.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) in Practice** refer to deployable, real-world tools, protocols, and cryptographic systems engineered to enforce **confidentiality**, **anonymity**, **unlinkability**, and **untraceability** of users, data, and transactions within operational systems — including Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), IoT, cloud, and networked infrastructures.

A PET, when deployed "in practice", must satisfy three simultaneous properties:

1. **Functional Utility** — It must perform the required service (e.g., query a database, send a message, authenticate a user) while withholding sensitive information from unintended parties.
2. **Provable Privacy Guarantee** — It must offer a *formal* (mathematical) or *cryptographic* assurance against specific adversarial models (honest-but-curious, malicious, global/passive adversary).
3. **Engineering Feasibility** — It must be implementable on realistic hardware, operate within reasonable latency and bandwidth budgets, and integrate with existing protocol stacks (e.g., TCP/IP, MQTT, OPC-UA in CPS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sealed Exam Paper"

Imagine a university examination:

- The student writes answers on a paper (raw data).
- A **sealed envelope** (encryption) hides the answer from the courier.
- The examiner's office opens the envelope and **grades without ever learning the student's name** (anonymous credentials).
- After grading, only the **aggregate pass percentage** is published, not individual scores (differential privacy).
- A **plagiarism check** is performed without revealing other students' papers (private information retrieval / secure multi-party computation).

This is precisely how layered PETs work in practice: each technology plugs a *specific privacy leak* in the data lifecycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Examinations**
> When answering questions on PETs, examiners look for three things: **(i)** the *threat model* addressed, **(ii)** the *cryptographic primitive* used, and **(iii)** the *practical trade-off* (e.g., overhead, trust assumptions). Always mention all three.

### 1.3 Categories of PETs (Survey Map)

| Category | Example PETs | Primary Privacy Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Anonymous Communications** | Tor, Mixnets, DC-Nets | Untraceability / Unlinkability |
| **Anonymous Authentication** | Idemix, U-Prove, BBS+ Signatures | Selective Disclosure / Unlinkability |
| **Cryptographic Computation** | Homomorphic Encryption (FHE/PHE), SMPC, ZKPs | Input Privacy during Computation |
| **Statistical Privacy** | Differential Privacy (DP), k-Anonymity, l-Diversity | Output Indistinguishability |
| **Data Minimization** | Private Information Retrieval (PIR), ORAM | Access-Pattern Hiding |
| **Hardware-based** | Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), Secure Enclaves (SGX, TrustZone) | Isolated Trust |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A PET, when operationalized "in practice", goes through a four-stage lifecycle:

1. **Threat Modeling** — The designer enumerates adversaries (e.g., network eavesdropper, curious cloud provider, malicious aggregator) and the privacy properties violated.
2. **Primitive Selection** — A cryptographic or statistical primitive is chosen: e.g., *ring signatures* for sender anonymity, *lattice-based FHE* for cloud computation, *Laplace mechanism* for DP.
3. **Protocol Composition** — The primitive is embedded into a system protocol (e.g., Tor circuits use 3 onion-routing relays; Intel SGX uses remote attestation).
4. **Deployment & Auditing** — Real-world integration, performance benchmarking, and formal verification (e.g., ProVerif, Coq).

### 2.2 Detailed Walkthrough of Major Practical PETs

#### A. Tor (The Onion Router)

- **How:** A client's message is wrapped in **three layers of encryption**. Each of the three relay nodes (Guard, Middle, Exit) peels off one layer. No single node knows both source and destination.
- **Privacy Property:** Sender anonymity against a *non-global* passive adversary.
- **Real-world Use:** Journalists, whistleblowers, censorship circumvention.
- **CPS Use-Case:** Privacy-preserving telemetry in vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs), where vehicles report traffic conditions without revealing location trajectories.

#### B. Mix Networks (Mixnets)

- **How:** A batch of ciphertexts is collected by a **mix node**, which permutes, delays, and re-encrypts the batch before forwarding.
- **Privacy Property:** Strong unlinkability between input and output messages.
- **Difference from Tor:** Mixnets provide *sender-receiver* unlinkability; Tor only provides *sender-destination* unlinkability from the exit node's perspective.
- **Real-world Use:** Mixmaster, Loopix (used by Nym), Vuvuzela.

#### C. Anonymous Credentials (Idemix, U-Prove, BBS+)

- **How:** A user obtains a credential from an Issuer; later, during a presentation, the user proves possession of the credential **without revealing which credential** and **without revealing any identifier** that would link successive presentations.
- **Privacy Property:** Unlinkability across multiple uses, selective disclosure (prove "age > 18" without revealing birthdate).
- **Real-world Use:** IRMA (Netherlands), national e-ID systems, e-prescriptions.

#### D. Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

- **How:** Computations are performed directly on ciphertexts, producing an encrypted result that, when decrypted, matches the result of the same operation on plaintexts.
- **Three Variants:**
  - **Partially HE (PHE):** Supports one operation unlimited times (e.g., Paillier — addition).
  - **Somewhat HE (SHE):** Supports both add and multiply, but limited depth.
  - **Fully HE (FHE):** Arbitrary computation (e.g., BFV, BGV, CKKS schemes; libraries: Microsoft SEAL, OpenFHE).
- **Real-world Use:** Privacy-preserving medical research (e.g., genomics analysis on encrypted patient data), encrypted machine learning inference.

#### E. Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)

- **How:** Multiple parties jointly compute a function $f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ such that each party learns **only the output** and nothing about other parties' inputs.
- **Core Building Blocks:** Secret sharing (Shamir's, additive), garbled circuits (Yao), oblivious transfer.
- **Real-world Use:** Danish sugar beet auctions (2018), Boston wage gap analysis, MPC for anti-money-laundering.

#### F. Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs)

- **How:** A prover convinces a verifier that a statement is true **without revealing any information beyond the validity of the statement itself**.
- **Variants:** Interactive (Schnorr, Sigma protocols), Non-Interactive (NIZK, Groth16), zk-SNARKs, zk-STARKs.
- **Real-world Use:** Zcash (zk-SNARKs for transaction privacy), Polygon Hermez, Filecoin proofs of storage.

#### G. Differential Privacy (DP)

- **How:** Random noise (Laplace, Gaussian, Exponential) is added to query results or to model outputs such that the inclusion or exclusion of any single record produces statistically indistinguishable outputs.
- **Privacy Property:** Indistinguishability quantified by the privacy budget $\varepsilon$.
- **Real-world Use:** Apple (emoji frequency collection), Google (RAPPOR), US Census 2020.

#### H. Private Information Retrieval (PIR)

- **How:** A user retrieves an item from a database *without revealing which item* was retrieved.
- **Computational PIR (cPIR):** Uses homomorphic encryption or lattice-based cryptography.
- **Real-world Use:** Privacy-preserving DNS (e.g., Pyeongtaek PIR), contact discovery in Signal.

#### I. Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)

- **How:** Hardware-isolated enclaves (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, AMD SEV) execute code with **confidentiality** and **integrity** guarantees, even from a malicious OS/hypervisor.
- **Real-world Use:** Microsoft's Azure Confidential Computing, Secret Network (blockchain with encrypted state).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for PECPT743 — Memorize for ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Symbols / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Differential Privacy (Laplace) | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\tfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget, $\Delta f$ = sensitivity |
| 2 | DP Composition (Sequential) | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | Additive across $k$ queries |
| 3 | DP Composition (Parallel) | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \max(\varepsilon_1, \dots, \varepsilon_k)$ | Max across disjoint datasets |
| 4 | Advanced Composition | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{2k \ln(1/\delta)} \cdot \varepsilon + k\varepsilon(e^{\varepsilon}-1)$ | Tighter bound for $k$ queries |
| 5 | k-Anonymity | Every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others | Equivalence class size $\geq k$ |
| 6 | Homomorphic Addition (Paillier) | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) = E(m_1 + m_2)$ | Additive homomorphism |
| 7 | Homomorphic Multiplication (RSA) | $E(m_1)^{m_2} = E(m_1 \cdot m_2)$ | Multiplicative homomorphism |
| 8 | FHE Scheme Noise Growth | $B_{\text{fresh}} < B_{\text{max}}$; bootstrapping resets noise | Bounded noise budget |
| 9 | SMPC Honest Majority Threshold | $t < n/2$ (semi-honest) or $t < n/3$ (malicious) | $n$ parties, $t$ corruptions |
| 10 | PIR Information-Theoretic Lower Bound | $O(n)$ download for IT-PIR; $O(\sqrt{n})$ for cPIR | $n$ = database size |
| 11 | ZKP Completeness | $\Pr[\langle P,V \rangle = 1 \mid x \in L] = 1$ | Honest prover convinces verifier |
| 12 | ZKP Soundness | $\Pr[\langle P^*, V \rangle = 1 \mid x \notin L] \leq \varepsilon_{\text{s}}$ | $\varepsilon_s$ = soundness error |
| 13 | Shamir Secret Sharing | $s = f(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} y_i \prod_{j \neq i} \tfrac{x_j}{x_j - x_i}$ | Threshold $t$, polynomial degree $t-1$ |
| 14 | Tor Circuit Latency | $T_{\text{circuit}} \approx 3 \cdot \bar{T}_{\text{relay}} + T_{\text{crypt}}$ | 3-hop by default |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the bare `|` symbol for absolute value. Use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) to avoid breaking the table syntax. E.g., write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ in plain text.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | PET Applied | Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| Smart Healthcare (CPS) | FHE, DP | Encrypted genomic analysis on cloud; aggregate-only clinical insights |
| Smart Grid | SMPC, ABE | Cross-utility load forecasting without exposing consumer data |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Tor, Mixnets, DP | Anonymous V2X telemetry, privacy-preserving map updates |
| Industrial IoT | TEE (SGX) | Confidential PLC logic execution, secure firmware attestation |
| Smart Cities | DP, k-Anonymity | Mobility analytics without re-identifying citizens |
| Blockchain / DLT | ZKP, Ring CT | Private transactions in Monero, Zcash; confidential smart contracts |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Differential Privacy: Noise Calibration (Laplace Mechanism)

**Problem Statement (KTU-typical):** A hospital database $D$ contains patient records. The query $f$ returns the count of patients with a particular disease. The global sensitivity is $\Delta f = 1$. Calibrate the Laplace noise such that $\varepsilon$-differential privacy is satisfied.

**Derivation (Exhaustive):**

We begin with the formal definition of $\varepsilon$-differential privacy.

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP if for all neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record, and for all measurable sets $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

The Laplace distribution with scale parameter $b$ has probability density function:

$$
\text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)
$$

For a query $f$ with global sensitivity $\Delta f = \max_{D,D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$, the Laplace mechanism is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Y, \quad \text{where } Y \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

To verify the DP guarantee, we compute the ratio of probabilities for neighbouring $D, D'$:

$$
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = x]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = x]} = \frac{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \cdot \vert x - f(D) \vert}{\Delta f}\right)}{\exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \cdot \vert x - f(D') \vert}{\Delta f}\right)}
$$

Using the reverse triangle inequality $\vert a \vert - \vert b \vert \leq \vert a - b \vert$:

$$
\leq \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \cdot \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert}{\Delta f}\right) \leq \exp(\varepsilon)
$$

Hence the mechanism is $\varepsilon$-DP. $\blacksquare$

**Numerical Example:** For $\Delta f = 1$ and $\varepsilon = 0.1$:

$$
b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

So we add noise $Y \sim \text{Lap}(10)$, which has standard deviation $\sigma = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14$.

> [!NOTE]
> Smaller $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy ⇒ larger noise ⇒ lower utility. This is the **privacy-utility trade-off** central to KTU questions.

### 3.2 Paillier Additive Homomorphism (Walkthrough)

**Setup:**
- Public key: $(n, g)$ where $n = pq$, $g \in \mathbb{Z}^*_{n^2}$.
- Plaintext: $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n$.
- Encryption of $m$: $E(m) = g^m \cdot r^n \bmod n^2$, where $r \in \mathbb{Z}^*_n$ is random.

**Property (Homomorphic Addition):**

$$
E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \bmod n^2 = g^{m_1} r_1^{n} \cdot g^{m_2} r_2^{n} \bmod n^2
$$

$$
= g^{m_1 + m_2} (r_1 r_2)^{n} \bmod n^2 = E(m_1 + m_2 \bmod n)
$$

**Property (Scalar Multiplication):**

$$
E(m)^{k} \bmod n^2 = g^{k m} r^{kn} \bmod n^2 = E(k \cdot m \bmod n)
$$

This enables, e.g., privacy-preserving aggregation: each smart meter encrypts its consumption $m_i$, the cloud computes $\prod_i E(m_i) = E(\sum_i m_i)$, and only the utility with the private key decrypts the **sum** — never individual readings.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Practical PETs

| PET | Adversary Model | Trust Assumption | Overhead | Maturity | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tor | Non-global passive | Honest relays | High latency (~sec) | ★★★★★ | Medium (VANETs) |
| Mixnets | Strong (even global) | Honest mix operators | Async, batched | ★★★★ | Medium |
| Idemix / BBS+ | Honest issuer | Trusted issuer setup | Moderate | ★★★★ | High (eID, IoT) |
| FHE | Malicious cloud | None (data-confidential) | Very high (1000×) | ★★★ | Medium |
| SMPC | Semi-honest / malicious | Honest majority | Communication-heavy | ★★★★ | High (grid, supply chain) |
| ZKP (zk-SNARK) | Malicious verifier | Trusted setup (or transparent) | Prover-heavy | ★★★★ | Medium |
| DP | Aggregator follows protocol | Aggregator is honest-but-curious | Statistical noise | ★★★★★ | Very high (analytics) |
| PIR | Curious DB server | None (cPIR) | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | ★★ | Medium |
| TEE (SGX) | Malicious OS / hypervisor | Hardware manufacturer | Low | ★★★★ | Very high (real-time) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: PET Taxonomy and Relationships

```mermaid

    A0[PETs in Practice] --> B0[Anonymity Oriented]

    E0 --> E1[Private Information Retrieval]

    E0 --> E3[Attribute Based Encryption]

    F0 --> F1[Intel SGX Enclaves]
    F0 --> F2[ARM TrustZone]
    F0 --> F3[Secure Hardware Modules]

    B1 --> G0[Use Case: VANET Privacy]
    B2 --> G1[Use Case: Anonymous Email]

    C3 --> G4[Use Case: Private Blockchain Tx]

```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Privacy-Preserving Smart Meter Aggregation (CPS Use-Case)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SM1[Smart Meter 1] -->|Encrypt Consumption| CLD
    SM2[Smart Meter 2] -->|Encrypt Consumption| CLD
    SM3[Smart Meter N] -->|Encrypt Consumption| CLD
    CLD[Cloud Aggregator: Paillier Homomorphic Sum] -->|Encrypted Sum| UTIL
    UTIL[Utility: Private Key Holder] -->|Decrypts Sum Only| BILL[Aggregate Bill / Grid Forecast]
    AD[(Adversary: Curious Cloud)] -.->|Sees Only Ciphertexts| CLD
    style CLD fill:#fff4cc
    style UTIL fill:#d4edda

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: DP Pipeline for Healthcare Analytics

```mermaid
graph LR
    RAW[Raw Patient Records] --> CUR[Data Curator]
    CUR --> QU[Statistical Query: f]
    QU --> NOI[Laplace Noise Injector scale equals delta f over epsilon]
    NOI --> OUT[Noisy Aggregate Statistic]
    RES[Researcher] --> QU
    RES -.->|Sees Only Noisy Output| OUT
    PRIV[(Privacy Guarantee: epsilon DP)] -.->|Binds| NOI
    style NOI fill:#cce5ff
    style PRIV fill:#d4edda
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Define Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs). List any two real-world examples of PETs currently in deployment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) are a class of tools, protocols, and systems designed to **protect personal data and identity** of individuals by minimizing the collection, exposure, and linkage of personally identifiable information during digital interactions. *(1 mark)*

> They address privacy at various stages — communication, computation, storage, and output — by applying cryptographic, statistical, or hardware-based techniques. *(1 mark)*

> **Two real-world examples:** *(1 mark)*
> 1. **Tor (The Onion Router)** — used for anonymous web browsing, employing layered encryption across volunteer relays.
> 2. **Differential Privacy** — used by Apple and the US Census Bureau to publish aggregate statistics without revealing individual records.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** Differentiate between Tor and Mix Networks as anonymous communication PETs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tor | Mix Networks |
|---|---|---|
| Routing | 3-hop onion routing, low-latency | Batched, high-latency, re-ordering |
| Adversary | Non-global passive | Global passive (stronger) |
| Use case | Real-time browsing | Email, asynchronous messaging |

*(1 mark for correct identification of each, 1 mark for at least two valid differences)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 2024 Equivalent Style]**

**(a)** Explain the operational mechanism of the **Laplace Mechanism** in Differential Privacy. Derive the noise scale required to achieve $\varepsilon$-differential privacy for a counting query with sensitivity $\Delta f = 1$ and $\varepsilon = 0.5$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss with a CPS use-case how **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** can be deployed in practice. What are the major performance bottlenecks? *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Laplace Mechanism Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of DP:** *(1 mark)*

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for all neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ (differing in at most one record) and all output sets $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Distribution:** *(1 mark)*

The Laplace distribution with scale $b > 0$ has density:

$$
\text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanism Definition:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Y, \quad Y \sim \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Privacy Proof:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = x]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = x]} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon(\vert x - f(D') \vert - \vert x - f(D) \vert)}{\Delta f}\right)
$$

Using the inequality $\vert a \vert - \vert b \vert \leq \vert a - b \vert$:

$$
\leq \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \cdot \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert}{\Delta f}\right) \leq \exp(\varepsilon)
$$

Hence $\varepsilon$-DP holds.

**Step 5 — Numerical Calculation:** *(1 mark)*

For $\Delta f = 1, \varepsilon = 0.5$:

$$
b = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2
$$

Therefore, noise drawn from $\text{Lap}(2)$ (standard deviation $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$) is added to each count.

**Step 6 — Utility Comment:** *(1 mark)*

Smaller $\varepsilon$ (stronger privacy) requires larger noise, reducing query accuracy — illustrating the **privacy–utility trade-off**.

---

**Part (b) — FHE in CPS (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** FHE permits arbitrary computation directly on ciphertexts: given $E(m_1), E(m_2)$, one can compute $E(f(m_1, m_2))$ for any computable $f$.

**CPS Use-Case — Encrypted Telemetry Analytics in Industrial IoT (3 marks):**

Consider a smart factory with 1000 PLCs reporting sensor data (temperature, vibration, pressure) to a cloud analytics platform. The factory owner wants:
- The cloud to run anomaly detection (e.g., a neural network).
- The cloud **must never see plaintext sensor values** (to protect proprietary process data and worker privacy).

Using FHE (e.g., CKKS scheme via Microsoft SEAL):
1. Each PLC encrypts its reading with the cloud's public key: $c_i = E(m_i)$.
2. The cloud evaluates a homomorphic neural network: $c_{\text{out}} = \text{EvalNet}(c_1, \dots, c_n)$.
3. Only the factory operator, holding the secret key, decrypts the anomaly score.

**Performance Bottlenecks (3 marks):**

| Bottleneck | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Ciphertext expansion** | Ciphertexts are 1000×–10000× larger than plaintexts. |
| **Computational cost** | Each homomorphic multiply consumes noise budget; bootstrapping is slow (seconds to minutes per refresh). |
| **Memory footprint** | Large polynomial rings (degree 4096–32768) require GB-scale RAM. |
| **Numerical precision** | Approximate schemes (CKKS) introduce rounding errors; exact schemes (BFV, BGV) are slower. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> - **Confusing FHE with SMPC** — FHE is single-key, single-server; SMPC uses multiple parties with secret sharing.
> - **Ignoring bootstrapping** — Without explaining how noise is "refreshed", the FHE claim is incomplete.
> - **Overstating maturity** — FHE is *not* yet production-ready for high-throughput CPS; mention hybrid deployments (FHE + TEE, or FHE + DP).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Tor Onion Routing** protocol. Explain how Tor provides sender anonymity and discuss its limitations against a global passive adversary. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Anonymous Credentials** (e.g., Idemix, BBS+). How do they differ from traditional PKI-based authentication in providing unlinkability and selective disclosure? *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Tor Onion Routing (7 marks):**

**Architecture (3 marks):**

```
[Client] --E1--> [Guard Relay] --E2--> [Middle Relay] --E3--> [Exit Relay] --> [Destination]
       ^              ^                     ^                     ^
   knows:        knows:                knows:               knows:
   - all 3 keys  - Guard, Middle       - Middle, Exit       - Exit, Dest
   - Destination - NOT destination     - NOT guard or dest  - NOT guard or middle
```

- Client constructs a 3-hop circuit.
- Message wrapped in **3 encryption layers** (E1, E2, E3).
- Each relay strips one layer, learning only the previous and next hop.

**Anonymity Guarantee (2 marks):**

- The Guard relay knows the client but not the destination.
- The Exit relay knows the destination but not the client.
- No single relay can correlate the two.

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Global Passive Adversary (GPA):** An adversary observing *all* network traffic (entry + exit) can perform **traffic correlation** and de-anonymize users.
2. **End-to-end timing attacks:** Statistical analysis of packet timing can break anonymity.
3. **Malicious Exit nodes:** If the destination is HTTP (unencrypted), the Exit sees plaintext.

---

**Part (b) — Anonymous Credentials (7 marks):**

**Traditional PKI Authentication (2 marks):**

- User presents certificate containing public key + identity attributes signed by a CA.
- Verifier checks signature and links all presentations via the same certificate (or serial number) → **linkable**.

**Anonymous Credentials Workflow (3 marks):**

1. **Issuance:** User obtains a credential from an Issuer via a blind signature (Issuer learns nothing about the resulting credential or its serial number).
2. **Show Protocol:** User proves possession of the credential in zero-knowledge.
3. **Selective Disclosure:** User can prove specific attributes only (e.g., "I am a student of ABC University") without revealing the full name or student ID.
4. **Unlinkability:** Two presentations of the same credential are computationally indistinguishable from two presentations of different credentials.

**Schemes (2 marks):**

- **Idemix (Identity Mixer):** Uses Camenisch-Lysyanskaya (CL) signatures; supports multi-show unlinkability.
- **BBS+ Signatures:** Modern pairing-based scheme used in verifiable credentials (W3C VC), mDLs (mobile driving licenses), and EU eIDAS-2.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | PKI Cert | Anonymous Credential |
|---|---|---|
| Linkability | Linked via serial | Unlinkable across shows |
| Selective disclosure | Full attribute disclosure | Per-attribute proofs |
| Issuer learns user identity | Yes | No (blind issuance) |
| Revocation | CRL/OCSP | Accumulators, ZK-revocation |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Patterns in PECPT743 ESE:**
> 1. **Skipping the adversary model** — Always state the threat model *before* describing the PET. (–1 mark)
> 2. **No formal/mathematical backing** — "Differential privacy adds noise" alone is incomplete. State $\varepsilon$ and the mechanism. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing primitives** — Tor ≠ Mixnets; FHE ≠ SMPC; ZKP ≠ DP. Examiners specifically test this confusion. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring real-world feasibility** — Always mention at least one *practical* deployment, library, or system. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Missing CPS context** — This is *Privacy in CPS*, not generic cryptography. Tie examples to IoT, smart grid, VANETs, or industrial control. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Examples of PETs in Practice"**

- ✅ **Definition:** PETs are *deployable* systems enforcing confidentiality, anonymity, unlinkability, and untraceability against a defined adversary.
- ✅ **Categories (6):** Anonymous Communication, Anonymous Authentication, Cryptographic Computation (HE/SMPC/ZKP), Statistical Privacy (DP), Data Minimization (PIR/ORAM), Hardware-rooted (TEEs).
- ✅ **Tor** — 3-hop onion routing; vulnerable to global passive adversary and traffic correlation.
- ✅ **Mixnets** — Batched, re-ordered, latency-tolerant; stronger anonymity than Tor.
- ✅ **Anonymous Credentials (Idemix, BBS+)** — Unlinkable multi-show authentication with selective disclosure.
- ✅ **Homomorphic Encryption:**
  - PHE (Paillier, RSA) — one operation.
  - FHE (CKKS, BFV, BGV) — arbitrary computation; heavy bootstrapping cost.
- ✅ **SMPC** — Joint computation via secret sharing / garbled circuits; honest-majority threshold.
- ✅ **ZKP** — Completeness + Soundness + Zero-Knowledge; zk-SNARKs (Groth16) for blockchain privacy.
- ✅ **Differential Privacy** — $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$; sequential composition is additive.
- ✅ **PIR** — IT-PIR $O(n)$, cPIR $O(\sqrt{n})$.
- ✅ **TEEs** — Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone; hardware-rooted confidentiality.
- ✅ **CPS Use-Cases to Remember:**
  - Smart meter aggregation (Paillier).
  - VANET privacy (Tor / Mixnets).
  - Healthcare analytics (FHE + DP).
  - Confidential PLC execution (TEE).
  - Smart grid forecasting (SMPC).

> **End of Notes — Module 1, PECPT743 — KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques for Data Anonymization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Techniques for Data Anonymization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Anonymization** is a set of irreversible, systematic transformation techniques applied to Personally Identifiable Information (PII) in datasets such that the re-identification of an individual becomes mathematically infeasible, while preserving the statistical utility and analytical validity of the data for legitimate secondary use. In the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), anonymization constitutes the foundational Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) layer mandated for data sharing between sensors, edge nodes, and cloud analytics platforms under frameworks such as GDPR Article 4(5) and the KTU PECPT743 2024 Scheme syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to memorize verbatim:**
> *Data Anonymization refers to the process of removing or modifying personally identifying information from datasets so that the data subject can no longer be identified directly or indirectly, either alone or in combination with other data.*

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Anonymization in CPS

| Pillar | Function | CPS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Identifiers removal** | Eliminate explicit fields (name, SSN) | Removing patient name from ECG telemetry |
| **Quasi-identifier generalization** | Reduce granularity of indirect fields | Mapping exact GPS (12.9716°N) → City (Bangalore) |
| **Data perturbation** | Inject statistical noise | Adding Laplace noise to smart-meter readings |
| **Pseudonymization support** | Replace IDs with reversible tokens | Replacing device UUID with hash |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Library Card Anonymizer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a librarian who has records of every book borrowed by every student. To protect student privacy while letting researchers study reading trends, the librarian does the following:
> 1. **Strips the name** (direct identifier) → like removing the cover label.
> 2. **Generalizes the class** (quasi-identifier): "12-A" becomes "Class 12" → like blurring the year.
> 3. **Adds small noise to the number of books borrowed** (perturbation) → ±1 book.
>
> Now, the researcher can still determine that "Class 12 students borrowed more novels than Class 9," but **no one can reverse-engineer** that *Ravi from 12-A borrowed exactly 7 books*. This is **anonymization in action**.

## 1.4 Classification of Anonymization Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major Families of Anonymization Techniques (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **Non-Cryptographic / Statistical Techniques:** k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, t-Closeness, Generalization, Suppression, Perturbation, Swapping, Microaggregation.
> - **Cryptographic & Formal Techniques:** Differential Privacy, Homomorphic Encryption, Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC), Tokenization, Hashing.
>
> KTU examiners **frequently** ask students to classify a given technique under the correct family. Memorize this binary split.

## 1.5 Why Anonymization is Critical in CPS

In a Cyber-Physical System, sensor data continuously leaks information about the human operator. For example:
- **Smart meter readings** → reveal when a person wakes up, cooks, sleeps.
- **Wearable heart-rate data** → reveals stress, illness, pregnancy.
- **Connected car GPS** → reveals home, workplace, religious/political visits.

Anonymization ensures this data can be **shared for analytics, ML training, and research** without violating the individual's privacy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Techniques

### 2.1.1 Generalization (Attribute Suppression Hierarchy)

Generalization reduces the granularity of quasi-identifier attributes by mapping specific values to broader semantic categories.

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Identify the quasi-identifier set **QI = {q₁, q₂, ..., qₙ}**.
2. Define a generalization hierarchy (Domain Generalization Hierarchy – DGH) for each qi.
3. Replace the original value with a value at a higher (less specific) level in the hierarchy.
4. Verify that the resulting table satisfies the target anonymity property (e.g., k-anonymity).

**Example Hierarchy for Age:**
- 25 → 20–29 → 20–39 → [0, 100] (numeric generalization)
- "Kerala" → "South India" → "Asia" → "Earth" (geographic)

### 2.1.2 Suppression (Record Suppression)

When generalization is insufficient, specific cells (`cell suppression`) or entire records (`record suppression`) are replaced with the special symbol `*`.

### 2.1.3 Perturbation (Noise Injection)

Replaces original values with synthetic ones such that:
- **Individual records** are altered.
- **Aggregate statistical properties** (mean, variance, correlation) are preserved.

**Two principal methods:**
- **Additive Noise:** X' = X + ε, where ε is drawn from a known distribution.
- **Multiplicative Noise:** X' = X · ε.

### 2.1.4 Data Swapping

Values of sensitive attributes are exchanged between records in the same equivalence class. Original individual records are no longer reconstructible, but the marginal distribution remains intact.

### 2.1.5 Microaggregation

Records are partitioned into homogeneous groups of at least k records, and each group's values are replaced with the group mean (or centroid).

### 2.1.6 Tokenization

A sensitive field (e.g., Aadhaar number) is replaced with a non-sensitive surrogate value (token). The mapping is held in a secure **token vault**.

### 2.1.7 Differential Privacy (DP)

A formal mathematical guarantee that the inclusion or exclusion of any single individual from the dataset does not substantially change the output of any query. This is the **gold standard** of modern anonymization.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Technique | Core Formula / Condition | Variables & Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **k-Anonymity** | For every record, at least **k−1** other records share the same quasi-identifier values. | k = anonymity threshold (integer ≥ 2) | HIPAA-compliant medical data release |
| 2 | **l-Diversity** | Each equivalence class has ≥ **l** "well-represented" values for the sensitive attribute. | l = diversity parameter | Prevents homogeneity / skewness attack |
| 3 | **t-Closeness** | Distance between distribution of sensitive attribute in class E and overall distribution ≤ **t**. | t ∈ [0, 1]; common metric: Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) | Mitigates attribute disclosure |
| 4 | **Differential Privacy (ε-DP)** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | ε = privacy budget; D, D' differ in one record | Census data, Google/Apple telemetry |
| 5 | **Laplace Mechanism (DP)** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | Δf = sensitivity; Lap(b) = $\frac{1}{2b}e^{-\vert x \vert / b}$ | Real-time aggregate CPS analytics |
| 6 | **Gaussian Mechanism (DP)** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \sigma^2\right),\ \sigma \ge \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | δ = failure probability (relaxation) | (ε, δ)-DP for high-dimensional data |
| 7 | **Microaggregation (k-Anon)** | Replace group values with: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i$ | k = group size | EU statistical agency data |
| 8 | **Additive Perturbation** | $X' = X + \varepsilon,\quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | σ = noise std. deviation | Industrial IoT sensor data sharing |
| 9 | **Information Loss (Precision)** | $\text{IL} = \frac{1}{N \cdot d}\sum_{i,j} \frac{\vert x'_{ij} - x_{ij} \vert}{\max(x_j) - \min(x_j)}$ | d = number of attributes | Quality benchmark metric |
| 10 | **Re-identification Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \frac{1}{\vert \text{Equivalence Class} \vert}$ | Lower bound = 1/k | Compliance auditing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Reminder for KTU Boards:** Always write `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` in formulas — **never** the bare `|` symbol, since it breaks the markdown table parser and may also be misinterpreted in PDF rendering.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Technique

- **Why k-Anonymity?** Because in 1997, Latanya Sweeney re-identified the Massachusetts Governor from "anonymized" hospital records using ZIP + DOB + Sex. k-Anonymity prevents such linkage attacks.
- **Why l-Diversity?** Because k-Anonymity fails when all k records in a class share the *same* sensitive value (homogeneity attack). l-Diversity forces variety.
- **Why t-Closeness?** Because l-Diversity can still leak *background knowledge* (e.g., "Japanese rarely have heart disease") — t-Closeness bounds the distribution gap.
- **Why DP?** Because it provides a **provable, composable, future-proof** guarantee — independent of attacker background knowledge. It is the only technique that survives auxiliary-information attacks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Anonymization Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Smart Grid (CPS)** | Differential privacy on smart-meter aggregate load to hide individual household profiles. |
| **Healthcare (IoT)** | k-Anonymity + l-Diversity on patient telemetry shared with insurance analytics. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Tokenization of driver ID in V2X communication logs. |
| **Smart Cities** | Geographic generalization on citizen mobility traces. |
| **Industrial IoT** | Microaggregation of factory KPIs shared with regulators. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: k-Anonymity Equivalence Class Definition

**Starting Point (Definition):**
A dataset D is k-anonymous if, for every tuple t ∈ D, the number of tuples in D that share the same quasi-identifier (QI) values as t is at least k.

**Step 1 — Formally define the equivalence class.**
For any tuple t, the equivalence class of t under the QI-projection is:
$$E(t) \;=\; \big\{\, t' \in D \;\big|\; t'[\text{QI}] = t[\text{QI}] \,\big\}$$

**Step 2 — State the cardinality constraint.**
The k-anonymity condition requires:
$$\forall\, t \in D \;:\; \big\vert E(t) \big\vert \;\ge\; k$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the bound.**
- If $k = 1$, the dataset is trivially identifiable (no anonymity).
- If $k = 5$, every individual is indistinguishable from at least 4 others.
- If $k = 100$, very strong anonymity, but high information loss.

**Step 4 — Information Loss Metric (Precision).**
For numeric QI values, the information loss after generalization is:
$$\text{IL} \;=\; \frac{1}{N \cdot d} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{\big\vert x'_{ij} - x_{ij} \big\vert}{\max(x_j) - \min(x_j)}$$

This formula uses $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) instead of the bare pipe symbol, preserving markdown table compatibility.

**Step 5 — Apply the trade-off.**
As k increases, the equivalence class size increases, so generalization must coarser, so IL increases. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Discuss the privacy-utility trade-off in k-anonymity."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Privacy Laplace Mechanism

**Starting Point (Goal):**
We want to publish the result of a query $f(D)$ on a private dataset D, while satisfying ε-differential privacy.

**Step 1 — Global Sensitivity of the query.**
The L1 (or L2) global sensitivity is defined as:
$$\Delta f \;=\; \max_{D, D'} \big\Vert f(D) - f(D') \big\Vert_{1}$$
where D and D' differ on exactly one record. The function $\Vert \cdot \Vert_1$ denotes the L1 norm, and the absolute difference inside is $\vert f_i(D) - f_i(D') \vert$.

**Step 2 — Laplace Distribution PDF.**
The Laplace distribution with scale b is:
$$\text{Lap}(x \mid b) \;=\; \frac{1}{2b} \, e^{-\vert x \vert / b}$$

**Step 3 — The Mechanism.**
The Laplace Mechanism adds i.i.d. Laplace noise scaled to $\Delta f / \varepsilon$:
$$\mathcal{M}_{\text{Lap}}(D) \;=\; f(D) \;+\; \text{Lap}\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Why does this give ε-DP?**
Take two neighboring datasets D, D'. Let $f(D) - f(D') = \delta$ where $\Vert \delta \Vert_1 \le \Delta f$. For any output y:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = y]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = y]}
&= \frac{\frac{\varepsilon}{2\Delta f} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \vert y - f(D) \vert}{\Delta f}\right)}{\frac{\varepsilon}{2\Delta f} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\varepsilon \vert y - f(D') \vert}{\Delta f}\right)} \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \big( \vert y - f(D') \vert - \vert y - f(D) \vert \big)}{\Delta f}\right) \\[4pt]
&\le \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \cdot \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_{1}}{\Delta f}\right) \\[4pt]
&\le \exp(\varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
The ratio of output probabilities on neighboring datasets is at most $e^{\varepsilon}$. The smaller ε is, the stronger the privacy guarantee. Standard KTU choices are ε ∈ {0.1, 1, ln 3}. This completes the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Microaggregation Group Mean

**Step 1 — Partition dataset D into g groups G₁, G₂, ..., G_g, each of size ≥ k.**
$$\bigcup_{j=1}^{g} G_{j} = D, \qquad G_{a} \cap G_{b} = \varnothing,\quad a \ne b,\qquad \big\vert G_j \big\vert \ge k$$

**Step 2 — For each group G_j, compute the per-attribute mean (or centroid).**
$$\bar{x}_{j\ell} \;=\; \frac{1}{\big\vert G_j \big\vert} \sum_{i \in G_{j}} x_{i\ell}, \quad \text{for attribute } \ell$$

**Step 3 — Replace every original value in G_j with the group mean.**
$$x'_{i\ell} \;=\; \bar{x}_{j\ell}, \qquad \forall\, i \in G_{j}$$

**Step 4 — Anonymity guarantee.**
Because every released record equals the group mean, any record is indistinguishable from at least k − 1 others in its group, giving a k-anonymity-like guarantee. Note: the mean alone does not guarantee l-diversity; both are usually applied together.

**Step 5 — Variance preserved?**
The within-group variance is reduced to zero for released values, but the global variance and mean are preserved for sufficiently homogeneous groupings, supporting statistical utility.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Anonymization Pipeline in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw CPS Sensor Data] --> B[Direct Identifier Removal]
    B --> C{QI Attributes Detected?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Generalization or Suppression]
    C -- No --> E[Apply Statistical Perturbation]
    D --> F{k-Anonymity Satisfied?}
    F -- No --> G[Increase Generalization Depth]
    G --> F
    F -- Yes --> H{l-Diversity Satisfied?}
    H -- No --> I[Apply Microaggregation or Swap]
    I --> H
    H -- Yes --> J[Add Laplace Noise for Differential Privacy]
    J --> K[Release Anonymized Dataset]
    E --> J
    K --> L[Audit Re-identification Risk]
    L --> M{Privacy Budget OK?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Publish to Analytics Layer]
    M -- No --> O[Recalibrate Noise Scale]
    O --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block (A, B, C, …) is a processing stage. Decision diamonds (C, F, H, M) test the satisfaction of a privacy property. Loops (G→F, O→J) indicate iterative tightening of the privacy guarantee. This is the canonical KTU board diagram for the data-anonymization pipeline.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: k-Anonymity Pre-processing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        I1[Patient ID]
        I2[Age]
        I3[ZIP]
        I4[Disease]
    end
    subgraph QI[Quasi-Identifier Set]
        Q1[Age]
        Q2[ZIP]
    end
    subgraph SA[Sensitive Attribute]
        S1[Disease]
    end
    subgraph ANON[Anonymization Engine]
        GEN1[Generalization: Age → Range]
        GEN2[Generalization: ZIP → Prefix]
        SUPC[Suppression of Rare Records]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Table]
        O1[ID Removed]
        O2[Age Range]
        O3[ZIP Prefix]
        O4[Disease]
    end
    I1 --> SUPC
    I2 --> GEN1
    I3 --> GEN2
    I4 --> O4
    GEN1 --> O2
    GEN2 --> O3
    SUPC --> O1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Differential Privacy Composition Across Multiple Queries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Query 1: ε1 = 0.5] --> C1[Composition Rule]
    Q2[Query 2: ε2 = 0.3] --> C1
    Q3[Query 3: ε3 = 0.2] --> C1
    C1{Composition Mode}
    C1 -- Basic Sequential --> R1[Total ε = 1.0]
    C1 -- Advanced RDP --> R2[Total ε ≈ 0.78]
    C1 -- Parallel --> R3[Total ε = max εi = 0.5]
    R1 --> AUD[Privacy Budget Audit]
    R2 --> AUD
    R3 --> AUD
    AUD{Budget Exceeded?}
    AUD -- No --> PUB[Publish Results]
    AUD -- Yes --> BLK[Block Query / Inject More Noise]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block: Classical vs. Modern Anonymization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLASS[Classical Techniques]
        K1[k-Anonymity]
        L1[l-Diversity]
        T1[t-Closeness]
    end
    subgraph MOD[Modern Formal Techniques]
        D1[Differential Privacy]
        H1[Homomorphic Encryption]
        S1[Secure MPC]
    end
    CLASS --> P1[Property-based: 1 / k]
    MOD --> P2[Guarantee-based: e^ε]
    P1 --> LIM[Limitation: Vulnerable to Auxiliary Info]
    P2 --> STR[Strength: Future-proof]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between data anonymization and data pseudonymization with an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Anonymization (1 mark):** An irreversible transformation of PII such that re-identification is no longer possible by any means reasonably likely to be used. Once anonymized, the data falls outside GDPR's scope. *Example:* Generalizing a patient's date of birth to "year of birth" or stripping names from a survey.
- **Pseudonymization (1 mark):** A reversible replacement of direct identifiers with tokens, with the mapping kept separately in a secure vault. It still constitutes personal data under GDPR. *Example:* Replacing an Aadhaar number with a UUID stored in a token vault.
- **Key Difference (1 mark):** Anonymization is **irreversible** and removes PII; pseudonymization is **reversible** with the token map and only **reduces** identifiability.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the homogeneity attack in k-anonymity? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A homogeneity attack exploits the fact that all k records within a k-anonymous equivalence class may share the *same* value for a sensitive attribute, allowing an attacker to deduce that attribute without needing to identify the individual precisely.
- **Example (2 marks):** Consider a k-anonymous table (k = 4) where a group of 4 patients all share `{Age: 30–39, ZIP: 682001–682099}` and *all four* have the sensitive attribute "HIV-positive." Even without knowing the exact name, the attacker — who knows the target is in this group — can conclude with certainty that the target has HIV.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.A.(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **k-Anonymity model** in detail. Describe generalization and suppression as the two primary techniques used to achieve k-anonymity, with a worked example on a medical dataset.

**Q.A.(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **limitations of k-Anonymity**, including homogeneity and background-knowledge attacks, and explain how **l-Diversity** and **t-Closeness** overcome these limitations.

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(a)
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** A dataset D is k-anonymous if every tuple t in D is indistinguishable from at least k − 1 other tuples on the quasi-identifier set QI. Formally, $|E(t)| \ge k$ for all t.
2. **QI and Sensitive Attribute (1 mark):** Direct identifiers (name, SSN) are removed. Quasi-identifiers (age, ZIP, sex) are generalized. Sensitive attributes (disease) are left intact.
3. **Generalization (1.5 marks):** Replace fine-grained QI values with coarser ones — e.g., age `29` → `20–29`, ZIP `682001` → `682***`.
4. **Suppression (1.5 marks):** Replace outlier values with `*` to ensure every equivalence class reaches size k.
5. **Worked Example (1.5 marks):**
   - Original table has 5 patients. Apply age-generalization and ZIP-prefix-suppression.
   - Result: all 5 records form one equivalence class with QI `(20–40, 682***)`.
   - Hence, the table is **5-anonymous**.

#### Model Solution — Q.A.(b)
1. **Homogeneity Attack (1.5 marks):** If all k records in a class share the same sensitive value, the attacker learns it with certainty.
2. **Background-Knowledge Attack (1.5 marks):** Even with diverse sensitive values, the attacker may know the target's prior condition and infer the answer (e.g., "If Japanese, low heart-disease risk").
3. **l-Diversity Remedy (2 marks):** Force every equivalence class to contain at least l "well-represented" sensitive values. Eliminates homogeneity but not skewness.
4. **t-Closeness Remedy (2 marks):** Force the distribution of the sensitive attribute within each class to be at Earth-Mover distance ≤ t from the global distribution. Eliminates skewness attack.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.B.(a)** [7 Marks] Explain **Differential Privacy** with its formal mathematical definition. Derive the **Laplace Mechanism** and show how it satisfies ε-differential privacy.

**Q.B.(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **differential privacy** with **k-anonymity** in terms of (i) privacy guarantee, (ii) assumption about attacker, (iii) composition under multiple queries, and (iv) resistance to auxiliary-information attacks.

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(a)
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-DP if for all neighboring datasets D, D' (differing in one record) and all S ⊆ Range(M):
   $$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S].$$
2. **Privacy Budget ε (1 mark):** Smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy. Typical KTU values: 0.1, 1, ln 2.
3. **Global Sensitivity (1 mark):** $\Delta f = \max_{D,D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert_1$.
4. **Laplace Mechanism (1.5 marks):** $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
5. **Derivation of ε-DP (2 marks):** Using the Laplace PDF, compute the ratio of output probabilities:
   $$\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = y]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = y]} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon \cdot (\vert y - f(D') \vert - \vert y - f(D) \vert)}{\Delta f}\right) \le e^{\varepsilon}.$$

#### Model Solution — Q.B.(b)
1. **Privacy Guarantee (1.75 marks):** k-Anonymity gives "1/k" re-identification probability; DP gives "$e^{\varepsilon}$" multiplicative bound — **stronger and more rigorous** in DP.
2. **Attacker Assumption (1.75 marks):** k-Anonymity assumes the attacker has *only* the released data; DP makes **no such assumption** — the bound holds against attackers with arbitrary auxiliary information.
3. **Composition (1.75 marks):** Under k-Anonymity, releasing two k-anonymous views can leak; under DP, sequential composition gives total ε_total = Σε_i, advanced RDP composition gives logarithmic improvement.
4. **Auxiliary Info (1.75 marks):** k-Anonymity is **vulnerable** to linkage + auxiliary attacks (Sweeney 1997, Netflix 2006); DP is **provably immune** to any background knowledge.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing anonymization with pseudonymization (–1 to –2 marks).** Memorize: anonymization is *irreversible*, pseudonymization is *reversible*.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` instead of `$\vert x \vert$` in formula sheets (–0.5 to –1 mark).** The bare pipe breaks markdown/PDF rendering and examiners mark it as a presentation error.
> 3. **Forgetting to specify k ≥ 2** in k-anonymity answers (–1 mark). k = 1 is the trivial case, not anonymity.
> 4. **Not stating the privacy budget ε** when defining differential privacy (–1 mark). The definition is *incomplete* without ε.
> 5. **Mixing up l-Diversity and t-Closeness (–1 mark).** l-Diversity is about *variety* of sensitive values; t-Closeness is about *distribution* of sensitive values.
> 6. **Skipping the privacy-utility trade-off discussion** in long answers (–1 to –2 marks). KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests Outcome-Based Education — the trade-off is a Course Outcome (CO) mapper.
> 7. **Omitting a worked example** in 14-mark answers (–1 mark). KTU board examiners expect at least one numerical or tabular illustration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Anonymization = irreversible privacy; Pseudonymization = reversible token replacement.**
- **Two main families:** (i) Statistical/Property-based (k-Anon, l-Div, t-Close, Generalization, Suppression, Microaggregation, Swapping) and (ii) Cryptographic/Formal (DP, HE, SMPC).
- **k-Anonymity bound:** $|E(t)| \ge k$, with the privacy-utility trade-off worsening as k increases.
- **l-Diversity fixes homogeneity; t-Closeness fixes skewness.** t-Closeness uses Earth-Mover's Distance.
- **Differential Privacy:** $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \le e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$.
- **Laplace Mechanism:** $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$. **Gaussian Mechanism:** for $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP.
- **Privacy budget ε composes:** sequential ⇒ additive, parallel ⇒ max, advanced ⇒ sub-linear (RDP/zCDP).
- **Generalization uses Domain Generalization Hierarchies (DGH); Suppression uses `*` for cells/records.**
- **Microaggregation replaces each group of k records with the group mean ⇒ satisfies k-anonymity-like property.**
- **Perturbation preserves aggregate statistics, not individual records.**
- **Tokenization needs a secure token vault; hashing is one-way but vulnerable to rainbow tables.**
- **For CPS applications:** smart meters, wearables, V2X, smart cities — DP is the modern preferred technique because it tolerates auxiliary information.
- **Common equation rendering tip:** always use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \vert$` in LaTeX — never bare `|x|`.
- **Quick-recall formula card:** $1/k$ for k-Anonymity, $e^{\varepsilon}$ for DP, $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ for noise.
- **KTU favourite exam hook:** *"Discuss privacy-utility trade-off in k-anonymity"* and *"Why is DP the gold standard?"* — always write the trade-off paragraph.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Masking Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Masking Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Masking** (also known as **Data Obfuscation** or **Data Anonymization at Rest**) is a privacy-preserving technique used in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and enterprise databases that replaces sensitive, confidential, or Personally Identifiable Information (PII) with structurally similar but inauthentic substitute values. The primary objective is to ensure that data remains usable for non-production environments (such as testing, development, training, analytics, and outsourcing) while preventing unauthorized access to the original sensitive information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Data masking is the process of hiding original data with modified content (such as characters or numbers) to protect sensitive information from unauthorized viewing, while preserving the data type, format, and referential integrity required for downstream business operations."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **movie director** shooting a film. You need an actual-looking passport, credit card, and Aadhaar number to display on camera — but you obviously do **not** want the cast to walk around with **your real** passport, or for the audience to see your real credit card number broadcast on national television.

So what does the props department do? They create a **realistic-looking replica**:
- The passport still says "Republic of India" with the same layout, font, and field structure.
- The credit card still has **16 digits** in the same groupings (XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-XXXX).
- The Aadhaar number still has exactly **12 digits**.

But every single piece of **sensitive information** is **fake, scrambled, or substituted**. The camera still gets a fully "functional-looking" prop, the actor can still "use" it, but no real-world PII is leaked.

> That prop-room passport **is** data masking. The **structure** is preserved. The **content** is fake. The **risk** is zero.

---

### 1.3 Why Data Masking is Critical in CPS

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU Board Context:**
> In Cyber-Physical Systems, data flows continuously between physical sensors, edge devices, cloud servers, and analytics platforms. A breach at *any* point can expose terabytes of sensitive industrial, medical, or personal data. Data masking ensures that even if a non-production environment is compromised, the attacker receives **valueless replicas**, not live data.

Key drivers for masking in CPS:
- **Regulatory Compliance** — GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India's DPDP Act 2023.
- **Insider Threat Mitigation** — Developers, testers, and outsourcing vendors do not need real data.
- **Safe Cloud Migration** — Masked data can be safely moved to public clouds.
- **Test Environment Realism** — Applications behave realistically only if data types/lengths are preserved.

---

### 1.4 Classification Overview of Data Masking Methods

| Category | Methods | Reversibility | Typical Use Case |
|----------|---------|---------------|------------------|
| **Static (SDM)** | Substitution, Shuffling, Nulling, Variance | Usually Irreversible | Test/Dev DB copies |
| **Dynamic (DDM)** | Policy-based real-time masking | Reversible by privileged users | Production DB access |
| **Deterministic** | Lookup Tables, Tokenization | Reversible via vault | Analytics |
| **Algorithmic** | Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE) | Reversible with key | Compliance |
| **Statistical** | k-Anonymity, Differential Privacy | Irreversible | Big data publishing |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Masking Methods — Operational Breakdown

#### **A. Substitution**
- Replaces the original value with a value from a **predefined lookup table** or a randomly generated value of the same type.
- *Example:* "Rahul" → "Aman"; `9876543210` → `8765432109`
- **Why it works:** Maintains data distribution and referential integrity across joined tables.

#### **B. Shuffling (Permutation)**
- Randomly **rearranges** values within a column across rows.
- The dataset still has "real-looking" names/values, but the *association* between an individual and their attribute is broken.
- *Example:* Row 1: "Rahul", Row 2: "Priya", Row 3: "Aman" → Permuted: Row 1: "Priya", Row 2: "Aman", Row 3: "Rahul"

#### **C. Number & Date Variance**
- Applies a **random delta** (±offset) to numeric or date values.
- *Example:* Salary `₹5,00,000` → `₹4,87,340`; DOB `1990-05-12` → `1989-11-23`
- Preserves the **statistical distribution** (mean, variance) of the original column.

#### **D. Masking Out (Character Replacement)**
- Replaces characters with a fixed mask symbol (commonly `X` or `*`), keeping only partial visible characters.
- *Example:* `9876543210` → `XXXXXX3210`; Email `rahul@gmail.com` → `rXXXl@gXXX.com`

#### **E. Nulling / Blanking**
- Replaces the sensitive value with `NULL` or empty strings.
- Most aggressive; **breaks** business logic in test environments, so rarely used alone.

#### **F. Tokenization**
- Replaces the original value with a **non-sensitive placeholder (token)**. The mapping `original → token` is securely stored in a separate **token vault**.
- *Example:* `4111-1111-1111-1111` → `tok_8aF3bZ91` (stored in vault)
- **Reversible only by the vault** — true two-way transformation.

#### **G. Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE)**
- Encryption algorithm that produces a **ciphertext in the same format** as the plaintext.
- *Example:* A 16-digit credit card `4111111111111111` → `4421435890126789` (still 16 digits, still Luhn-valid)
- Standard: **NIST FF1, FF3, FF3-1**.

#### **H. Pseudonymization**
- Replaces identifying fields with **pseudonyms** (artificial identifiers). Similar to substitution but typically **deterministic** (same input → same output every time).
- Required as an explicit safeguard under **GDPR Article 32**.

#### **I. Data Aging (Date Shifting)**
- Shifts all date values by a **fixed offset** (e.g., +45 days) so temporal relationships within records are preserved, but absolute timestamps are hidden.

#### **J. Lookup Transformation**
- The most common form of substitution. Uses a **precomputed lookup table** seeded with valid substitute values.
- Deterministic and supports consistent masking across multiple datasets.

#### **K. Expression Masking**
- Applies a **mathematical expression** to derive the masked value: `masked = f(original)`.
- *Example:* `masked_salary = (salary MOD 10000) + 50000`

#### **L. Key Masking**
- A specialized FPE variant for encryption keys. Only a **portion** of the key is masked (e.g., last 4 bytes zeroed) to allow validation but prevent key extraction.

---

### 2.2 Static vs Dynamic Data Masking

> [!NOTE]
> **SDM vs DDM — High-Yield Board Distinction:**

| Feature | Static Data Masking (SDM) | Dynamic Data Masking (DDM) |
|---------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| When masking happens | **At rest** — masked copy is created and stored | **At query time** — masking applied in real-time to the response |
| Original data location | Moved / replaced in a separate DB | **Never leaves the production database** |
| Performance impact | None on production | Slight overhead per query |
| Reversibility | Usually irreversible | Masked on-the-fly, original recoverable by privileged role |
| Use case | Dev/Test/Outsourcing | Role-based access in production (e.g., call center agent vs. manager) |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | Substitution (random) | $M(x_i) = R_i$ where $R_i \sim U(\Sigma)$ | $M$ = mask function, $R_i$ = random substitute |
| 2 | Shuffling (column permutation) | $M(X) = \pi(X)$ for a random permutation $\pi$ | $\pi$ over the column domain |
| 3 | Numeric Variance | $M(x) = x + \delta$ where $\delta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Gaussian noise injection |
| 4 | Date Aging | $M(d) = d + \Delta$ where $\Delta$ is a fixed offset (days) | Constant shift |
| 5 | Tokenization (vault) | $M(x) = T_{\text{id}}$ and $x = V[T_{\text{id}}]$ | $V$ = secure token vault |
| 6 | FPE (NIST FF1) | $E_K(x) = \text{FF1}(K, x, \text{tweak})$ | $K$ = symmetric key |
| 7 | Masking Out (k of n chars) | $M(x) = x[1..k] \cdot \text{mask}^{n-k}$ | Partial reveal |
| 8 | Pseudonymization (deterministic) | $M(x) = H_K(x)$ | $H_K$ = keyed hash (HMAC) |
| 9 | Luhn Validation (credit card format) | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \equiv 0 \pmod{10}$ | $w_i$ = weighted digits |
| 10 | k-Anonymity Compliance | Every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others | $\vert Q^\star \vert \geq k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These methods are deployed in real-time at the **Edge layer of CPS** (e.g., in MQTT brokers to mask sensor telemetry before forwarding to cloud), in **SCADA HMI** applications, in **cloud data lakes** for test data provisioning, and in **ETL pipelines** feeding Machine Learning models.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE-FF1)

The **NIST FF1** algorithm is the gold standard for FPE. We derive its mathematical backbone for a numeric string of length $n$.

**Goal:** Given a plaintext integer $X \in \mathbb{Z}_N$ (where $N = 10^n$ for $n$-digit numbers), produce a ciphertext $Y \in \mathbb{Z}_N$ of the same length, encrypted under key $K$ and tweak $T$.

**Step 1 — Split into Halves**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= X \mod 10^{\lceil n/2 \rceil} \\
B &= \lfloor X / 10^{\lceil n/2 \rceil} \rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

So $X = A \cdot 10^{\lceil n/2 \rceil} + B$, with $\vert A \vert = \lceil n/2 \rceil$ and $\vert B \vert = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ digits.

**Step 2 — Feistel Round (rounds = 10)**

For $i = 1$ to $10$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_i &= A_{i-1} \\
A_i &= B_{i-1} \oplus \text{F}_K(B_{i-1}, T, i) \mod 10^{\lceil n/2 \rceil}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\text{F}_K$ is a PRF (typically AES-CBC-MAC).

**Step 3 — Ciphertext Assembly**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{10} &\rightarrow A_{\text{out}} \\
B_{10} &\rightarrow B_{\text{out}} \\
Y &= A_{\text{out}} \cdot 10^{\lfloor n/2 \rfloor} + B_{\text{out}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Format Check**
$$
Y \in [0, 10^n - 1] \Rightarrow \text{format preserved}
$$

> **Reasoning:** The Feistel structure is **invertible** — to decrypt, you run the rounds in **reverse order** with the same $K$ and $T$, recovering $X$ exactly. This is why FPE is **reversible with a key** but appears random without one.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Numeric Variance Masking (Statistical)
