Omitting this loses 1-2 marks. > 5. **Diffie-Hellman without authentication:** If you describe DH but omit the MITM vulnerability and the fix (signed DH or certificates), expect to lose 2 marks — this is a high-yield point. > 6. **Hash vs Encryption confusion:** Hashing is **one-way** (no decryption possible). Writing "decrypt the hash" in a Part A answer is a fundamental conceptual error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fundamentals of Encryption (Module 2):** ### **Core Conceptual Points** - ☐ Encryption transforms **plaintext → ciphertext** using a cipher and key; decryption reverses it. - ☐ **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** Security must reside in the key, not in the secrecy of the algorithm. - ☐ **CIA Triad:** Encryption provides **confidentiality**; hashes provide **integrity**; digital signatures provide **authentication + non-repudiation**. ### **Symmetric Encryption** - ☐ Same key for both operations; $C = E_K(P)$. - ☐ **AES** is the modern standard — 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit keys → 10/12/14 rounds. - ☐ AES is hardware-accelerated (AES-NI) and extremely fast. - ☐ Key distribution is the Achilles' heel — solved by hybrid encryption with DH/ECDH. ### **Asymmetric Encryption** - ☐ **Key pair:** Public key (PU) for encryption, Private key (PR) for decryption. - ☐ **RSA** is based on integer factorization; $n = pq$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. - ☐ RSA is slow — used for **key exchange and signatures**, not bulk data. - ☐ **ECC** (Elliptic Curve) provides equivalent security with smaller keys (P-256 ≈ RSA-3072). ### **Hash Functions** - ☐ Three properties: **pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance**. - ☐ Use **SHA-256** (not MD5 or SHA-1) for any new system — they are broken. - ☐ Output is fixed length regardless of input length. ### **Key Protocols** - ☐ **Diffie-Hellman** = key exchange over an insecure channel; vulnerable to MITM without authentication. - ☐ **Digital Signature** = hash encrypted with sender's private key; verified with sender's public key. - ☐ **TLS 1.3** = hybrid: asymmetric for handshake, symmetric (AES-GCM) for bulk data. ### **CPS-Specific Recall** - ☐ CPS uses **AES-128/256-GCM** for sensor stream confidentiality and integrity. - ☐ **ECC (P-256)** preferred over RSA in resource-constrained IoT devices (shorter keys, faster). - ☐ **Firmware signing** with RSA-2048 + SHA-256 prevents malicious code injection into PLCs, ECUs, smart meters. - ☐ **Hardware Root of Trust** (TPM / Secure Element) stores keys to resist physical tampering. ### **Numerical Must-Knows** - ☐ $N = 2^n$ — keyspace size for $n$-bit key. - ☐ RSA key generation: $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. - ☐ Encryption: $C = M^e \bmod n$. Decryption: $M = C^d \bmod n$. - ☐ XOR decryption: $P = C \oplus K$ (self-inverse property). - ☐ DH shared secret: $K = \alpha^{x_A x_B} \bmod p$. ### **Golden Rules for KTU Answers** - ☐ Always state the **algorithm name, key size, and key properties** explicitly in definitions. - ☐ In numerical questions, show **every modular arithmetic step** — partial work gets partial credit. - ☐ End RSA/DH derivations with a **verification step** ($ed \bmod \phi = 1$ or matching shared secret) — examiners reward completeness. - ☐ Draw **clean block diagrams** with labeled arrows; avoid crowded or un-labeled sketches.

topics.title: Encryption Techniques for CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Encryption Techniques for CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Encryption Techniques for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refer to a specialized class of cryptographic primitives, protocols, and algorithms designed to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** of data exchanged between the cyber (computation/communication) and physical (sensing/actuation) layers of a CPS. Unlike generic IT encryption, CPS-oriented encryption must satisfy **strict constraints on latency, energy consumption, memory footprint, and real-time deterministic behavior**, while simultaneously resisting both classical and quantum-era adversaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECPT743 – Module 2):**
> Encryption in CPS is **not** the same as encryption in traditional computing. The defining differentiator is the **co-design of security with physical/control-loop constraints**, where cryptographic overhead directly impacts control stability, jitter, and actuation deadlines (typically **1 ms – 100 ms** in industrial CPS).

## 1.2 Core Taxonomy of CPS Encryption

CPS encryption techniques are broadly classified into the following families:

| Family | Representative Algorithms | Typical Use-Case in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric Block Ciphers** | AES-128/256, PRESENT, SIMON, SKINNY | Sensor-to-gateway data confidentiality |
| **Symmetric Stream Ciphers** | Grain, Trivium, ChaCha20 | Real-time telemetry in IIoT |
| **Asymmetric / Public-Key** | RSA, ECC (secp256r1), Ed25519 | Device attestation, key exchange |
| **Lightweight Cryptography (LWC)** | ASCON, GIFT, KATAN | Resource-constrained sensor motes |
| **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** | Paillier, CKKS, BFV | Privacy-preserving aggregation |
| **Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE)** | CP-ABE, KP-ABE, MA-ABE | Fine-grained access in smart grids |
| **Identity-Based Encryption (IBE)** | Boneh–Franklin, Sakai–Kasahara | Certificate-less device identity |
| **Searchable Encryption (SE)** | SSE, PEKS | Encrypted log/query retrieval |
| **Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE)** | FF1, FF3-1 | Tokenization of legacy CPS identifiers |
| **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC)** | Kyber, Dilithium, SPHINCS+ | Quantum-resilient CPS key exchange |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Tamper-Evident Pneumatic Post Tube"**
> Imagine a factory where orders are physically transported between machines through sealed pneumatic tubes. **Symmetric encryption** is like both machines having an identical wax-seal stamp: fast, cheap, and identical on both ends. **Asymmetric encryption** is like one machine owning a uniquely carved public stamp (anyone can stamp) but only the other owning the matching un-stamper (private key) — slower, but solves the *key-distribution* problem. **Homomorphic encryption** is a magical tube that lets a sorting station **count the envelopes without ever opening them** — perfect for privacy-preserving analytics on encrypted sensor data. **Attribute-based encryption** is a tube that only delivers mail to a destination *if the envelope's label* (attributes: "dept = maintenance AND clearance ≥ 3") matches a policy.

## 1.4 The CPS-Specific Encryption Triad

Every encryption technique in CPS must be evaluated against three orthogonal axes:

$$\text{Quality}_{\text{CPS-Enc}} = f(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{T})$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = **Security Strength** (bits of security, e.g., **128-bit** for AES-128)
- $\mathcal{R}$ = **Resource Footprint** (Gate Equivalents, RAM bytes, energy in µJ)
- $\mathcal{T}$ = **Temporal Determinism** (worst-case latency, jitter, throughput)

A technique is *CPS-grade* only if it offers a defensible trade-off across all three — for instance, AES-128 has excellent $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ but moderate $\mathcal{R}$, whereas PRESENT is optimized for $\mathcal{R}$ at the cost of $\mathcal{S}$.

## 1.5 Threat Model Anchors

CPS encryption must defend against:

1. **Eavesdropper (Passive)** — reads wireless sensor traffic (e.g., ZigBee, BLE, LoRa).
2. **Active Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** — injects false actuator commands.
3. **Compromised Insider Device** — a legitimate sensor node turned malicious.
4. **Side-Channel Attacker** — exploits power/EM/timing leakage from the physical layer.
5. **Quantum Adversary (Future)** — Shor's algorithm breaks RSA/ECC; Grover's halves symmetric key strength.
6. **Physical Tampering** — invasive probing of an embedded CPS controller.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"Why not use RSA everywhere in CPS?"*, the expected KTU answer is the **three-point rebuttal**: (i) key sizes > 2048 bits inflate CPS message overhead, (ii) modular exponentiation drains battery, and (iii) lacks real-time deterministic latency guarantees required by control loops.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Stack of Encryption in CPS

Encryption in CPS is layered atop the **perception → transport → application** stack, with each layer adopting a specific technique family:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensors/Actuators):** Lightweight symmetric ciphers (PRESENT, SIMON) and physical-layer key generation.
2. **Network/Transport Layer:** Authenticated encryption (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) and group key agreement.
3. **Application/Service Layer:** ABE, IBE, HE, and searchable encryption for cloud-edge analytics.
4. **Cross-Layer:** Post-quantum hybrids (e.g., **X25519 + Kyber-768** for forward-secret device onboarding).

## 2.2 Symmetric Encryption — The CPS Workhorse

### 2.2.1 AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)
- **Block size:** 128 bits
- **Key sizes:** 128 / 192 / **256 bits** (CPS-grade = 128 or 256)
- **Rounds:** 10 / 12 / 14 (Nr)
- **Structure:** Substitution–Permutation Network (SPN)

The round transformation of AES operates on a 4×4 byte matrix called the **State**:

$$\text{State}_{i+1} = \text{MixColumns}\bigl(\text{ShiftRows}(\text{SubBytes}(\text{State}_i))\bigr) \oplus \text{RoundKey}_{i+1}$$

(SubBytes is omitted in the final round; MixColumns is omitted in the final round for both encryption and decryption in the inverse cipher.)

### 2.2.2 Modes of Operation in CPS

| Mode | Confidentiality | Integrity | Parallelism | CPS Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECB** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (deterministic — leaks patterns) |
| **CBC** | ✅ | ❌ (with HMAC) | ❌ (sequential) | ⚠️ adds latency |
| **CTR** | ✅ | ❌ (with HMAC) | ✅ | ✅ telemetry streams |
| **GCM** | ✅ | ✅ (AEAD) | ✅ | ✅✅ **gold standard for CPS** |
| **CCM** | ✅ | ✅ (AEAD) | ⚠️ | ✅ IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** **AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data)** modes such as **GCM** and **CCM** are *mandatory* in CPS because they simultaneously provide confidentiality and integrity, preventing attackers from silently tampering with control packets.

### 2.2.3 Lightweight Block Ciphers for Constrained CPS Nodes

| Cipher | Block (bits) | Key (bits) | Area (GE) | Energy/Block |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PRESENT** | 64 | 80 / 128 | ~1570 | Low |
| **SIMON** | 32–128 | 64–256 | ~1230 (smallest) | Very low |
| **SKINNY** | 64 / 128 | 64–256 | ~2391 | Low |
| **GIFT** | 64 / 128 | 128 | ~1985 | Low |
| **ASCON (NIST LWC winner)** | 128 | 128 / 160 | ~5300 (with AEAD) | Very low |

## 2.3 Asymmetric Encryption in CPS

### 2.3.1 RSA
Security rests on the **integer factorization problem (IFP)**.

$$C = M^{e} \bmod n, \qquad M = C^{d} \bmod n, \qquad n = p \cdot q$$

where $e$ is the public exponent, $d$ is the private exponent, and $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\lambda(n)}$, with $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ being the Carmichael function.

**Recommended 2024 key size:** **RSA-3072** (≈128-bit security equivalent).

### 2.3.2 Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)
Security rests on the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**.

For a point $P$ on an elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$ and an integer $k$:

$$Q = k \cdot P \quad \text{(point multiplication)}$$

Given $P$ and $Q$, recovering $k$ is computationally infeasible.

**CPS-grade curves:** secp256r1 (NIST P-256), Curve25519 (X25519), Ed25519 for signatures.

**Advantage over RSA:** A 256-bit ECC key offers security ≈ 3072-bit RSA — vastly better for bandwidth-constrained CPS messages.

## 2.4 Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

HE allows computation on ciphertexts without decryption.

**Paillier (Additive HE):** Given $E(m_1)$ and $E(m_2)$:

$$D\bigl(E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \bmod n^2\bigr) = m_1 + m_2 \bmod n$$

This is **gold for CPS** because it enables privacy-preserving aggregation of smart-meter readings across a neighborhood — the utility computes the **total consumption** without learning individual household usage.

## 2.5 Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE)

### 2.5.1 Ciphertext-Policy ABE (CP-ABE)
A message is encrypted under an **access policy** $\mathbb{A}$ (e.g., a tree of attributes). A user with secret key $SK_{\mathcal{S}}$ can decrypt **iff** their attribute set $\mathcal{S}$ satisfies $\mathbb{A}$:

$$\text{Decrypt}(C, SK_{\mathcal{S}}) = M \iff \mathbb{A}(\mathcal{S}) = \text{TRUE}$$

**CPS example:** A smart-grid substation can decrypt load-balancing commands *only if* attributes {`role = operator`, `region = Kerala`, `clearance ≥ 4`} are all satisfied.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AES Round Count | $N_r = 6 + \max(N_k, N_b)$ where $N_k$ = key length/32, $N_b$ = block length/32 | 10, 12, 14 rounds |
| 2 | AES Security Strength | $\mathcal{S}_{\text{AES}} = \min(k, 2^{n/2})$ for key $k$ bits, block $n=128$ | $2^{128}, 2^{192}, 2^{256}$ |
| 3 | RSA Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ with $p, q$ prime, $\vert p \vert \approx \vert q \vert$ | bits |
| 4 | RSA Key Relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\lambda(n)}$, $\lambda(n) = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ | modular inverse |
| 5 | ECC Security | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA $\approx$ 128-bit AES | NIST SP 800-57 |
| 6 | Diffie–Hellman (ECDH) | $K_{AB} = a \cdot b \cdot G$ where $a, b$ are private, $G$ generator | Shared secret |
| 7 | Paillier Add HE | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 + m_2) \pmod{n^2}$ | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| 8 | Birthday Bound | Collision after $\approx 2^{n/2}$ operations | $n$ = hash output bits |
| 9 | Grover on AES | Effective key strength $= k/2$ | Use AES-256 post-quantum |
| 10 | Shor on RSA/ECC | Polynomial-time break of IFP/ECDLP | Migrate to PQC |
| 11 | CPS Real-Time Bound | Encryption latency $\le$ control-loop deadline (typ. **1–10 ms**) | ms |
| 12 | NIST LWC Security | $\ge 128$-bit classical, $\ge 64$-bit quantum | ASCON default |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **derivable from first principles** in the next section. The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value (rendered using $\lvert$ and $\rvert$ in LaTeX to preserve table integrity).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid:** ABE/HE protect customer consumption data from utility operators.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** AES-GCM secures V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) messages; ECC authenticates beacons.
- **Industrial IoT:** Lightweight ciphers (ASCON) protect Modbus-TCP and OPC-UA payloads.
- **Medical CPS:** HE allows hospital analytics on encrypted patient telemetry.
- **Drones/UAVs:** Symmetric + ECC hybrid for low-latency command-and-control.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AES Round Count $N_r$

The AES round count is a function of both key length and block size, since both determine the number of 32-bit words ($N_k$ and $N_b$) in the expanded key schedule.

**Step 1 — Define word counts.**
For AES-128: key length $= 128$ bits, so number of 32-bit key words $N_k = 128 / 32 = 4$.
For AES-128: block size $= 128$ bits, so number of 32-bit state words $N_b = 128 / 32 = 4$.

**Step 2 — Apply the AES specification formula.**
The AES standard (FIPS 197) defines the number of rounds as:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_r &= 6 + \max(N_k, N_b) \\
N_r &= 6 + \max(4, 4) \\
N_r &= 6 + 4 \\
N_r &= 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Verify for AES-256.**
For AES-256: $N_k = 256/32 = 8$, $N_b = 4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_r &= 6 + \max(8, 4) \\
N_r &= 6 + 8 \\
N_r &= 14
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the FIPS-197 specification. The "extra rounds" (over 6 base rounds) defend against **square attacks** and related-key cryptanalysis.

## 3.2 Derivation: RSA Encryption–Decryption Correctness

We must show that for any message $M \in \mathbb{Z}_n$:

$$(M^{e})^{d} \bmod n \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

**Step 1 — By definition of $d$.**
$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\lambda(n)}$, therefore there exists an integer $k$ such that:

$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \lambda(n)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the decryption operation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C^{d} \bmod n &= (M^{e})^{d} \bmod n \\
&= M^{e \cdot d} \bmod n \\
&= M^{1 + k \cdot \lambda(n)} \bmod n \\
&= M \cdot \bigl(M^{\lambda(n)}\bigr)^{k} \bmod n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler/Carmichael's theorem.**
By Carmichael's theorem, for any $M$ coprime to $n$:

$$M^{\lambda(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

For $\gcd(M, n) \neq 1$, the result still holds for $n = p \cdot q$ (CRT argument), giving:

$$\begin{aligned}
C^{d} \bmod n &\equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \bmod n \\
&\equiv M \bmod n
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore decryption recovers the plaintext.** This is the foundational correctness proof of RSA.

## 3.3 Derivation: ECDH Shared Secret

Let Alice have private key $a$, Bob have $b$, and $G$ be the generator of an elliptic curve group of order $n$.

**Step 1 — Alice computes her public key.**
$A = a \cdot G$ (point multiplication on $E$).

**Step 2 — Bob computes his public key.**
$B = b \cdot G$.

**Step 3 — Each computes the shared secret.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Alice:}\quad K_{AB} &= a \cdot B = a \cdot (b \cdot G) = (a \cdot b) \cdot G \\
\text{Bob:}\quad K_{AB} &= b \cdot A = b \cdot (a \cdot G) = (a \cdot b) \cdot G
\end{aligned}$$

Both compute the **same point** because elliptic-curve point multiplication is associative over a finite field. The shared secret is the x-coordinate of that point, used to derive a symmetric session key (e.g., via HKDF).

## 3.4 Derivation: Paillier Additive Homomorphism

Let $n = p \cdot q$, $g \in \mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^{*}$, and $\lambda = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$. The Paillier encryption of message $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ with randomness $r$ is:

$$E(m, r) = g^{m} \cdot r^{n} \bmod n^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Encrypt two messages.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(m_1, r_1) &= g^{m_1} \cdot r_1^{n} \bmod n^{2} \\
E(m_2, r_2) &= g^{m_2} \cdot r_2^{n} \bmod n^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply ciphertexts.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(m_1, r_1) \cdot E(m_2, r_2) &= (g^{m_1} \cdot r_1^{n}) \cdot (g^{m_2} \cdot r_2^{n}) \bmod n^{2} \\
&= g^{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (r_1 r_2)^{n} \bmod n^{2} \\
&= E(m_1 + m_2,\, r_1 r_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Decrypt to recover the sum.**
By applying the Paillier decryption function $D$:

$$D\bigl(E(m_1, r_1) \cdot E(m_2, r_2)\bigr) = m_1 + m_2 \bmod n$$

This proves **additive homomorphism** — the cornerstone of privacy-preserving aggregation in CPS (e.g., encrypted smart-meter summation).

## 3.5 Derivation: Security Loss under Grover's Algorithm

Grover's algorithm offers a **quadratic speedup** for unstructured search, affecting symmetric ciphers and hash pre-images.

**Step 1 — Classical brute force complexity.**
Searching through $2^{k}$ keys requires $2^{k}$ operations on average.

**Step 2 — Grover's quantum complexity.**
Quantum search requires:

$$O(\sqrt{2^{k}}) = O(2^{k/2})$$

**Step 3 — Effective security for AES-128 vs AES-256.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AES-128 under Grover: } & 2^{128/2} = 2^{64} \quad \text{(insufficient)} \\
\text{AES-256 under Grover: } & 2^{256/2} = 2^{128} \quad \text{(still secure)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** AES-256 is the **post-quantum-safe** symmetric choice; AES-128 is acceptable for the next decade but may need migration to AES-256 for long-lived CPS deployments (e.g., smart-grid meters with 20-year lifespans).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Key Length | Ops/Block (approx) | Quantum-Safe? | CPS Energy Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AES-128 (hardware) | 128 b | 10 rounds | ⚠️ (Grover halves) | Low (HW accel) |
| PRESENT-80 | 80 b | 31 rounds | ❌ | Very Low |
| ChaCha20 | 256 b | 20 rounds/64 B | ⚠️ | Low |
| RSA-2048 | 2048 b | 1 exp (slow) | ❌ (Shor) | Very High |
| ECDSA-P256 | 256 b | 1 EC mul | ❌ (Shor) | High |
| Kyber-512 (PQC) | ~8000 b | 1 polynomial | ✅ | Moderate |
| ASCON-128 (AEAD) | 128 b | 6/12 rounds | ✅ (NIST LWC) | Very Low |
| Paillier-2048 | 2048 b | 2 exp | ❌ | Very High |
