# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Central Differential Privacy (CDP) and Local Differential Privacy (LDP) in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. Give one CPS example of each.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

| Aspect | Central DP (CDP) | Local DP (LDP) |
|---|---|---|
| Trust assumption | Trusted aggregator collects raw data | No trusted party; device perturbs locally |
| Noise level | Lower (good utility) | Higher (lower utility) |
| Privacy boundary | Protected at the *output* | Protected at the *source* |
| CPS example | Utility publishes DP-noised average household power consumption to the grid operator | Vehicle sends a randomised-response bit to a road authority indicating whether it passed a sensitive zone |

LDP protects against an *insider* aggregator attack; CDP protects against an *outsider* who only sees the released statistics. **(1 mark for definition of CDP, 1 mark for LDP, 1 mark for examples.)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is Differential Privacy preferred over simple anonymization in CPS data sharing? Illustrate with a smart-meter scenario.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

Anonymization removes direct identifiers (name, address), but an adversary can still re-identify a household by linking quasi-identifiers such as *number of occupants, typical load curve shape, EV charging schedule*. This is called a **linkage attack**, famously demonstrated on the Netflix Prize and AOL search datasets.

In a smart-meter setting, even with the name removed, the hourly load profile of an EV owner is almost as unique as a fingerprint. **Differential Privacy (DP)** solves this by adding mathematically calibrated noise at the *query* level (or at the *device*, for LDP). The guarantee is **algorithmic**, holding against *any* auxiliary information an adversary might possess, including future data correlations.

Hence DP is preferred because:
1. It gives a **provable, parameterised guarantee** ($\varepsilon$).
2. It is **robust to linkage attacks**.
3. It composes predictably across many queries / time periods.

**(1 mark each for limitation of anonymization, the linkage-attack argument, and DP's robustness.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy. With a neat sketch, explain how a typical DP mechanism operates in a Cyber-Physical System that releases aggregated sensor data to a third party. Discuss the role of **sensitivity** in determining the noise level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart grid has 50,000 households. The utility wants to publish the **hourly mean consumption** with $(\varepsilon = 0.5, \delta = 10^{-5})$-DP. The maximum hourly consumption of any household is $C_{\max} = 8$ kWh. Use the **Gaussian mechanism** to compute the noise standard deviation $\sigma$ required. Comment on the resulting utility. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Definition (2 marks):*
A randomised algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-differential privacy if for every pair of neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ (differing in one record) and every measurable output set $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S] + \delta$$

*Sketch of operation in CPS (3 marks):*
- **Sensors** (smart meters, IIoT devices) stream readings to a **trusted aggregator**.
- The aggregator receives a *query* $f$ from a third party (e.g., "hourly mean consumption in substation X").
- It computes the true answer $f(D)$.
- It adds **noise** sampled from a distribution whose scale depends on $\Delta f$ and $\varepsilon$.
- It releases the noisy answer $\tilde{f}$ to the third party.
- The **privacy guarantee** ensures that no single household's data changes the output distribution by more than a factor of $e^{\varepsilon}$ (with probability slack $\delta$).

*Sensitivity (2 marks):*
The **global sensitivity** $\Delta f$ measures the maximum change in the query output when one record changes:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \Vert f(D) - f(D') \Vert$$

For the mean of $N$ values bounded in $[0, C_{\max}]$, $\Delta f = C_{\max}/N$. The noise scale is **proportional** to $\Delta f / \varepsilon$, so understanding sensitivity is *essential* — it dictates how much noise is needed to hide one record.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Sensitivity (1 mark):*
$$\Delta_2 f = \frac{C_{\max}}{N} = \frac{8}{50{,}000} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kWh}$$

*Step 2 — Apply Gaussian mechanism formula (3 marks):*
$$\sigma = \frac{\Delta_2 f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$

Compute the inner term:

$$2 \ln\!\left(\frac{1.25}{\delta}\right) = 2 \ln\!\left(\frac{1.25}{10^{-5}}\right) = 2 \ln(1.25 \times 10^{5}) = 2 \cdot 11.736 \approx 23.47$$

$$\sqrt{23.47} \approx 4.844$$

*Step 3 — Final $\sigma$ (1 mark):*
$$\sigma = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 4.844}{0.5} \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kWh}$$

*Step 4 — Utility comment (2 marks):*
The standard deviation of the released value is $\approx 1.55 \times 10^{-3}$ kWh, i.e., $\approx 0.002$ kWh. The true hourly mean in a residential feeder is typically $0.5$–$2$ kWh per household, so the **relative error** is below $0.2\%$. This is **excellent utility** for grid planning, while the privacy of every individual household is mathematically guaranteed.

**Total: 14 marks**

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain **Local Differential Privacy** with its formal definition. Compare it with Central DP in terms of accuracy, trust, and typical CPS use cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fleet of 5,000 connected vehicles uses a **randomised-response LDP protocol** with $\varepsilon = \ln 3$ to report whether they entered a sensitive area in the last 24 hours. Estimate the *true* count of vehicles that entered the area, given that the aggregator received 1,750 "yes" responses. Compute the standard error of your estimate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Definition (2 marks):*
A randomised mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-**Local DP** if for every pair of inputs $v, v'$ belonging to the data owner, and every output $y$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(v) = y] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \Pr[\mathcal{M}(v') = y]$$

The data is *perturbed on the user's own device* before it leaves the user's control.

*Comparison table (3 marks):*

| Property | Central DP | Local DP |
|---|---|---|
| Trusted aggregator | Required | Not required |
| Noise magnitude | Smaller | Larger (often $\sqrt{N}$ factor more) |
| Accuracy for fixed $\varepsilon$ | Higher | Lower |
| Typical CPS use | Smart utility, hospital publishing stats | Telemetry from phones, vehicles, browsers |
| Privacy threat model | Outside attacker, honest-but-curious server | Malicious aggregator |
| Example mechanism | Laplace, Gaussian | Randomised response, k-RR, local Laplace |

*Key insight (2 marks):* LDP's higher noise stems from the fact that *each* data point must be protected *individually*, whereas in CDP the noise is *amortised* across the entire dataset. Hence LDP requires much larger populations to achieve comparable statistical accuracy.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Randomised response probabilities (1 mark):*
With $\varepsilon = \ln 3$:

$$p = \frac{e^{\varepsilon}}{1 + e^{\varepsilon}} = \frac{3}{4}, \quad 1 - p = \frac{1}{4}$$

*Step 2 — Unbiased estimator (2 marks):*
Let $N_1$ = true count of "entered"; $\tilde{N}_1 = 1750$ is the noisy count.

$$\mathbb{E}[\tilde{N}_1] = p \cdot N_1 + (1-p)(N - N_1) = 0.75\, N_1 + 0.25(N - N_1) = 0.5\, N_1 + 0.25 N$$

Solving:

$$N_1 = \frac{\tilde{N}_1 - 0.25 N}{0.5} = 2 \tilde{N}_1 - 0.5 N$$

*Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):*
$$N_1 = 2(1750) - 0.5(5000) = 3500 - 2500 = 1000 \text{ vehicles}$$

*Step 4 — Standard error (2 marks):*
The per-response variance is $p(1-p) = 0.75 \cdot 0.25 = 0.1875$, and the estimator rescales by $1/(2p-1)^2 = 1/0.25 = 4$:

$$\mathrm{Var}(\hat{N}_1) = N \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{(2p-1)^2} = 5000 \cdot \frac{0.1875}{0.25} = 3750$$

$$\mathrm{SE}(\hat{N}_1) = \sqrt{3750} \approx 61.24 \text{ vehicles}$$

*Step 5 — Confidence interval (1 mark):*
A 95% confidence interval is $1000 \pm 1.96 \times 61.24 \approx 1000 \pm 120$ vehicles, i.e., roughly **880 to 1120** vehicles. The estimate is precise enough for traffic management, and the privacy of *every* driver is protected at the device level.

**Total: 14 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $\Delta f$ with the range of the data.** Students often write $\Delta f = C_{\max}$ instead of $C_{\max}/N$ for a mean. The mean's sensitivity is *always* divided by $N$. **Lose 1–2 marks if missed.**
> 2. **Using the Laplace formula with the Gaussian context (or vice-versa).** State the mechanism explicitly before applying its noise formula.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2\ln(1.25/\delta)$ term** in the Gaussian mechanism — a very common KTU trap.
> 4. **Local vs. Central DP confusion:** Randomised response is LDP; Laplace over an aggregate is CDP. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Privacy composition under time:** Always use **advanced composition** for streams; naive summation over-estimates the budget and is *conservative* but shows the student does not understand the theorem.
> 6. **Unit handling:** Keep kWh, kW, and sample counts with units; examiners often deduct for dimensional inconsistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Applications of DP in CPS"**

- **Two operating modes:** **CDP** (trusted aggregator, low noise) vs **LDP** (per-device perturbation, higher noise).
- **Three core mechanisms to memorise:** Laplace (pure $\varepsilon$-DP, $\ell_1$ sensitivity), Gaussian ($(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP, $\ell_2$ sensitivity), Exponential (non-numeric queries).
- **Sensitivity is everything:** $\Delta f$ decides the noise. For the mean of $N$ bounded values, $\Delta f = \text{range}/N$ — the larger the CPS deployment, the easier DP becomes.
- **Composition theorems:** Sequential sums the $\varepsilon_i$; parallel takes the max; **advanced composition** gives sublinear $\sqrt{k}$ growth — the mathematical enabler of streaming DP for CPS.
- **Smart Grid** = canonical example of CDP; **vehicle telemetry / browser apps** = canonical examples of LDP.
- **Federated learning** in CPS uses **user-level DP** with **Gaussian noise + RDP composition**.
- **Randomised response** for LDP on binary CPS data: $p = e^{\varepsilon}/(1+e^{\varepsilon})$; unbiased estimator is $\hat{N}_1 = (\tilde{N}_1 - (1-p)N)/(2p-1)$.
- **Privacy budget $\varepsilon$:** $0.1$–$1$ for "strong" CPS privacy; $1$–$3$ for moderate; $>5$ is considered weak.
- **DP beats anonymization** because it guarantees protection against *all* auxiliary-information linkage attacks — a critical exam point.
- **Real-world deployments to remember for context:** Google RAPPOR (LDP, Chrome), Apple emoji & health data (LDP), US Census 2020 (CDP), smart-meter pilots in the EU (CDP).
- **KTU-favourite calculation:** Mean of bounded data → Laplace noise = $C_{\max}/(N\varepsilon)$ → MSE $= 2b^2$.
- **Engineering trade-off mantra:** *More devices ⇒ smaller sensitivity ⇒ less noise ⇒ higher utility at the same $\varepsilon$.*

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Privacy by Design (PbD)

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privacy by Design (PbD)** is a foundational engineering and policy framework, formally articulated in 2009 by Dr. Ann Cavoukian (former Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, Canada), which mandates that **privacy assurance and data protection mechanisms must be proactively embedded into the architecture, design, and operation of information technologies, networked systems, and business practices** — rather than retrofitted as an afterthought. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, PbD extends beyond software into the physical sensing, actuation, communication, and control layers, ensuring that privacy is a *non-negotiable design constraint* from the very inception of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests students on the **seven Foundational Principles of PbD** and their direct application to CPS architectures (IoT, IIoT, Smart Grids, Autonomous Vehicles, Healthcare CPS, etc.). Students must be able to map each principle to a concrete engineering design decision.

### 1.2 The 7 Foundational Principles — Overview List

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial**
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting**
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design**
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum**
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection**
6. **Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open**
7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart House Builder"**
> Imagine you are building a house. There are two builders to choose from:
> - **Builder A (Reactive Approach):** Builds the house first, then you discover it has poor ventilation and weak walls. You hire a security guard *after* a robbery occurs. You install blinds *after* the neighbors have been peeking in for months. You pay extra for retrofits.
> - **Builder B (Privacy by Design Approach):** Before laying a single brick, the architect includes window placement for natural privacy, reinforced walls in the design, a pre-wired alarm system, a smart lock by default, and a clear user manual explaining all privacy features. Privacy is *part of the blueprint*, not an upgrade.
>
> In a **Cyber-Physical System** (e.g., a wearable health monitor), Builder A would be: collect all patient biometric data to the cloud, then add encryption *after* a data breach. Builder B (PbD) would be: design the device to process heart rate *locally on the wearable*, transmit only anonymized summaries, and require explicit consent before any cloud sync.

### 1.4 Why PbD Matters in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)

CPS uniquely amplify privacy risks because they:

- **Bridge the physical and digital worlds** — sensors capture real-world behavior (location, biometrics, voice, video).
- **Operate continuously and autonomously** — generating uninterrupted data streams.
- **Involve multiple stakeholders** — manufacturers, cloud providers, end users, regulators.
- **Have long operational lifespans** — a smart meter installed today may operate for **15–20 years**, outliving its original threat model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Recap**
> **PbD = Engineering privacy INTO the system (anticipatory) vs. Bolting privacy ONTO the system (reactive).**
> The fundamental shift is from a **"fix-it-later"** mentality to a **"build-it-right-from-the-start"** engineering philosophy.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Principle 1 — Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial

**Operational Logic:**

- The organization must **anticipate** privacy risks *before* the system is deployed, not *after* a privacy incident occurs.
- Privacy risks in CPS are evaluated at the **design phase** through techniques such as **Privacy Impact Assessments (PIA)**, **Threat Modeling (STRIDE, LINDDUN)**, and **Data Flow Diagrams (DFD)**.
- The principle inverts the cost curve: it is exponentially cheaper to fix a privacy flaw at design time than after deployment across thousands or millions of devices.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- **Privacy Risk Register** maintained during system design.
- **STRIDE-based threat modeling** for each CPS subsystem (sensing, communication, control, actuation).
- **Pre-deployment penetration testing** of the CPS architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A reactive approach treats privacy as a *liability*; a proactive approach treats it as a *competitive advantage* and a *trust-enabler*.

### 2.2 Principle 2 — Privacy as the Default Setting

**Operational Logic:**

- The system must be configured so that the **maximum level of privacy protection is automatically active** without any action required by the end user.
- Users should not have to *opt-in* to privacy; they should have to *opt-out* of sharing, and even then, the default must be conservative.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- Default-deny access control lists (ACLs).
- Local-only data processing as the default (cloud upload is opt-in).
- Default anonymization/pseudonymization of sensor data.
- Default "minimal data collection" — collect only what is strictly necessary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key CPS Example:** A smart speaker must default to having its microphone *muted*, requiring an explicit physical button press to activate listening — not the other way around.

### 2.3 Principle 3 — Privacy Embedded into Design

**Operational Logic:**

- Privacy must be an **integral component of the system's core architecture**, not a separable add-on layer.
- This means privacy is treated with the same rigor as **functional requirements, safety requirements, and performance requirements** in CPS engineering.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- Privacy requirements are written into the **System Requirements Specification (SRS)** document.
- Architectural Design Patterns: *Privacy-aware middleware*, *on-device federated learning*, *homomorphic encryption primitives* embedded at the protocol level.
- Privacy considerations are part of the **Verification & Validation (V&V)** plan.

### 2.4 Principle 4 — Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum

**Operational Logic:**

- The principle **rejects the false dichotomy** that privacy must be traded off against functionality, security, usability, or business objectives.
- PbD seeks **win-win outcomes** where all design goals are achieved simultaneously through *innovative architecture* rather than *compromise*.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- Using **differential privacy** to extract population-level insights from CPS sensor data without exposing individual records.
- Using **edge computing** to deliver personalization (functionality) without sending raw personal data to the cloud (privacy).
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)** for authentication — proving identity without revealing credentials.

### 2.5 Principle 5 — End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection

**Operational Logic:**

- Privacy protection must span the **entire data lifecycle**: collection → storage → processing → transmission → sharing → archival → destruction.
- In CPS, this lifecycle is particularly complex because data flows across **sensors, gateways, networks, cloud servers, mobile apps, and third-party APIs**.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- **Encryption at rest** (AES-256 in CPS databases).
- **Encryption in transit** (TLS 1.3, DTLS for IoT protocols).
- **Encryption in use** (secure enclaves, Trusted Execution Environments like ARM TrustZone).
- **Secure data destruction** at End-of-Life (EoL) of CPS devices.
- **Lifecycle key management** with rotation and revocation.

### 2.6 Principle 6 — Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open

**Operational Logic:**

- All privacy-related processes, algorithms, and policies of the CPS must be **verifiable, auditable, and independently scrutinized**.
- Stakeholders (users, regulators, auditors) must be able to inspect the system's privacy behavior.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- **Open-source firmware** (where possible) for transparency.
- **Public privacy policies** written in plain language.
- **Audit logs** of all data access events.
- **Algorithmic transparency** — disclosing what data is collected, why, how it is used, and for how long.
- **Third-party privacy certifications** (e.g., ISO 27701, SOC 2).

### 2.7 Principle 7 — Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric

**Operational Logic:**

- The system must be designed **around the legitimate interests and expectations of the user**, not the data collector.
- Users must be given **meaningful control**: granular consent, data access, correction, deletion (GDPR-style rights), and the ability to withdraw consent without penalty.

**Engineering Mechanisms in CPS:**

- **Granular privacy dashboards** in CPS companion apps.
- **Consent management platforms (CMP)** integrated into the CPS.
- **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) portals** allowing users to download or delete their data.
- **Privacy-preserving user feedback channels**.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Principles Summary

| **#** | **Principle** | **Core Mandate** | **CPS Engineering Implementation** | **Failure Mode if Violated** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Proactive not Reactive | Anticipate privacy risks at design time | PIA, STRIDE, LINDDUN threat modeling | Privacy breach after deployment; costly retrofits |
| 2 | Privacy as Default | Max privacy ON by default | Default-deny ACLs, local-only processing | Mass unauthorized data collection |
| 3 | Embedded into Design | Privacy in core architecture | Privacy in SRS, V&V plan, middleware | Privacy as bolted-on add-on; easily bypassed |
| 4 | Full Functionality (Positive-Sum) | No privacy-vs-functionality trade-off | Differential privacy, edge AI, ZKP | Forced user/data collector adversarial framing |
| 5 | End-to-End Security | Full lifecycle data protection | AES-256, TLS 1.3, secure enclaves, EoL wipe | Data exposed at weak link in lifecycle |
| 6 | Visibility and Transparency | Open, auditable privacy processes | Audit logs, open firmware, certifications | Hidden surveillance; loss of public trust |
| 7 | User-Centric | User has meaningful control | Granular consent, DSAR portals | User treated as data product, not stakeholder |

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of PbD Principles:**
> - **Smart Healthcare CPS** (e.g., insulin pumps, ECG monitors): PbD principles 2, 5, 7 are critical — defaults protect patient data, lifecycle encryption is mandatory, and patients must control who sees their health data.
> - **Smart Grids**: PbD principles 1, 4 are key — proactively model privacy risks from smart meter data (which can reveal household behavior), and use differential privacy for load forecasting without exposing individual households.
> - **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**: PbD principles 3, 5, 6 — privacy embedded in V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication protocols, end-to-end encryption of telemetry, and transparent disclosure of what data leaves the vehicle.
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: PbD principles 1, 3 — anticipate insider threats, embed privacy in SCADA/DCS architectures.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Operationalizing PbD in CPS

This section provides an **exhaustive analytical breakdown** of how each PbD principle is operationalized across the layers of a generic CPS architecture. This is the type of structured analysis KTU examiners reward with full marks.

### 3.1 Generic CPS Layered Architecture (Reference Model)

A Cyber-Physical System typically consists of the following layers:

- **L1 — Perception Layer (Sensors/Actuators):** Physical world data acquisition.
- **L2 — Edge/Fog Layer (Gateways):** Local processing, filtering, aggregation.
- **L3 — Network Layer (Communication):** Wired/wireless data transmission.
- **L4 — Cloud/Backend Layer (Storage + Compute):** Big data analytics, ML, long-term storage.
- **L5 — Application Layer (User Interfaces):** Dashboards, mobile apps, APIs.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — PbD Principle × CPS Layer

Below is the **step-by-step reasoning** for each principle applied to each CPS layer.

#### Principle 1 — Proactive not Reactive

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 1.1:** During the system design phase, before any sensor is manufactured, conduct a **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)**.
- **Step 1.2:** Map the CPS data flows using **Data Flow Diagrams (DFDs)** showing data movement from sensor (L1) → edge (L2) → network (L3) → cloud (L4) → app (L5).
- **Step 1.3:** Apply **LINDDUN** (a privacy-specific threat modeling framework: **L**inkability, **I**dentifiability, **N**on-repudiation, **D**etectability, **D**isclosure of information, **U**nawareness, **N**on-compliance) to identify privacy risks at each layer.
- **Step 1.4:** Document a **Privacy Risk Register** with risk severity ratings (Low/Medium/High/Critical) and mitigations for each.
- **Step 1.5:** Conduct **pre-deployment tabletop exercises** simulating privacy attacks (e.g., sensor spoofing, man-in-the-middle on gateway).

#### Principle 2 — Privacy as the Default Setting

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 2.1:** At the sensor layer (L1), set the **default sampling rate to the minimum** required for functional correctness.
- **Step 2.2:** At the edge layer (L2), default to **on-device processing**; cloud upload must be an **opt-in** toggle.
- **Step 2.3:** At the network layer (L3), use **default-deny firewall rules**; only explicitly whitelisted endpoints are accessible.
- **Step 2.4:** At the cloud layer (L4), default to **encrypted-at-rest** storage with **default pseudonymization** of user identifiers.
- **Step 2.5:** At the application layer (L5), default to **"private mode"** in user dashboards; public sharing is opt-in.

#### Principle 3 — Privacy Embedded into Design

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 3.1:** Write **privacy requirements** into the System Requirements Specification (SRS) as first-class requirements, e.g., *"REQ-PRIV-001: The system shall pseudonymize user identifiers before storage in the cloud database."*
- **Step 3.2:** Choose **architectural patterns** that bake in privacy: e.g., *federated learning* (ML models trained on-device, no raw data leaves the device), *homomorphic encryption* (compute on encrypted data), *secure multi-party computation*.
- **Step 3.3:** Integrate privacy verification into the **V&V plan**: privacy test cases must pass before release.
- **Step 3.4:** Use **privacy-enhancing hardware**: ARM TrustZone, Intel SGX, Apple Secure Enclave for trusted execution.

#### Principle 4 — Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 4.1:** Identify the apparent privacy-vs-functionality trade-off (e.g., "personalization requires user data").
- **Step 4.2:** Apply an **innovative technique** to break the trade-off:
  - For personalization: use **on-device personalization** (e.g., Apple's on-device Siri personalization).
  - For analytics: use **differential privacy** to learn population trends without exposing individuals.
  - For authentication: use **zero-knowledge proofs** to verify identity without revealing credentials.
- **Step 4.3:** Validate that **both privacy and functionality objectives** are met through testing.

**Mathematical illustration — Differential Privacy:**

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ satisfies $\epsilon$-differential privacy if for all datasets $D_1$ and $D_2$ differing in at most one record, and for all output sets $S$:

$$
\Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_2) \in S]
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the **privacy budget** (smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy).

#### Principle 5 — End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection

**Step-by-step operationalization across the data lifecycle:**

- **Step 5.1 — Collection (L1):** Encrypt sensor data at the source using **lightweight ciphers** (e.g., AES-128 in CBC or GCM mode) suitable for resource-constrained IoT devices.
- **Step 5.2 — Transmission (L3):** Use **TLS 1.3** for TCP-based traffic; **DTLS 1.3** for UDP-based IoT protocols (CoAP, MQTT-SN).
- **Step 5.3 — Storage (L4):** Apply **AES-256 encryption at rest**; use **envelope encryption** with key management services (KMS).
- **Step 5.4 — Processing (L4):** Use **secure enclaves (TEE)** for any operation involving plaintext personal data.
- **Step 5.5 — Sharing (L4 → Third Parties):** Apply **tokenization** or **data masking** before any third-party data transfer.
- **Step 5.6 — Archival:** Migrate to **cold storage with re-encryption** every **5 years** (or per regulatory mandate).
- **Step 5.7 — Destruction:** At End-of-Life (EoL) of CPS devices, perform **cryptographic erasure** (destroy keys) and **physical destruction** of storage media.

#### Principle 6 — Visibility and Transparency

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 6.1:** Publish a **plain-language privacy policy** describing: what data is collected, why, how long it is stored, who it is shared with, and how to exercise user rights.
- **Step 6.2:** Maintain **immutable audit logs** of all data access events (who accessed what, when, why).
- **Step 6.3:** Open-source CPS firmware **where commercial and security constraints allow**.
- **Step 6.4:** Obtain independent **third-party privacy certifications** (e.g., ISO/IEC 27701 Privacy Information Management).
- **Step 6.5:** Provide **transparency reports** to regulators and the public at regular intervals.

#### Principle 7 — Respect for User Privacy — User-Centric

**Step-by-step operationalization:**

- **Step 7.1:** Provide **granular consent options** in the CPS companion app (e.g., "Share anonymized location data for traffic improvement: Yes/No").
- **Step 7.2:** Implement a **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) portal** allowing users to: view their data, export their data (data portability), correct inaccuracies, request deletion (right to be forgotten).
- **Step 7.3:** Ensure **consent withdrawal is as easy as consent granting** (one-click withdrawal).
- **Step 7.4:** Provide **just-in-time privacy notices** at the moment of data collection (e.g., a popup when a new sensor is activated).
- **Step 7.5:** Honor **do-not-track and Global Privacy Control (GPC)** signals sent by the user's browser or device.

### 3.3 Summary Equation — PbD Maturity in CPS

We can express a conceptual **PbD Maturity Score (PMS)** for a CPS as:

$$
PMS = \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \cdot L_i
$$

where:
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to the $i^{th}$ PbD principle (typically $w_i = 1$ for equal weighting).
- $L_i$ = implementation level of principle $i$, scored on an ordinal scale (e.g., $0$ = not implemented, $1$ = partially implemented, $2$ = fully implemented, $3$ = exceeds standard).
- $PMS \in [0, 21]$ for equal weighting.

A CPS with $PMS \geq 18$ is considered to exhibit **strong PbD posture**; $PMS \leq 7$ indicates **critical privacy gaps**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**
**Define Privacy by Design (PbD). State any TWO of its foundational principles with one-line explanations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Privacy by Design (PbD), conceptualized by **Dr. Ann Cavoukian in 2009**, is a framework that mandates the **proactive embedding of privacy controls and data protection safeguards into the design, architecture, and operation** of information systems, networks, and business processes — ensuring privacy is assured from the earliest stages of system engineering rather than retrofitted after deployment.

**Two foundational principles (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Privacy as the Default Setting:** The system is configured so that maximum privacy protection is automatically active without any user action; users must opt-out of data sharing rather than opt-in.
2. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection:** Privacy protection extends across the complete data lifecycle — from collection through storage, processing, transmission, sharing, archival, and final destruction — with strong encryption and access controls applied at every stage.

*(Any two of the seven principles accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**Why is Privacy by Design particularly critical in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) compared to traditional IT systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PbD is critical in CPS for three principal reasons:

1. **Continuous and pervasive data collection:** CPS sensors (e.g., cameras, microphones, biometric sensors, smart meters) operate **24/7**, capturing highly sensitive real-world data such as location, behavior patterns, health vitals, and home activities. Without PbD, the data deluge itself becomes a privacy hazard.
2. **Convergence of physical and digital risk:** A privacy failure in a CPS is not merely a data breach — it can lead to **physical harm** (e.g., unauthorized access to an insulin pump or autonomous vehicle control system), unlike pure IT systems.
3. **Long operational lifetimes:** CPS devices such as industrial controllers and smart meters operate for **15–20+ years**, far exceeding the threat model horizon. PbD ensures the architecture remains privacy-resilient across evolving threat landscapes, regulatory changes, and technological shifts (e.g., quantum computing threats).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECPT743 Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven foundational principles of Privacy by Design** as articulated by Ann Cavoukian. For each principle, provide **one concrete engineering example** of how it is implemented in a Cyber-Physical System. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **block diagram**, describe how PbD principles are operationalized across the **five layers of a generic CPS architecture** (Perception, Edge/Fog, Network, Cloud/Backend, Application). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Seven Foundational Principles with CPS Examples (7 marks — 1 mark per principle)**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* Before deploying a smart city surveillance system, the city conducts a **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** and uses **LINDDUN threat modeling** to identify re-identification risks from camera feeds, designing countermeasures (face blurring at the edge) before any camera is installed.

2. **Privacy as the Default Setting (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* A fitness wearable ships with **heart rate data processed locally**; cloud synchronization is **disabled by default** and requires explicit user opt-in via the companion app.

3. **Privacy Embedded into Design (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* An autonomous vehicle's **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication stack** is designed from the ground up with **pseudonymized short-term certificates** to prevent vehicle tracking, rather than relying on real, persistent identities.

4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* A smart grid uses **differential privacy** to provide load forecasting (functionality) to the utility while mathematically guaranteeing that individual household consumption patterns cannot be reverse-engineered (privacy).

5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* A medical infusion pump uses **AES-128 encryption** on the sensor, **TLS 1.3** for hospital network transmission, **AES-256 at rest** in the cloud EHR database, and **cryptographic erasure** of local logs when the device is decommissioned.

6. **Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* A smart home hub publishes its **firmware source code** on a public repository, maintains a **public privacy policy** in plain language, and provides **audit logs** that users can export and review.

7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric (1 mark):**
   *CPS Example:* A smart speaker provides a **physical microphone mute button** (consent to listen must be explicit, not implicit), a **voice history dashboard** (users can review and delete recordings), and **one-click data deletion**.

**(b) — Block Diagram of PbD Across CPS Layers (7 marks)**

*Block Diagram (describe verbally for written exam):*

```
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
  | L5: APPLICATION LAYER                                          |
  |     PbD: Granular consent UI, DSAR portal, transparency logs  |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              ^
                              |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
  | L4: CLOUD / BACKEND LAYER                                     |
  |     PbD: AES-256 at rest, secure enclaves, differential       |
  |     privacy for analytics, ISO 27701 certification            |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              ^
                              |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
  | L3: NETWORK LAYER                                             |
  |     PbD: TLS 1.3 (TCP), DTLS 1.3 (UDP/CoAP), default-deny    |
  |     firewall, mutual authentication                           |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              ^
                              |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
  | L2: EDGE / FOG LAYER                                          |
  |     PbD: Local pseudonymization, on-device ML, edge          |
  |     encryption, minimal data forwarding                       |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              ^
                              |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
  | L1: PERCEPTION LAYER (Sensors / Actuators)                    |
  |     PbD: Minimum sampling rate, local-only processing,        |
  |     physical consent indicators (e.g., LED for recording)     |
  +---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct identification and labeling of all 5 CPS layers: **2 marks**
- Mapping of at least 5 distinct PbD mechanisms to specific layers: **3 marks**
- Explanation of cross-layer privacy flow (e.g., how Principle 5 — End-to-End Security — spans all layers): **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, PECPT743 Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **"Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum"** principle of PbD. Explain with examples how three modern privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs) can break the perceived trade-off between privacy and functionality in CPS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the PbD framework to design a **privacy-respecting Smart Healthcare CPS** for remote patient monitoring. Identify the privacy risks at each CPS layer and specify the PbD principles and technical countermeasures you would employ. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Positive-Sum Principle & PETs (7 marks)**

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
The **"Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum"** principle, one of Ann Cavoukian's seven foundational principles, **rejects the false dichotomy** that privacy must be sacrificed to achieve functionality, or vice versa. It asserts that well-architected systems can deliver **both strong privacy and full functional capability simultaneously** through *innovative design* rather than *compromise*. In CPS, this is particularly important because stakeholders often wrongly assume that personalized services require centralized personal data collection.

**Three PETs that break the trade-off (5 marks — approx. 1.5–2 marks each):**

1. **Differential Privacy (DP) (2 marks):**
   - **Functionality preserved:** Population-level analytics, trend detection, and statistical insights.
   - **Privacy preserved:** Calibrated noise is added to query results so that no individual record can be re-identified.
   - **CPS Example:** A smart grid utility injects **Laplace noise** with privacy budget $\epsilon = 0.1$ into aggregate load reports, enabling demand forecasting while mathematically guaranteeing household-level privacy.
   - **Formal definition (1 mark):**
     An algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ is $\epsilon$-differentially private if for neighbouring datasets $D_1, D_2$:
     $$
     \Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_2) \in S]
     $$

2. **Federated Learning (FL) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Functionality preserved:** A high-accuracy global ML model is trained across many devices.
   - **Privacy preserved:** Raw data never leaves the device; only model gradients are shared.
   - **CPS Example:** Smartwatches from thousands of users collaboratively train a cardiac arrhythmia detection model, with raw ECG data remaining on each device.

3. **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Functionality preserved:** A prover can demonstrate possession of a credential (e.g., medical insurance) to a verifier.
   - **Privacy preserved:** The credential itself is never revealed.
   - **CPS Example:** A patient proves insurance coverage to a medical IoT device without disclosing policy number, personal ID, or medical history.

**(b) — PbD Design for Smart Healthcare CPS (7 marks)**

**Privacy Risks and Countermeasures by Layer (5 marks):**

| **CPS Layer** | **Privacy Risk** | **PbD Principle Applied** | **Countermeasure** |
|---|---|---|---|
| L1 Perception (Wearable sensors — ECG, SpO2) | Continuous capture of sensitive biometric data; risk of covert recording | P2 (Default), P7 (User-Centric) | Min sampling rate by default; physical LED indicates active recording; user-controlled mute |
| L2 Edge (Smartphone gateway) | Local storage of unencrypted health data on compromised device | P3 (Embedded), P5 (Lifecycle) | On-device encryption (AES-256); secure enclave processing |
| L3 Network (Wi-Fi/Cellular to cloud) | Man-in-the-middle interception of health telemetry | P5 (Lifecycle) | TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication; certificate pinning |
| L4 Cloud (EHR database) | Mass data breach; insider threats; re-identification risk | P3 (Embedded), P4 (Positive-Sum), P5 (Lifecycle) | AES-256 at rest; pseudonymization; homomorphic encryption for analytics; access logs; differential privacy for research datasets |
| L5 Application (Patient/Doctor apps) | Uncontrolled data sharing; lack of transparency | P6 (Transparency), P7 (User-Centric) | Granular consent dashboard; DSAR portal; plain-language privacy notices; one-click data export and deletion |

**Design Philosophy Summary (2 marks):**
The system embodies PbD because:
- Privacy is **engineered in from the start** (P1, P3) — not added later.
- The default configuration is the **most private one** (P2) — local processing, opt-in cloud.
- Privacy and clinical functionality coexist (P4) via FL, DP, and on-device ML.
- The user is the **center of the design** (P7) with full data sovereignty rights.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PbD Questions (PECPT743):**
> 1. **Memorization without application:** Many students list the 7 principles from memory but **fail to provide a concrete CPS example** for each. The KTU 2024 scheme specifically rewards *application-level* understanding over rote recall. Always pair each principle with a CPS-specific example (smart grid, healthcare, IoT, AV, etc.).
> 2. **Confusing PbD with PbS (Privacy by Stealth) or PbP (Privacy by Policy):** PbD is **engineering-driven and proactive**. Statements like *"PbD means writing a privacy policy"* are **incorrect** and will be marked down. Privacy policies relate to **Principle 6 (Transparency)**, not the entire PbD framework.
> 3. **Treating principles as independent:** The 7 principles are **interlocking and mutually reinforcing**. A complete answer should demonstrate how they work *together* (e.g., Principles 2 + 7 jointly support user-centric defaults; Principles 3 + 5 jointly support embedded security).
> 4. **Forgetting the lifecycle dimension:** For Principle 5, students often mention encryption but **omit data destruction at End-of-Life**. KTU examiners explicitly test the *full lifecycle* (collection → destruction).
> 5. **No mention of Ann Cavoukian:** Omitting the originator of PbD in a 14-mark question is a **sure loss of 1 mark** in the definition component.
> 6. **Skipping the "Why" — Cost Asymmetry:** Examiners love the **cost-asymmetry argument** (cheap to fix at design, expensive after deployment). Mentioning this proactively earns bonus understanding marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Principles of PbD (PECPT743 Module 3):**

- **PbD Originator:** Dr. Ann Cavoukian, 2009. (Mandatory to mention.)
- **Core Philosophy:** Proactive, anticipatory, embedded, user-centric — *privacy in by design, not bolted on after*.
- **Number of Principles:** **7 Foundational Principles** (memorize all 7 by name and a one-line essence).
- **Principle 1:** Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial → use **PIA, LINDDUN, STRIDE** at design time.
- **Principle 2:** Privacy as the Default Setting → max privacy **ON by default**; users opt-out of sharing, not opt-in.
- **Principle 3:** Privacy Embedded into Design → privacy in **SRS, V&V, architecture** — first-class requirement.
- **Principle 4:** Full Functionality (Positive-Sum) → no privacy-vs-functionality trade-off; use **DP, FL, ZKP**.
- **Principle 5:** End-to-End Security (Full Lifecycle) → protection from **collection to destruction**; EoL crypto-erase.
- **Principle 6:** Visibility and Transparency → **audit logs, open-source firmware, certifications, plain-language policies**.
- **Principle 7:** Respect for User Privacy (User-Centric) → **granular consent, DSAR, just-in-time notices, easy withdrawal**.
- **Differential Privacy Formula:** $\Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_1) \in S] \leq e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{A}(D_2) \in S]$; smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy.
- **CPS Layers (L1–L5):** Perception → Edge → Network → Cloud → Application. Each layer requires distinct PbD mechanisms.
- **Key CPS Examples to remember:** Smart Grid (DP for load forecasting), Wearable Healthcare (local processing), Autonomous Vehicles (V2X pseudonyms), Smart Speakers (physical mute + local processing), Smart Meters (lifecycle encryption + EoL wipe).
- **PbD vs Related Concepts:**
  - **PbD ≠ Privacy Policy** (a policy is documentation; PbD is engineering).
  - **PbD ≠ GDPR Compliance** (GDPR mandates *some* PbD elements, but PbD is broader and engineering-driven).
  - **PbD ≠ Security** (security is a *subset* of PbD — Principle 5 is one of seven).
- **KTU 2024 Mark Weightage:** Expect at least **one 14-mark question** on PbD principles per ESE; the topic also features in **Part A** as 3-mark definitions.
- **PbD Maturity Score (PMS):** Conceptual metric $\sum w_i L_i$ ranging from 0 to 21; $\geq 18$ is strong PbD posture.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing PbD in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Implementing PbD in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privacy by Design (PbD)** is a proactive, systemic engineering paradigm that embeds privacy guarantees into the very architecture, algorithms, and operational lifecycle of a system, rather than retrofitting them post-deployment. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, implementing PbD refers to the disciplined application of Ann Cavoukian's **7 Foundational Principles** (originally formulated in 2009) across the sensing, computation, actuation, and communication layers of systems that tightly couple physical processes with digital control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Recall:** A CPS is a system in which physical processes are monitored, coordinated, and controlled by a computational and communication core. PbD in CPS therefore must protect not only **digital data** but also **derived inferences about the physical world** (location, behavior, identity, biometrics).

Formally, PbD implementation in CPS can be modeled as a constrained optimization:

$$\mathcal{P} = \arg\min_{\text{design}} \; \mathcal{L}_{\text{utility}}(\text{System}) \quad \text{subject to} \; \forall i \in \mathcal{I},\; \mathcal{P}_i^{\text{guarantee}} \geq \tau_i$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}$ is the privacy-preserving design configuration.
- $\mathcal{L}_{\text{utility}}$ is the system's functional loss function.
- $\mathcal{I}$ is the set of all 7 PbD principles.
- $\mathcal{P}_i^{\text{guarantee}}$ is the privacy guarantee for principle $i$.
- $\tau_i$ is the minimum threshold of compliance.

---

### 1.2 The 7 Foundational Principles of PbD (Cavoukian, 2009)

These are the **canonical pillars** that drive the KTU Module 3 syllabus:

| # | Principle | Core Tenet (Board Definition) |
|---|-----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Proactive not Reactive** | Prevent privacy breaches *before* they occur; anticipatory, not remedial. |
| 2 | **Privacy as the Default Setting** | No action required by the individual to protect privacy — it is built-in. |
| 3 | **Privacy Embedded into Design** | PbD is an essential component of the core functionality, not an add-on. |
| 4 | **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum** | Avoid false trade-offs (e.g., privacy *vs.* security). Achieve *both*. |
| 5 | **End-to-End Security** | Cryptographic and lifecycle protection from cradle to grave. |
| 6 | **Visibility and Transparency** | Operate per stated promises, verified by independent audits. |
| 7 | **Respect for User Privacy** | User-centric: keep the individual informed and empowered. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Out of the 14-mark questions, at least one sub-part will directly ask you to *"List and explain the 7 principles of PbD with CPS examples."* Memorize the **single-phrase keywords** in Column 2.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Privacy-First Smart House"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** Imagine you are designing a **smart home**.
>
> - **Reactive Privacy** = Installing CCTV *after* a burglary and then claiming you secured the house.
> - **Proactive Privacy (PbD)** = Building the house with **reinforced doors, one-way glass windows, and a peephole as the default architecture** — privacy is *part of the blueprint*, not a patch.
>
> In a **CPS** (like a smart car or pacemaker), the "house" is constantly *sensing* (cameras, LiDAR, ECGs), *computing* (edge AI, cloud analytics), and *actuating* (brakes, insulin pumps). A breach isn't just data loss — it can be **physical harm**. PbD ensures the blueprint itself never leaks the *physical* state of the occupant.

---

### 1.4 What Makes Implementing PbD in CPS *Uniquely Hard*?

Unlike traditional IT systems, CPS have three properties that complicate PbD:

1. **Convergence of Physical and Cyber** — Privacy leakage crosses into *physical safety* (e.g., inferring a patient's home occupancy from insulin pump data).
2. **Heterogeneity** — Sensors, actuators, PLCs, cloud, and edge devices each have different privacy capabilities.
3. **Real-Time Constraints** — Many CPS operate under hard deadlines (e.g., $\leq 10$ ms control loops), making heavy cryptographic overheads unacceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Statement:** "PbD in CPS is not a single technology; it is a **systems engineering discipline** combining PETs (Privacy-Enhancing Technologies), legal compliance (GDPR Art. 25), and architectural patterns."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Levels of PbD Implementation in CPS

PbD is implemented at **four architectural layers** of a CPS, each requiring distinct techniques:

#### Layer 1: **Perception/Sensing Layer**
- **What it does:** Raw data acquisition (cameras, microphones, accelerometers, biosensors).
- **PbD Techniques Applied:**
  - On-sensor **compression and quantization** (reduce raw fidelity).
  - **K-anonymity** at the sensor node before transmission.
  - **Differential privacy noise injection** directly on the sensor ADC output.
- **Why:** Even *before* data leaves the chip, the privacy footprint is shrunk.

#### Layer 2: **Communication/Network Layer**
- **What it does:** Wired/wireless data transport (CAN bus, MQTT, 5G, BLE).
- **PbD Techniques Applied:**
  - **End-to-end encryption** (TLS 1.3, DTLS).
  - **Pseudonymization** — replace device IDs with rotating tokens.
  - **Mix-zones** in vehicular CPS to prevent location tracking.
  - **Network slicing** with dedicated privacy SLAs.

#### Layer 3: **Computation/Edge-Cloud Layer**
- **What it does:** Data aggregation, ML inference, analytics.
- **PbD Techniques Applied:**
  - **Federated Learning (FL):** Train models *without* centralizing raw data.
  - **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC):** Joint computation over secret-shared inputs.
  - **Homomorphic Encryption (HE):** Compute on ciphertext.
  - **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs):** Hardware-isolated enclaves (e.g., ARM TrustZone, Intel SGX).
  - **Differential Privacy (DP)** during model training and release.

#### Layer 4: **Actuation/Control Layer**
- **What it does:** Closes the loop by acting on the physical world.
- **PbD Techniques Applied:**
  - **Purpose limitation** — actuator only fires for explicitly whitelisted commands.
  - **Consent-aware actuation** — system refuses to act on data the user revoked.
  - **Privacy-preserving feedback control** — e.g., masking actuator telemetry that could leak state.

---

### 2.2 The PbD Implementation Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

A KTU-correct end-to-end implementation pipeline:

1. **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA / DPIA)** — Identify personal data flows, threats, and required safeguards.
2. **Threat Modeling** — Use **LINDDUN**, **STRIDE**, or **MITRE ATT&CK for CPS** to map privacy threats.
3. **Data Minimization by Design** — Collect only the *minimum* fields and *minimum* granularity.
4. **Select PETs** — Choose a PET stack (DP, FL, HE, TEE, SMPC) per layer.
5. **Embed Default Privacy** — Make the most private configuration the *default boot state* of the device.
6. **Implement Lifecycle Controls** — Cryptographic erasure, retention expiry, granular consent.
7. **Verification & Audit** — Independent privacy audits; certification (e.g., ISO 27701, GDPR Art. 25).
8. **Continuous Monitoring** — Drift detection on privacy guarantees; re-PIA on system updates.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Note:** Most KTU 2024 numerical/derivation questions in Module 3 of PECPT743 are based on the **mathematical primitives** of Differential Privacy and information-theoretic privacy leakage.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Legend | Use in CPS |
|---------|----------------------|---------------|------------|
| **Differential Privacy (ε-DP)** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\mathcal{M}$ = mechanism, $D, D'$ = neighboring datasets differing in 1 record, $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget | Add Laplace/Gaussian noise to sensor data, ML model updates. |
| **Sensitivity** | $\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert_1$ or $\vert f(D) - f(D') \vert_2$ | $\Delta f$ = global sensitivity of query $f$ | Determines noise scale needed. |
| **Laplace Mechanism** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | $\text{Lap}(b)$ = Laplace distribution with scale $b$ | Standard DP for numeric queries. |
| **Gaussian Mechanism** | $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | $\delta$ = failure probability (for $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP) | Used in DP-SGD for ML training. |
| **k-Anonymity** | Every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on quasi-identifiers. | $k$ = anonymity set size | Anonymize sensor databases. |
| **l-Diversity** | Every equivalence class has at least $l$ *well-represented* sensitive values. | $l$ = diversity parameter | Strengthens k-anonymity. |
| **t-Closeness** | Distribution of sensitive attribute in each class is within distance $t$ of global distribution. | $t$ = Earth Mover's distance bound | Prevents attribute disclosure. |
| **Mutual Information Leakage** | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | $X$ = private variable, $Y$ = observable output, $H$ = Shannon entropy | Quantifies CPS privacy leakage. |
| **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | bits | Measures uncertainty / privacy level. |
| **Federated Averaging Update** | $w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \nabla F_k(w_t)$ | $w$ = global model, $K$ = clients, $n_k$ = local samples | FL on CPS edge devices. |
| **Homomorphic Encryption Eval.** | $\text{Dec}(\text{Eval}(f, \text{Enc}(x_1), \ldots, \text{Enc}(x_n))) = f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$ | $\text{Enc}, \text{Dec}, \text{Eval}$ | Compute on encrypted CPS data. |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | PbD Implementation in CPS | KTU-Relevant Example |
|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| **Healthcare** | Federated learning across hospitals; DP on biosensor streams. | Pacemaker sending ECG with DP noise. |
| **Smart Grid** | SMPC between utility companies; HE on smart meter data. | Aggregating consumption without revealing individual households. |
| **Vehicular (V2X)** | Pseudonymous certificates (V2X PKI); mix-zones; edge DP. | Self-driving car rotating its ID every 5 min. |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | TEEs on PLCs; purpose-limited actuation. | Factory sensor only reports aggregates to cloud. |
| **Smart Cities** | k-anonymous cameras; edge AI with on-device anonymization. | Traffic cameras blurring faces before storage. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Laplace Mechanism Noise Calibration for ε-DP

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical):** A CPS temperature sensor reports a noisy value $f(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ where $T_i$ is the reading from sensor $i$. Adjacent datasets $D$ and $D'$ differ by one record (one sensor reading). The controller demands that the published aggregate be $\varepsilon$-differentially private.

**Goal:** Derive the minimum noise scale $b$ for the Laplace mechanism.

---

**Step 1 — Formal Definition of ε-DP (from first principles).**

A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP if for all neighboring datasets $D, D'$ (where $D \sim D'$) and all measurable subsets $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

---

**Step 2 — Express the probability ratio using the Laplace PDF.**

The Laplace distribution with scale $b$ has density:

$$p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid \mu, b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert x - \mu \vert}{b}\right)$$

If $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Z$ where $Z \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)$, then $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] = \int_S p_{\text{Lap}}(z \mid f(D), b)\, dz$.

---

**Step 3 — Bound the ratio for any $x \in S$.**

For any $x \in S$:

$$\frac{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D), b)}{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D'), b)} = \frac{\exp(-\frac{\vert x - f(D) \vert}{b})}{\exp(-\frac{\vert x - f(D') \vert}{b})}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\ln \frac{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D), b)}{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D'), b)} = \frac{\vert x - f(D') \vert - \vert x - f(D) \vert}{b}$$

By the **reverse triangle inequality**:

$$\vert x - f(D') \vert - \vert x - f(D) \vert \leq \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert$$

---

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of Global Sensitivity.**

The $\ell_1$ global sensitivity of a function $f$ is:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert_1$$

Substituting the bound from Step 3:

$$\ln \frac{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D), b)}{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D'), b)} \leq \frac{\Delta f}{b}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D), b)}{p_{\text{Lap}}(x \mid f(D'), b)} \leq \exp\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right)$$

---

**Step 5 — Set the bound equal to $e^{\varepsilon}$ to find $b$.**

For the mechanism to satisfy $\varepsilon$-DP, we need:

$$\exp\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) = e^{\varepsilon}$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\frac{\Delta f}{b} = \varepsilon$$

Solving for $b$:

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$$

---

**Step 6 — Final Result (KTU Board Answer).**

$$\boxed{\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The smaller the privacy budget $\varepsilon$ (stronger privacy), the larger the noise $b$ (worse utility). This is the **fundamental privacy-utility trade-off** in CPS sensor noise calibration.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) in CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** Any 14-mark question on *"Compare PETs for CPS"* should present a **structured matrix** like the one below with at least **5 dimensions** of comparison.

| Dimension | Differential Privacy (DP) | Federated Learning (FL) | Homomorphic Encryption (HE) | Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC) | Trusted Execution Environments (TEE) |
|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Privacy Guarantee** | Mathematical (ε,δ) | Heuristic + DP-augmented | Computational (cryptographic) | Cryptographic (information-theoretic or crypto) | Hardware-rooted trust |
| **Computation Overhead** | Low–Medium | Medium (per-client training) | **High** (10×–1000×) | High (communication-bound) | Low (hardware-isolated) |
| **Communication Overhead** | Low (no extra rounds) | Medium (gradient updates) | Medium (ciphertext expansion) | **Very High** (interaction rounds) | Low |
| **Suitability for Real-Time CPS** | **High** (sensor-level DP) | Medium (training is async) | Low (latency too high for control loops) | Low (multi-round) | **High** (in-enclave compute) |
| **Threat Model Coverage** | Output privacy | Training data privacy | Data-at-rest & in-use | Joint computation privacy | Software/OS-level adversaries |
| **Trusted Base** | None beyond noise | Aggregator (honest-but-curious) | Crypto parameters | Honest majority / dishonest minority | Hardware vendor (Intel, ARM) |
| **Energy Cost on Edge** | Very Low | High (gradient compute) | **Very High** | High | Low–Medium |
| **Post-Quantum Resistance** | Yes (structure-agnostic) | Depends on underlying crypto | No (most schemes not PQ) | Depends on protocol | Depends on hardware |
| **KTU Example** | ECG noise injection in pacemaker | Smart-grid load forecasting | Encrypted traffic flow aggregation | Privacy-preserving V2X collision check | Secure boot in industrial PLC |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — Privacy Budget Composition in CPS

> [!TIP]
> **Question Type:** "A CPS applies DP three times during one data flow. If $\varepsilon_1 = 0.3$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$, $\varepsilon_3 = 0.2$, what is the total privacy loss?"

**Step 1 — State the Sequential Composition Theorem.**

For a sequence of mechanisms $\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{M}_k$ each providing $\varepsilon_i$-DP, the total privacy loss is bounded by:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

---

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute the result.**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.0}$$

---

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation (mandatory for full marks).**

A total $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 1.0$ is considered **moderately private**. KTU exam tip: state whether this is *acceptable* per the application — healthcare typically needs $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$, while aggregate analytics tolerates $\varepsilon \leq 3.0$.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step — Federated Averaging Update for CPS Edge Devices

Given $K$ edge devices each holding $n_k$ samples, the global model update under FL is:

**Step 1 — Local loss on device $k$:**

$$F_k(w) = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} \ell(w; x_i^{(k)}, y_i^{(k)})$$

**Step 2 — Compute local gradient:**

$$g_k = \nabla F_k(w_t)$$

**Step 3 — Server-side weighted aggregation (FedAvg):**

$$w_{t+1} = w_t - \eta \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} g_k$$

Where $N = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k$.

**Step 4 — Privacy Augmentation (DP-FedAvg):**

Each $g_k$ is clipped to norm $\leq C$ and Gaussian noise is added:

$$g_k' = \frac{g_k}{\max(1, \frac{\vert\vert g_k \vert\vert_2}{C})} + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 C^2 I)$$

The server never sees raw $g_k$, only $g_k'$. The raw CPS sensor data **never leaves the device** — embodying Principle 3 of PbD (Privacy Embedded into Design).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: 7-Principle PbD Mapping onto the 4-Layer CPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["Privacy by Design Implementation Map"]
    nodeL1["Layer 1: Perception/Sensing"]
    nodeL2["Layer 2: Communication/Network"]
    nodeL3["Layer 3: Edge Cloud Computation"]
    nodeL4["Layer 4: Actuation Control"]
    nodeP1["P1: Proactive not Reactive"]
    nodeP2["P2: Privacy as Default"]
    nodeP3["P3: Embedded into Design"]
    nodeP4["P4: Full Functionality Positive Sum"]
    nodeP5["P5: End to End Security"]
    nodeP6["P6: Visibility and Transparency"]
    nodeP7["P7: Respect for User Privacy"]
    nodeS1["On sensor DP noise K anonymity"]
    nodeS2["TLS DTLS Pseudonymization Mix zones"]
    nodeS3["Federated Learning SMPC Homomorphic TEE"]
    nodeS4["Purpose limited actuation Consent aware"]
    nodeAudit["Independent privacy audit"]
    nodeConsent["Granular user consent UI"]

    nodeStart --> nodeL1
    nodeStart --> nodeL2
    nodeStart --> nodeL3
    nodeStart --> nodeL4
    nodeL1 --> nodeS1
    nodeL2 --> nodeS2
    nodeL3 --> nodeS3
    nodeL4 --> nodeS4
    nodeP1 --> nodeS1
    nodeP2 --> nodeS3
    nodeP3 --> nodeS1
    nodeP3 --> nodeS2
    nodeP3 --> nodeS3
    nodeP3 --> nodeS4
    nodeP4 --> nodeS3
    nodeP5 --> nodeS2
    nodeP6 --> nodeAudit
    nodeP7 --> nodeConsent
    nodeAudit --> nodeL3
    nodeConsent --> nodeL4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each CPS layer (L1–L4) is the *application surface*; each Principle (P1–P7) is the *governance driver*. PETs (S1–S4) are the *technical instruments* that translate principles into implementation.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: End-to-End PbD Implementation Lifecycle in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeE["Step 5: Default Privacy Configuration"]

    nodeG["Step 7: Independent Audit ISO 27701"]

    nodeI["Re PIA on System Update"]

```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The loop back from "Continuous Monitoring" to "Privacy Impact Assessment" is the **proactive, not reactive** property (Principle 1). State this explicitly in your answer.

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: PET Trade-off Triangle in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTri["CPS PET Trade off Triangle"]
    nodePriv["PRIVACY GUARANTEE"]
```

**Reading guide:** HE and SMPC are strong on **privacy** but weak on **real-time performance**. DP and TEE are strong on **real-time performance**, making them the *default choice* for CPS control loops. Federated learning is the *sweet spot* for **utility + privacy** in CPS analytics workloads.
