## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A carries 2 marks for the answer + 1 mark for keyword precision / neatness. Keep answers **≤ 6 lines** with bullet keywords.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define Privacy by Design. Mention any three of its foundational principles.*

**Model Answer:**

Privacy by Design (PbD) is a proactive engineering paradigm formulated by **Ann Cavoukian (2009)** that embeds privacy into the architecture, algorithms, and operations of a system rather than treating it as an add-on.

Three foundational principles:

1. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — No user action is required to be protected.
2. **End-to-End Security** — Full lifecycle cryptographic protection of personal data.
3. **Respect for User Privacy** — User-centric design with informed consent and empowerment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write "Privacy by Design is a software patch" — **lose 1 mark** instantly. The correct phrasing is *"architectural and systemic embedding."*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*List any three Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) used in CPS and give one CPS example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| PET | CPS Example |
|-----|-------------|
| **Differential Privacy** | Injecting Laplace noise into a smart-meter reading before transmission. |
| **Federated Learning** | Training a predictive maintenance model across factory PLCs without sharing raw sensor data. |
| **Homomorphic Encryption** | Computing traffic density over encrypted V2X messages in a smart-city control center. |
| **TEE (Trusted Execution Environment)** | Running medical inference inside an ARM TrustZone enclave on a wearable. |
| **Secure Multi-Party Computation** | Privacy-preserving billing between competing EV charging networks. |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Both alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) must be of **equal cognitive level** (per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy). Solve completely for full marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** List and explain the **seven foundational principles of Privacy by Design** as proposed by Ann Cavoukian. For each principle, give **one concrete CPS example**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Design a **Privacy-by-Design implementation framework** for a **Smart Healthcare CPS** (e.g., wearable cardiac monitor → edge gateway → hospital cloud). Show how principles P2, P3, and P5 are operationalized at the sensing, communication, and computation layers. Identify the PETs chosen and justify. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a) — Seven Principles with CPS Examples (7 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** — *Example:* Designing a smart meter with DP noise *before* the data leaves the chip, rather than anonymizing after a breach.

2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — *Example:* A smart speaker that ships with **voice recording disabled by default**; the user must opt-in.

3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** — *Example:* TEE enclave is a hardware-level design element, not a software add-on. The pacemaker has purpose-limited actuation wired into firmware.

4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** — *Example:* Federated learning on wearable ECGs enables accurate arrhythmia detection *without* centralizing patient data — both utility and privacy are maximized.

5. **End-to-End Security** — *Example:* AES-256 encryption on the wearable, TLS 1.3 in transit, encrypted-at-rest with **cryptographic erasure** capability in the cloud.

6. **Visibility and Transparency** — *Example:* Publishing the privacy policy as machine-readable metadata; independent third-party audits; certification under ISO 27701.

7. **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric** — *Example:* Granular consent dashboard on a hospital portal where the patient can revoke data sharing for a specific research study at any time.

---

**Part (b) — PbD Implementation Framework for Smart Healthcare CPS (7 marks):**

**Layer-wise PET Selection (3 marks):**

| Layer | PET | Operationalization |
|-------|-----|---------------------|
| Sensing (Wearable) | **Differential Privacy** | ECG samples are quantized to 8-bit, then Laplace noise with $\varepsilon = 0.5$ added. |
| Communication (BLE → Phone → Cloud) | **TLS 1.3 + Pseudonymization** | Device ID is a rotating 128-bit token. |
| Computation (Hospital Cloud) | **Federated Learning + TEE** | Multi-hospital arrhythmia model trained FL-fashion; aggregations run inside an Intel SGX enclave. |

**Operationalization of P2, P3, P5 (4 marks):**

- **P2 (Privacy as Default):** Default boot state of wearable is "minimal telemetry mode" — only heart rate at 1 Hz. Higher-frequency ECG requires explicit patient opt-in via companion app.
- **P3 (Privacy Embedded into Design):** The TEE enclave is a *hardware root of trust* — the device physically cannot load aggregation code outside the enclave. This is a *design-time* architectural decision, not a runtime patch.
- **P5 (End-to-End Security):** AES-256 on device → TLS 1.3 in transit → server-side encryption with **cryptographic erasure** keys tied to patient consent. On consent revocation, the encryption key is destroyed, making the data cryptographically unreadable.

**Justification (1 mark):** Differential Privacy is chosen over HE because cardiac monitoring is *real-time* (control loop latency $\leq 100$ ms), and HE is too slow. FL is chosen over centralized training to satisfy *data minimization* and keep PHI on-premises.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Differential Privacy** with mathematical formulation. A CPS sensor network aggregates temperature readings using the Laplace mechanism. If global sensitivity $\Delta f = 2$ and privacy budget $\varepsilon = 0.5$, compute the noise scale and the resulting $\varepsilon$-DP guarantee. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Compare **Differential Privacy, Federated Learning, and Homomorphic Encryption** as Privacy-Enhancing Technologies in CPS, using a structured matrix covering at least 5 dimensions. Conclude with the recommendation for a **real-time vehicular CPS** scenario. *(7 marks)*

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a) — Differential Privacy Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of ε-DP (2 marks):** A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for all neighboring datasets $D \sim D'$ and all measurable sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

2. **Global Sensitivity (1 mark):** $\Delta f = \max_{D \sim D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert_1$. Here, $\Delta f = 2$ (given).

3. **Laplace Mechanism (2 marks):** The mechanism is $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + Z$, where $Z \sim \text{Lap}(0, b)$ and $b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$.

4. **Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given $\Delta f = 2$ and $\varepsilon = 0.5$:

$$b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4$$

**Result:** The CPS should add Laplace noise with scale **b = 4** to each released aggregate to satisfy $\varepsilon = 0.5$ differential privacy.

---

**Part (b) — PET Comparison Matrix + Recommendation (7 marks):**

**Structured Matrix (5 marks — 1 per row):**

| Dimension | DP | FL | HE |
|-----------|----|----|-----|
| Privacy Guarantee | Mathematical (ε,δ) | Heuristic + DP-augmented | Computational (crypto) |
| Real-Time Suitability | **High** | Medium (async) | **Low** (10×–1000× overhead) |
| Communication Cost | Low | Medium (gradients) | Medium (ciphertext blow-up) |
| Trusted Base | None | Aggregator | Crypto parameters |
| Energy on Edge Device | Low | High | Very High |

**Recommendation for Real-Time Vehicular CPS (2 marks):** Use **Differential Privacy** at the sensor layer for live telemetry (e.g., vehicle speed, GPS) because:
- Control loop latency budget is $\leq 10$ ms — HE/SMPC are too slow.
- FL is unsuitable for *per-message* privacy in safety-critical V2X braking events; it is better suited for *periodic* model training of collision-prediction models.
- DP can be implemented directly on the CAN bus gateway with negligible overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing "Privacy by Design" with "Privacy Enhancing Technologies"** — PbD is the *philosophy/framework*; PETs are the *tools*. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 2. **Writing "(D) P" instead of "ε-DP"** — Always use the correct Greek letter ε (epsilon), never the Latin "e".
> 3. **Forgetting units / context** in numeric answers — Always end a Laplace mechanism solution with the **concrete numerical value of b** and a one-line *engineering interpretation*.
> 4. **Skipping the example for each principle** — In Q-A(a), each principle MUST be paired with a CPS-specific example, not a generic one (e.g., not "a website" — must be "a smart meter," "a pacemaker," etc.).
> 5. **No mention of lifecycle** in framework answers — PbD demands cradle-to-grave; mention *cryptographic erasure* and *post-revocation* behavior.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and read **twice** before the exam. Each bullet is a potential **2–3 mark** pickup.

- [ ] **PbD = Philosophy; PETs = Tools.** Always distinguish between the two in answers.
- [ ] **7 Principles** = Proactive, Default, Embedded, Full-Functionality, End-to-End Security, Transparency, User-Centric. (Mnemonic: **"ProD Em Fun End Trans User"**)
- [ ] **CPS has 4 layers** for PbD: Sensing, Network, Edge-Cloud Computation, Actuation.
- [ ] **Differential Privacy** is the only **mathematically proven** PET — others are heuristic or crypto-assumption-based.
- [ ] **Laplace Mechanism formula:** $b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}$ — memorized cold for any 14-mark question.
- [ ] **Composition Theorem:** Sequential privacy loss is **additive** — $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.
- [ ] **K-Anonymity ≠ DP**: k-anonymity is a *syntactic* property, DP is *semantic*; DP is strictly stronger.
- [ ] **HE is slow** — never recommend it for *real-time CPS control loops*; use DP or TEE instead.
- [ ] **FL + DP** is the *de facto* gold standard for CPS analytics with formal privacy.
- [ ] **GDPR Article 25** is the **legal anchor** of PbD in the EU — the KTU 2024 syllabus often pairs PbD with this article.
- [ ] **PbD ≠ "Privacy by Policy"** — Policies are documents; PbD is **code, hardware, and architecture**.
- [ ] **CPS-specific threats:** re-identification from sensor metadata, location inference from actuation logs, timing side-channels in control loops.
- [ ] **Default boot state** must be the **most private** configuration (Principle 2).
- [ ] **Continuous monitoring** + **re-PIA** closes the loop on Principle 1 (proactive).
- [ ] **Auditability** requires *machine-readable* privacy policies (PrivacyML, P3P) for automated verification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies of PbD in Action
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Studies of PbD in Action # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Privacy by Design (PbD) in Action** refers to the practical, real-world implementation, deployment, and operational evaluation of the seven foundational principles of PbD (originally articulated by **Dr. Ann Cavoukian, former Information & Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, Canada, in the 1990s**) within production-grade systems, products, services, and regulatory frameworks. A "case study" in PbD is a documented instance where one or more of the seven principles have been engineered into the system's architecture *before* deployment, rather than being retrofitted after a privacy breach. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECPT743 — Privacy in Cyber-Physical Systems), case studies of PbD in action are examined to bridge the gap between **theoretical privacy engineering** and **operational deployment** in environments where physical processes, IoT sensor data, and cloud analytics intersect. ### The 7 Foundational Principles of PbD (Mandatory Recall) > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic — "PFPFEVR"**: *Proactive, Default, Full-functionality, Positive-sum, End-to-end, Visible, Respect user.* | # | Principle | Core Idea | |---|-----------|-----------| | 1 | **Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial** | Anticipate privacy invasions *before* they occur. | | 2 | **Privacy as the Default Setting** | No user action required to be protected. | | 3 | **Privacy Embedded into Design** | Privacy is integral to architecture, not a bolt-on. | | 4 | **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** | Privacy ≠ loss of functionality ("win-win"). | | 5 | **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection** | Secure from cradle to grave, data to destruction. | | 6 | **Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open** | Components & practices are verifiable. | | 7 | **Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric** | Strong privacy defaults, user-empowering controls. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Privacy House" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: PbD is like building a *safe house* rather than a *house with safe retrofit*.** > > Imagine you are constructing a new residence. There are two schools of thought: > - **Retrofit Approach (Privacy as Afterthought)**: Build the house with beautiful glass walls (open data), then *after* a burglary attempt, install steel shutters. The breach has already happened, and the retrofit is always incomplete. > - **Privacy by Design**: The architect *first* consults a security engineer. The walls are made of **privacy-preserving smart glass** (data minimization), the doors have **biometric locks by default** (zero-touch privacy), and there is a **safe room** in the foundation (end-to-end security). The homeowner *lives securely without lifting a finger* — privacy is the *default*, not a setting they must toggle. > > In the same way, when Apple bakes **Differential Privacy** into iOS before telemetry is ever collected, or when the EU **GDPR Article 25** mandates "Data Protection by Design and by Default," privacy is treated as a **structural engineering material** — not a coat of paint applied after construction. ## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in CPS (Cyber-Physical Systems) In a **Cyber-Physical System** (CPS) — e.g., a smart grid, autonomous vehicle, medical infusion pump, or smart factory — privacy is uniquely challenging because: - **Physical data** (location, gait, heart-rate, energy-usage) is **non-revocable** — once leaked, the secret is permanently out. - **Sensor data is continuous**, generating millions of data points per device per day. - **Multiple stakeholders** (manufacturer, cloud provider, user, regulators) share the data lifecycle. - **Consequences are tangible**: a privacy breach in CPS can lead to physical harm, not just reputational loss. Therefore, studying **PbD case studies in action** demonstrates how industry leaders and regulators have *engineered* privacy into CPS at scale. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Analytical Framework for Evaluating a PbD Case Study When answering KTU questions on this topic, every case study must be evaluated against a **structured 4-layer analytical lens**: ### Layer 1 — *Threat Model Identification* - **Who** is the adversary? (e.g., honest-but-curious cloud provider, malicious insider, network sniffer, statistical re-identifier) - **What** is the sensitive asset? (raw sensor stream, derived statistics, identity, behavior pattern) - **What** is the attack vector? (re-identification, linkage, inference, side-channel) ### Layer 2 — *Mapping to the 7 PbD Principles* For each case study, explicitly map *at least 3* of the 7 principles that are **engineered into the system architecture** (not just claimed in marketing). ### Layer 3 — *Privacy Mechanism Used* - **Differential Privacy (DP)** — Apple, Google, US Census Bureau. - **Federated Learning (FL)** — Google Gboard, Apple QuickType. - **k-Anonymity / l-Diversity / t-Closeness** — healthcare data releases. - **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** — encrypted cloud analytics on CPS data. - **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE / Secure Enclaves)** — Apple Secure Enclave, Android StrongBox. - **End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)** — Signal, WhatsApp, iMessage. - **On-Device Processing** — Apple Private Cloud Compute, federated analytics. ### Layer 4 — *Trade-off Analysis (Principle #4 — Positive-Sum)* - **Utility loss** vs. **Privacy gain** (e.g., adding more noise to DP reduces accuracy but increases privacy). - **Latency cost** vs. **Confidentiality gain** (on-device ML is slower per cloud ML but data never leaves the device). - **User friction** vs. **User empowerment** (mandatory vs. opt-in consent dialogs). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Essential Quantitative Foundations) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following are the **core mathematical building blocks** underlying the most-tested PbD mechanisms. Memorize the formula, the *meaning* of every variable, and the *boundary condition*. | # | Mechanism | Core Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit / Domain | |---|-----------|--------------|------------------|---------------| | 1 | **Differential Privacy (Laplace Mechanism)** | $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | $D$: dataset, $f$: query function, $\Delta f$: global sensitivity, $\varepsilon$: privacy budget | $\varepsilon \in (0, \infty)$ — *smaller* = *stronger* privacy | | 2 | **Differential Privacy (Gaussian Mechanism)** | $M(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^2\right)$ with $\sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$ | $\delta$: failure probability, $\sigma$: std. deviation | $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP | | 3 | **Composition Theorem (Sequential Queries)** | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | Each query on the same dataset *consumes* budget additively | Budget exhaustion ≈ privacy erosion | | 4 | **k-Anonymity** | Every record is *indistinguishable* from at least $k-1$ other records | $k$: anonymity set size | $k \geq 5$ recommended; $k = 1$ ⇒ no anonymity | | 5 | **Entropy (Information Leakage)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $H$: Shannon entropy in *bits* | $H = 0$ ⇒ no leakage; higher = more uncertainty | | 6 | **Mutual Information (Privacy Leakage Bound)** | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | $X$: secret, $Y$: observable | Lower bound ⇒ better privacy | | 7 | **Re-identification Probability** | $P_{\text{re-id}} = \frac{1}{k}$ for $k$-anonymous dataset | $k$: equivalence class size | $P \to 0$ ⇒ strong anonymity | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall — never confuse the symbol `|x|` inside markdown tables with the column separator `|`.** In all formulas above, **vertical bars** denoting set membership, conditional probability, or absolute value must be typeset using $\vert$ or $\mid$ when rendered in LaTeX, and never as a raw pipe character that breaks table syntax. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These mathematical primitives are not academic curiosities — they are the **privacy backbone** of: - **US Census 2020** (first DP-protected decennial census, $\varepsilon = 12.6$ for redistricting, $\varepsilon = 17.14$ for demographic data). - **Apple's iOS telemetry** ($\varepsilon \approx 1$–$8$ per report, per-device, per-day). - **Google's COVID-19 Community Mobility Reports** (DP-aggregated mobility flows). - **Smart Meter Data** (k-anonymity + DP for household energy profiling in EU). ## 2.4 Architectural Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right PbD Mechanism | CPS Scenario | Recommended PbD Mechanism | Reasoning | |--------------|----------------------------|-----------| | Smart-grid load forecasting | **Differential Privacy + Aggregation** | Aggregate noise hides individual household patterns while preserving grid statistics. | | Autonomous vehicle V2X communication | **E2EE + Short-lived Pseudonyms** | Prevents long-term location tracking between vehicles. | | Wearable health monitor (continuous PPG/ECG) | **On-device ML + TEE** | Raw biometrics never leave the wrist; only model outputs cross the trust boundary. | | Industrial IoT predictive maintenance | **Federated Learning** | Multiple factories collaboratively train one model without sharing raw vibration data. | | Smart home voice assistant | **Local wake-word + Cloud opt-in** | Wake-word detection on-device (PbD default); cloud STT only on user invocation. | | Genomic data sharing | **Homomorphic Encryption + DP** | Computation on encrypted genomes, with DP noise on aggregate statistics. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > The following two derivations are the **most frequently tested quantitative components** in KTU Part B questions on PbD case studies. Master both. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Laplace Mechanism for Differential Privacy ### 3.1.1 Setup We are given a dataset $D$ of size $n$, representing, for example, the **daily energy consumption** (in kWh) of $n$ households in a smart-grid CPS. The utility wants to publish the *mean consumption* $f(D) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ to the public for grid planning. However, publishing the raw mean would let an attacker with **auxiliary information** (e.g., a neighbor's exact consumption) re-identify individual households. ### 3.1.2 Define Global Sensitivity The **global sensitivity** $\Delta f$ of a function $f$ is the maximum change in its output when *one record* in $D$ is arbitrarily modified. For the *mean* function on a numeric attribute bounded in $[a, b]$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta f_{\text{mean}} &= \max_{D, D'} \left\vert f(D) - f(D') \right\vert \quad \text{where } D' \text{ differs from } D \text{ in exactly one record} \\ &= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \left\vert x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}} \right\vert \\ &\leq \frac{b - a}{n} \end{aligned} $$ For example, if $n = 1000$ households and $a = 0$, $b = 50$ kWh: $$ \Delta f_{\text{mean}} = \frac{50 - 0}{1000} = 0.05 \ \text{kWh} $$ ### 3.1.3 Sample Noise from Laplace Distribution The **Laplace distribution** with scale parameter $b$ has probability density function: $$ \text{Lap}(x \mid b) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right) $$ For $(\varepsilon)$-differential privacy, the scale $b$ is set to: $$ b = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} $$ The **DP-protected** mean is therefore: $$ M(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \text{where} \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right) $$ ### 3.1.4 Numerical Example Suppose the true mean is $f(D) = 12.43$ kWh, $\Delta f = 0.05$ kWh, and we choose $\varepsilon = 1.0$ (a typical Apple-scale privacy budget). $$ \begin{aligned} b &= \frac{0.05}{1.0} = 0.05 \\ \eta &\sim \text{Lap}(0.05) \\ M(D) &= 12.43 + \eta \end{aligned} $$ A typical realization: if $\eta = +0.038$ kWh, then the published value is $M(D) = 12.468$ kWh — within **1.4% error** of the true mean, while guaranteeing that the *presence or absence of any single household* in the dataset changes the probability of this output by at most a factor of $e^{\varepsilon} = e^{1.0} \approx 2.718$. ### 3.1.5 Interpretation > [!NOTE] > **Privacy guarantee:** The published noisy mean $M(D)$ would have come from any *neighboring* dataset $D'$ (one household added/removed) with probability at least $1/e \approx 36.8\%$. The attacker, therefore, cannot confidently claim *which specific household* influenced the published statistic. This is precisely how **Apple's iOS Differential Privacy** works when collecting emoji usage statistics, Safari URL visit counts, and Health data type histograms. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Achieving k-Anonymity via Generalization ### 3.2.1 Setup A hospital wants to release a dataset of patients for medical research. The schema includes **Quasi-Identifiers (QIs)**: ZIP code, age, gender — and a **Sensitive Attribute (SA)**: diagnosis. Direct identifiers (name, SSN) are removed, but a **linkage attack** is still possible using QIs. ### 3.2.2 Definition A release is **$k$-anonymous** if, for every tuple of QI values, **at least $k$ records** in the released dataset share that exact QI tuple. Formally: $$ \forall \ \mathbf{q} \in \text{QI}, \quad \left\vert \{ r \in D : \text{QI}(r) = \mathbf{q} \} \right\vert \geq k $$ ### 3.2.3 Generalization Strategy ZIP codes (e.g., 5-digit, ~40,000 possible values) and exact age (0–120) are *too granular*. We **suppress** or **generalize** them: | Original (pre-PbD) | Generalized (PbD-applied, k=5) | |--------------------|-------------------------------| | ZIP: 682001 | ZIP: 682*** (first 3 digits) | | Age: 47 | Age: 45–50 (5-year band) | | Gender: M | Gender: M | | Diagnosis: Diabetes | Diagnosis: Diabetes | After generalization, suppose the equivalence class `{(682***, 45-50, M)}` contains 8 patients. Then $k = 8 \geq 5$ ✓. ### 3.2.4 Information Loss Metric A common metric for utility loss is the **Normalized Certainty Penalty (NCP)**: $$ \text{NCP} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\text{height}(G_i)}{h_i} $$ where $G_i$ is the generalization hierarchy of the $i$-th QI and $h_i$ is its full domain size. Higher NCP = more privacy but lower utility. ### 3.2.5 Interpretation > [!NOTE] > **k-Anonymity in PbD Case Studies:** k-anonymity is the privacy backbone of healthcare PbD case studies (e.g., the US HIPAA Safe Harbor method, the UK's ONS census tabular releases). It satisfies PbD Principle #2 (Default Privacy — re-identification is mitigated *by default* without user action) and Principle #6 (Transparency — the generalization rules are openly published). ## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix — PbD Mechanism vs. Property | PbD Mechanism | Privacy Property | Utility Cost | Default? | Verifiable? | |---------------|------------------|--------------|----------|-------------| | **Differential Privacy (Laplace)** | $(\varepsilon, 0)$-DP | Bounded (noise $\propto 1/\varepsilon$) | ✓ | ✓ (composition theorems) | | **Federated Learning** | Local DP + secure aggregation | Lower model accuracy | ✓ | Partial | | **k-Anonymity** | Equivalence class $\geq k$ | Information loss via generalization | ✓ | ✓ | | **Homomorphic Encryption** | IND-CPA semantic security | High compute overhead | ✓ | ✓ (cryptographic proof) | | **E2EE (Signal Protocol)** | Forward + post-compromise secrecy | Negligible | ✓ | ✓ (open protocol) | | **On-device TEE** | Hardware-isolated computation | Reduced feature set | ✓ | ✓ (attestation) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The 7 PbD Principles as an Engineered System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User Data Source
CPS Sensor Stream] --> B{Threat Model
Adversary Defined} B --> C[PbD Architectural Layer] C --> P1[Principle 1
Proactive Not Reactive] C --> P2[Principle 2
Privacy as Default] C --> P3[Principle 3
Embedded into Design] C --> P4[Principle 4
Positive-Sum Full Functionality] C --> P5[Principle 5
End-to-End Lifecycle Security] C --> P6[Principle 6
Visibility and Transparency] C --> P7[Principle 7
User-Centric Control] P1 --> D[Engineered Privacy Outcome] P2 --> D P3 --> D P4 --> D P5 --> D P6 --> D P7 --> D D --> E[Audit and Verification
Principle 6 Output] D --> F[User Empowerment
Principle 7 Output] E --> G[Continuous Improvement Loop
Principle 1 Output] F --> G G -.feedback.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Apple Differential Privacy Pipeline (PbD Case Study) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[User iOS Device
Emoji Typing Event] --> L[Local Tally
On Device Aggregation] L --> N[Noise Sampling
Laplace epsilon equals 1 to 8] N --> R[Record Submission
Once Per Day Per Device] R --> AS[Apple Aggregation Server
Secure Multiparty Computation] AS --> P[Published Statistic
e g Emoji Frequency Histogram] H[Adversary with Auxiliary Data] -.re identification attempt.-> P P -.noise bound.-> H H --> X[Attack Fails
Guaranteed by epsilon DP] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Federated Learning in Google Gboard (PbD Case Study) ```mermaid flowchart TB D1[Phone 1 Local Typing Data] --> M1[Local Model Update
Trained On Device] D2[Phone 2 Local Typing Data] --> M2[Local Model Update] D3[Phone N Local Typing Data] --> MN[Local Model Update] M1 --> SA[Secure Aggregation
Cryptographic Shuffling] M2 --> SA MN --> SA SA --> G[Global Server
Averages Updates] G --> GM[Updated Global Model] GM -.dispatch.-> D1 GM -.dispatch.-> D2 GM -.dispatch.-> D3 R[Raw Typing Data Never Leaves Device] -.guarantee.-> D1 R -.guarantee.-> D2 R -.guarantee.-> D3 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Apple App Tracking Transparency ATT (PbD Case Study) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Third Party App Launched] --> Q[ATT Prompt Displayed
Privacy as Default] Q --> U{User Choice} U -->|Allow Tracking| T[Identifier for Advertisers ID Shared] U -->|Ask App Not to Track| NT[IDFA Zeroed Out
Tracking Blocked] T --> AD[Personalized Ads Enabled] NT --> AD2[Contextual Ads Only
No Profile Built] AD --> M[Privacy Loss Risk] AD2 --> M2[PbD Default Preserved] M --> H[User Re Evaluation] M2 --> H H --> Q ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Block Level Functional Architecture of GDPR Article 25 PbD Mandate ```mermaid flowchart LR O[Data Collection Point] --> A1[Step 1 Minimalization Check] A1 --> A2[Step 2 Purpose Limitation Check] A2 --> A3[Step 3 Storage Limitation Check] A3 --> A4[Step 4 Default Privacy Setting] A4 --> A5[Step 5 User Consent Flow] A5 --> A6[Step 6 DPIA Trigger] A6 --> V{Verification Audit Log} V -->|Pass| P[Processing Authorized] V -->|Fail| H[Processing Halted] P --> C[Continuous Monitoring] H --> C C -.feedback.-> A1 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **Differentiate between "Privacy by Design" and "Privacy as an Afterthought" with a CPS example.** **Model Answer:** | Dimension | Privacy by Design | Privacy as Afterthought | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Timing of integration | Built *before* deployment | Added *after* a breach or complaint | | Architectural position | Foundational layer | Surface-level patch | | Cost profile | Lower long-term cost | Higher remediation cost (fines, retrofit) | | User trust | High (default protection) | Low (opt-in burden) | | **CPS Example** | **Apple's on-device Siri wake-word detection — the audio is never sent to the cloud unless the wake-word triggers a user-invoked request.** | **Early smart-meter rollouts that transmitted raw household energy data to a central server, then retroactively added aggregation only after consumer privacy complaints.** | *(Word count target: ~100–120 words = 3 marks under KTU valuation key.)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **List the 7 Foundational Principles of Privacy by Design proposed by Ann Cavoukian.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The 7 principles are: **(1)** Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial; **(2)** Privacy as the Default Setting; **(3)** Privacy Embedded into Design; **(4)** Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum; **(5)** End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection; **(6)** Visibility and Transparency — Keep it Open; **(7)** Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric. *(1 mark per principle ≈ 3–4 principles expected for full 3 marks, list format acceptable.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, **Part B questions carry an internal choice**. You must attempt **ONE of the two alternatives** offered. Each sub-part is structured as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) > `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **Differential Privacy in Apple's iOS is widely cited as a flagship PbD case study.** **(a)** Explain the **operational mechanism** by which Apple collects aggregate emoji usage statistics from iPhones **without recording any individual's input**, identifying the specific PbD principles (minimum 3) embedded in the design. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A smart-grid CPS wants to publish the **mean daily energy consumption (in kWh)** of $n = 2000$ households. The true mean is $\mu = 8.42$ kWh. Assume each household's consumption lies in $[0, 40]$ kWh. **Compute the global sensitivity**, and then **derive the Laplace-noise value** to be added for $\varepsilon = 2.0$ differential privacy. **Interpret** the privacy guarantee in plain English. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Apple's Differential Privacy Operational Mechanism (7 marks)** Apple's Differential Privacy (DP) pipeline, as published in their 2017 white paper, operates in the following structured sequence: 1. **Local On-Device Tally (Privacy Default — Principle #2, Embedded — Principle #3):** Each iOS device maintains a *local histogram* of events (e.g., emoji typed, Safari domain visited, HealthKit data type used). The event is counted in a **noisy local counter** before leaving the device. The raw event (e.g., the exact emoji, the exact URL) is **never transmitted**. 2. **Noise Sampling (Privacy Embedded — Principle #3, End-to-End Security — Principle #5):** The local counter is perturbed by a sample from the Laplace distribution, scaled by $\Delta f / \varepsilon$, where: - $\Delta f$ = global sensitivity (typically 1 for count queries). - $\varepsilon$ = privacy budget, set per "report" between **1 and 8** (Apple's stated range). 3. **Report Submission with Opt-in (User-Centric — Principle #7):** The noisy local histogram is sent to Apple's aggregation server **at most once per day per device**, and only if the user has **opted in** to iOS Analytics ("Share with Apple"). This embeds **Principle #7** (Respect for User Privacy). 4. **Server-Side Aggregation (Positive-Sum — Principle #4, End-to-End — Principle #5):** Apple sums the noisy histograms from millions of devices. Because Laplace noise is *zero-mean*, the aggregate converges to the true statistic as $n \to \infty$. **Utility is preserved (Principle #4)** despite the per-device noise. 5. **Transparency Publication (Visibility — Principle #6):** Apple publishes a **Privacy White Paper** detailing the algorithm, the $\varepsilon$ ranges, and the opt-out path. Researchers can (in principle) audit and replicate the guarantees. **PbD Principles Mapped (must list at least 3 for full marks):** - ✓ Principle #2: Default — privacy is on by default; no user action needed to be protected. - ✓ Principle #3: Embedded — noise injection is part of the *data path* itself. - ✓ Principle #5: End-to-End — protection spans device → network → server → publication. - ✓ Principle #6: Transparency — algorithm is openly documented. - ✓ Principle #7: User-Centric — explicit opt-in/opt-out. --- **Part (b) — Smart-Grid DP Mean Publication (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Compute Global Sensitivity:** The query function is $f(D) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$, where each $x_i \in [a, b] = [0, 40]$ and $n = 2000$. $$ \Delta f = \frac{b - a}{n} = \frac{40 - 0}{2000} = 0.02 \ \text{kWh} $$ **Step 2 — Laplace Scale Parameter:** $$ b_{\text{noise}} = \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.02}{2.0} = 0.01 \ \text{kWh} $$ **Step 3 — Sampled Noise & Published Value:** The DP-protected release is: $$ M(D) = f(D) + \eta, \quad \eta \sim \text{Lap}(0.01) $$ For illustration, suppose $\eta = +0.0084$ kWh is sampled. Then: $$ M(D) = 8.42 + 0.0084 = 8.4284 \ \text{kWh} $$ The relative error is: $$ \text{Relative Error} = \frac{\vert 0.0084 \vert}{8.42} \times 100\% \approx 0.0998\% \ \ (\text{negligible utility loss}) $$ **Step 4 — Plain-English Privacy Guarantee:** > For *any* household that is added to or removed from the dataset, the probability of the published value $M(D) = 8.4284$ changes by **at most a factor of $e^{2.0} \approx 7.389$**. Equivalently, an attacker observing the published mean cannot statistically determine whether *their specific neighbor* is in the dataset with confidence greater than $\frac{1}{e^{2}} \approx 13.5\%$. This is the **mathematical privacy promise** that the homeowner enjoys *by default*, with no opt-in required (PbD Principles #2 and #3). --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2) > `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **The European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Article 25 is often called a "regulatory embodiment" of Privacy by Design.** **(a)** Explain how **GDPR Article 25 ("Data Protection by Design and by Default")** operationalizes the 7 PbD principles, with **at least two concrete CPS examples** (e.g., a smart thermostat, a fitness tracker). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a **fitness-tracker CPS** releasing a healthcare dataset containing the QIs `{age, ZIP, gender}` and the sensitive attribute `{diagnosis}`. After generalization, the QI tuple `(age: 25–30, ZIP: 682***, gender: F)` covers only **2 patients**, while the tuple `(age: 25–30, ZIP: 682***, gender: M)` covers **9 patients**. **Identify the privacy failure**, name the **attack vector**, and **recommend a corrective PbD mechanism** with numerical justification. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — GDPR Article 25 as a Regulatory PbD Mandate (7 marks)** GDPR Article 25, effective from **May 25, 2018**, codifies the *legal obligation* of PbD for any organization processing EU residents' data. It operationalizes the 7 PbD principles as follows: | GDPR Article 25 Mandate | Mapped PbD Principle | CPS Example: Smart Thermostat | CPS Example: Fitness Tracker | |--------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------------| | "By Design" — measures implemented *before* processing | Principles #1, #3 (Proactive, Embedded) | The thermostat is **engineered** to store temperature schedules **on-device**, not in the cloud. | Raw PPG/heart-rate data is processed **on the wrist**; only model outputs (e.g., step count) leave the device. | | "By Default" — only data necessary for the purpose is processed | Principle #2 (Default Privacy) | **No microphone is included** in the device (data minimization by default — speech recognition is impossible). | Location precision is **reduced to city-level** by default; precise GPS requires explicit user opt-in. | | "State of the Art" — costs and technology considered | Principle #5 (End-to-End Security) | All firmware updates are **signed and verified**; root-access is cryptographically blocked. | All transmitted data is **end-to-end encrypted** (TLS 1.3 + per-device keys). | | "Transparency" — data subjects informed | Principle #6 (Visibility) | A **public privacy label** (energy dots, like Apple's) lists exactly what data is collected. | A **data-flow diagram** is published in the privacy policy. | | "Empowerment" — user can exercise rights | Principle #7 (User-Centric) | Users can **delete all local history** with one tap; no backup persists. | Users can **export, correct, or erase** all health data via the companion app. | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's note:** A 7-mark answer should name **at least 5 distinct principles** and provide **2 CPS examples**. Failure to name specific PbD principles = loss of 2 marks. Failure to provide a CPS context = loss of 2 marks. --- **Part (b) — Fitness Tracker k-Anonymity Failure Analysis (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Identify the Privacy Failure:** For the QI tuple `(age: 25–30, ZIP: 682***, gender: F)`, the equivalence class contains only $k = 2$ records. This is a **violation of $k$-anonymity for $k \geq 5$** (the typical de facto standard for health-data releases). The minimum equivalence class size across the dataset is $k_{\min} = 2$. **Step 2 — Name the Attack Vector:** This enables a **Homogeneity / Re-identification Attack**. Because the equivalence class has only 2 patients, an attacker with **auxiliary information** (e.g., knowing a single woman in her late-20s living in ZIP 682*** has a particular diagnosis) can pinpoint her record with **50% probability** (since $P_{\text{re-id}} = 1/k = 1/2 = 50\%$). > [!WARNING] > **High-Risk Patient Sub-Population:** The small equivalence class is a *rare demographic combination* — a classic "outlier" in QI space. Outliers are the *Achilles' heel* of k-anonymity. **Step 3 — Recommend a Corrective PbD Mechanism:** **Recommendation:** Apply **additional generalization** to the ZIP code (e.g., from `682***` to `68****`, retaining only the first 2 digits) AND/OR broaden the age band (e.g., from `25–30` to `25–35`). After re-generalization, the equivalence class for `(age: 25–35, ZIP: 68****, gender: F)` should grow to at least 5 records. **Numerical Justification:** After re-generalization, suppose $k_{\text{new}} = 7$ for the female sub-class. The re-identification probability drops to: $$ P_{\text{re-id, new}} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 14.3\% $$ This is a **64.3% reduction in re-identification risk** (from 50% to 14.3%), achieving the industry-recommended $k \geq 5$ threshold and complying with **GDPR Article 25's "data minimization" mandate**. **Alternative/Complementary Mechanism:** Apply **Differential Privacy** on top of the $k$-anonymous release. Add Laplace noise with $\varepsilon = 1.0$ to the count of patients per equivalence class, providing a *mathematical* (not just empirical) privacy guarantee. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on "Case Studies of PbD in Action" questions:** > > 1. **Listing the 7 principles without mapping them to a specific case study** (loses 2–3 marks). Always tie each principle to a *concrete feature* of the case (e.g., "Apple's on-device tally implements Principle #2: Default Privacy"). > 2. **Confusing Differential Privacy with k-Anonymity** (loses 2 marks). DP is *mathematical* (worst-case, post-hoc); k-Anonymity is *syntactic* (release-time, equivalence-class-based). > 3. **Ignoring the Positive-Sum Principle #4** (loses 1–2 marks). Examiners reward students who *quantify* the utility-privacy trade-off (e.g., "DP added 0.1% error while reducing re-identification risk by 64%"). > 4. **Failing to give CPS-specific examples** (loses 2–3 marks). This course is PECPT743 — *Privacy in CPS*. Generic GDPR/Apple examples without a CPS context (sensor, actuator, control loop) earn partial credit only. > 5. **Omitting the numerical computation** in Part B sub-parts. Always show the **formula substitution**, **intermediate result**, and **final answer with units**. > 6. **Using the pipe character `|` inside markdown tables** (a LaTeX-rendering pitfall) — the system will collapse your table. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Case Studies of PbD in Action"** - [x] **PbD = 7 Principles**, coined by **Ann Cavoukian**; legally mandated by **GDPR Article 25** and increasingly by **India's DPDP Act 2023** (Section 8). - [x] **Differential Privacy (DP)** is the **flagship mathematical PbD mechanism** — mastered by Apple, Google, and the US Census. - [x] **Laplace Mechanism formula** to memorize: $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$. Smaller $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stronger privacy, more noise. - [x] **k-Anonymity** requires every equivalence class to contain at least $k$ records; $k \geq 5$ is the de facto industry minimum for health data. - [x] **Apple's iOS PbD case study** = Local noisy tally + on-device aggregation + opt-in reporting + transparent white paper. - [x] **Federated Learning (Google Gboard)** = Model trained locally, updates aggregated centrally; raw data never leaves the device (PbD Principles #2, #3, #5). - [x] **Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT)** = IDFA zeroed by default; opt-in required (Principle #2 + #7). - [x] **Signal Protocol** = E2EE with forward secrecy + post-compromise security (PbD Principles #3 + #5). - [x] **GDPR Article 25** = legal codification of PbD; mandates "by design" and "by default" privacy in *all* CPS products sold in the EU. - [x] **PbD Principle #4 (Positive-Sum)** is the *most under-answered* on KTU exams — always quantify the **utility vs. privacy** trade-off. - [x] For **CPS-specific** answers, name the **sensor**, the **actuator**, the **control loop**, and the **threat model** — generic IoT answers are penalized. - [x] Always close a Part B answer with a **plain-English interpretation** of the math; KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for *interpretation quality*. > **End of Notes — Module 3: Case Studies of PbD in Action | PECPT743 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Privacy Risk Assessment: Identifying Privacy Risks in CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Privacy Risk Assessment: Identifying Privacy Risks in CPS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Privacy Risk Assessment (PRA)** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is a *systematic, continuous, and structured engineering process* used to identify, analyze, evaluate, and prioritize privacy risks arising from the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability of personally identifiable information (PII)** processed by the tightly coupled cyber (computation, communication, control) and physical (sensors, actuators, environment) subsystems of a CPS. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based framework, privacy risk identification is defined as the *first phase* of the broader **Privacy Risk Management (PRM) lifecycle**, which itself is a sub-domain of **Privacy Engineering in CPS**. The identification phase aims to produce a *Privacy Risk Register* — a living artifact cataloguing all foreseeable privacy hazards, their associated assets, threat sources, vulnerabilities, and preliminary likelihood-impact ratings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Terminology to Memorize:** > - **CPS (Cyber-Physical System):** An engineered system wherein computational (cyber) algorithms directly govern physical processes via feedback loops. > - **PII (Personally Identifiable Information):** Any data that can identify a natural person, either directly or in combination with other data. > - **Privacy Risk:** The probability that a CPS operation will result in an *adverse privacy consequence* (e.g., re-identification, location disclosure, behavioral profiling) multiplied by the *severity* of that consequence. > - **Threat:** A potential cause of an unwanted privacy incident. > - **Vulnerability:** A weakness in the CPS that can be exploited to compromise privacy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Think of a Smart Home like a Hospital with Many Wards:** > Imagine your CPS is like a **large hospital building**: > - The **patients** are your *privacy data* (PII — names, heart rates, location). > - The **nurses and doctors** are the *cyber components* (servers, algorithms, apps) that handle the patients. > - The **building's doors, windows, and ventilation** are the *physical components* (sensors, cameras, smart meters). > - **Privacy Risk Assessment** is like walking through the hospital with a clipboard, asking: *"Where could a thief sneak in? Where are the unguarded medicine cabinets? Who has unchecked access to patient files?"* > - A *Privacy Risk* is finding that, say, the **medicine room window is open** (= a vulnerability), **a thief is prowling the area** (= a threat), and the **room contains patient narcotics** (= an asset of high sensitivity). Combining these gives you the risk. > - In a real CPS (say, a smart grid), the *window* could be an **unencrypted smart meter transmission**, the *thief* could be a **man-in-the-middle attacker**, and the *medicine* could be **real-time household occupancy data**. ### 1.3 The CPS Privacy Landscape — Quick Mental Model A CPS does not just process bits; it interacts with the **physical world**, meaning privacy harms can be *physical* (e.g., stalker locates victim via smart-home geofencing) as well as *informational* (e.g., health data leak from wearable). | CPS Layer | Example Component | Typical Privacy Asset Held | Common Privacy Risk | |---|---|---|---| | **Sensing** | Wearable ECG sensor | Heart-rate, biometric signature | Re-identification, health disclosure | | **Communication** | Zigbee, BLE, MQTT | Device ID, network metadata | Traffic analysis, linkability | | **Computation** | Edge gateway, cloud | Aggregated behavioral profile | Profiling, inference attacks | | **Actuation** | Smart lock, HVAC | Occupancy patterns | Physical intrusion, surveillance | | **Control Feedback** | Closed-loop controller | Real-time decision context | Manipulation, forced disclosure | > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Metric (NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1):** Privacy risk levels are commonly expressed in **three ordinal tiers: Low (L), Moderate (M), High (H)**, with a fourth tier **Very High (VH)** reserved for critical CPS such as medical implants and autonomous vehicles. ### 1.4 Why Identification is the *First* and *Most Critical* Step According to the KTU 2024 syllabus mapping for PECPT743, Module 3 outcomes emphasize: - **CO3 (Module 3):** *"Identify, analyze, and evaluate privacy risks in CPS architectures and propose mitigation strategies."* If identification is incomplete, every downstream phase (analysis, evaluation, treatment, monitoring) becomes unreliable — leading to the classic engineering failure mode: *garbage in, garbage out*. The identification phase feeds the **Privacy Risk Register**, which is the central artifact used in KTU lab evaluations and end-semester questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Privacy Risk Identification Pipeline (Structured) Privacy Risk Identification in CPS is performed through a **five-stage structured pipeline**. Each stage is essential and feeds the next. #### Stage 1 — CPS Asset Enumeration (What are we protecting?) - **Goal:** Produce an exhaustive inventory of *privacy-sensitive assets*. - **Assets in CPS include:** - **PII data at rest** (e.g., user profiles stored in cloud). - **PII data in motion** (e.g., sensor readings transmitted over BLE). - **PII data in use** (e.g., data being processed in a real-time control loop). - **Derived/Inferred data** (e.g., behavioral fingerprints). - **Metadata** (e.g., timing, packet size, communication frequency). - **Physical manifestations** (e.g., LED status indicators revealing system state). #### Stage 2 — CPS Architecture Decomposition (Where are the assets?) - **Cyber Side:** Cloud, edge, fog nodes, mobile apps, APIs, firmware. - **Physical Side:** Sensors (cameras, microphones, motion detectors), actuators (locks, valves, motors), embedded MCUs. - **Communication Channels:** Wired (CAN, Ethernet), Wireless (Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, 5G), Hybrid (PLC). - **Human-Machine Interfaces:** Touchscreens, voice assistants, mobile apps, dashboards. #### Stage 3 — Threat Source Cataloguing (Who or what can attack?) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Adversary Classes (KTU-Mandated Taxonomy):** > - **Insider threats:** Malicious employees, curious administrators. > - **External cyber attackers:** Hackers, APT groups, botnets. > - **Passive observers:** ISPs, network sniffers, side-channel analysts. > - **Physical intruders:** Stalkers, thieves, nosy neighbors. > - **System failures:** Bugs, misconfigurations, firmware corruption. > - **Legal/regulatory adversaries:** Lawful surveillance, subpoenas, government access. #### Stage 4 — Vulnerability Discovery (How can the threat succeed?) Vulnerabilities in CPS can be categorized using the well-known **STRIDE/LINDDUN** families: - **S — Spoofing:** Impersonation of legitimate devices (e.g., fake smart meter). - **T — Tampering:** Modifying sensor data or actuator commands. - **R — Repudiation:** Denying actions (e.g., unauthenticated actuator trigger). - **I — Information Disclosure:** Unauthorized PII leakage. - **D — Denial of Service:** Disrupting CPS operation to force unsafe privacy states. - **E — Elevation of Privilege:** Gaining root/admin in the CPS. For *privacy specifically*, the **LINDDUN** framework (a privacy extension of STRIDE) maps more accurately: - **L — Linkability:** Linking two actions/data to the same user. - **I — Identifiability:** Re-identifying a user from anonymized data. - **N — Non-repudiation:** Inability to deny an action (can be privacy-violating). - **D — Detectability:** Detecting that a user is using the system. - **D — Disclosure of Information:** Information leakage. - **U — Unawareness:** User unaware of data collection. - **N — Non-compliance:** Violation of regulations (GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 India). #### Stage 5 — Privacy Risk = Likelihood × Impact This is the **mathematical core** of risk identification. A risk is *identified* (not yet treated) by computing a preliminary level. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes every formula, expression, and qualitative rule that a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to prevent markdown table breakage. | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | KTU Exam Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Privacy Risk Level (PRL)** | $PRL = L \times I$ | $L$ = Likelihood, $I$ = Impact | Very High (asked every year) | | 2 | **Likelihood Ordinal Scale** | $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | 1=Rare, 2=Unlikely, 3=Possible, 4=Likely, 5=Almost Certain | Very High | | 3 | **Impact Ordinal Scale** | $I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ | 1=Negligible, 2=Minor, 3=Moderate, 4=Major, 5=Catastrophic | Very High | | 4 | **Composite Risk Score** | $R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i \cdot I_i$ | Sum of risks for all $n$ identified assets | High | | 5 | **Re-identification Probability (k-anonymity)** | $P_{re-id} \leq 1/k$ | Probability of re-identification in a $k$-anonymous dataset | High | | 6 | **Differential Privacy Budget** | $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \varepsilon_i$ | Cumulative privacy loss over $m$ queries | High | | 7 | **Shannon Entropy (Information Disclosure)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Information content in bits | Medium | | 8 | **Unlinkability (Information Theoretic)** | $I(E_1; E_2) \approx 0$ | Mutual information between two events $\approx 0$ for unlinkability | Medium | | 9 | **Adversary Success Probability (Bayesian)** | $P(\text{success} \vert \text{attack}) = \frac{P(A \vert B) \cdot P(B)}{P(A)}$ | Bayesian inference of attack success | Medium | | 10 | **Risk Tolerance Threshold** | $R_{accept} \leq R_{threshold}$ | Risk is acceptable only if below threshold | High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units and Conventions (KTU Board Examiner's Standard):** > - $L$, $I$, and $R$ are **dimensionless ordinal quantities**. > - $H(X)$ is measured in **bits** (or nats if natural log is used). > - $\varepsilon$ in differential privacy is **dimensionless** (lower = stronger privacy). > - $P_{re-id}$ is a **probability**, hence $0 \leq P_{re-id} \leq 1$. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The risk identification process is not academic. It directly informs: - **GDPR Article 35 (DPIA):** Data Protection Impact Assessment mandates systematic risk identification. - **India's DPDP Act 2023, Section 33:** Data fiduciaries must conduct privacy risk assessments for "significant data fiduciaries." - **NIST Privacy Framework (2020):** The "Identify-P" function is identical in spirit to KTU's Module 3 outcomes. - **Automotive (ISO/SAE 21434):** Privacy risks in connected vehicles are identified via TARA (Threat Analysis and Risk Assessment), of which *privacy* is an explicit variant. - **Medical CPS (FDA Pre-market Guidance 2023):** Connected medical devices require documented privacy risk identification. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk-Level Decision Matrix (5×5 Grid) The KTU 2024 scheme examiners *frequently* ask students to derive or apply the 5×5 risk matrix. We will derive it explicitly. > [!NOTE] > **Why 5×5?** Five ordinal levels of Likelihood × five ordinal levels of Impact = 25 cells, providing sufficient granularity without overwhelming the risk register. NIST SP 800-30 uses 3×3, but the 5×5 is the KTU/Kerala Engineering Board standard. #### Step 1: Define the Likelihood Vector We define $L$ as a discrete ordinal variable representing the probability that a *threat source* will successfully exploit a *vulnerability* within a one-year window: $$ L = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } 0 \leq P < 0.1 \quad (\text{Rare}) \\ 2 & \text{if } 0.1 \leq P < 0.3 \quad (\text{Unlikely}) \\ 3 & \text{if } 0.3 \leq P < 0.5 \quad (\text{Possible}) \\ 4 & \text{if } 0.5 \leq P < 0.7 \quad (\text{Likely}) \\ 5 & \text{if } 0.7 \leq P \leq 1.0 \quad (\text{Almost Certain}) \end{cases} $$ **Reasoning:** These probability bands are aligned with NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1. Each band is wide enough to accommodate subjective judgment yet narrow enough to discriminate between risk levels. #### Step 2: Define the Impact Vector We define $I$ as the ordinal severity of the privacy harm, considering both *informational* and *physical* dimensions (since CPS can cause physical harm): $$ I = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{Negligible: no observable harm} \\ 2 & \text{Minor: embarrassment or minor inconvenience} \\ 3 & \text{Moderate: financial loss, reputational damage} \\ 4 & \text{Major: discrimination, identity theft, stalking risk} \\ 5 & \text{Catastrophic: physical harm, life-threatening} \end{cases} $$ **Reasoning:** Notice that the Impact scale is **expanded** beyond pure privacy to include physical harm, which is a defining feature of CPS privacy risk identification. #### Step 3: Compute the Risk Score For each identified (Threat, Vulnerability, Asset) triple, we compute: $$ R = L \times I $$ where $R$ is the privacy risk score, ranging from 1 to 25. #### Step 4: Map the Score to a Risk Tier $$ \text{Risk Tier} = \begin{cases} \text{Very Low} & \text{if } 1 \leq R \leq 3 \\ \text{Low} & \text{if } 4 \leq R \leq 8 \\ \text{Moderate} & \text{if } 9 \leq R \leq 12 \\ \text{High} & \text{if } 13 \leq R \leq 16 \\ \text{Very High} & \text{if } 17 \leq R \leq 25 \end{cases} $$ **Reasoning:** This tiering follows the natural log-like bucketing: each successive tier covers a larger score range as we approach catastrophic risk. #### Step 5: Construct the 5×5 Decision Matrix We populate the matrix by computing $R = L \times I$ for each cell: $$ M_{risk} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline L \backslash I & I=1 & I=2 & I=3 & I=4 & I=5 \\ \hline L=5 & 5 & 10 & 15 & 20 & 25 \\ \hline L=4 & 4 & 8 & 12 & 16 & 20 \\ \hline L=3 & 3 & 6 & 9 & 12 & 15 \\ \hline L=2 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 \\ \hline L=1 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each cell value $R$ is the product of its row's $L$ and column's $I$. The risk tier color (in the actual KTU worksheet) is then assigned by mapping the score to the tier from Step 4. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Trick Question:** *"Why not use $R = L + I$ instead of $L \times I$?"* The answer: additive scoring would let a high Likelihood + low Impact equal a low Likelihood + high Impact (e.g., $5+1=6$ vs $1+5=6$), which is misleading. **Multiplication correctly captures the multiplicative compounding of risk**, which is the standard in NIST, ISO 27005, and the KTU PECPT743 model answer key. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Smart Health Wearable CPS > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** A wearable ECG device (sensor) transmits heart-rate data via BLE to a smartphone, which forwards it to a cloud server for arrhythmia detection. Identify three privacy risks and compute their risk tiers. #### Risk 1: BLE Eavesdropping of Heart-Rate Data - **Asset:** Real-time heart-rate (PII, sensitive health data). - **Threat:** Passive BLE sniffer within 10m. - **Vulnerability:** BLE pairing uses default PIN / no encryption in some firmware. - **Likelihood:** $L = 4$ (Likely, because default PINs are well-known). - **Impact:** $I = 4$ (Major — health data disclosure). - **Risk Score:** $R_1 = 4 \times 4 = 16$. - **Risk Tier:** **High** (since $13 \leq 16 \leq 16$). #### Risk 2: Cloud Server Breach Exposing Historical ECG - **Asset:** 1-year ECG history of 100,000 users. - **Threat:** External APT (Advanced Persistent Threat). - **Vulnerability:** Unpatched cloud API with weak authentication. - **Likelihood:** $L = 3$ (Possible). - **Impact:** $I = 5$ (Catastrophic — mass health data leak, regulatory fines). - **Risk Score:** $R_2 = 3 \times 5 = 15$. - **Risk Tier:** **High** (since $13 \leq 15 \leq 16$). #### Risk 3: Re-identification from Anonymized ECG Dataset - **Asset:** Anonymized 1000-record ECG dataset shared with researchers. - **Threat:** Adversary with auxiliary side-channel data (e.g., age, weight). - **Vulnerability:** Dataset only $k=2$ anonymous, low entropy. - **Likelihood:** $L = 3$ (Possible). - **Impact:** $I = 3$ (Moderate — re-identification is concerning but reversible). - **Risk Score:** $R_3 = 3 \times 3 = 9$. - **Risk Tier:** **Moderate** (since $9 \leq 9 \leq 12$). #### Composite Privacy Risk Score for the Wearable CPS $$ R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 16 + 15 + 9 = 40 $$ **Interpretation:** A composite score of 40 on a maximum 75 (3 risks × 25 max) means this CPS has a **High cumulative privacy risk profile**, requiring immediate mitigation (encryption, MFA, differential privacy, etc.). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Risk Identification Tools Comparison > [!NOTE] > This comparative matrix is a *favorite KTU question type* (often a 7-mark Part B sub-question). | Framework | Primary Focus | Best Suited For CPS? | Privacy-Specific? | Strengths | Weaknesses | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **STRIDE** | Security threats | Partially | No | Widely understood, simple | Ignores privacy-specific issues like linkability | | **LINDDUN** | Privacy threats | Yes (with adaptation) | Yes | Maps directly to GDPR | Less known, requires privacy expertise | | **NIST SP 800-30** | General risk management | Yes | Partial | Authoritative, government-grade | Generic, not CPS-aware | | **ISO 27005** | Information security risk | Yes | No | International standard | Heavy, expensive certification | | **TARA (Automotive)** | Vehicle CPS | Yes (vehicular) | Partial | Industry-proven | Limited to automotive | | **DPIA (GDPR)** | Data protection impact | Yes | Yes | Legally mandated in EU | Regulatory, not engineering | | **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** | Industrial CPS | Yes | No | Detailed adversary TTPs | Security, not privacy | | **PRISMA (IoT Privacy)** | IoT/embedded | Yes | Yes | CPS-aware, privacy-focused | Academic, less mature | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU exams**, the recommended answer to *"Which framework to use?"* is often: **"LINDDUN for privacy-specific identification, augmented with NIST SP 800-30 for the broader risk management structure, and tailored to the CPS architecture using data flow diagrams."** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — CPS Privacy Risk Identification Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Start: CPS Scope Definition]) --> A1[Asset Enumeration

The loop is critical because CPS privacy risks are **dynamic** — a firmware update or new sensor deployment can introduce novel risks, requiring re-identification. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CPS Layered Privacy Risk Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CyberLayer[Cyber Layer] CL1[Cloud Database

Each block is an *asset location*; each arrow is a *potential vulnerability path*. This type of diagram is what KTU examiners expect in 7-mark Data Flow Diagram (DFD) questions. ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — LINDDUN Privacy Threat Tree for CPS ```mermaid flowchart TD L[LINDDUN Privacy Threat Tree] --> L1[Linkability
