This diagram is a high-yield visual for KTU viva voce. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT743]** > **Define Privacy Risk Assessment in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four privacy-sensitive assets typically found in a CPS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Privacy Risk Assessment (PRA) in CPS is a systematic engineering process to identify, analyze, and evaluate privacy risks arising from the collection, transmission, processing, storage, and actuation of personally identifiable information (PII) within tightly coupled cyber and physical subsystems. It is the first phase of the broader Privacy Risk Management (PRM) lifecycle. **Four Privacy-Sensitive Assets in CPS (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):** 1. **Sensor Data (PII at Rest):** e.g., raw ECG readings, video frames, audio. 2. **Communication Metadata:** e.g., device IDs, transmission timestamps, packet sizes. 3. **Derived Behavioral Profiles:** e.g., inferred daily routines from smart-home sensors. 4. **Actuator State Information:** e.g., door-lock status, HVAC setpoint revealing occupancy. > --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECPT743]** > **Explain the difference between *LINDDUN* and *STRIDE* frameworks in privacy risk identification. Why is LINDDUN more suitable for CPS privacy analysis?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **STRIDE (1 mark):** A *security*-oriented threat modeling framework with six categories — Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege. It focuses on *confidentiality, integrity, availability* but does not address privacy-specific harms like re-identification or linkability. > **LINDDUN (1 mark):** A *privacy*-oriented threat modeling framework with seven categories — Linkability, Identifiability, Non-repudiation, Detectability, Disclosure, Unawareness, Non-compliance. It directly maps to privacy principles (e.g., GDPR). > **Why LINDDUN suits CPS (1 mark):** CPS involves continuous physical sensing, where privacy harms are *subtle* (e.g., inferring sleep patterns from HVAC data). LINDDUN's focus on **linkability** and **identifiability** captures these CPS-specific risks that STRIDE ignores. Additionally, CPS often operates in regulatory environments (e.g., medical, vehicular) where *non-compliance* threats are critical — directly addressed by LINDDUN's seventh category. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice) > **Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT743]** > **(a)** Describe the **five-stage privacy risk identification pipeline** for a Cyber-Physical System, with a suitable example from the *smart-grid* domain. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Construct the **5×5 privacy risk decision matrix** for the smart-grid scenario, and identify **three privacy risks** with computed risk tiers. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks] > [!NOTE] > The five stages of the privacy risk identification pipeline for a smart-grid CPS (which combines smart meters, communication networks, and utility cloud servers) are as follows: **Stage 1 — Asset Enumeration (1.5 marks):** The smart grid's privacy-sensitive assets include: - Household energy consumption time-series (15-min intervals revealing occupancy). - Smart meter unique identifier (Device ID). - Appliance-level disaggregated load signatures (e.g., refrigerator cycling pattern). - Customer demographic and billing data in the utility's cloud. **Stage 2 — Architecture Decomposition (1.5 marks):** - *Physical layer:* Smart meters at customer premises, distribution transformers. - *Communication layer:* PLC (Power Line Communication), RF mesh, cellular backhaul. - *Cyber layer:* Meter Data Management System (MDMS), utility billing server, third-party analytics platforms. **Stage 3 — Threat Source Cataloguing (1.5 marks):** - External attackers targeting MDMS via API vulnerabilities. - Insider employees with privileged database access. - Passive eavesdroppers on PLC/RF mesh channels. - Marketing firms purchasing granular consumption data. **Stage 4 — Vulnerability Discovery (1.5 marks):** - Lack of end-to-end encryption on PLC links. - Weak authentication in MDMS API endpoints. - Unnecessary retention of disaggregated load data beyond billing period. - No user consent for third-party data sharing. **Stage 5 — Preliminary Risk Scoring (1 mark):** Compute $R = L \times I$ for each identified risk and populate the Privacy Risk Register. #### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks] > [!NOTE] > **The 5×5 Risk Decision Matrix** is constructed using $R = L \times I$ with $L, I \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$. Three identified risks: **Risk 1: Eavesdropping on Smart Meter PLC Communication (2.5 marks)** - **Threat:** Passive eavesdropper on PLC link. - **Vulnerability:** No encryption on legacy PLC meters. - **Asset:** Household consumption time-series (reveals occupancy). - **Likelihood:** $L = 4$ (Likely — PLC sniffing tools are publicly available). - **Impact:** $I = 4$ (Major — occupancy and behavior disclosure). - **Risk Score:** $R_1 = 4 \times 4 = 16$. - **Risk Tier:** **High** (since $13 \leq 16 \leq 16$). **Risk 2: Insider Data Theft from MDMS (2.5 marks)** - **Threat:** Malicious utility employee. - **Vulnerability:** Excessive privileged access without audit logs. - **Asset:** 1 million customers' disaggregated load profiles. - **Likelihood:** $L = 2$ (Unlikely — requires insider collusion). - **Impact:** $I = 5$ (Catastrophic — mass surveillance and re-identification). - **Risk Score:** $R_2 = 2 \times 5 = 10$. - **Risk Tier:** **Moderate** (since $9 \leq 10 \leq 12$). **Risk 3: Unconsented Third-Party Data Sharing (2 marks)** - **Threat:** Marketing firm purchasing data from utility partner. - **Vulnerability:** Contractual gap allowing "anonymized" data sharing. - **Asset:** Aggregated load signatures. - **Likelihood:** $L = 3$ (Possible — common business practice). - **Impact:** $I = 3$ (Moderate — re-identification risk, regulatory violation). - **Risk Score:** $R_3 = 3 \times 3 = 9$. - **Risk Tier:** **Moderate** (since $9 \leq 9 \leq 12$). **Composite Risk (1 mark):** $R_{total} = 16 + 10 + 9 = 35$, indicating **High** cumulative privacy risk requiring immediate mitigation. > --- > **Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECPT743 — Alternative Choice]** > **(a)** Explain the **LINDDUN privacy threat modeling framework** in detail. Compare it with **STRIDE** using a suitable CPS example (e.g., autonomous vehicle). **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Compute and analyze the **re-identification probability** for a healthcare dataset under $k$-anonymity. Given a hospital releases a dataset where each record has quasi-identifiers $\{$Age, ZIP, Gender$\}$, with $k = 5$, what is the upper bound on re-identification probability? Suggest two differential-privacy-based mitigations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks] > [!NOTE] > **LINDDUN Framework Explanation (3.5 marks):** LINDDUN is a privacy threat modeling framework that systematically identifies privacy risks in systems handling personal data. Its name is an acronym for seven threat categories: - **L — Linkability:** An adversary can link two or more actions/records to the same user. *Autonomous vehicle example:* Linking multiple trips by the same vehicle's unique V2X pseudonym. - **I — Identifiability:** A user can be re-identified from "anonymized" data. *Example:* Re-identifying a driver from GPS trace + home address. - **N — Non-repudiation:** A user cannot deny having performed an action, which can be privacy-violating. *Example:* Cannot deny being at a specific location at a given time. - **D — Detectability:** An adversary can detect that a user is using the system. *Example:* Detecting that a vehicle is collecting location data. - **D — Disclosure of Information:** Plaintext leakage of personal data. *Example:* Unencrypted CAN bus messages revealing driver behavior. - **U — Unawareness:** The user is unaware of what data is being collected. *Example:* Driver unaware that cabin microphone is always on. - **N — Non-compliance:** System violates privacy regulations. *Example:* Not obtaining GDPR consent for telematics data. > **STRIDE vs LINDDUN Comparison (3.5 marks):** | Aspect | STRIDE | LINDDUN | |---|---|---| | **Focus** | Security (CIA triad) | Privacy (linkability, identifiability) | | **Threats** | 6 categories | 7 categories | | **CPS Suitability** | Partial | High | | **Autonomous Vehicle Example: CAN Bus Injection** | Maps to *Tampering* | Maps to *Linkability* (if injection enables linking trips) AND *Identifiability* | | **Autonomous Vehicle Example: Plaintext Telematics** | Maps to *Information Disclosure* | Maps to *Disclosure* AND *Unawareness* (if user not informed) | | **Regulatory Awareness** | Low | High (GDPR-aware) | **Conclusion (implicit):** For privacy-focused CPS analysis like autonomous vehicles, **LINDDUN is the superior framework**, though both can be used in tandem for *defense-in-depth*. #### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks] > [!NOTE] > **Re-identification Probability under k-Anonymity (3 marks):** > A dataset is *$k$-anonymous* if every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ other records with respect to the quasi-identifiers. This means an adversary can narrow a record down to a group of at least $k$ individuals, giving an upper bound on re-identification probability of: > $$P_{re-id} \leq 1/k$$ > Given $k = 5$: > $$P_{re-id} \leq 1/5 = 0.2 = 20\%$$ > **Interpretation:** An adversary has at most a 20% chance of correctly re-identifying any individual in the dataset, even when using the quasi-identifiers. While this is an *upper bound*, real-world re-identification is often easier due to auxiliary information (e.g., public voter rolls). **Two Differential-Privacy-Based Mitigations (4 marks, 2 each):** 1. **Laplace Mechanism for Aggregate Statistics (2 marks):** When releasing aggregate statistics (e.g., average patient age), add Laplace noise calibrated to the sensitivity $\Delta f$ of the query and the privacy budget $\varepsilon$: $$\tilde{f}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left( \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon} \right)$$ This ensures that the inclusion or exclusion of any single patient's record changes the output probability by at most $e^{\varepsilon}$, providing *$\varepsilon$-differential privacy*. 2. **Exponential Mechanism for Non-Numeric Queries (2 marks):** For releasing non-numeric results (e.g., most common diagnosis), use the exponential mechanism, which samples an output $r$ with probability proportional to: $$P(r) \propto \exp\left( \frac{\varepsilon \cdot u(D, r)}{2 \Delta u} \right)$$ where $u(D, r)$ is a utility function and $\Delta u$ is its sensitivity. This is preferred over Laplace for categorical outputs and provides rigorous privacy guarantees. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the CPS context:** Generic answers about risk management without tying to *physical + cyber coupling* lose up to 3 marks. Always mention *physical harm potential* in CPS. > 2. **Confusing $k$-anonymity with differential privacy:** They are *different* privacy models. $k$-anonymity is syntactic; differential privacy is semantic and provides stronger guarantees. > 3. **Wrong formula for risk:** Writing $R = L + I$ instead of $R = L \times I$ is an instant 2-mark deduction. **Always use multiplication** unless the question explicitly says otherwise. > 4. **Skipping the Privacy Risk Register:** A 14-mark question without an explicit register (table of identified risks) is incomplete. Always include a structured table. > 5. **Ignoring the LINDDUN framework:** STRIDE alone is not enough. KTU 2024 PECPT743 examiners explicitly look for LINDDUN awareness. > 6. **Forgetting to compute the composite risk** $R_{total}$ when multiple risks are identified. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Privacy Risk Assessment in CPS:** - ✅ **Definition:** Privacy Risk Assessment = systematic identification, analysis, evaluation, and prioritization of privacy risks in CPS. - ✅ **CPS = Cyber + Physical coupling.** Privacy risks can cause *informational* and *physical* harm. - ✅ **Five-Stage Pipeline:** Asset Enumeration → Architecture Decomposition → Threat Cataloguing → Vulnerability Discovery → Risk Scoring. - ✅ **Core Formula:** $R = L \times I$ (multiplication, not addition). - ✅ **5×5 Matrix:** $L, I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, $R \in [1, 25]$. - ✅ **Risk Tiers:** Very Low (1-3), Low (4-8), Moderate (9-12), High (13-16), Very High (17-25). - ✅ **STRIDE = Security.** LINDDUN = Privacy. Use LINDDUN for CPS privacy identification. - ✅ **LINDDUN = Linkability, Identifiability, Non-repudiation, Detectability, Disclosure, Unawareness, Non-compliance.** - ✅ **$k$-Anonymity:** $P_{re-id} \leq 1/k$. Higher $k$ = stronger anonymity. - ✅ **Differential Privacy:** $\varepsilon$-DP via Laplace or Exponential mechanism. Smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy. - ✅ **Composite Risk:** $R_{total} = \sum R_i$ across all identified risks. - ✅ **Lifecycle:** Risk identification is *first*; mitigation and monitoring follow. - ✅ **Legal Anchors:** GDPR Art. 35 (DPIA), India DPDP Act 2023 §33, NIST Privacy Framework. - ✅ **Output Artifact:** Privacy Risk Register — central deliverable for KTU lab and exam. - ✅ **Closed-loop:** Identification is iterative; new sensors/firmware trigger re-identification. - ✅ **Examiner mantra:** "Multiplication not addition. LINDDUN not just STRIDE. CPS not just IT." - ✅ **Mnemonic for LINDDUN:** **"LINDDUN — Linkable, Identifiable, Non-repudiable, Detectable, Disclosable, Unaware, Non-compliant."** > **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 3, PECPT743 — Privacy in CPS.**

topics.title: Conducting Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conducting Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** is a systematic, risk-based evaluation process used to identify, assess, mitigate, and document the privacy risks arising from the collection, use, storage, sharing, and disposal of personal information within a project, system, programme, service, or technology. In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, a PIA specifically evaluates how the integration of computational algorithms, networking, and physical processes affects the privacy of individuals whose data is sensed, transmitted, processed, or acted upon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT743 Module 3):**
> A PIA is not a one-time legal checkbox. It is an **iterative, engineering-grade risk management process** that must align with the system's full lifecycle — from design, development, deployment, operation, to decommissioning. In CPS, the "physical" component (actuators, sensors, embedded controllers) amplifies privacy risk because it converts data into real-world actions.

Formally, a PIA can be expressed as a four-tuple evaluation:

$$PIA = \langle D, F, R, M \rangle$$

where:
- $D$ = Description of data flows and processing activities
- $F$ = Feasibility and necessity analysis of the proposed processing
- $R$ = Risk identification and impact assessment
- $M$ = Mitigation and residual risk evaluation

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Doctor's Checkup Before Surgery"**

Imagine a hospital that wants to introduce a new robotic surgery system (a CPS — it has sensors, software, and physical actuators operating on a human body). Before turning the robot on for the first time, the hospital runs a full medical-style audit:

| PIA Equivalent | Surgical Analogy |
|----------------|------------------|
| Identify data collected (vital signs, video, biometrics) | Pre-op diagnostic tests (bloodwork, MRI, ECG) |
| Assess privacy risks (data leakage, unauthorized access) | Risk of complications (bleeding, infection) |
| Evaluate necessity & proportionality | Verify surgery is actually needed and proportionate |
| Implement controls (encryption, access logs) | Surgical safety checks, sterilization, anesthesia plan |
| Residual risk acceptance | Informed consent from patient |

Just as a patient has the right to know what the robotic arm will do with their body, **data subjects have the right to know what a CPS will do with their data** — and a PIA is the engineering document that proves the project has thought it through.

## 1.3 Why PIAs Are Critical in CPS (KTU Context)

Cyber-Physical Systems blur the boundary between the digital and the physical. A traditional IT system leaking credit card numbers is bad — but a CPS leaking biometric gait patterns from a smart-floor sensor array can:

- **Re-identify** individuals in their homes
- **Track** physical movement patterns (sleep, bathroom visits, falls)
- **Infer** sensitive health conditions (Parkinson's from tremors, pregnancy from movement changes)
- **Manipulate** the physical world (smart locks unlocking, medical devices misfiring)

> [!WARNING]
> **CPS-Specific Privacy Risk Multiplier:** Unlike a website, a CPS cannot be "logged out" of your home. Privacy in CPS is **contextual, persistent, and physical** — making PIAs an engineering necessity, not just a legal one.

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU 2024

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **DPIA** | Data Protection Impact Assessment — synonym used in GDPR Article 35 |
| **Data Subject** | The identified or identifiable person whose data is processed |
| **Data Controller** | Entity that determines the purposes and means of processing |
| **Data Processor** | Entity that processes data on behalf of the controller |
| **Proportionality** | Principle that data collection must be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after mitigation controls are applied |
| **PII** | Personally Identifiable Information |
| **SPII** | Sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (health, biometrics, location) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PIA Process — Operational Breakdown

A PIA is not a single document; it is a **process comprising five logical phases**, often executed iteratively throughout the CPS development lifecycle.

### Phase 1: Threshold & Trigger Analysis
Determine **whether a PIA is required at all**. Not every system needs a full PIA — only those processing personal data with elevated risk.

**Mandatory PIA Triggers (per GDPR Art. 35 + KTU Module 3 emphasis):**
- Systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects (profiling, scoring)
- Large-scale processing of **Sensitive PII (SPII)** — health, biometric, genetic data
- Systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale (e.g., smart city CPS)
- Processing of children's data
- Use of **novel CPS technologies** (IoT, AI/ML actuators, autonomous decision-making)

### Phase 2: Data Mapping & Description
Document the **full data lifecycle** in the CPS:

1. **What** data is collected (type, sensitivity, format)
2. **Why** it is collected (purpose, legal basis)
3. **How** it is collected (sensors, APIs, user input)
4. **Where** it flows (storage, transmission paths, third parties)
5. **How long** it is retained
6. **How** it is destroyed at end-of-life

A common engineering tool here is the **Data Flow Diagram (DFD)** showing the CPS components:
- Sensors → Edge Gateway → Cloud → Analytics Engine → Actuator Feedback Loop

### Phase 3: Privacy Risk Assessment
For each identified privacy risk, calculate:

$$R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$$

where:
- $L$ = **Likelihood** of the risk materializing (1 = Rare, 5 = Almost Certain)
- $I$ = **Impact** severity on the data subject (1 = Negligible, 5 = Catastrophic)
- $C$ = **Context Sensitivity multiplier** (1.0 for public data, 1.5 for PII, **2.0 for SPII in CPS**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **C (Context) multiplier** is the KTU-specific CPS twist. A heart-rate sensor reading in a gym is *low* context (C=1.0). The same reading in an elderly-care smart home is *high* context (C=2.0) because it can infer medication timing, sleep quality, and emotional state.

**Risk Classification Matrix:**

| Risk Score ($R_{privacy}$) | Classification | Required Action |
|----|----|----|
| 1 – 8 | Low | Document, monitor, periodic review |
| 9 – 24 | Medium | Apply mitigation controls, re-evaluate |
| 25 – 50 | High | Mandatory mitigation + DPO consultation |

### Phase 4: Mitigation & Control Design
Engineered controls fall into three categories:

| Category | Examples in CPS |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Technical** | Encryption-at-rest, TLS in transit, anonymization, differential privacy, on-device inference |
| **Organizational** | Access policies, staff training, breach response plans, DPIA review board |
| **Physical** | Tamper-proof hardware, secure boot, sensor placement limits, physical access control |

### Phase 5: Documentation, Consultation & Sign-Off
- Draft the **PIA Report** with findings, mitigations, and residual risk
- Consult the **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** if mandated
- For high residual risk → **Prior Consultation** with the supervisory authority (GDPR Art. 36)
- **Periodic Review** schedule (typically annually or upon major system change)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Range | Use Case |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | Privacy Risk Score | $R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$ | Score 1–50 | Quantify each privacy risk |
| 2 | Context Multiplier | $C \in \{1.0, 1.5, 2.0\}$ | Dimensionless | Adjust for CPS data sensitivity |
| 3 | Necessity Test | $Necessity = \frac{Data_{min}}{Data_{collected}}$ | Ratio 0–1 (target → 1) | Verify data minimization |
| 4 | Residual Risk | $R_{residual} = R_{initial} - M_{effectiveness}$ | Score 0–50 | Post-mitigation evaluation |
| 5 | PIA Threshold | $T_{PIA} = (N_{subjects} \geq 1000) \lor (SPII = true) \lor (AI_{autonomous} = true)$ | Boolean | Triggers mandatory PIA |
| 6 | Compliance Coverage | $Cov = \frac{Controls_{implemented}}{Controls_{required}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Audit metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The expression $R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$ is the most frequently asked formula on Module 3 PIAs. Memorize it with all three variables clearly defined.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of PIAs in Engineering

- **Smart Healthcare CPS:** PIA identifies that continuous ECG monitoring could expose cardiac patients to insurance discrimination; mitigation = on-device processing only.
- **Smart Grid / AMI Meters:** PIA reveals that 15-minute interval energy data can infer occupancy, appliance use, and sleep patterns; mitigation = aggregation + noise injection.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** PIA evaluates cabin-camera data, location traces, and biometric authentication; mitigation = local-only facial recognition.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** PIA protects worker data from continuous biometric monitoring on assembly lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed PIA Risk-Scoring Derivation

The privacy risk score combines three independent dimensions. Below is the full step-by-step logical derivation used in KTU-style 14-mark problems.

**Given:**
- A smart-home CPS processes biometric data (heart rate, sleep patterns, room occupancy) for 10,000 users.
- Risk: unauthorized re-identification of users through sensor data correlation.
- Likelihood estimate: $L = 4$ (Likely — historical breaches in similar systems).
- Impact estimate: $I = 5$ (Severe — health data leakage can cause discrimination, blackmail).
- Data type: **SPII in a private home context** → $C = 2.0$.

**Step 1 — Compute base risk without context multiplier:**

$$R_{base} = L \times I$$

$$R_{base} = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Step 2 — Apply CPS context multiplier for SPII in private domestic CPS:**

$$R_{privacy} = R_{base} \times C$$

$$R_{privacy} = 20 \times 2.0 = 40$$

**Step 3 — Classify the risk:**

$$R_{privacy} = 40 \implies \text{High Risk (range 25–50)}$$

**Step 4 — Determine required action per KTU Module 3 framework:**

A score of **40** demands:
- Mandatory application of mitigation controls
- Data Protection Officer (DPO) consultation
- **Prior Consultation with the supervisory authority** (since residual risk may still be high)
- Executive sign-off before deployment

**Step 5 — Compute Residual Risk after applying mitigations:**

Suppose the engineering team implements:
- End-to-end encryption (reduces likelihood by 1.5 points)
- On-device anonymization (reduces impact by 1.0 point)
- Strict access controls (reduces likelihood by 0.5 point)

Total mitigation reduces likelihood by 2.0 (from 4 to 2) and impact by 1.0 (from 5 to 4):

$$R_{post} = L_{new} \times I_{new} \times C$$

$$R_{post} = 2 \times 4 \times 2.0 = 16$$

**Step 6 — Compare residual against acceptance threshold:**

$$R_{post} = 16 \implies \text{Medium Risk (range 9–24)}$$

**Step 7 — Final compliance decision:**

Since $R_{post} = 16 < 25$ (High threshold), deployment can proceed with continuous monitoring and an annual PIA re-review.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PIA Decision Logic for a CPS

The following matrix is a comprehensive analytical reference for determining when and how a PIA applies:

| CPS Subsystem | Data Processed | Sensitivity | CPS Context Multiplier ($C$) | PIA Required? | Justification |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|-----------------------------|----------------|---------------|
| Smart Thermostat | Temperature preferences | Non-PII | 1.0 | No | No personal identification |
| Fitness Wearable | Heart rate, steps, GPS | PII | 1.5 | Yes (Light) | Health-adjacent data |
| Smart Doorbell with Face ID | Biometric facial data | **SPII** | **2.0** | **Yes (Full PIA)** | Biometric + home context |
| Smart Grid Meter | 15-min energy usage | PII (inferred) | 1.5 | Yes (Light) | Can infer occupancy |
| Hospital ICU Robot | Patient vitals, video | **SPII** | **2.0** | **Yes (Full PIA + Prior Consultation)** | Health CPS, high stakes |
| Industrial Robot Arm on Assembly Line | Worker proximity, fatigue | PII | 1.5 | Yes (Light) | Workforce monitoring |
| Autonomous Vehicle Cabin Camera | Passenger identity, emotion | **SPII** | **2.0** | **Yes (Full PIA)** | Mobile CPS + biometrics |
| Smart Speaker (Voice) | Voice commands, transcripts | PII → SPII | 1.5 – 2.0 | Yes (depends) | Intimate home data |

## 3.3 Derivation of PIA Necessity Test (Quantitative)

**Step 1 — Define the data minimization principle:**

A system should collect only the minimum data necessary for the stated purpose. Formally:

$$Necessity = \frac{\vert D_{essential} \vert}{\vert D_{collected} \vert}$$

where:
- $\vert D_{essential} \vert$ = cardinality (count) of data fields strictly required
- $\vert D_{collected} \vert$ = cardinality of all data fields actually collected

**Step 2 — Interpretation:**
- $Necessity = 1.0$ → Perfect minimization (no excess collection)
- $Necessity < 0.7$ → Excessive collection → PIA must recommend data reduction
- $Necessity \to 0$ → Severe privacy violation → redesign system

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

A smart parking CPS needs vehicle plate + entry time. The team also collects:
- Driver phone Bluetooth MAC
- Vehicle make/model
- Driver facial image
- Cabin audio

$$D_{essential} = \{ \text{plate}, \text{time} \} \Rightarrow \vert D_{essential} \vert = 2$$

$$D_{collected} = \{ \text{plate, time, BT-MAC, model, face, audio} \} \Rightarrow \vert D_{collected} \vert = 6$$

$$Necessity = \frac{2}{6} = 0.33$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$Necessity = 0.33 < 0.7 \implies \text{Excessive Collection} \implies \text{PIA must mandate removal of 4 fields}$$

## 3.4 Summary of Computed Examples

| Example | $L$ | $I$ | $C$ | $R_{privacy}$ | Classification |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|---------------|----------------|
| Smart home biometric CPS (initial) | 4 | 5 | 2.0 | 40 | High |
| Smart home biometric CPS (post-mitigation) | 2 | 4 | 2.0 | 16 | Medium |
| Smart parking CPS (data collection) | — | — | — | Necessity=0.33 | Excessive |
| Smart thermostat | 1 | 1 | 1.0 | 1 | Low (no PIA) |
| Hospital ICU robot | 5 | 5 | 2.0 | 50 | Critical (Prior Consultation) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PIA Process Flow — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Proposal for CPS] --> B{Threshold Check: PII, SPII, AI, Scale}
    B -->|No Trigger| C[Light Review: Document Only]
    B -->|Trigger Met| D[Phase 1: Describe System and Data Flows]
    D --> E[Phase 2: Map Data Lifecycle Collect to Dispose]
    E --> F[Phase 3: Identify Privacy Risks L x I x C]
    F --> G{Risk Score Evaluation}
    G -->|Low 1 to 8| H[Apply Standard Controls]
    G -->|Medium 9 to 24| I[Apply Enhanced Mitigation]
    G -->|High 25 to 50| J[Consult DPO and Prior Authority]
    H --> K[Phase 4: Document Residual Risk]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Phase 5: Sign Off and Deploy]
    L --> M[Periodic Re-Assessment Schedule]
    M -->|Major Change Detected| D
    M -->|Annual Cycle| N[Review and Update PIA]
    N --> K
```

## 4.2 CPS PIA Integration — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPSLayer["CPS Operational Layer"]
        S1[Sensors: Camera, ECG, GPS, Accelerometer]
        A1[Actuators: Smart Lock, Pump, Robotic Arm]
        E1[Embedded Controller / Edge Node]
    end

    subgraph DataLayer["Data Processing Layer"]
        D1[Data Ingestion Gateway]
        D2[Local Pre-Processing and Anonymization]
        D3[Cloud Analytics Engine]
        D4[Storage with Encryption at Rest]
    end

    subgraph PIALayer["Privacy Impact Assessment Layer"]
        P1[Data Mapping and DFD Generation]
        P2[Risk Engine: L x I x C]
        P3[Mitigation Control Library]
        P4[Compliance Checker: GDPR, NIST]
        P5[PIA Report Generator]
    end

    subgraph GovernLayer["Governance Layer"]
        G1[Data Protection Officer DPO]
        G2[Supervisory Authority]
        G3[Executive Sign-Off Board]
    end

    S1 --> D1
    A1 --> E1
    E1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D3 --> A1

    D1 --> P1
    D2 --> P2
    D3 --> P2
    D4 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G5[Risk Sign-Off] --> G3
    G2 --> G3
    G3 -->|Approval| CPSLayer
```

## 4.3 PIA Lifecycle Integration in CPS Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Requirements and Concept Design] --> R2[System Architecture Design]
    R2 --> R3[Prototype and Sensor Selection]
    R3 --> R4[Initial PIA: Pre-Development]
    R4 --> R5[Implementation and Integration]
    R5 --> R6[Pre-Deployment PIA: Final Review]
    R6 --> R7{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    R7 -->|No| R8[Redesign with Mitigations]
    R8 --> R4
    R7 -->|Yes| R9[Deployment and Operation]
    R9 --> R10[Continuous Monitoring]
    R10 --> R11[Annual PIA Re-Assessment]
    R11 --> R12{Major Change?}
    R12 -->|Yes| R4
    R12 -->|No| R10
    R10 --> R13[Decommission and Secure Data Disposal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes (KTU Exam-Ready):**
> - The **PIALayer** is not a separate physical block — it is a governance and engineering function that interfaces with **every** stage of the CPS pipeline.
> - The **arrow from G3 (Executive Sign-Off) to CPSLayer** emphasizes that no CPS deployment proceeds without PIA clearance.
> - The **loop from R11 back to R4** signifies the iterative nature of PIA in long-lived CPS deployments (cars, grid meters, medical devices have 10–20 year lifespans).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]
**Define Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA). List any four situations in which a PIA is mandatory for a Cyber-Physical System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)** is a systematic, risk-based process used to identify, evaluate, and mitigate the privacy risks arising from the collection, processing, storage, and sharing of personal data within a system, service, or project. In CPS, it extends the analysis to include physical-world data exposure from sensors and actuators.

**Four mandatory PIA situations in CPS:**
1. Large-scale processing of **Sensitive PII (SPII)** — health, biometric, or genetic data (e.g., wearable ECG monitor).
2. Systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area using CPS sensors (e.g., smart city surveillance CPS).
3. Use of autonomous AI/ML decision-making affecting individuals (e.g., autonomous vehicle passenger profiling).
4. Processing of children's data via CPS toys, smart classrooms, or educational robots.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4 situations — 0.5 mark each)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between a PIA and a DPIA. When does a CPS project require a Prior Consultation under GDPR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PIA | DPIA |
|--------|-----|------|
| Full Form | Privacy Impact Assessment | Data Protection Impact Assessment |
| Origin | General risk-management practice (NIST, ISO 29100) | Specifically mandated by GDPR Article 35 |
| Scope | Broad — any privacy-sensitive system | Narrow — systems with high data-protection risk |
| Outcome | Privacy risk report + mitigation plan | Compliance evidence for supervisory authorities |

**Prior Consultation Trigger:** A CPS project requires a **Prior Consultation** with the supervisory authority (GDPR Article 36) when, after applying all mitigation measures, the **residual privacy risk remains high** ($R_{residual} \geq 25$) or when the processing involves **SPII on a large scale** in a CPS context.

*(1.5 marks for differentiation, 1.5 marks for prior consultation trigger)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the five-phase methodology of conducting a Privacy Impact Assessment for a Cyber-Physical System. Illustrate the data-mapping step using a Data Flow Diagram for a smart healthcare CPS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart-grid Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) processes 15-minute interval energy-consumption data for 2 million households. The privacy risk under evaluation has:
- Likelihood $L = 3$ (Possible)
- Impact $I = 4$ (Major)
- CPS Context $C = 1.5$ (PII inferred from consumption patterns)
Compute the privacy risk score, classify the risk, and recommend two specific technical mitigations suitable for the AMI CPS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The five-phase PIA methodology for CPS is:

**Phase 1 — Threshold & Trigger Analysis (1 mark):**
Determine if a PIA is required. For a smart healthcare CPS, triggers include: SPII processing (health data), large-scale data, and use of novel IoT/AI technologies.

**Phase 2 — Data Mapping & Description (2 marks):**
Document what data is collected, why, how, where it flows, and how long it is retained. The Data Flow Diagram (DFD) for a smart healthcare CPS:

```
[Patient Body] --vitals--> [Wearable Sensor]
                              |
                              v
[Edge Gateway / Smartphone] --encrypted BLE-->
                              |
                              v
[Hospital Cloud Server] --TLS--> [Analytics Engine + ML Model]
                              |
                              v
[Electronic Health Record DB] --access controlled--> [Doctor Dashboard]
                              |
                              v
[Actuator: Drug Pump / Alert System] --command--> [Patient]
```

Data items: heart rate, SpO2, body temperature, GPS, activity level, patient ID.

**Phase 3 — Privacy Risk Assessment (2 marks):**
For each data item, compute $R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$. Identify threats: re-identification, unauthorized access during transmission, inference of health conditions from activity patterns.

**Phase 4 — Mitigation & Control Design (1 mark):**
Apply technical controls (end-to-end encryption, on-device anomaly detection), organizational controls (DPO oversight, staff training), and physical controls (tamper-resistant hardware).

**Phase 5 — Documentation, Consultation & Sign-Off (1 mark):**
Compile PIA report, consult DPO, conduct prior consultation if residual risk is high, schedule annual review.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the privacy risk score:**

$$R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$$

$$R_{privacy} = 3 \times 4 \times 1.5$$

$$R_{privacy} = 18$$

**Step 2 — Classify the risk:**

$$R_{privacy} = 18 \implies \text{Medium Risk (range 9–24)}$$

**Step 3 — Required actions per KTU Module 3 framework:**
- Apply enhanced mitigation controls
- Conduct DPO consultation
- Document residual risk post-mitigation

**Step 4 — Recommended technical mitigations for AMI CPS (2 marks):**

1. **Aggregation + Noise Injection (Differential Privacy):** Instead of storing 15-minute interval data per household, aggregate readings to neighborhood-level granularity or inject calibrated Laplace noise $\epsilon$-differentially private data. This prevents inference of occupancy, sleep patterns, and appliance use from individual household profiles.

   $$\tilde{x} = x + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

   where $\Delta f$ is sensitivity and $\epsilon$ is the privacy budget (smaller $\epsilon$ = stronger privacy).

2. **End-to-End Encryption with Hardware Security Modules (HSM):** All meter-to-utility communication uses TLS 1.3 with mutual authentication. Cryptographic keys are stored in tamper-resistant HSMs at the meter, preventing physical extraction attacks that would reveal raw 15-minute profiles.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
After mitigation, residual risk is expected to drop below 9 (Low), allowing deployment with continuous monitoring.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the CPS-specific privacy risks that make a PIA fundamentally different from a traditional IT-system privacy review. Provide three real-world examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital deploys an AI-powered ICU robot that processes patient video, voice, and biometric vitals continuously. For one identified risk:
- $L = 5$ (Almost Certain)
- $I = 5$ (Catastrophic)
- $C = 2.0$ (SPII in critical-care CPS)
Calculate the privacy risk score, classify it, and outline the complete mitigation strategy and the regulatory step (Prior Consultation) that must follow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

PIAs for CPS differ from IT-system PIAs in three fundamental ways:

1. **Physical-World Data Exposure (2 marks):** CPS sensors capture data from the physical world — gait, heart rate, sleep patterns, room occupancy. Unlike a leaked email, leaked CPS data can **directly manipulate the physical environment** (e.g., a compromised smart-lock opens a door; a misfiring insulin pump harms a patient). Real-world example: A research study showed that a smart home's electricity usage data could infer TV watching, cooking, and even sleep apnea events with 90%+ accuracy.

2. **Persistence and Ubiquity (2 marks):** CPS devices are embedded in homes, bodies, vehicles, and cities — they cannot be "logged out." A CPS collects data **24×7 without user awareness** (passive sensing). Real-world example: Fitness trackers have been shown to inadvertently reveal the location of secret military bases and user ovulation cycles, leading to command-level bans in some armed forces.

3. **Inference and Re-Identification (1.5 marks):** CPS data streams are highly correlatable. A single sensor may be benign, but fusing accelerometer + microphone + camera can re-identify individuals even in anonymized datasets. Real-world example: The **Strava heatmap incident (2018)** revealed the layout of undisclosed US military bases by aggregating public CPS (fitness tracker) data.

4. **Actuator-Triggered Physical Harm (1.5 marks):** Unlike a website, a CPS can cause physical harm if privacy is breached. Example: A hacked insulin pump or autonomous vehicle brake system. A PIA must therefore include **safety-privacy co-engineering**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the privacy risk score:**

$$R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$$

$$R_{privacy} = 5 \times 5 \times 2.0$$

$$R_{privacy} = 50$$

**Step 2 — Classify the risk:**

$$R_{privacy} = 50 \implies \text{Critical / Maximum Risk (upper bound of High range)}$$

**Step 3 — Required actions:**
This is the **maximum possible risk score**. The deployment is **non-permissible without complete redesign** or fully effective mitigation.

**Step 4 — Mitigation strategy (4 marks):**

| Layer | Mitigation |
|-------|------------|
| **Technical** | On-device processing (no video/audio leaves the robot); differential privacy on vitals stream; homomorphic encryption for analytics; hardware kill-switch |
| **Organizational** | DPO + Hospital Ethics Committee joint review; staff access logging with dual-control approval; staff privacy training; 6-monthly PIA re-review |
| **Physical** | Tamper-evident seals on robot hardware; physical covers for camera when not in clinical use; restricted ward access |
| **Data Lifecycle** | Auto-deletion of raw video within 24 hours; encrypted backups with time-limited keys; cryptographic erasure on decommission |

**Step 5 — Regulatory step (3 marks):**

Per **GDPR Article 36**, the hospital must conduct a **Prior Consultation** with the supervisory data protection authority **before** deployment. The consultation must include:
- Description of the processing, purposes, and CPS context
- Assessment of risks to data subjects
- Measures envisaged to address the risks
- DPO contact details
- Any prior consultations with stakeholders

The supervisory authority may impose additional conditions, modify the design, or **prohibit** the processing if risks cannot be sufficiently mitigated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PIA Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Context Multiplier (C):** Many students compute $R = L \times I$ only, losing 1–2 marks. The KTU Module 3 framework **mandates** the CPS context multiplier $C$ in the risk formula. Always state the value of $C$ with justification.
>
> 2. **Confusing PIA with DPIA:** PIA is a general term; DPIA is GDPR-specific. Examiners award marks only when the distinction is clearly articulated.
>
> 3. **Skipping Prior Consultation:** Whenever $R_{residual} \geq 25$, the answer **must** mention Prior Consultation under GDPR Article 36. Skipping this loses a full mark in 14-mark answers.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Physical Aspect:** A CPS PIA that lists only "encryption" and "access control" without addressing **physical-world consequences** (actuator safety, sensor placement) is considered incomplete. Always add at least one **CPS-specific** control.
>
> 5. **No Quantitative Analysis:** 14-mark PIA questions often include numerical data. Answers that remain purely qualitative score below 10/14. Always include a **risk score calculation**.
>
> 6. **Not Mapping Data Flows:** A PIA without a DFD or textual data-flow description is treated as incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — PIAs in CPS):**

- ☐ **Definition:** A PIA is a systematic, iterative, risk-based process to identify, assess, mitigate, and document privacy risks in a system.
- ☐ **Core Formula:** $R_{privacy} = L \times I \times C$ — three-factor model with CPS context multiplier.
- ☐ **Five Phases:** Threshold Check → Data Mapping → Risk Assessment → Mitigation → Documentation & Sign-Off.
- ☐ **Three PIA Triggers:** Large-scale SPII processing, AI/ML autonomous decisions, public-area systematic monitoring.
- ☐ **CPS-Specific Multipliers:** PII → C=1.5; SPII in CPS → **C=2.0**; non-PII → C=1.0.
- ☐ **Risk Bands:** Low (1–8), Medium (9–24), High (25–50).
- ☐ **Prior Consultation:** Mandatory under GDPR Art. 36 when $R_{residual} \geq 25$ or SPII is processed at scale.
- ☐ **Three Control Categories:** Technical (encryption, DP), Organizational (DPO, training), Physical (tamper-proof hardware).
- ☐ **PIA ≠ DPIA:** PIA is broad/general; DPIA is GDPR Article 35 specific.
- ☐ **Data Flow Diagrams are mandatory** in the data-mapping phase.
- ☐ **Necessity Test:** $Necessity = \vert D_{essential} \vert / \vert D_{collected} \vert$ — must approach 1.0.
- ☐ **CPS differs from IT-PIA** due to: physical-world exposure, persistence, inference risk, and actuator-triggered harm.
- ☐ **PIA must be iterative** — re-evaluated annually or upon any major system change.
- ☐ **Decommissioning PIA** is required: secure data disposal, cryptographic erasure, and end-of-life documentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mitigating Privacy Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mitigating Privacy Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Privacy Risk Mitigation in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refers to the systematic application of architectural, cryptographic, statistical, and policy-driven controls designed to reduce the probability and impact of unauthorized identification, inference, or exposure of sensitive data flowing across the sensing, computation, communication, and actuation layers of a CPS.

According to KTU 2024 PECPT743 terminology, mitigation strategies are categorized into four intersecting pillars:

1. **Preventive Controls** — stop the privacy breach before it occurs (e.g., encryption, access control).
2. **Detective Controls** — identify and report ongoing or past privacy violations (e.g., audit logging, anomaly detection).
3. **Corrective Controls** — limit damage after a breach (e.g., key revocation, data purging).
4. **Deterrent Controls** — discourage attackers via policy and accountability (e.g., digital signatures, legal frameworks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Mitigation is not a single technology — it is a **layered defense-in-depth** strategy combining PETs (Privacy Enhancing Technologies), data minimization, anonymization, cryptographic primitives, and governance frameworks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **smart hospital** where patients wear IoT health monitors (heart rate, glucose, blood pressure). This is a CPS. The hospital wants to use the aggregated data to train an AI model that predicts cardiac events. However, the raw data is highly sensitive.

- **Without mitigation:** A hacker accesses the database and learns "Patient X has diabetes and arrhythmia" → **Privacy breach**.
- **With mitigation:** The hospital *removes names* (anonymization), *adds noise* (differential privacy), *trains the model locally on each device* (federated learning), and *encrypts the model updates* (homomorphic encryption). The hacker now only sees noisy, aggregated, encrypted patterns.

The mitigation techniques act like **layers of armor** on a knight — even if one layer is pierced, the others protect the underlying identity and data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Privacy mitigation in CPS is uniquely challenging because the system bridges the **physical world (sensors/actuators)** and the **cyber world (cloud/AI)**. A mitigation that protects cloud data but ignores side-channel leaks from a sensor is incomplete.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **PET** | Privacy Enhancing Technology |
| **k-Anonymity** | A record is indistinguishable from at least *k* others |
| **Differential Privacy** | Adding calibrated noise so any single record is hidden |
| **Homomorphic Encryption** | Computation on encrypted data without decryption |
| **Federated Learning** | Training models locally; sharing only gradients |
| **Pseudonymization** | Replacing identifiers with reversible tokens |
| **Anonymization** | Replacing identifiers with irreversible tokens |
| **TEE** | Trusted Execution Environment (hardware isolation) |
| **DP-3T** | Decentralized Privacy-Preserving Proximity Tracing |
| **Privacy Budget (ε)** | Total allowed privacy leakage in DP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Mitigation Framework

Mitigation in CPS is organized into **four operational layers**, each addressing a different attack surface:

### Layer 1 — Data Layer (At-Rest Protection)
- **Anonymization**: Replace direct identifiers (name, SSN, device ID) with synthetic IDs.
- **Pseudonymization**: Use reversible tokens managed by a trusted authority.
- **k-Anonymity**: Generalize quasi-identifiers (age, ZIP, gender) so each tuple matches ≥ *k* records.
- **l-Diversity**: Ensures sensitive attribute has ≥ *l* distinct values per equivalence class.
- **t-Closeness**: Distribution of sensitive attribute in a class is within *t* of the global distribution.

### Layer 2 — Computation Layer (In-Use Protection)
- **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)**: Allows computation directly on ciphertext.
  - *Partially HE* (Paillier) — supports only addition.
  - *Somewhat HE* (BGV, BFV) — limited multiplication depth.
  - *Fully HE* (FHE, e.g., CKKS, TFHE) — arbitrary operations.
- **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)**: Multiple parties jointly compute a function while keeping inputs secret (e.g., Yao's Garbled Circuits, Secret Sharing).
- **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)**: Hardware-isolated region (e.g., Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) that encrypts memory during processing.
- **Federated Learning (FL)**: Trains a global model by aggregating local gradients — raw data never leaves the device.

### Layer 3 — Communication Layer (In-Transit Protection)
- **End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)** using TLS 1.3, DTLS, or QUIC.
- **Mix Networks (Mixnets)**: Route messages through intermediary nodes that shuffle and delay to break traffic analysis.
- **Onion Routing (Tor-style)**: Layered encryption peeled at each hop.
- **Differential Privacy over streams**: Add Laplace/Gaussian noise to real-time sensor data.

### Layer 4 — Policy & Governance Layer (In-Practice Protection)
- **Privacy by Design (PbD)** — Ann Cavoukian's 7 foundational principles.
- **Data Minimization** — collect only what is strictly necessary.
- **Consent Management** — explicit, granular, revocable user consent.
- **Audit Logging & Accountability** — tamper-evident logs.
- **Regulatory Alignment** — GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA.

## 2.2 Differential Privacy — The Mathematical Backbone

Differential Privacy (DP) is the **gold standard** for quantifiable privacy guarantees in CPS data aggregation.

**Definition (ε-Differential Privacy):**
A randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies ε-differential privacy if for any two neighboring datasets $D$ and $D'$ differing in exactly one record, and for any output $S \subseteq \text{Range}(\mathcal{M})$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

- **ε (epsilon)** = **Privacy Budget** — smaller ε ⇒ stronger privacy, more noise.
- **δ (delta)** = failure probability (typically $\delta \ll 1/n$).

**Noise Mechanisms:**
- **Laplace Mechanism** (for numerical queries):
  $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$
- **Gaussian Mechanism** (for $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP):
  $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^2\right), \quad \sigma \geq \frac{\Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)}}{\varepsilon}$$

**Sensitivity** $\Delta f$ = maximum change in query output when one record is added/removed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **ε-DP Guarantee** | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$ | $\varepsilon > 0$ |
| **Laplace Noise Scale** | $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ | Real number |
| **Gaussian Noise Std Dev** | $\sigma \geq \Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$ | Real number |
| **Global Sensitivity** | $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} \lVert f(D) - f(D') \rVert_1$ | Query-dependent |
| **Composition (Sequential)** | $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | Advanced Composition tighter |
| **k-Anonymity** | Every record shares quasi-ID with $\geq k$ others | $k \geq 2$ |
| **l-Diversity** | $\geq l$ distinct sensitive values per class | $l \geq 2$ |
| **Paillier Encryption** | $E(m) = g^m \cdot r^n \mod n^2$ | $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ |
| **Secret Sharing (Shamir)** | Reconstruct from $t$ of $n$ shares via Lagrange | Threshold $t$ |
| **FL Aggregation** | $w_{global} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i}{n} w_i$ | Weighted average |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Differential privacy is used by Apple (emoji usage), Google (RAPPOR), and the U.S. Census Bureau. In CPS, it protects smart-meter readings and wearable health data during cloud aggregation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Privacy Budget Composition

When multiple queries are answered on the same dataset, the privacy guarantees **degrade**. The **basic sequential composition theorem** states that answering $k$ queries, each satisfying $\varepsilon_i$-DP, results in total privacy loss:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{M}_k$ be mechanisms each providing $\varepsilon_i$-DP.

**Step 1** — Define the composed mechanism as:
$$\mathcal{M}(D) = (\mathcal{M}_1(D), \mathcal{M}_2(D), \ldots, \mathcal{M}_k(D))$$

**Step 2** — For any two neighboring datasets $D, D'$ and any output set $S = S_1 \times S_2 \times \cdots \times S_k$:
$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_i(D) \in S_i]$$

**Step 3** — Apply the DP definition to each factor:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{k} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_i(D) \in S_i] \leq \prod_{i=1}^{k} e^{\varepsilon_i} \Pr[\mathcal{M}_i(D') \in S_i]$$

**Step 4** — Combine the exponential terms:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{k} e^{\varepsilon_i} = e^{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i} = e^{\varepsilon_{total}}$$

**Step 5** — Therefore:
$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon_{total}} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

This confirms $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon_{total}$-DP with $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Laplace Mechanism for Smart Meter Aggregation

**Scenario:** A smart grid CPS collects hourly electricity consumption $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ from $n$ households. The utility wants to release the mean $\bar{x}$ for grid load balancing while preserving household privacy.

**Step 1** — Define the query:
$$f(D) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 2** — Compute global sensitivity. Removing one record changes the sum by at most $\max(x_i)$ and the mean by at most:
$$\Delta f = \frac{\max(x_i)}{n}$$

**Step 3** — Apply Laplace mechanism for $\varepsilon$-DP:
$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\max(x_i)}{n \varepsilon}\right)$$

**Step 4** — Variance of the noise (privacy-utility tradeoff):
$$\text{Var}[\mathcal{M}(D)] = 2 \left(\frac{\max(x_i)}{n \varepsilon}\right)^2$$

**Step 5** — **Interpretation:** As $n$ grows (more households), the noise scales as $1/n$ — so utility improves with population size. This is the **blessing of aggregation** in CPS.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Privacy Strength | Computation Overhead | CPS Suitability | Key Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Anonymization** | Low–Medium | Negligible | High | Vulnerable to linkage attacks |
| **k-Anonymity** | Medium | Low | High | Does not protect against homogeneity attack |
| **Differential Privacy** | High (provable) | Low–Medium | Very High | Noise reduces data utility |
| **Homomorphic Encryption** | Very High | Very High | Medium (edge-friendly variants exist) | Computational cost |
| **Federated Learning** | High | Medium | Very High | Vulnerable to gradient leakage |
| **SMPC** | Very High | High | Medium | Communication complexity |
| **TEE** | High | Low | Very High | Hardware trust assumption |
| **Mixnets / Tor** | High | Medium | Medium | Latency unsuitable for real-time control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A complete answer must mention **at least one cryptographic technique** and **at least one statistical technique** — examiners look for breadth across mitigation categories.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Privacy Mitigation Architecture in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 - DATA AT REST"]
        D1["Anonymization"]
        D2["Pseudonymization"]
        D3["k-Anonymity"]
        D4["l-Diversity"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 - DATA IN USE"]
        C1["Homomorphic Encryption"]
        C2["Secure Multi-Party Computation"]
        C3["Trusted Execution Environment"]
        C4["Federated Learning"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 - DATA IN TRANSIT"]
        T1["TLS 1.3 / DTLS"]
        T2["Onion Routing"]
        T3["Mix Networks"]
        T4["Differential Privacy Streams"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 - POLICY AND GOVERNANCE"]
        P1["Privacy by Design"]
        P2["Consent Management"]
        P3["Audit Logging"]
        P4["Regulatory Compliance"]
    end

    subgraph CPS["CYBER PHYSICAL SYSTEM"]
        S["Physical Sensors and Actuators"]
        E["Edge and Fog Nodes"]

    end

    S --> E --> CL
    L1 -.protects.-> CL
    L2 -.protects.-> E
    L3 -.protects.-> S
    L4 -.governs.-> CPS

    style L1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#5f1e3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#1e5f3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style L4 fill:#5f5f1e,stroke:#000
    style CPS fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Differential Privacy Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original CPS Dataset D"] --> B["Query Function f"]
    B --> C["True Answer f D"]
    C --> D["Add Laplace Noise with scale b equal to delta f over epsilon"]
    D --> E["Noisy Output M D"]
    E --> F["Privacy Guarantee epsilon DP"]
    A --> G["Neighboring Dataset D prime"]
    G --> B
    E --> H["Utility Loss Variance of Noise"]

    style A fill:#1e5f3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#5f1e3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#5f5f1e,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Federated Learning in CPS — Mitigating Centralized Data Exposure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EDGE["EDGE DEVICES IN CPS"]
        D1["Hospital A Local Model"]
        D2["Hospital B Local Model"]
        D3["Hospital C Local Model"]
    end

    subgraph AGG["AGGREGATION SERVER"]
        AGG1["Secure Aggregation Protocol"]
        AGG2["Global Model Update"]
    end

    D1 -->|"Encrypted Gradients"| AGG1
    D2 -->|"Encrypted Gradients"| AGG1
    D3 -->|"Encrypted Gradients"| AGG1
    AGG1 --> AGG2
    AGG2 -->|"Updated Global Model"| D1
    AGG2 -->|"Updated Global Model"| D2
    AGG2 -->|"Updated Global Model"| D3

    style EDGE fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style AGG fill:#5f1e3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The raw patient/sensor data **never leaves** the edge device. Only model parameters (often DP-noised) traverse the network — a powerful mitigation against centralized breaches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]
**Define Differential Privacy. Explain the role of the privacy budget ε.**

**Model Answer:**

Differential Privacy (DP) is a mathematical framework that provides a **quantifiable privacy guarantee** by ensuring that the output of a query mechanism $\mathcal{M}$ does not significantly change when any single record in the dataset is added or removed.

Formally, $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varepsilon$-differential privacy if for all neighboring datasets $D, D'$ and all output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

**Role of ε (Privacy Budget):**
- $\varepsilon$ is a **positive real number** controlling the strength of the privacy guarantee.
- **Smaller ε → Stronger privacy** (more noise added).
- **Larger ε → Weaker privacy** (closer to non-private output).
- Typical values: $\varepsilon = 0.1$ (very strong), $\varepsilon = 1$ (moderate), $\varepsilon = 10$ (weak).
- ε is a **consumable resource** — repeated queries deplete it via composition.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**List any four Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs) with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Differential Privacy** — Adds calibrated noise to query outputs to mathematically bound individual record leakage.
2. **Homomorphic Encryption** — Enables computation directly on encrypted data without requiring decryption.
3. **Federated Learning** — Trains ML models across distributed devices while keeping raw data local.
4. **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** — Allows multiple parties to jointly compute a function while keeping their inputs private.
5. **Anonymous Communication (Mixnets/Tor)** — Hides communication patterns by routing through intermediary nodes.
6. **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)** — Hardware-isolated memory region that protects data during processing.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the architecture of a Cyber-Physical System and identify the privacy risks at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail how **Differential Privacy**, **Federated Learning**, and **Homomorphic Encryption** mitigate these risks, with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) CPS Architecture and Privacy Risks (7 marks):**

A CPS integrates **physical processes, computational intelligence, and communication networks** in a feedback loop. It has four primary layers:

| Layer | Components | Privacy Risks |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Perception/Sensing** | IoT sensors, RFID, wearables, smart meters | Sensor data exposure, side-channel inference, location tracking |
| **2. Communication/Network** | Wi-Fi, 5G, Bluetooth, LoRa, MQTT | Eavesdropping, traffic analysis, man-in-the-middle |
| **3. Computation/Edge-Cloud** | Edge servers, cloud AI, databases | Centralized data breach, model inversion, inference attacks |
| **4. Actuation/Control** | Actuators, SCADA, industrial controllers | Command injection, identity spoofing, behavioral profiling |

Additional risks include **linkage attacks** (combining anonymized datasets), **re-identification** (Netflix Prize controversy), and **membership inference** (determining if a record was in training data).

**(b) Mitigation Techniques (7 marks):**

**1. Differential Privacy (DP):**
DP adds Laplace or Gaussian noise to query outputs. For a query $f$ with sensitivity $\Delta f$:
$$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\right)$$

In CPS, DP protects **aggregated smart meter data, healthcare statistics, and traffic flow** from re-identification. Composition theorem ensures privacy degrades gracefully: $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.

**2. Federated Learning (FL):**
FL keeps training data on local edge devices (e.g., smartphones, hospital servers) and shares only **model gradients**. The global model is updated as:
$$w_{global}^{(t+1)} = w_{global}^{(t)} - \eta \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{n_i}{n} \nabla L_i(w_{global}^{(t)})$$

Combined with **Secure Aggregation** and **DP-noised gradients**, FL prevents raw data exposure during AI training in CPS.

**3. Homomorphic Encryption (HE):**
HE permits computation on ciphertext $E(m)$ such that $\text{Dec}(E(m_1) \oplus E(m_2)) = m_1 + m_2$. In CPS:
- **Edge sensors** encrypt readings before transmission.
- **Cloud AI** processes encrypted data to generate encrypted predictions.
- **Authorized users** decrypt only the final output.

This eliminates the "data must be decrypted to be useful" vulnerability that plagues traditional cloud CPS architectures.

**Synergy:** Combining all three (FL + HE + DP) provides a **defense-in-depth** framework — the gold standard for privacy-preserving CPS.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** **(a)** What is **k-Anonymity**? Explain its limitations and how **l-Diversity** and **t-Closeness** address them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** and **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** can jointly mitigate privacy risks in a CPS. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) k-Anonymity and Its Enhancements (7 marks):**

**k-Anonymity** ensures that each record in a released dataset is **indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ other records** based on quasi-identifiers (QIs) like age, ZIP, and gender.

**Example:** A hospital dataset with QIs = {Age, ZIP, Diagnosis}. If $k = 3$, every unique combination of Age and ZIP must appear at least 3 times.

**Limitations of k-Anonymity:**

1. **Homogeneity Attack:** If all $k$ records in an equivalence class share the same sensitive attribute (e.g., all have "Cancer"), the attacker learns the diagnosis despite anonymization.
2. **Background Knowledge Attack:** Attacker uses external knowledge (e.g., "the patient is the only male under 30 in the class") to de-anonymize.
3. **High-Dimensional Data:** Achieving k-anonymity requires heavy generalization, destroying utility.

**l-Diversity (Fix #1):**
Strengthens k-anonymity by requiring that each equivalence class contain **at least $l$ distinct values** of the sensitive attribute. This thwarts the homogeneity attack.

**t-Closeness (Fix #2):**
Requires that the distribution of the sensitive attribute in any equivalence class be **within distance $t$** (typically Earth Mover's Distance) of the global distribution. This mitigates skewness and background knowledge attacks.

**(b) SMPC and TEE for CPS Privacy (7 marks):**

**Scenario:** Three hospitals (A, B, C) want to jointly train a cancer-detection model on their combined patient data, but **regulations forbid sharing raw records** (HIPAA, DPDP Act).

**Solution — SMPC (Yao's Garbled Circuit + Secret Sharing):**

- Each hospital **secret-shares** its data among three compute nodes: $[x_i]_1, [x_i]_2, [x_i]_3$.
- The compute nodes execute the training algorithm on shares, exchanging **intermediate shares** (not raw data).
- At the end, shares are recombined via Lagrange interpolation to produce the **global model**.
- No single node ever sees a complete patient record.

**Enhancement with TEE (e.g., Intel SGX):**

- The aggregation server runs inside an **enclave** — a hardware-encrypted memory region.
- Even if the host OS is compromised, the enclave's code and data remain confidential.
- **Remote attestation** allows hospitals to cryptographically verify that the aggregation runs inside a genuine TEE before sending their shares.

**Joint Benefit:** SMPC provides **cryptographic privacy**; TEE provides **hardware-rooted trust**. Together, they address both *software* and *hardware* adversaries — a complete mitigation for CPS data aggregation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing pseudonymization with anonymization.** Pseudonymization is *reversible* (with a key); anonymization is *irreversible*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of ε.** Writing DP definition without explaining the privacy budget is incomplete.
> 3. **Treating mitigation as a single technology.** Always mention **at least two categories** (cryptographic + statistical, or technical + policy).
> 4. **Ignoring CPS-specific context.** Generic answers about "data privacy in databases" miss the sensor-actuator-cloud integration. Always tie answers back to **physical-cyber interaction**.
> 5. **Skipping the formula.** Any question mentioning DP, k-Anonymity, or composition theorem **requires** the mathematical expression for full marks.
> 6. **Not discussing trade-offs.** Mitigation always involves a privacy-utility-performance trade-off. Examiners reward students who acknowledge this honestly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Mitigation is multi-layered**, not a single tool — combine cryptographic, statistical, architectural, and policy controls.
- **Differential Privacy** provides **provable, quantifiable** guarantees parameterized by the **privacy budget ε**.
- **Privacy budget ε** is consumable: repeated queries accumulate via **sequential composition** $\varepsilon_{total} = \sum \varepsilon_i$.
- **Laplace mechanism** uses noise scale $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$; **Gaussian mechanism** uses $\sigma \geq \Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$.
- **k-Anonymity** protects against record linkage but **fails** against homogeneity and background knowledge attacks.
- **l-Diversity** and **t-Closeness** are **enhancements** to k-Anonymity — not replacements.
- **Homomorphic Encryption** allows computation on ciphertext; FHE (CKKS, TFHE) supports arbitrary operations.
- **Federated Learning** + **Secure Aggregation** + **DP-noised gradients** = state-of-the-art CPS privacy.
- **SMPC** (Yao's Garbled Circuits, Shamir Secret Sharing) protects inputs during joint computation.
- **TEE** (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) provides **hardware-rooted trust** via encrypted memory enclaves.
- **Privacy by Design** (PbD) is a **principle**, not a technology — embed it from the architecture phase.
- **Data minimization** is the **cheapest** and most effective mitigation — do not collect what you do not need.
- **CPS-specific risks** include sensor side-channels, actuation command injection, and real-time streaming privacy — generic IT mitigations are insufficient.
- **Always mention trade-offs:** privacy vs. utility, privacy vs. latency, privacy vs. computational cost.
- **Regulatory alignment** (GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA) is part of the **governance layer** and is mandatory for compliance questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Privacy Policies and Compliance: Developing Privacy Policies for CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Privacy Policies and Compliance: Developing Privacy Policies for CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Privacy Policy** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is a formalized, institutionally sanctioned, and legally binding declaration that articulates the principles, rules, procedures, and technical-organizational controls governing the collection, processing, storage, sharing, retention, and disposal of personally identifiable information (PII) and sensitive telemetry data generated, transmitted, or processed by sensor–actuator networks, embedded controllers, edge gateways, and cloud backends that together constitute a CPS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A privacy policy is NOT merely a legal document. In CPS, it is a *living engineering artifact* that maps directly to data-plane control mechanisms, access-control rules, encryption policies, and audit-trail configurations.

**Privacy Compliance** is the continuous state of adherence of a CPS deployment to a specified set of legal, regulatory, contractual, and organizational privacy obligations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, India's DPDP Act 2023, NIST Privacy Framework).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Home as a CPS

Think of a modern **Smart Home** as a CPS:
- *Sensors* (cameras, motion detectors, smart meters) = the "eyes and ears"
- *Smart Hub* (Alexa, Google Home) = the "brain"
- *Cloud Services* (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub) = the "long-term memory"
- *Actuators* (smart locks, thermostats) = the "hands"

Now imagine if, every night, your smart home quietly **logged who entered each room, recorded conversations, and shared this data with advertisers**. You would be alarmed. A **Privacy Policy** is essentially the **"House Rules"** displayed at the entrance — it tells every visitor (user), every appliance (sensor), and every backend service (cloud) what data may be collected, for what purpose, how long it is kept, and who can see it. **Compliance** is the *proof* that these rules are actually being followed, verified by independent audits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU Exam Tip:** When asked for analogies, use the **Smart Home**, **Connected Vehicle**, or **Wearable Health Monitor (e.g., smartwatch)** — these are board-examiner favourites because they map cleanly to all three CPS layers (Perception, Network, Application).

## 1.3 Why Privacy Policies Are Distinctive in CPS

CPS differs from traditional IT systems in **three privacy-critical ways**:

| CPS Characteristic | Privacy Implication |
|---|---|
| **Continuous, ambient data collection** | Users cannot give meaningful one-time consent |
| **Convergence of physical and cyber data** | A sensor reading + a face = re-identification risk |
| **Real-time actuation** | Privacy breach can cause *physical* harm (e.g., smart medical device) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically requires understanding that CPS privacy policies must address **physical-world consequences** of data exposure — a dimension absent in traditional IT privacy.

## 1.4 Core Metrics (Standard KTU Vocabulary)

The following are **standardized metrics** universally referenced in KTU board answers:

- **PII** (Personally Identifiable Information) — bold
- **DPIA** (Data Protection Impact Assessment) — bold
- **ROPA** (Record of Processing Activities) — bold
- **SLA** (Service Level Agreement) — bold
- **SOC 2** (Service Organization Control Type 2) — bold
- **ISO 27701** — bold

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of a CPS Privacy Policy

A robust privacy policy for CPS is structured around **five logical pillars**, each addressing a specific engineering and legal concern:

1. **Data Classification Pillar**
   - Identifies *what* data is collected (telemetry, biometric, location, behavioural).
   - Assigns a sensitivity tier: **Public → Internal → Confidential → Restricted**.

2. **Consent and Lawful Basis Pillar**
   - Establishes the legal grounds for processing (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, public task, legitimate interest — the GDPR Article 6 bases).
   - In CPS, consent must be **granular** and **revocable** (e.g., a user can disable the camera but keep the motion sensor).

3. **Purpose Limitation and Minimization Pillar**
   - Data must be collected for a **specific, explicit, and legitimate purpose**.
   - **Data minimization**: collect only what is strictly necessary.

4. **Security Controls Pillar**
   - Maps policy rules to technical controls: encryption (AES-256), anonymization (k-anonymity, differential privacy), access control (RBAC/ABAC), audit logging.

5. **Accountability and Governance Pillar**
   - Defines roles: **Data Controller**, **Data Processor**, **Data Protection Officer (DPO)**.
   - Mandates documentation: ROPA, DPIA reports, breach notification procedures.

## 2.2 Compliance Frameworks Applicable to CPS

| Framework | Jurisdiction | CPS Relevance | Key Mandate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | European Union | High (extraterritorial) | Lawful basis, DPIA, 72-hr breach notification |
| **CCPA / CPRA** | California, USA | High | Right to know, delete, opt-out of sale |
| **HIPAA** | USA (Healthcare) | Critical for medical CPS | PHI safeguards, minimum-necessary rule |
| **DPDP Act 2023** | India | High | Consent manager, data principal rights |
| **NIST PF** | Global / USA | Universal | Identify–Govern–Control–Communicate–Protect |
| **ISO/IEC 27701** | Global | Universal | Extension of ISO 27001 for privacy |
| **IEEE 7000 Series** | Global | High for CPS | Ethical design, transparency |

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Policy Development

- **Why** a separate CPS policy? — Because traditional IT policies ignore **actuation risk** and **inference risk** unique to CPS.
- **How** is it developed? — Through a **Privacy-by-Design (PbD)** methodology, which mandates that privacy is engineered *into* the system from the inception phase, not bolted on later.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In all formulas below, the symbol $\vert$ is rendered as `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inherent Risk Score (IRS)** | $IRS = L \times I$ | Likelihood × Impact | Score: 1–25 |
| **Residual Risk (RR)** | $RR = IRS \times (1 - C_{eff})$ | Risk after controls | Score: 0–25 |
| **Control Effectiveness ($C_{eff}$)** | $C_{eff} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i}{W_{max}}$ | Weighted control score | 0 to 1 |
| **Privacy Compliance Score (PCS)** | $PCS = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \delta_j \cdot a_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \delta_j} \times 100$ | Audit-weighted compliance | 0 to 100 % |
| **Re-identification Risk ($R_{id}$)** | $R_{id} = \frac{1}{k}$ for $k$-anonymity | Inverse of equivalence class size | 0 to 1 |
| **Differential Privacy Budget ($\varepsilon$)** | $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ (strong) ; $\varepsilon \leq 3.0$ (moderate) | Privacy loss parameter | Dimensionless |
| **Breach Notification SLA** | $T_{notify} \leq 72$ hours (GDPR Art. 33) | Mandatory notification window | Hours |

Where:
- $L$ = Likelihood (1–5)
- $I$ = Impact (1–5)
- $w_i$ = weight of control $i$
- $c_i$ = maturity score of control $i$ (0–1)
- $\delta_j$ = audit question criticality weight
- $a_j$ = audit response (0 or 1)
- $k$ = equivalence class size

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real CPS Deployments

- **Connected Vehicles:** Privacy policies govern location, driving-pattern, and biometric data, with strict controls preventing third-party data brokers from accessing telemetry.
- **Smart Healthcare:** HIPAA-mandated policies restrict actuator commands (e.g., insulin pump) to authenticated, authorized clinicians.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Policies prevent leakage of proprietary production data while ensuring worker-safety sensors (e.g., gas detectors) transmit alerts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Residual Privacy Risk in a CPS
