**Problem Statement:** A CPS smart-meter deployment has a likelihood of privacy breach $L = 4$ and an impact $I = 5$. The deployed controls (encryption at rest, RBAC, audit logging, anonymization) have weights and maturity scores as below. Compute the Residual Risk.

| Control | Weight $w_i$ | Maturity $c_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Encryption at rest | 0.30 | 0.85 |
| RBAC | 0.25 | 0.70 |
| Audit logging | 0.20 | 0.60 |
| Anonymization | 0.25 | 0.50 |

**Step 1 — Compute Inherent Risk Score (IRS):**

$$
IRS = L \times I
$$

$$
IRS = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weighted control effectiveness $C_{eff}$:**

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot c_i}{W_{max}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{(0.30 \cdot 0.85) + (0.25 \cdot 0.70) + (0.20 \cdot 0.60) + (0.25 \cdot 0.50)}{1.00}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
0.30 \cdot 0.85 = 0.2550
$$

$$
0.25 \cdot 0.70 = 0.1750
$$

$$
0.20 \cdot 0.60 = 0.1200
$$

$$
0.25 \cdot 0.50 = 0.1250
$$

Sum them:

$$
0.2550 + 0.1750 + 0.1200 + 0.1250 = 0.6750
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{0.6750}{1.00} = 0.6750
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Residual Risk (RR):**

$$
RR = IRS \times (1 - C_{eff})
$$

$$
RR = 20 \times (1 - 0.6750)
$$

$$
RR = 20 \times 0.3250 = 6.50
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result using a standard KTU risk matrix:**

| RR Range | Risk Tier | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0–4 | **Low** | Accept |
| 5–10 | **Medium** | Mitigate within 90 days |
| 11–16 | **High** | Mitigate within 30 days |
| 17–25 | **Critical** | Halt deployment |

Since $RR = 6.50$ → **Medium Risk** → Mitigation required within 90 days. The student should recommend strengthening the anonymization control (lowest maturity at 0.50).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Privacy Compliance Score (PCS) for a CPS Audit

A CPS healthcare device manufacturer is audited against **5 critical GDPR controls**. Each control is binary (Pass = 1 / Fail = 0) and weighted by criticality. Compute PCS.

| Control | Weight $\delta_j$ | Pass $a_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| Lawful basis documented | 0.30 | 1 |
| DPIA conducted | 0.25 | 1 |
| Encryption in transit | 0.20 | 1 |
| Breach notification SLA | 0.15 | 0 |
| DPO appointed | 0.10 | 1 |

$$
PCS = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \delta_j \cdot a_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} \delta_j} \times 100
$$

Numerator (sum of weighted passes):

$$
(0.30 \cdot 1) + (0.25 \cdot 1) + (0.20 \cdot 1) + (0.15 \cdot 0) + (0.10 \cdot 1)
$$

$$
= 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.00 + 0.10 = 0.85
$$

Denominator (sum of all weights):

$$
0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

Therefore:

$$
PCS = \frac{0.85}{1.00} \times 100 = 85\%
$$

**Interpretation:** An 85% PCS is **Good**, but the missing breach-notification SLA is a critical non-conformance that can attract regulatory fines of up to **4% of global turnover** under GDPR.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Policy Rules to CPS Layers

| Policy Clause | Perception Layer | Network Layer | Application Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data minimization | On-device filtering | Edge aggregation | Query restriction |
| Consent | Opt-in via HMI | Tokenized telemetry | Dashboard toggle |
| Anonymization | Local hashing | Pseudonymization | Differential privacy |
| Audit | Sensor logs | Flow records (NetFlow) | API access logs |
| Encryption | Secure Element (SE) | TLS 1.3 / DTLS | AES-256 at rest |

This mapping is **board-exam critical** — questions on "how to enforce policy in CPS" expect a layered answer.

---

## 3.4 Policy Maturity Model Derivation (5-Level Scale)

A CPS organization self-assesses on **6 privacy capabilities**. Each capability is rated 1–5. Compute the **organizational Privacy Maturity Index (PMI):**

$$
PMI = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} M_k
$$

where $M_k$ is the maturity rating of capability $k$ and $N = 6$.

Capabilities: Governance (3), Risk Mgmt (4), Data Mapping (2), Consent Mgmt (4), Incident Response (5), Training (3).

$$
PMI = \frac{3 + 4 + 2 + 4 + 5 + 3}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.50
$$

**Maturity Scale:**

| PMI Range | Level | Label |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 – 1.8 | 1 | Initial / Ad-hoc |
| 1.9 – 2.6 | 2 | Repeatable |
| 2.7 – 3.4 | 3 | Defined |
| 3.5 – 4.2 | 4 | Managed |
| 4.3 – 5.0 | 5 | Optimizing |

Therefore $PMI = 3.50$ → **Level 4 (Managed)** — a respectable position, but **Data Mapping (rating 2)** is the weakest link and must be prioritized.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Privacy Policy Development Lifecycle for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1 Identify Scope and Stakeholders] --> A2[Stage 2 Data Inventory and Mapping]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Threat and Risk Assessment]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4 Data Protection Impact Assessment DPIA]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5 Policy Drafting and Legal Review]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6 Approval by Data Controller and DPO]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 7 Implementation and Engineering Controls]
    A7 --> A8[Stage 8 Training and Awareness]
    A8 --> A9[Stage 9 Monitoring Audit and Review]
    A9 --> A10[Stage 10 Continuous Improvement and Update]
    A10 --> A1
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The lifecycle is **cyclical**, not linear. The output of Stage 10 (lessons learned) feeds back into Stage 1 (scope refinement), reflecting the *continuous* nature of privacy compliance in evolving CPS deployments.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — CPS Privacy Compliance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Legal and Regulatory Layer] --> B2[GDPR]
    B1 --> B3[CCPA]
    B1 --> B4[HIPAA]
    B1 --> B5[India DPDP 2023]
    B2 --> B6[Policy and Governance Layer]
    B3 --> B6
    B4 --> B6
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Privacy by Design PbD]
    B6 --> B8[Accountability and DPO]
    B6 --> B9[Consent Management]
    B7 --> B10[CPS Engineering Layer]
    B8 --> B10
    B9 --> B10
    B10 --> B11[Perception Sensors and Actuators]
    B10 --> B12[Network and Edge]
    B10 --> B13[Cloud and Application]
    B11 --> B14[Continuous Audit and Monitoring]
    B12 --> B14
    B13 --> B14
    B14 --> B6
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The bottom-up enforcement flow (B11 → B12 → B13 → B14) and the top-down governance flow (B6 → B7/B8/B9) converge at the audit layer, which is the *single source of truth* for compliance evidence.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Data Flow in CPS with Embedded Privacy Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[User and Physical Environment] --> C2[CPS Sensors and IoT Devices]
    C2 --> C3{On Device Privacy Filter}
    C3 -- PII Detected --> C4[Local Anonymization and Hashing]
    C3 -- Sensitive Raw Data --> C5[Secure Element Encryption]
    C4 --> C6[Edge Gateway Aggregation]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7{Policy Decision Point PDP}
    C7 -- Consent Valid --> C8[TLS 1.3 Encrypted Channel]
    C7 -- Consent Revoked --> C9[Data Discarded with Audit Log]
    C8 --> C10[Cloud Storage AES 256 at Rest]
    C10 --> C11[Access Control RBAC ABAC]
    C11 --> C12[Authorized Applications and Users]
    C12 --> C13[Continuous Differential Privacy Analytics]
    C13 --> C14[Compliance Dashboard and Reporting]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** at C7 — this is the *technical embodiment* of the privacy policy. The PDP consults the consent database in real time and either routes, encrypts, or discards data. This is a frequent KTU board question: *"How is a privacy policy technically enforced in a CPS?"*

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Privacy-by-Design (PbD) Foundational Principles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Privacy by Design 7 Foundational Principles] --> D2[1 Proactive not Reactive]
    D1 --> D3[2 Privacy as the Default]
    D1 --> D4[3 Privacy Embedded into Design]
    D1 --> D5[4 Full Functionality Positive Sum]
    D1 --> D6[5 End to End Security]
    D1 --> D7[6 Visibility and Transparency]
    D1 --> D8[7 Respect for User Privacy]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Ann Cavoukian's **7 Principles of Privacy by Design** form the philosophical backbone of every CPS privacy policy. The board examiner frequently asks students to list and explain these principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a privacy policy in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any four essential components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A privacy policy in CPS is a formal, binding document specifying the principles, rules, and technical/organizational controls governing the collection, processing, storage, and sharing of personal and telemetry data across the sensor–edge–cloud continuum of a Cyber-Physical System.

**Four essential components** (1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for components):

1. **Data classification scheme** — categorizing data as Public, Internal, Confidential, or Restricted.
2. **Consent and lawful basis clause** — specifying the GDPR-style six legal bases and the consent mechanism.
3. **Purpose limitation clause** — restricting data use to explicitly stated purposes.
4. **Security controls mapping** — linking policy rules to encryption, access control, and audit mechanisms.
5. **Accountability clause** — defining DPO, Data Controller, and Data Processor roles.

*(Any 4 of the above)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)? Why is it especially critical for CPS deployments?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **DPIA** (1 mark) is a systematic, risk-based process used to identify, assess, and mitigate the privacy risks of a data-processing activity *before* it is launched. It is mandated under **GDPR Article 35** for processing that is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.

**Why critical for CPS** (2 marks):
- CPS often involves **continuous, invisible data collection** (e.g., smart meters, wearables), making pre-deployment assessment essential.
- CPS data can trigger **physical-world harm** (e.g., re-identification of a domestic-abuse victim through smart-meter usage patterns).
- DPIA forces the engineering team to consider **inference risks** unique to CPS (e.g., aggregating motion-sensor + microphone data to reconstruct private conversations).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a) Discuss in detail the step-by-step methodology for developing a privacy policy for a Cyber-Physical System.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The methodology follows the **10-stage Privacy Policy Development Lifecycle** (refer to Diagram 1 in Section 4):

**Stage 1 — Identify Scope and Stakeholders (1 mark):**
Define the CPS boundary (which sensors, actuators, networks, clouds are in scope). Identify stakeholders: data subjects (users), Data Controller, Data Processor, DPO, regulators, third-party integrators.

**Stage 2 — Data Inventory and Mapping (1 mark):**
Catalogue all data flows: what is collected, where it is stored, who has access, how long it is retained. Use tools like data flow diagrams (DFDs) and the **Record of Processing Activities (ROPA)**.

**Stage 3 — Threat and Risk Assessment (1 mark):**
Identify privacy threats (re-identification, inference, unauthorized actuation, secondary use). Compute the **Inherent Risk Score** $IRS = L \times I$ for each threat.

**Stage 4 — Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) (1 mark):**
For high-risk processing, conduct a formal DPIA. Document necessity, proportionality, risks to data subjects, and mitigations.

**Stage 5 — Policy Drafting and Legal Review (1 mark):**
Draft the policy using a structured template covering the five pillars (classification, consent, purpose, security, accountability). Have legal counsel review for GDPR/CCPA/HIPAA compliance.

**Stage 6 — Approval by Data Controller and DPO (½ mark):**
Formal sign-off by the Data Controller and the Data Protection Officer.

**Stage 7 — Implementation and Engineering Controls (1 mark):**
Translate policy into technical controls — encryption (AES-256/TLS 1.3), RBAC, audit logging, anonymization, on-device privacy filters (as per Diagram 3).

**Stage 8 — Training and Awareness (½ mark):**
Train engineers, operators, and end-users on policy provisions.

**Stage 9 — Monitoring, Audit, and Review (½ mark):**
Continuous monitoring via SIEM tools, periodic internal audits, and external SOC 2 / ISO 27701 audits.

**Stage 10 — Continuous Improvement (½ mark):**
Update the policy in response to new threats, regulatory changes, or post-incident lessons learned. The lifecycle is **cyclical**.

---

**(b) Explain the major legal and regulatory compliance frameworks applicable to CPS privacy, highlighting their key requirements.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Framework | Key Requirement | CPS Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **GDPR (EU)** (2 marks) | Lawful basis, DPIA for high-risk processing, 72-hr breach notification, data subject rights (access, erasure, portability) | Any CPS deployed in/with EU users must appoint a DPO and implement privacy-by-design |
| **CCPA/CPRA (California)** (1 mark) | Right to know, delete, correct, and opt-out of sale; sensitive personal information (SPI) handling | Connected products sold in California must expose "Do Not Sell" toggles |
| **HIPAA (USA Healthcare)** (1 mark) | Protected Health Information (PHI) safeguards, minimum-necessary rule, Business Associate Agreements | Medical CPS (e.g., insulin pumps, remote monitors) must enforce PHI controls |
| **India DPDP Act 2023** (1 mark) | Consent manager, data principal rights, significant data fiduciary obligations | Indian CPS deployments must register consent managers and honor withdrawal |
| **NIST Privacy Framework** (1 mark) | Identify–Govern–Control–Communicate–Protect functions; integrates with NIST CSF | Provides a universal risk-based blueprint for CPS privacy programs |
| **ISO/IEC 27701** (½ mark) | Extends ISO 27001 with privacy-specific controls (PIMS) | Certifiable framework for global CPS vendors |
| **IEEE 7000 Series** (½ mark) | Ethical design, transparency, accountability in autonomous systems | Applies to autonomous vehicles, drones, robotic CPS |

The CPS organization typically adopts a **layered compliance strategy** — selecting the strictest applicable framework as the baseline and reconciling conflicting requirements through a unified governance structure (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]*

**(a) Describe the NIST Privacy Framework and its application to CPS environments.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **NIST Privacy Framework (PF) v1.0** is a voluntary, risk-based toolkit released in 2020 to help organizations identify and manage privacy risks. It is structured around **five core functions**, each with categories and subcategories:

**1. Identify-P (1 mark):**
Inventory of data, systems, and risks. In CPS: catalogue all sensors, actuators, edge devices, data flows, third-party processors, and contractual obligations.

**2. Govern-P (1.5 marks):**
Establish the governance structure — policies, roles (DPO, Data Controller), and risk-tolerance criteria. In CPS: define CPS-specific governance for OTA updates, firmware signing, and incident response.

**3. Control-P (1.5 marks):**
Implement data-management policies, technical controls (encryption, anonymization, access control), and monitoring. In CPS: deploy on-device privacy filters, edge anonymization, and real-time policy decision points (PDPs).

**4. Communicate-P (1 mark):**
Transparency to data subjects through privacy notices, consent mechanisms, and breach disclosures. In CPS: provide in-app privacy dashboards for smart devices, multilingual consent flows.

**5. Protect-P (1 mark):**
Data security, identity management, and protective technology. In CPS: secure boot, hardware root-of-trust, secure elements for cryptographic key storage.

**Application to CPS** (1 mark): The framework is **technology-agnostic** and can be overlaid on the three CPS layers (perception, network, application). It integrates seamlessly with the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** — making it ideal for organizations already using CSF for CPS security. The output is a **Target Profile** that specifies the desired privacy posture, benchmarked against the **Current Profile** to produce a **Gap Analysis** and remediation roadmap.

---

**(b) Analyze the challenges in implementing privacy policies in CPS and propose mitigation strategies.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Challenge 1: Continuous, ambient data collection (1 mark):**
CPS sensors collect data 24/7, often invisibly. *Mitigation:* Deploy **on-device privacy filters** that suppress or coarsen data at the perception layer (e.g., face-blurring on smart cameras).

**Challenge 2: Inference and re-identification risk (1 mark):**
Combining innocuous data streams (motion + sound) can re-identify individuals. *Mitigation:* Apply **differential privacy** with $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ and **k-anonymity** with $k \geq 5$ at the edge.

**Challenge 3: Consent management complexity (1 mark):**
Granular, revocable consent across thousands of devices is technically hard. *Mitigation:* Implement a centralized **Consent Management Platform (CMP)** linked to a real-time **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** (refer to Diagram 3 in Section 4).

**Challenge 4: Cross-jurisdictional compliance (1 mark):**
A single CPS may be subject to GDPR + CCPA + DPDP simultaneously. *Mitigation:* Adopt the **strictest baseline** (usually GDPR) and design a **unified control matrix** (refer to Analytical Matrix in Section 3.3).

**Challenge 5: Resource-constrained devices (1 mark):**
Sensors and embedded MCUs cannot run heavy cryptographic stacks. *Mitigation:* Use **lightweight cryptography** (e.g., ASCON, NIST Lightweight finalists) and offload heavy operations to secure edge gateways.

**Challenge 6: Long device lifecycles and patching (1 mark):**
CPS devices may be deployed for 10–20 years, outlasting regulatory frameworks. *Mitigation:* Build **policy-updateable firmware** with signed OTA channels, and contractually mandate security/policy support windows.

**Challenge 7: Lack of skilled privacy engineers (1 mark):**
*Mitigation:* Cross-train cybersecurity engineers in privacy engineering; adopt **Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)** toolkits; engage external auditors for SOC 2 / ISO 27701 certification.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CPS Privacy Policy Answers:**
> 1. **Treating privacy as a "legal-only" topic** — Examiners *expect* engineering controls (encryption, RBAC, anonymization) explicitly mapped to CPS layers. Pure legal answers lose 30–40% of marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "P" in DPIA** — DPIA must be conducted *before* deployment, not after a breach. Students who reverse the order are penalized.
> 3. **Confusing Data Controller vs. Data Processor** — The Controller determines the purpose and means of processing; the Processor acts on the Controller's instructions. This is a frequently tested distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring CPS-specific risks** — Answers that read like generic IT privacy answers (e.g., focusing only on database encryption) miss the *physical-actuation* and *inference* dimensions unique to CPS.
> 5. **No numerical example** — Wherever a formula is asked, students who skip the arithmetic substitution lose 2–3 marks. Always show the substituted values and the final answer.
> 6. **Mishandling $\varepsilon$ in differential privacy** — Lower $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy. Reversing this is an instant 0 for the sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Privacy Policy** = formal rules for *what*, *why*, *how*, *who*, and *how long* data is processed in CPS.
- ✅ **CPS** integrates sensing, computation, communication, and actuation — privacy must address all four.
- ✅ **Five Pillars**: Classification, Consent, Purpose, Security, Accountability.
- ✅ **PbD = Privacy by Design** (Cavoukian's 7 principles) — embed privacy *into* the system, not bolt on.
- ✅ **DPIA** is mandatory under GDPR Article 35 for high-risk processing.
- ✅ **GDPR breach notification SLA** = **72 hours**.
- ✅ **Differential privacy** budget: lower $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy; $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$ is the standard "strong" threshold.
- ✅ **k-anonymity** re-identification risk: $R_{id} = 1/k$.
- ✅ **IRS = L × I** (Inherent Risk Score, range 1–25).
- ✅ **Residual Risk = IRS × (1 − $C_{eff}$)**.
- ✅ **NIST PF** has 5 functions: **Identify-P, Govern-P, Control-P, Communicate-P, Protect-P**.
- ✅ **Three CPS layers** for policy enforcement: Perception, Network, Application.
- ✅ **Data Controller ≠ Data Processor** — know the distinction.
- ✅ **Cross-jurisdictional** deployments → adopt the strictest baseline (usually GDPR).
- ✅ **Lightweight cryptography** (e.g., ASCON) is needed for resource-constrained CPS endpoints.
- ✅ **Continuous monitoring + cyclical policy updates** — privacy compliance is *never* a one-time activity.
- ✅ Always end a policy answer with a **layered enforcement strategy** — examiners reward engineering depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliance with Privacy Regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compliance with Privacy Regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Privacy Compliance** in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) is the continuous, auditable process of ensuring that the collection, processing, storage, transmission, and deletion of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other sensitive data generated or handled by interconnected physical and computational components adheres to a defined set of legal, regulatory, and organizational obligations.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *Privacy in CPS*, compliance specifically addresses how regulations such as the **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)** (European Union, enforced from **25 May 2018**) and the **California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)** (California, USA, enforced from **1 January 2020**) shape the architecture, governance, and operational lifecycle of CPS — including IoT sensor networks, smart grids, autonomous vehicles, industrial control systems, and wearable medical devices.

The two principal pillars are:

1. **GDPR (EU) 2016/679** — A comprehensive, extraterritorial regulation governing the processing of personal data of individuals within the European Economic Area (EEA). It is built upon **lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity & confidentiality, and accountability**.

2. **CCPA / CPRA (California Civil Code § 1798.100 et seq.)** — A US state-level statute granting California residents rights over their personal information, with the **California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA)** amendments taking effect on **1 January 2023** introducing the concept of *Sensitive Personal Information (SPI)*.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPS-Specific Context**: Unlike traditional IT systems, CPS generate a continuous stream of sensor data (location, biometrics, environmental readings, telemetry) that often constitutes *personal data* under GDPR/CCPA. The physical actuation component of CPS makes privacy breaches potentially **safety-critical**, elevating compliance from a legal checkbox to an engineering design constraint.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Smart Home as a Regulated Café"**
>
> Imagine your CPS (a smart home) is a trendy café where every visitor (data subject) is being observed by cameras, microphones, and smart sensors (CPS components). Without rules, the café owner can record, share, or sell anything they overhear. Privacy regulations like **GDPR** and **CCPA** act as the *city's hospitality code*:
>
> - **GDPR** is like the **strict European café code**: A clearly posted menu of what is being recorded, an explicit "may I take your order?" (consent) before serving data, the right to ask the waiter to forget your visit (right to erasure), and heavy fines if the café secretly sells your taste profile to advertisers.
>
> - **CCPA** is like the **California café code**: A little looser on upfront consent, but every customer can walk up to the counter and demand "Tell me what you have on me" (right to know), "Stop selling my data" (right to opt-out of sale), and "Delete my records" (right to delete).
>
> The *CPS engineer* is the café's **architect** — they must design the building (system architecture), train the staff (algorithms/ML models), and maintain the guestbook (data inventory) so that the café *automatically* complies with whichever code applies to its location and customer base.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Standards

The following terms, standardized under **GDPR Article 4** and **CCPA § 1798.140**, are foundational:

| Term | GDPR Equivalent | CCPA Equivalent | CPS Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Subject** | Natural person whose data is processed | Consumer (California resident) | The end-user whose sensor data is captured |
| **Data Controller** | Determines purpose & means of processing | Business | CPS manufacturer / system integrator |
| **Data Processor** | Processes data on controller's behalf | Service Provider | Cloud analytics backend, edge AI processor |
| **Personal Data** | Any information relating to an identified person | Personal Information (PI) | GPS traces, heart rate, voice recordings |
| **Consent** | Freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous | Opt-in for minors, opt-out for sale | Required before activating biometric sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight**: KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT743) explicitly requires students to map **CPS data flows** to the obligations of these regulations. The default answer should always invoke both **GDPR** (the global gold-standard) and **CCPA** (the dominant US benchmark), then discuss their differences in enforcement scope, extraterritoriality, and penalty structure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Compliance Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Foundational Principles of GDPR (Article 5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These principles are the **root obligations** of any GDPR-compliant CPS. Every architectural decision, from sensor selection to cloud storage, must be evaluated against them.

1. **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency** — Data must be processed legally, fairly, and with transparent communication to the data subject. *CPS Engineering Hook*: Privacy notice must be embedded in the device's first-boot wizard.

2. **Purpose Limitation** — Data may only be collected for *specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes*. *CPS Engineering Hook*: A wearable ECG monitor may collect cardiac data for arrhythmia detection, but not for insurance risk profiling without separate consent.

3. **Data Minimization** — Data collected must be *adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary*. *CPS Engineering Hook*: A smart thermostat should not capture room audio "just in case."

4. **Accuracy** — Personal data must be *accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date*. *CPS Engineering Hook*: Periodic re-calibration of biometric sensors; rectification APIs for user-corrected data.

5. **Storage Limitation** — Data must be kept *no longer than is necessary* for the purposes for which it is processed. *CPS Engineering Hook*: Automatic deletion of raw sensor logs after feature extraction; retention policies in the edge gateway.

6. **Integrity and Confidentiality** — Data must be processed in a manner that ensures *appropriate security*. *CPS Engineering Hook*: AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, secure boot, hardware root-of-trust.

7. **Accountability** — The controller is responsible for, and must be able to demonstrate, compliance. *CPS Engineering Hook*: Immutable audit logs, Records of Processing Activities (RoPA), Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA).

---

### 2.2 CCPA's Five Core Consumer Rights

Unlike GDPR's *principle-based* architecture, CCPA is *right-based* (more like a consumer protection statute):

1. **Right to Know** (§ 1798.110, § 1798.115) — Consumers may request disclosure of categories and specific pieces of PI collected, sources, business purposes, and third parties with whom data is shared.

2. **Right to Delete** (§ 1798.105) — Consumers may request deletion of their PI, subject to statutory exceptions (e.g., completing a transaction, security incidents, legal obligations).

3. **Right to Opt-Out of Sale or Sharing** (§ 1798.120, § 1798.135) — Businesses must provide a "Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information" link. *CPRAspecific*: Right to limit use of *Sensitive Personal Information*.

4. **Right to Correct** (CPRA, § 1798.106) — Consumers may request correction of inaccurate PI.

5. **Right to Non-Discrimination** (§ 1798.125) — Businesses cannot deny service, charge different prices, or provide a different quality of service for exercising rights.

---

### 2.3 The Six Lawful Bases for Processing (GDPR Article 6)

A CPS engineer must identify at least one of the following before processing personal data:

| # | Lawful Basis | CPS Use Case Example |
|---|---|---|
| **a** | **Consent** | User explicitly opts in to fitness tracker cloud sync |
| **b** | **Contract** | Processing needed to deliver the smart-lock service the user purchased |
| **c** | **Legal Obligation** | Telematics black-box data required by transport regulators |
| **d** | **Vital Interests** | Fall-detection alert for elderly living alone |
| **e** | **Public Task** | Smart city traffic monitoring for municipal planning |
| **f** | **Legitimate Interests** | Anonymized industrial IoT analytics for predictive maintenance (requires LIA test) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction**: GDPR's **Consent** is *opt-in* and freely revocable. CCPA's default for *sale/sharing* is often interpreted as **opt-out** (the business can collect by default but must honor a "Do Not Sell" signal). This asymmetry is a frequent KTU exam question.

---

### 2.4 KTU Compliance Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important revision artifact** for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions on privacy compliance. Memorize the penalty figures and territorial reach.

| Parameter | **GDPR (EU 2016/679)** | **CCPA / CPRA (California)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Year Enforced** | 25 May 2018 | 1 Jan 2020 (CCPA) / 1 Jan 2023 (CPRA) |
| **Territorial Scope** | Extraterritorial — applies to any org processing data of EEA residents | Applies to businesses that operate in California & meet thresholds (≥$25M revenue, OR ≥100K consumers' data, OR 50%+ revenue from selling PI) |
| **Maximum Penalty** | **€20 million** or **4% of annual global turnover**, whichever is higher | **$2,500 per violation**; **$7,500 per intentional violation** or per violation involving a minor |
| **Consent Model** | Opt-in, explicit, freely revocable | Opt-out (Do Not Sell), with opt-in only for minors under 16 |
| **Data Protection Officer** | Mandatory for large-scale monitoring or sensitive data | Not required, but Privacy Officer required under CPRA |
| **Right to Data Portability** | Yes (Article 20) | No direct equivalent (right to know partially overlaps) |
| **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** | Mandatory for high-risk processing (Article 35) | Risk assessments required for significant risk processing (CPRA § 1798.160) |
| **Sensitive Data Handling** | Special category data (Art. 9) — explicit consent/exemption | Sensitive Personal Information (SPI) under CPRA — right to limit use |
| **Cross-border Transfer Mechanism** | Adequacy decision, SCCs, BCRs, derogations | No specific mechanism; CPRA allows risk-based assessment |
| **Breach Notification** | 72 hours to supervisory authority (Art. 33) | "Most expedient time" — no fixed hours; to AG if >500 residents |
| **Definition of "Personal Data"** | Broad: any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person | PI: broadly similar but historically narrower; SPI added by CPRA |
| **Enforcement Authority** | National Supervisory Authorities (e.g., CNIL in France, Garante in Italy) | California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) + California Attorney General |

> **Penalty Reference Numbers (Memorize!)**:
> - **GDPR maximum**: €20,000,000 or 4% of global turnover.
> - **CCPA maximum**: $7,500 per intentional violation.

---

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering (Real-World Utility)

- **Risk-Based Engineering**: GDPR Article 25 (*Privacy by Design*) and Article 32 mandate that engineers embed compliance into the *System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)*. This shifts privacy from a legal review at deployment to a **design-time constraint**.

- **Liability Allocation**: In CPS supply chains (e.g., sensor vendor → gateway → cloud → analytics), **Article 28 of GDPR** requires a binding *Data Processing Agreement (DPA)* between controllers and processors, clarifying engineering responsibilities for encryption, pseudonymization, and breach response.

- **Cross-Border CPS Deployments**: A CPS product designed in India, manufactured in China, and sold in Germany must simultaneously satisfy GDPR (deployment jurisdiction), Indian DPDP Act 2023, and possibly CCPA (if any California user uses it). The **extraterritoriality** of GDPR means even non-EU companies are bound.

- **Pseudonymization vs. Anonymization**: GDPR applies to *pseudonymized* data (reversible with a key); fully *anonymized* data (irreversible) falls outside the regulation. Engineers must therefore design CPS pipelines that either **irreversibly anonymize at the edge** or **securely manage re-identification keys**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Achieving Compliance in a CPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a **structured 7-step methodology** that a CPS engineering team would follow to move from a non-compliant prototype to a GDPR/CCPA-compliant product. This is also the **canonical answer skeleton** for KTU 14-mark questions on this topic.

---

### **Step 1 — Data Inventory & Mapping (RoPA)**

Identify *every* data element flowing through the CPS, classify it, and record it in a **Record of Processing Activities (RoPA)** as mandated by GDPR Article 30.

$$
\text{RoPA} = \{ \text{Data Element},\ \text{Source Sensor},\ \text{Purpose},\ \text{Lawful Basis},\ \text{Recipient},\ \text{Retention} \}
$$

> *Example*: A smart-meter CPS records: `{kWh_consumption, household_id, timestamp, GPS_lat, GPS_lon}`. The GPS coordinates could *indirectly identify* a household → they are **personal data** under GDPR.

---

### **Step 2 — Data Classification & Sensitivity Tiering**

Categorize each data element into one of four tiers:

$$
\text{Tier}(d) = \begin{cases} \text{Tier-0: Non-personal} & \text{if } d \text{ is fully anonymized} \\ \text{Tier-1: PII} & \text{if } d \text{ directly identifies a person} \\ \text{Tier-2: Sensitive PII} & \text{if } d \text{ is biometric, health, location, etc.} \\ \text{Tier-3: Special Category (GDPR Art. 9)} & \text{if } d \text{ is racial, political, genetic, etc.} \end{cases}
$$

> *CPS Hook*: A wearable heart-rate monitor captures `ECG_signal` (Tier-2/3), which is **Sensitive Personal Information** under both CPRA and GDPR.

---

### **Step 3 — Lawful Basis Assignment**

For each Tier-1+ data element, select one of the six Article 6 bases. For Tier-3 elements, additionally satisfy an Article 9 condition (e.g., *explicit consent* or *vital interests*).

$$
\forall\, d \in \text{Tier-1} \cup \text{Tier-2}: \exists\, b \in \{a,b,c,d,e,f\} \text{ (Article 6)}
$$

$$
\forall\, d \in \text{Tier-3}: \exists\, c \in \{\text{Art. 9 conditions}\} \text{ AND } \exists\, b \in \{a,b,c,d,e,f\}
$$

---

### **Step 4 — Privacy by Design (PbD) Implementation (GDPR Art. 25)**

Apply the **7 Foundational Principles of PbD (Ann Cavoukian)** to the CPS architecture:

1. Proactive not Reactive; Preventative not Remedial
2. Privacy as the Default Setting
3. Privacy Embedded into Design
4. Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum
5. End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection
6. Visibility and Transparency
7. Respect for User Privacy — Keep it User-Centric

> *CPS Implementation*:
> - **Default Setting**: Telematics device ships with location-sharing *off*.
> - **Embedded into Design**: Edge-AI model runs locally; only aggregated metrics leave the device.
> - **End-to-End Security**: Hardware root-of-trust → encrypted TLS → AES-256 at rest → secure key management.

---

### **Step 5 — Data Subject Rights Engineering (DSR Pipeline)**

For GDPR (rights under Articles 15–22) and CCPA (5 rights listed in §2.2), engineer the following modules:

$$
\text{DSR Pipeline} = \text{Intake} \rightarrow \text{Identity Verification} \rightarrow \text{Discovery} \rightarrow \text{Action} \rightarrow \text{Response}
$$

| Right | GDPR Article | CCPA Section | CPS Technical Implementation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access | Art. 15 | § 1798.110 | API endpoint `/subject/{id}/data` |
| Rectification | Art. 16 | § 1798.106 | API endpoint `/subject/{id}/correct` |
| Erasure | Art. 17 | § 1798.105 | Cascading delete across DB, backups (within SLA), edge caches |
| Portability | Art. 20 | (Partial) | Export to JSON/CSV at `/subject/{id}/export` |
| Restrict/Object | Art. 18, 21 | Limit use of SPI (CPRA) | Toggle flag in consent manager |
| Opt-out of Sale | (N/A — consent) | § 1798.120 | Honor Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal |

**SLA Constraints**:
- GDPR: 1 calendar month (extendable to 3) for response.
- CCPA: **45 days** from verified request receipt.

---

### **Step 6 — Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) — GDPR Art. 35**

A DPIA is mandatory when processing is *likely to result in a high risk* to the rights and freedoms of natural persons — common in CPS scenarios involving:

- Systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects (e.g., AI-driven behavior profiling).
- Processing of sensitive data on a large scale (e.g., biometric access control).
- Systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale (e.g., smart city surveillance).

The DPIA process is:

$$
\text{DPIA} = \text{Describe Processing} \rightarrow \text{Assess Necessity \& Proportionality} \rightarrow \text{Identify \& Assess Risks} \rightarrow \text{Mitigate Risks} \rightarrow \text{Document \& Review}
$$

---

### **Step 7 — Ongoing Accountability & Breach Response**

- **Audit Logging**: Immutable, time-stamped logs of all data access events.
- **Breach Notification (GDPR Art. 33)**: Notify supervisory authority within **72 hours** of awareness; notify affected data subjects if risk is high.
- **CCPA Breach**: Notify California AG if ≥500 residents are affected (CPRA § 1798.82).
- **Periodic Review**: Annual or upon significant change of processing.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example: Smart Wearable Compliance Walkthrough

**Scenario**: A wearable fitness tracker captures:
- Step count (Tier-0 or Tier-1? — debatable; aggregate is non-personal, individual pattern can re-identify)
- Heart rate (Tier-2)
- GPS route (Tier-2)
- Sleep cycles (Tier-2)
- Voice recordings for the virtual assistant (Tier-2)

**Walkthrough**:

| Step | Output |
|---|---|
| 1. Data Inventory | Catalog: `hr_bpm, gps_polyline, sleep_stage_array, audio_chunk, step_count` |
| 2. Classification | Heart rate, GPS, sleep, audio = **Tier-2/3**; step count = **Tier-1 (context-dependent)** |
| 3. Lawful Basis | Health monitoring = **(a) Consent** OR **(d) Vital Interests** (if used for medical alerts) |
| 4. PbD | Edge-AI for heart anomaly detection; only alerts leave device; GPS coarsened to 100m grid before transmission |
| 5. DSR | In-app "Download My Data," "Delete My Account," "Stop Sharing" buttons |
| 6. DPIA | Required (sensitive data, large scale, profiling) |
| 7. Accountability | 72-hour breach notification clause in vendor contracts; quarterly DPO audits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **Mermaid diagrams** map the abstract regulatory text to concrete CPS engineering components. These are KTU board-exam-friendly schematics that an examiner would award full marks for in a 14-mark answer.

---

### 4.1 Diagram A — GDPR / CCPA Compliance Workflow in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Interaction with CPS Device] --> B{Data Sensitivity Tier?}
    B -- Tier 0: Non-Personal --> C[Process Freely]
    B -- Tier 1: PII --> D[Apply Lawful Basis Check]
    B -- Tier 2: Sensitive PII --> E[Apply Lawful Basis AND Special Category Check]
    B -- Tier 3: Special Category --> F[Require Explicit Consent OR Vital Interest]
    D --> G{Valid Lawful Basis?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- No --> H[STOP: Do Not Process]
    G -- Yes --> I[Execute Privacy by Design Controls]
    I --> J[Process at Edge or Encrypted Cloud]
    J --> K[Maintain Audit Log Entry]
    K --> L[Honor Data Subject Rights on Request]
    L --> M[Periodic Compliance Review]
    M --> N[End of Processing or Deletion]
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram B — Cross-Regulation Compliance Decision Tree for CPS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[CPS Processes Personal Data] --> Q1{Is the user in the EEA?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> GDPR[Apply GDPR 2016/679]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the user a California resident and business meets CCPA thresholds?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CCPA[Apply CCPA plus CPRA]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Are there other local laws? e.g., India DPDP, Brazil LGPD, China PIPL}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Multi[Apply Stricter of Applicable Laws]
    Q3 -- No --> Org[Apply Organization Internal Privacy Policy]
    GDPR --> DPIA{DPIA Required?}
    CCPA --> SPI{Sensitive Personal Information Involved?}
    Multi --> DPIA
    DPIA -- Yes --> Conduct[Conduct DPIA and Mitigate]
    DPIA -- No --> Conduct
    SPI -- Yes --> Limit[Implement Right to Limit Use]
    SPI -- No --> Conduct
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Compliant CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EdgeLayer[Edge Layer: CPS Device]
        Sensor[Physical Sensors: Camera, GPS, Biometric]
        EdgeAI[Edge AI Inference Engine]
        LocalStore[Tier 0 Encrypted Local Store]
        UserUI[User Interface: Consent Manager and Privacy Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph GatewayLayer[Gateway Layer]
        Anonymizer[Anonymization and Pseudonymization Module]
        Encrypt[TLS 1.3 Encryption Module]
    end

    subgraph CloudLayer[Cloud Layer: Controller and Processor]
        DSRPortal[Data Subject Rights Portal: Access, Delete, Export]
        ConsentDB[Consent Management Database]
        AuditLog[Immutable Audit Log]
        Retention[Automated Retention and Deletion Engine]
    end

    subgraph ComplianceLayer[Compliance and Governance Layer]
        DPO[Data Protection Officer Dashboard]
        DPIAModule[DPIA and Risk Assessment Engine]
        BreachAlert[Breach Notification Module: 72 hour GDPR, 45 day CCPA]
    end

    Sensor --> EdgeAI
    EdgeAI --> UserUI
    UserUI --> ConsentDB
    EdgeAI --> Anonymizer
    Anonymizer --> Encrypt
    Encrypt --> DSRPortal
    DSRPortal --> AuditLog
    AuditLog --> Retention
    ConsentDB --> DPIAModule
    DPO --> DPIAModule
    DPIAModule --> BreachAlert
    UserUI -. Right Requests .-> DSRPortal
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram D — Data Subject Rights (DSR) Response Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Data Subject (CPS User)
    participant I as Identity Verification Service
    participant D as Data Discovery Engine
    participant S as Storage Systems
    participant R as Response Generator
    U->>I: Submit Request (Access or Delete or Export or Correct)
    I-->>U: Authentication Challenge
    U->>I: Provide Credentials
    I->>D: Verified Request Forwarded
    D->>S: Query All Linked Data Stores
    S-->>D: Return Data Records
    D->>R: Compile Response or Execute Deletion
    R-->>U: Deliver Export or Confirmation of Deletion
    Note over U,R: GDPR SLA: 1 month, CCPA SLA: 45 days
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Personal Data" as per GDPR. How does its scope differ from the term "Personal Information" used in CCPA?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Under **GDPR Article 4(1)**, *personal data* means any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person (*data subject*); an identifiable person is one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, identification number, location data, online identifier, or to one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural, or social identity of that person.

Under **CCPA (§ 1798.140, amended by CPRA)**, *personal information* is information that identifies, relates to, describes, is reasonably capable of being associated with, or could reasonably be linked, directly or indirectly, with a particular consumer or household.

**Key Difference** (1 mark): GDPR explicitly mentions *"location data"* and *"online identifiers"* as part of the definition, making its scope *broader* for CPS contexts. CCPA historically distinguished between "consumers" (individuals) and "households," and CPRA further introduced the *Sensitive Personal Information (SPI)* subcategory absent in GDPR's basic definition (though GDPR has its own "special categories" under Article 9).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*List any THREE lawful bases for processing personal data under GDPR Article 6, with one CPS example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Consent (Art. 6(1)(a))** — A user explicitly agrees to share GPS data from a fitness tracker with a health-insurance wellness program.
2. **Contract (Art. 6(1)(b))** — A smart-lock manufacturer processes access logs to deliver the lock-unlocking service purchased by the user.
3. **Vital Interests (Art. 6(1)(d))** — A medical wearable automatically alerts emergency services when a cardiac arrest is detected, even without prior consent, to save the user's life.

*(Alternative accepted bases: Legal Obligation, Public Task, Legitimate Interests — any three with correct CPS examples earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the seven foundational principles of GDPR Article 5. For each principle, give one concrete example of how it is implemented in a Cyber-Physical System.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the architecture and steps involved in handling a **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR)** in a CPS environment, ensuring both GDPR and CCPA compliance.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3A(a) [7 marks]**

The seven principles of **GDPR Article 5(1)** are:

**1. Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: A smart speaker displays a clear, plain-language privacy notice during first-time setup, listing the categories of audio data collected and the third parties with whom it may be shared. No hidden microphone is enabled without user activation.

**2. Purpose Limitation** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: A smart meter collects electricity consumption for billing and grid-balancing. It does not repurpose the same data stream to infer occupant sleep patterns without a separate, explicit consent.

**3. Data Minimization** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: An industrial robotic arm uses a depth camera to detect human proximity, but the video feed is processed locally and discarded in real time — only a binary "human-near" flag is transmitted to the safety controller.

**4. Accuracy** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: A wearable ECG device provides a user-facing interface to manually flag incorrect arrhythmia events; the cloud ML model is retrained with the corrected label.

**5. Storage Limitation** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: An autonomous vehicle's raw lidar point clouds are retained for 30 days for accident investigation, then automatically purged; only anonymized trajectory summaries are kept longer.

**6. Integrity and Confidentiality** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: Telematics data is encrypted using AES-256 at rest in the vehicle's secure enclave, and TLS 1.3 is used for cloud transmission. Secure boot prevents firmware tampering.

**7. Accountability** *(1 mark)*
- *CPS Example*: The manufacturer maintains an immutable, time-stamped audit log of every data access request, retains a Record of Processing Activities (RoPA), and publishes a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) on its website.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3A(b) [7 marks]**

A **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR)** in a CPS environment is handled through the following structured pipeline:

**Step 1 — Request Intake (1 mark)**
The user submits a request via a privacy portal embedded in the CPS companion app (e.g., "Download My Data" button). The request is routed to a **DSR Management Module** that tags it with a unique ticket ID.

**Step 2 — Identity Verification (1 mark)**
To prevent unauthorized disclosure, the user is challenged with multi-factor authentication (e.g., password + biometric + one-time code). Under CCPA, businesses may request identifying information reasonably necessary to verify the requester (CCPA § 1798.130(a)(2)).

**Step 3 — Discovery Across Distributed CPS Data Stores (2 marks)**
The data may be fragmented across:
- The CPS device's local storage (e.g., raw sensor logs on the wearable).
- The gateway/edge node (e.g., intermediate aggregations).
- The cloud backend (e.g., long-term analytics databases).
- Third-party processors (e.g., map-service providers who received location pings).

A **data-discovery crawler** queries all linked repositories using the user's verified identity token.

**Step 4 — Compilation and Export (1 mark)**
The retrieved data is compiled into a structured format (JSON or CSV) along with metadata explaining sources, processing purposes, and retention periods (satisfying GDPR Art. 15 "right of access" and CCPA § 1798.110 "right to know").

**Step 5 — Delivery and Logging (1 mark)**
The export is delivered via a secure, time-limited download link. Every action (request, verification, discovery, export, delivery) is appended to an immutable audit log for accountability.

**SLA Constraints**:
- **GDPR Article 12(3)**: 1 calendar month from receipt (extendable to 3 for complex requests).
- **CCPA § 1798.130**: 45 calendar days from verified receipt.

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Question — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Compare GDPR and CCPA across at least SIX compliance dimensions. Highlight the differences in territorial scope, maximum penalties, consent model, and rights granted to data subjects.

**(b) [7 marks]** What is a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** under GDPR Article 35? Explain its step-by-step methodology and justify when it is mandatory in a CPS context, with two examples.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3B(a) [7 marks]**

| Dimension | **GDPR** | **CCPA / CPRA** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Territorial Scope** *(1 mark)* | Extraterritorial — applies to any organization processing data of EEA residents, regardless of the org's location. | Limited to businesses operating in California that meet thresholds (≥$25M revenue, ≥100K consumers/households' PI, or 50%+ revenue from selling PI). |
| **2. Maximum Penalty** *(1 mark)* | Up to **€20 million** or **4% of annual global turnover**, whichever is higher. | Up to **$2,500 per violation**; **$7,500 per intentional violation** or per violation involving a minor. |
| **3. Consent Model** *(1 mark)* | **Opt-in** — processing requires one of six lawful bases; consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. | **Opt-out** of *sale or sharing*; explicit opt-in only for minors under 16. |
| **4. Data Subject Rights** *(1 mark)* | Rights to access, rectify, erase, restrict, port, object, and not be subject to automated decision-making (Art. 15–22). | Rights to know, delete, opt-out of sale, correct, and limit use of SPI; **no direct portability right**. |
| **5. Data Protection Officer** *(1 mark)* | Mandatory for core activities involving large-scale monitoring or special-category data. | Not required under CCPA; CPRA requires a **Privacy Officer** for businesses handling SPI. |
| **6. Cross-Border Data Transfer** *(1 mark)* | Governed by adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses, or Binding Corporate Rules. | No specific transfer mechanism; CPRA requires risk-based assessment for international transfers. |
| **7. Breach Notification** *(1 mark)* | Within **72 hours** to the supervisory authority (Art. 33). | "Most expedient time and no later than" the statutory deadline; to AG if ≥500 residents affected. |

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3B(b) [7 marks]**

**Definition (1 mark)**
A **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** is a process designed to *describe the processing, assess its necessity and proportionality, and help manage the risks to the rights and freedoms of natural persons* arising from personal data processing, as mandated by **GDPR Article 35**.

**When is it mandatory in CPS? (1 mark)**
DPIA is required when processing is *likely to result in a high risk* to data subjects — for instance:
- Systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects (including profiling) on which decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects are based.
- Processing on a large scale of special categories of data (Art. 9) or criminal-conviction data.
- Systematic monitoring of a publicly accessible area on a large scale.

**Step-by-Step Methodology (4 marks)**:

1. **Describe the Processing** — Nature, scope, context, and purposes of processing; categories of data; data flows; recipients.

2. **Assess Necessity and Proportionality** — Is the processing lawful? Is the data necessary for the purpose? Could a less-invasive approach achieve the same goal?

3. **Identify and Assess Risks** — For each risk (e.g., re-identification, unauthorized access, function creep), assess likelihood and severity.

4. **Identify Mitigations** — Encryption, pseudonymization, access controls, data minimization, retention limits.

5. **Document and Review** — Produce a written DPIA report; review when the processing context changes.

**CPS Examples (1 mark)**:
- **Smart City Surveillance**: Network of CCTV + AI-based crowd analytics — systematic monitoring of a public area on a large scale → DPIA mandatory.
- **Wearables for Health Insurance Risk Profiling**: Continuous biometric monitoring feeding into pricing algorithms — large-scale processing of special-category health data → DPIA mandatory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the maximum penalties.** Many students incorrectly quote GDPR's penalty as a flat 4% OR €20M and forget that it is *"whichever is HIGHER."* Marks are lost on this wording.
> 2. **GDPR breach notification is 72 hours to the AUTHORITY, not to the data subject.** Notification to the data subject is required only if the breach poses a *high risk* (Art. 34).
> 3. **CCPA does NOT require a DPO.** It requires a Privacy Officer (CPRA) — these are NOT the same role; a DPO has more proactive duties and is a GDPR-specific construct.
> 4. **"Personal Data" under GDPR is broader than "Personal Information" under CCPA** for CPS purposes because of explicit mention of location data and online identifiers.
> 5. **Lawful basis ≠ consent.** Students often answer "consent" for every scenario. Always evaluate the other 5 bases (Contract, Legal Obligation, Vital Interests, Public Task, Legitimate Interests) before defaulting to consent.
> 6. **Pseudonymization is NOT anonymization.** Pseudonymized data is still personal data under GDPR; only irreversible anonymization removes data from GDPR's scope.
> 7. **CCPA has NO direct right to data portability** — it is partially covered by the right to know. GDPR has a distinct, explicit portability right under Art. 20.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **GDPR** is the **EU's 2016/679 regulation**, enforced from **25 May 2018**, governing personal data processing of individuals in the **EEA**, with **extraterritorial reach** and a maximum penalty of **€20M or 4% of global turnover (whichever is higher)**.
- **CCPA** is the **California Civil Code §§ 1798.100–1798.199**, enforced from **1 Jan 2020**, with amendments from **CPRA** effective **1 Jan 2023**, applying to qualifying businesses operating in California, with penalties up to **$2,500 per violation ($7,500 for intentional / minor-related)**.
- **GDPR's 7 Principles**: Lawfulness/Fairness/Transparency, Purpose Limitation, Data Minimization, Accuracy, Storage Limitation, Integrity/Confidentiality, Accountability.
- **CCPA's 5 Rights**: Know, Delete, Opt-Out of Sale/Sharing, Correct (CPRA), Non-Discrimination.
- **GDPR's 6 Lawful Bases** (Art. 6): Consent, Contract, Legal Obligation, Vital Interests, Public Task, Legitimate Interests.
- **GDPR Breach Notification Window**: **72 hours** to the supervisory authority (Art. 33).
- **CCPA Response SLA**: **45 days** from verified consumer request.
- **DPIA** is mandatory for high-risk processing under GDPR Art. 35; CPRA requires risk assessments for "significant risk" processing (§ 1798.160).
- **CPS-Specific Risks**: Continuous sensor streams, biometric data, location traces, and physical-actuation safety implications all elevate privacy to a *safety-critical* engineering concern.
- **Privacy by Design (Art. 25)**: 7 Ann Cavoukian principles — proactive, default-private, embedded, full-functionality, end-to-end secure, transparent, user-centric.
- **Pseudonymization vs. Anonymization**: Only irreversible anonymization takes data outside GDPR's scope.
- **Data Subject Rights Pipeline (5 stages)**: Intake → Identity Verification → Discovery → Action → Response.
- **Cross-Border Transfers (GDPR)**: Adequacy decisions, Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs), or specific derogations (Art. 49).
- **CPRAsensitive Personal Information (SPI)**: Includes government IDs, precise geolocation, racial/ethnic origin, religious beliefs, union membership, mail/email/text contents, genetic data, biometric data, health info, sex life/sexual orientation — and grants the **right to limit use** of SPI.
- **Accountability (Art. 5(2))**: The controller bears the burden of demonstrating compliance — supported by RoPA, DPIA, audit logs, DPO oversight, and vendor DPAs (Art. 28).
- **Engineering Hotspots for CPS Compliance**: Edge-AI for local processing, hardware root-of-trust, on-device consent management, automated retention/deletion, encrypted firmware updates, and secure key management.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Monitoring and Enforcement of Privacy Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monitoring and Enforcement of Privacy Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Privacy Policy Monitoring** in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) refers to the continuous, systematic observation, collection, and analysis of system runtime behaviour, data flows, access patterns, and sensor-actuator interactions to verify adherence to a formally defined set of privacy rules and obligations. It is an *active, evidence-based surveillance* process that compares actual operational telemetry against an expected baseline policy specification.

**Privacy Policy Enforcement**, in contrast, is the proactive *interventional* mechanism that translates a privacy policy into concrete control actions (allow, deny, mask, anonymize, encrypt, perturb, or terminate) executed by the CPS middleware, access-control engines, and trusted execution environments. Where monitoring *detects* deviation, enforcement *prevents or corrects* it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction**: Monitoring is **diagnostic** (Observe → Detect → Report), while Enforcement is **corrective/prescriptive** (Decide → Intervene → Audit). The two operate in a closed feedback loop analogous to a control system: $M(t) \rightarrow E(t) \rightarrow M(t+1)$.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **high-security pharmaceutical laboratory**:
- **Privacy Policies** = the written lab rulebook: "Only certified researchers may access patient DNA data", "Samples must be de-identified before sharing with external collaborators", "Audit trails of all access must be retained for 7 years."
- **Monitoring** = the CCTV cameras, biometric entry logs, electronic access cards, and motion sensors — they *observe* what is happening and *flag* anomalies (e.g., an unauthorized person tailgating through a door).
- **Enforcement** = the electromagnetic locks, the software access-control lists (ACLs), the de-identification pipeline that strips PII (Personally Identifiable Information) before export, and the auto-shutdown that triggers when a rule is violated.

In a CPS — say, a **smart hospital ICU** — sensors (heart-rate, SpO₂, infusion pump logs) generate continuous streams of physiological data. The privacy policy says: *"ECG data leaving the bedside monitor must be anonymized for any non-clinical consumer."* The monitoring subsystem continuously inspects the egress packets, while the enforcement subsystem injects a re-identification token remover or refuses the transmission outright.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Bodies & Frameworks referenced in KTU syllabus**: **GDPR** (EU 2016/679), **HIPAA Privacy Rule** (45 CFR §164), **ISO/IEC 27701:2019**, **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 (Privacy Controls)**, and **IEEE 7002-2022** (Data Privacy Process Standard).

## 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

- **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** for privacy violations: typically measured in **seconds** for CPS, often targeted as **< 5 s** in safety-critical medical CPS.
- **Mean Time to Enforce/Remediate (MTTR)**: usually **< 30 s** for policy enforcement in industrial CPS.
- **Policy Coverage Ratio (PCR)**: the fraction of system actions governed by at least one policy rule. KTU benchmark: target **PCR ≥ 0.95**.
- **False Positive Rate (FPR)** of policy violation detection: critical for CPS — typically constrained to **FPR ≤ 0.02 (2%)** to prevent alarm fatigue.
- **Anonymity Set Size (k)** for $k$-anonymity: must satisfy **k ≥ 5** in most KTU-accepted healthcare scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Operational Stack

Privacy policy monitoring and enforcement in CPS is best understood as a layered control system:

**Layer 1 — Policy Specification (The "What")**
- Formal languages: **XACML 3.0** (eXtensible Access Control Markup Language), **EPAL** (Enterprise Privacy Authorization Language), **P3P** (Platform for Privacy Preferences), and the newer **ODRL** (Open Digital Rights Language) profile for CPS.
- A policy rule is typically a 4-tuple: $\langle Subject, Action, Object, Condition \rangle$ plus a *Duty* (post-action obligation such as "log the access").

**Layer 2 — Instrumentation & Data Plane Telemetry (The "Observe")**
- Taps into CPS buses (CAN, LIN, Modbus, OPC-UA, MQTT, DDS).
- Hooks at the *sensor-to-fog*, *fog-to-cloud*, and *device-to-device* boundaries.

**Layer 3 — Compliance Evaluation Engine (The "Compare")**
- A reference monitor (Kim–Lampson–Schroeder model, 1973) that mediates every privileged operation.
- Uses either *syntactic* (pattern matching on data fields) or *semantic* (information-flow analysis, taint tracking) techniques.

**Layer 4 — Decision & Enforcement Actuator (The "Act")**
- Translates verdicts **PERMIT / DENY / OBLIGATION** into primitives: block, redact, perturb (differential privacy noise), k-anonymize, encrypt, or rate-limit.

**Layer 5 — Audit & Accountability Ledger (The "Prove")**
- Append-only log, often implemented as a **blockchain-anchored** or **TPM-sealed** tamper-evident log for non-repudiation.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why it exists | KTU-typical failure if omitted |
|---|---|---|
| Specification | Codifies legal text into machine-checkable rules | Ad-hoc, un-auditable privacy decisions |
| Telemetry | Provides ground truth on data movement | Cannot detect exfiltration |
| Evaluation | Removes human latency from decisions | Inconsistent enforcement |
| Enforcement | Closes the loop, makes policy binding | Policy is a "paper tiger" |
| Audit | Provides legal evidence (GDPR Art. 30) | No accountability, no remediation |

## 2.3 Enforcement Modes (Granular Spectrum)

1. **Static / Pre-execution enforcement** — Policy checked *before* the action (compile-time / request-time, like XACML PDP).
2. **Runtime / In-line enforcement** — Mediated by a *reference monitor* in the hot path.
3. **Post-hoc / Forensic enforcement** — Detected after the fact, only logs/penalizes (forensic auditing).
4. **Adaptive enforcement** — Policy decisions evolve based on context (e.g., risk-based access control).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept**: *Reference Monitor Concept* requires three properties — **tamperproofness**, **complete mediation** (every access checked), and **verifiability** (small enough to be formally validated). In CPS, the **TPM 2.0** or **TrustZone** provides tamperproofness; **SELinux** or **OP-TEE** provide complete mediation.

## 2.4 Monitoring Paradigms

- **Event-based monitoring**: triggered on data-plane events (publish, subscribe, write).
- **Periodic polling**: snapshots taken at fixed intervals (suitable for low-rate CPS like smart meters).
- **Streaming / Complex Event Processing (CEP)**: real-time pattern matching on data streams (e.g., Esper, Flink CEP).
- **Information Flow Tracking (IFT)**: also called *taint analysis*; tracks how a privacy-labelled datum propagates through the CPS pipeline.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Information Leakage (Mutual Information) | $I(X;O) = H(X) - H(X \mid O)$ where $X$ = private input, $O$ = observable output | Measured in **bits**; **0 bits** = perfect privacy |
| 2 | Differential Privacy (ε-DP) | $\Pr[M(D) \in S] \le e^{\epsilon} \cdot \Pr[M(D') \in S]$ for neighbouring $D, D'$ | $\epsilon$ is the *privacy budget*; smaller = stronger |
| 3 | $k$-Anonymity | Every equivalence class in released dataset has $\ge k$ records | $k \ge 5$ typical for KTU |
| 4 | $\ell$-Diversity | Each equivalence class has $\ge \ell$ "well-represented" sensitive values | Strengthens $k$-anonymity |
| 5 | $t$-Closeness | Earth Mover's Distance between class distribution and global distribution $\le t$ | Tightest of the three |
| 6 | Compliance Score | $C_{score} = \dfrac{\vert\text{Compliant Events}\vert}{\vert\text{Total Events}\vert}$ | Range $[0,1]$; 1 = fully compliant |
| 7 | Policy Coverage Ratio | $PCR = \dfrac{\vert\text{Governed Actions}\vert}{\vert\text{Total Actions}\vert}$ | Target $\ge 0.95$ |
| 8 | False Positive Rate (FPR) | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Constraint $\le 0.02$ in CPS |
| 9 | F1-Score of Detector | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ where $P$=precision, $R$=recall | Range $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | Entropy of Anonymity Set | $H(A) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Higher = stronger anonymity |
| 11 | Laplace Mechanism Noise | $M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$ | $\Delta f$ = sensitivity of $f$ |
| 12 | Kullback–Leibler Divergence | $D_{KL}(P \mid \vert Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \log \dfrac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ | Measures distribution shift in policy drift |
| 13 | MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) | $MTTD = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{detect,i} - t_{occur,i})$ | **seconds** in CPS |
| 14 | MTTR (Mean Time to Remediate) | $MTTR = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{remediate,i} - t_{detect,i})$ | **seconds** in CPS |
| 15 | Information Flow Confidentiality (Bell–LaPadula inspired) | No read-up, no write-down across privacy levels | Used in multilevel CPS |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Formatting Rule**: Note that all set-cardinality and absolute-value expressions are written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert X \vert$) and **never** the unescaped `|` pipe, which would otherwise break markdown table rendering.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid**: monitoring detects unusual load patterns that may infer individual household behaviour; enforcement uses **differential privacy** noise injection on smart-meter aggregates.
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X)**: enforcement of "location must be fuzzed within 50 m before broadcast" using $k$-anonymity over neighbouring vehicles.
- **Implantable Medical Devices (IMD)**: continuous monitoring of wireless transmissions; enforcement via proximity-based access tokens.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: enforcement of data-residency policies ("no telemetry leaves EU region") via geo-fenced PDPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Compliance Score Aggregation

**Problem:** A CPS processes 1,000 events. Of these, 920 comply with the privacy policy, 60 are direct violations, and 20 are "ambiguous" (require human review). Compute the *Hard Compliance Score*, the *Weighted Compliance Score* (violations weighted 1.0, ambiguous 0.5), and the *F1-Score* of the policy violation detector if its confusion matrix yields TP = 55, FP = 8, FN = 5, TN = 932.

**Step 1 — Define the Hard Compliance Score:**

$$
C_{hard} = \frac{\vert \text{Compliant Events} \vert}{\vert \text{Total Events} \vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (with explicit conversion logic):**

The "compliant events" are the events that did NOT violate the policy. The ambiguous events did not technically violate, so they are counted as compliant. Therefore, compliant events = 920 (unambiguous compliant) + 20 (ambiguous, treated as compliant) = 940. Total events = 1,000.

$$
C_{hard} = \frac{940}{1000} = 0.94
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Weighted Compliance Score (where ambiguous events get a 0.5 weight):**

$$
C_{weighted} = \frac{w_{compl} \cdot N_{compl} + w_{amb} \cdot N_{amb}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $w_{compl} = 1.0$, $w_{amb} = 0.5$, $w_{viol} = 0.0$:**

$$
C_{weighted} = \frac{1.0 \cdot 920 + 0.5 \cdot 20 + 0.0 \cdot 60}{1000}
$$

$$
C_{weighted} = \frac{920 + 10 + 0}{1000} = \frac{930}{1000} = 0.93
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Precision and Recall for the violation detector:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{55}{55 + 8} = \frac{55}{63} \approx 0.8730
$$

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{55}{55 + 5} = \frac{55}{60} \approx 0.9167
$$

**Step 6 — Compute F1-Score (harmonic mean of P and R):**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8730 \cdot 0.9167}{0.8730 + 0.9167} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80025}{1.7897} \approx 0.8942
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation for the KTU examiner:** $C_{hard} = 0.94$ means 6% policy violation rate; $F_1 \approx 0.89$ indicates the *detector* (not the system) is ~89% effective. The KTU answer must distinguish *system compliance* from *detector accuracy*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Privacy Budget Accounting

**Problem:** A CPS queries a smart-meter database using three queries $f_1, f_2, f_3$, each with sensitivity $\Delta f_i$ and privacy budget $\epsilon_i$. Compute the total privacy leakage under **sequential composition** and **parallel composition**.

**Step 1 — State the sequential composition theorem:**

$$
\epsilon_{total}^{seq} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

Let $\epsilon_1 = 0.3$, $\epsilon_2 = 0.5$, $\epsilon_3 = 0.2$ (each query consumes a portion of the total $\epsilon$).

$$
\epsilon_{total}^{seq} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — State the parallel composition theorem (queries on disjoint subsets):**

$$
\epsilon_{total}^{par} = \max_{i} \epsilon_i
$$

**Step 4 — Apply parallel composition (since each query reads a *different* household's data):**

$$
\epsilon_{total}^{par} = \max(0.3, 0.5, 0.2) = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Conclude: parallel composition is the privacy-optimal design** for disjoint-record CPS queries. The KTU-expected insight is:

$$
\epsilon_{total}^{par} \le \epsilon_{total}^{seq} \quad \text{when queries are disjoint}
$$

$$
0.5 \le 1.0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the required Laplace noise scale for one query of $\Delta f = 1$ at $\epsilon = 0.5$:**

$$
\text{Scale } b = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2.0
$$

Therefore, the noised answer is:

$$
M(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}(0, \; 2.0)
$$

The variance of the added noise is $2 b^2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8.0$. KTU students must remember that **lower $\epsilon$ ⇒ higher noise ⇒ lower utility**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: $k$-Anonymity and Re-identification Risk

**Problem:** A hospital releases a patient table with quasi-identifiers $\{$Age, ZIP, Gender$\}$. Given the equivalence class sizes $\{4, 3, 7, 2, 6, 5\}$, determine whether the release is 3-anonymous. Then compute the re-identification probability for a record in the smallest class.

**Step 1 — Apply the $k$-anonymity predicate:**

A release is $k$-anonymous iff every equivalence class has $\ge k$ records. Here the class sizes are $\{4, 3, 7, 2, 6, 5\}$.

**Step 2 — Check the predicate for $k=3$:**

The minimum class size is $\min = 2$, which is **less than 3**. Hence the release is **NOT 3-anonymous**.

**Step 3 — Check for $k=2$:**

The minimum is $2 \ge 2$, so it **IS 2-anonymous**.

**Step 4 — Compute the re-identification probability for a record in the smallest class (size 2):**

$$
P_{reid} = \frac{1}{\text{class size}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average re-identification probability across all classes:**

$$
\bar{P}_{reid} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{c=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\vert c \vert}
$$

With total records $N = 4+3+7+2+6+5 = 27$:

$$
\bar{P}_{reid} = \frac{1}{27}\left(\frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{7} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{5}\right)
$$

$$
\bar{P}_{reid} = \frac{1}{27}(0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.1429 + 0.5000 + 0.1667 + 0.2000)
$$

$$
\bar{P}_{reid} = \frac{1}{27}(1.5929) \approx 0.0590
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Although the average re-identification risk is ~5.9%, the *worst-case* record in the size-2 class has a **50% re-identification risk**, which violates the KTU-accepted $k \ge 5$ benchmark. The fix is **generalization** (e.g., coarsen ZIP to first 2 digits) until every class $\ge 5$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Information-Flow Confidentiality Bound

**Problem:** A CPS sensor $X$ has Shannon entropy $H(X) = 4$ bits. An adversary observes an output $O$ that reduces the uncertainty of $X$ to $H(X \mid O) = 1$ bit. Compute the information leakage and the percentage of $X$'s entropy exposed.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of mutual information as leakage:**

$$
I(X;O) = H(X) - H(X \mid O)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
I(X;O) = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage leakage:**

$$
\% \text{leakage} = \frac{I(X;O)}{H(X)} \times 100 = \frac{3}{4} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**Step 4 — KTU conclusion:** A 75% information leakage is unacceptable for a privacy-sensitive CPS. To reduce leakage, the enforcement layer must (a) **coarsen** the output, or (b) **inject controlled noise** (differential privacy), or (c) **encrypt** $O$ end-to-end so the adversary never observes it in clear.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Enforcement Mechanism

| CPS Scenario | Policy Goal | Recommended Mechanism | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart meter billing | Aggregate statistics only | **Differential Privacy (Laplace)** | Quantitative privacy guarantee with provable bounds |
| Patient record release | Linkage prevention | **$k$-Anonymity + $\ell$-Diversity** | Syntactic anonymity against linkage attacks |
| V2X beaconing | Real-time location | **Spatial cloaking (k-anonymous regions)** | Real-time constraints; delay-tolerant |
| Industrial control commands | Authenticity + secrecy | **Encrypted command signing (HMAC + AEAD)** | Confidentiality + integrity |
| Wearable health data export | Aggregate trend | **Federated learning + DP-SGD** | Model never sees raw data |
| Smart home camera | Faces of visitors | **Edge-side face blurring (on-device PET)** | Minimizes data leaving the device |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference Monitor Architecture for CPS Privacy Enforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Policy Authority / Admin] -->|Uploads Rules| B[Policy Repository XACML]
    B -->|Provides Policy| C[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    D[Subject: User / Device / Sensor] -->|Access Request| E[Policy Enforcement Point PEP]
    E -->|Decision Request| C
    C -->|PERMIT or DENY| E
    E -->|Allows or Blocks| F[Target Object: Data Resource]
    F -->|Action Notification| G[Obligation Handler]
    G -->|Log and Notify| H[Tamper-evident Audit Ledger]
    H -->|Feeds Metrics| I[Compliance Dashboard]
    I -->|Anomaly Alert| J[Privacy Operations Center]
    J -->|Policy Updates| A
    F -.->|Telemetry Tap| K[Runtime Monitor]
    K -->|Compares Behaviour vs Policy| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilance Notes:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `PDPSvc`). Labels contain only clean uppercase text; no markdown bolding, no HTML tables, no reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` are used as node names. The flow shows the closed-loop feedback from runtime monitoring back into the PDP, which is the *defining* characteristic of an enforceable (vs. paper) privacy policy.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Monitoring Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[CPS Data Plane Sensors Actuators] --> M2[Edge Tap and Sniffer]
    M2 --> M3[Stream Normalizer]
    M3 --> M4[Taint Tracker Labels Privacy Class]
    M4 --> M5[Policy Evaluator Streams CEP Engine]
    M5 -->|Match| M6[Violation Classifier]
    M5 -->|No Match| M7[Compliant Log Entry]
    M6 --> M8[Enforcement Action Trigger]
    M8 -->|Allow Redact Block Anonymize| M1
    M7 --> M9[Audit Pipeline]
    M8 --> M9
    M9 --> M10[Compliance Metrics and Reports]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the data path; the dashed semantic relationship between *Taint Tracker* and the data plane indicates that labelling happens *in parallel* to data movement, not by intercepting the data itself (this is a non-intrusive monitoring pattern that preserves CPS real-time constraints).

## 4.3 Feedback Control Loop: Monitoring ↔ Enforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Define Privacy Policy] --> S2[Translate to Machine-readable Rules]
    S2 --> S3[Deploy to PDP and PEP]
    S3 --> S4[Instrument CPS Data Plane]
    S4 --> S5[Runtime Monitoring and Taint Tracking]
    S5 --> S6[Policy Compliance Check]
```
