```mermaid
    A1[Theoretical Foundations] --> B1[Adversary Models]
    A1 --> B2[Information-Theoretic Bounds]
    A1 --> B3[Differential Privacy Theory]
    
    A2[Algorithmic PETs] --> C1[Differential Privacy]
    A2 --> C2[Federated Learning]
    A2 --> C3[Homomorphic Encryption]
    A2 --> C4[Zero-Knowledge Proofs]
    
    A3[System Architectures] --> D1[Edge Fog Cloud]
    A3 --> D2[Trusted Execution Env]
    A3 --> D3[Blockchain Ledgers]
    A3 --> D4[Split Learning]
    
    A4[Socio Technical Research] --> E1[GDPR DPDP Compliance]
    A4 --> E2[Usability Studies]
    A4 --> E3[Adversarial ML Defense]
    A4 --> E4[Post Quantum Crypto]
    
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    C3 --> D2
    D3 --> E1
    E3 --> C2
    E4 --> C3
    
    F1[Open Research Challenges] --> F2[Privacy Utility Latency Trilemma]
    F1 --> F3[Tighter DP Composition]
    F1 --> F4[AML Robustness]
    F1 --> F5[Lightweight PQC]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture – Privacy-Preserving CPS Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS["Physical Layer"]
        S1[Sensors]
        S2[Actuators]
    end
    
    subgraph EDGE["Edge Layer - Local PETs"]
        E1[Local DP Noise]
        E2[Gradient Masking]
        E3[Hardware Masking]
    end
    
    subgraph FOG["Fog Layer - Aggregation"]
        F1[Secure Aggregation]
        F2[TEE Enclave]
        F3[FL Server]
    end
    
    subgraph CLOUD["Cloud Layer - Analytics"]
        C1[Encrypted MPC]
        C2[Model Training]
        C3[Audit Blockchain]
    end
    
    S1 --> E1 --> F1 --> C1
    S1 --> E2 --> F3 --> C2
    S1 --> E3 --> F2 --> C3
    C2 --> S2
    C3 --> S2
    C1 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Research Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify CPS Domain] --> P2[Threat Modeling]
    P2 --> P3[Define Adversary]
    P3 --> P4[Select PET Candidates]
    P4 --> P5[Prototype on Testbed]
    P5 --> P6[Measure Trilemma Metrics]
    P6 --> P7{Acceptable?}
    P7 -- No --> P4
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Publish and Iterate]
    P8 --> P9[Real Deployment]
    P9 --> P2
```

## 4.4 DP Budget Composition Over Time (Conceptual Mermaid)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Privacy Budget Erosion in Long-Running CPS"
    x-axis "Time (queries)" [0, 1000, 5000, 10000, 50000, 100000]
    y-axis "Cumulative Epsilon" 0 --> 20
    line [0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.2, 5.0, 15.2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs) and list any four with a one-line description.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
PETs are tools that protect user data by minimising, anonymising, or encrypting information in CPS pipelines.
*(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for any 2 PETs with ½ mark each; 1 mark for remaining 2 PETs with ½ mark each.)*

1. **Differential Privacy (DP):** Adds calibrated noise so any single record's presence is statistically invisible.
2. **Homomorphic Encryption (HE):** Allows computation on ciphertexts without decryption.
3. **Federated Learning (FL):** Trains a shared model while keeping raw data on local devices.
4. **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP):** Proves a fact without revealing the underlying secret.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the privacy-utility-latency trilemma in CPS?**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):
*(1 mark for naming the three axes.)*
- **Privacy:** Resistance to inference and re-identification.
- **Utility:** Preservation of analytical value (e.g., accuracy of control).
- **Latency:** Real-time responsiveness of the CPS control loop.

*(2 marks for the trade-off explanation.)* No single PET simultaneously achieves *perfect* privacy, *zero* utility loss, and *real-time* latency. For example, HE provides cryptographic privacy but adds 10³–10⁴× compute overhead, breaking the latency bound. Hence research seeks *optimal* trade-offs.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **research opportunities in applying Differential Privacy to streaming Cyber-Physical Systems**. Cover the limitations of classical DP composition and the role of event-level and w-event privacy.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a suitable example, explain how **Federated Learning combined with DP-SGD** can address privacy in **smart-healthcare CPS**. Identify at least two open research problems.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Problem Statement (1.5 marks):**
Classical DP (Dwork 2006) assumes **i.i.d., bounded, one-shot queries**. CPS sensors (e.g., ECG, smart meters) emit **continuous, unbounded, time-correlated streams**, breaking these assumptions. Naive composition explodes the privacy budget ε_total.

**2. Event-level vs. w-event Privacy (2 marks):**
- **Event-level DP:** Privacy loss per *event* (e.g., one heart-beat). Useful for short bursts.
- **w-event DP:** Privacy loss for any *w consecutive events*. Tighter; allows long-running streams.

**3. Streaming DP Algorithms (1.5 marks):**
- **Binary Tree Mechanism (Dwork, Naor, Pitassi, Rothblum 2010):** O(log T) noise with constant ε.
- **Online-Streaming DP (Chan, Shi, Song 2011):** O(ε) per-step guarantee.

**4. Open Problems (2 marks):**
- (i) Adaptive adversaries with side-channel correlations.
- (ii) Tight composition for **unbounded** streams without logarithmic blow-up.
- (iii) Joint optimization with control-loop deadline.

> *[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 2 = 7]*

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**1. Smart-Healthcare CPS Setting (1 mark):**
Wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch, ECG patches) collect patient vitals; cloud AI predicts arrhythmias. Regulations: **HIPAA, GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023** forbid raw data egress.

**2. FL + DP-SGD Architecture (2 marks):**
- Local model trains on patient device.
- **DP-SGD** (Abadi et al. 2016): clip per-sample gradients to norm ≤ C, then add Gaussian noise σ·C.
- Secure aggregation at the central server.

**3. Concrete Example (1.5 marks):**
A 100-hospital FL system predicts sepsis 6h early. Each round: per-hospital DP ε=0.5, δ=10⁻⁶. After 100 rounds, advanced composition gives ε_total ≈ 5.5 – acceptable for clinical research.

**4. Open Research Problems (2.5 marks):**
- (i) **Non-IID heterogeneity** across hospitals (CPS devices vary).
- (ii) **Gradient leakage attacks** (Zhu et al. 2019) recover raw data from FL updates → requires *adaptive* DP.
- (iii) **Asynchronous participation** in FL with intermittent CPS connectivity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) – [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Analyse the role of **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** and **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)** as complementary PETs in CPS. Discuss the latency–security trade-off with concrete numbers.

**(b) [7 marks]** Examine **research challenges in post-quantum privacy** for long-lifecycle CPS such as smart grids. Compare lattice-based and hash-based approaches.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. HE Recap (1.5 marks):**
HE (Gentry 2009) permits addition and multiplication on ciphertexts. **CKKS** scheme supports *approximate* real-number arithmetic – ideal for control.

**2. TEE Recap (1 mark):**
Hardware-isolated regions (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, AMD SEV) where code + data are encrypted in DRAM.

**3. Complementary Use-Case (2 marks):**
- **HE** protects data **at rest and in transit**; slow but cryptographically strong.
- **TEE** protects data **during use**; fast (≈ 10–100 ms overhead) but relies on HW vendor trust.

**4. Concrete Latency Numbers (1.5 marks):**
- CKKS multiplication: 5–50 ms (depth-limited).
- SGX enclave call: 1–10 ms (round-trip).
- Plain-text control: < 1 ms.

**5. Hybrid Architecture (1 mark):**
Run **MPC-in-the-head** inside a TEE for cloud-based MPC of CPS state estimation.

> *[Examiner Note: 7 marks = 1.5 + 1 + 2 + 1.5 + 1]*

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**1. Why PQC matters for CPS (1 mark):**
Smart grids have **20–40 year** lifecycles. A *harvest-now-decrypt-later* (HNDL) adversary stores ciphertexts today to decrypt once quantum computers mature.

**2. Lattice-based Schemes (2 marks):**
- **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (KEM), **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (signatures).
- Based on LWE / Module-LWE hardness.
- Key size ≈ 1–2 kB, ciphertext ≈ 1 kB.

**3. Hash-based Schemes (2 marks):**
- **XMSS, SPHINCS+** (NIST PQC standards).
- Security based only on hash-function pre-image resistance.
- Larger signatures (8–50 kB) but minimal assumptions.

**4. Open Research Challenges in CPS (2 marks):**
- (i) **Constrained-device cost**: Lattice PQC increases handshake latency 5–10× on MCUs.
- (ii) **Side-channel resistance** of lattice operations in TEEs.
- (iii) **Hybrid migration paths** for legacy CPS without downtime.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT** list PETs without explaining *why* they fit a *specific* CPS sub-domain. Marks are docked when answers remain generic.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse **ε-DP** with **k-anonymity** or **l-diversity** – they are *not* the same and have *different* mathematical guarantees.
> 3. **Always** anchor your answer to the **trilemma** (privacy-utility-latency) – it is the *single most rewarded* conceptual link.
> 4. **Avoid** writing only the *name* of a paper (e.g., "Abadi 2016"). The examiner expects a *1-line* contribution summary.
> 5. **For 14-mark answers**, structure must be: *Problem → Existing Solutions → Their Gaps → Your Proposal / Open Problem → Quantitative Metric*. Missing any of these forfeits 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision – KTU 2024 PECPT743 Module 4**

- ☐ **Trilemma** = Privacy ↔ Utility ↔ Latency. **Always cite it.**
- ☐ **Differential Privacy (DP)** uses **ε (epsilon)**. Lower = stronger privacy.
- ☐ **Gaussian mechanism** is preferred over **Laplace** for *composability*.
- ☐ **Advanced composition theorem** for k queries: $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \approx \sqrt{2k\ln(1/\delta')}\,\varepsilon + \dfrac{k\varepsilon^{2}}{\delta'-\varepsilon}$.
- ☐ **FL + DP-SGD** is the *de-facto* privacy stack for **smart-healthcare** and **smart-grid** CPS.
- ☐ **HE** (CKKS) → encrypted *computation*; **TEE** (SGX) → encrypted *execution*. They are **complementary**, not competing.
- ☐ **Zero-Knowledge Proofs** → V2X and identity CPS; proof generation is *expensive* (1–10 s).
- ☐ **Blockchain (private/permissioned)** → audit trails and tamper-evident logs; throughput is the *bottleneck*.
- ☐ **Adversarial ML** attacks (membership inference, model inversion) *can break* vanilla FL + DP.
- ☐ **Post-Quantum Privacy** is *non-optional* for long-lifecycle CPS (grids, pipelines, defence).
- ☐ **Research roadmap answers** must follow: **Problem → SOTA → Gap → Proposal → Metric**.
- ☐ **DPDP Act 2023 (India), GDPR (EU), HIPAA (US)** are the *three* regulatory anchors.
- ☐ **Open research problems** to remember: tighter DP composition, AML-robust FL, lightweight PQC, edge-fog-cloud budget allocation, HNDL defence.
- ☐ **Reading list (no specific editions):**
  - Dwork & Roth – *Algorithmic Foundations of DP* (foundational).
  - Abadi et al. (2016) – DP-SGD.
  - McMahan et al. (2017) – FedAvg.
  - Gentry (2009) – Fully HE.
  - NIST PQC Round-3 Reports.
  - ENISA Annual Privacy Research Agendas.

> **End of Module 4 Notes – Best of luck for KTU 2024 ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Future Trends in Privacy for CPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Future Trends in Privacy for CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Future Trends in Privacy for CPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Future Trends in Privacy for Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refers to the emerging research directions, technological paradigms, regulatory frameworks, and architectural patterns that are expected to define the next generation of privacy-preserving mechanisms in interconnected physical-computational environments. According to the **KTU 2024 PECPT743 syllabus (Module 4)**, this encompasses the evolution beyond classical cryptographic and access-control mechanisms toward **adaptive, intelligent, decentralized, and regulation-aware** privacy frameworks designed to cope with heterogeneous CPS deployments spanning Industrial IoT (IIoT), smart cities, autonomous vehicles, healthcare CPS, and critical infrastructure.

Formally, it can be defined as:

> *A multidisciplinary convergence of advanced cryptography, distributed ledger technologies, machine intelligence, hardware-rooted trust, and policy-driven governance models engineered to anticipate, preserve, and continuously validate privacy guarantees in dynamically evolving CPS architectures.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The module emphasizes *forward-looking* concepts — students are expected to demonstrate awareness of **emerging technologies** rather than master deep mathematical implementations. The focus is on **conceptual understanding, comparative analysis, and application mapping**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern hospital's patient monitoring system** (a classic CPS — biomedical sensors + network + actuator alerts). Today, patient data flows through hospital servers, third-party analytics platforms, and sometimes insurance networks — every node being a *potential privacy leak*. 

**Future trends in privacy** can be visualized as the hospital upgrading to a system where:
- Patient data is **encrypted even while being analyzed** (Homomorphic Encryption).
- Doctors' AI models are **trained on-device** without raw data ever leaving the patient's wearable (Federated Learning).
- Patients hold **digital health passports** they personally control (Self-Sovereign Identity).
- A **privacy "weather forecast"** warns the system about emerging threats in real time (AI-driven privacy risk prediction).
- The entire infrastructure is **quantum-safe**, so even a future quantum computer cannot retroactively decrypt records.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Future privacy in CPS is moving from *"keep attackers out"* to *"make data useless even if attackers get in"* — a paradigm shift from perimeter defense to **data-centric, intelligence-driven, and mathematically provable** privacy.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **FL** | **Federated Learning** — collaborative model training without centralizing raw data. |
| **HE** | **Homomorphic Encryption** — computation on ciphertext producing encrypted results. |
| **ZKP** | **Zero-Knowledge Proof** — proving a statement true without revealing the underlying data. |
| **DP** | **Differential Privacy** — adding calibrated noise to obscure individual records. |
| **SSI** | **Self-Sovereign Identity** — user-owned, decentralized digital identity. |
| **TEE** | **Trusted Execution Environment** — hardware-isolated secure computation region. |
| **ε (epsilon)** | **Privacy budget** in differential privacy — smaller ε implies stronger privacy. |
| **CPS** | **Cyber-Physical System** — tight integration of computation, networking, and physical processes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** In Differential Privacy, the **privacy budget (ε)** is the canonical metric. Industry-standard values: **ε ≤ 1** for strong privacy (Apple), **ε ≤ 3** for moderate privacy, **ε ≤ 10** for weak privacy (US Census uses ≈ 9–17 historically).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of Future Privacy in CPS

The future of CPS privacy rests upon **eight convergent pillars**, each addressing a specific vulnerability class. Understanding their roles — and limitations — is the cornerstone of this module.

### Pillar 1: Federated Learning (FL) & Edge Intelligence

Federated Learning decentralizes model training. Instead of aggregating raw sensor data at a central server, the model travels to the edge devices (CPS nodes), trains locally, and only **model gradients** are sent back for aggregation.

**Why this matters for CPS:**
- Sensors in autonomous vehicles, wearables, smart meters generate *highly sensitive* data.
- Centralized ML pipelines are **privacy honeypots** — single breach exposes millions of users.
- FL keeps raw data *at the source* (vehicle ECU, wearable SoC, smart meter), drastically reducing attack surface.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Global Model Broadcast** — Server pushes initial model $M_0$ to $N$ CPS edge nodes.
2. **Local Training** — Each node $i$ trains on its private dataset $D_i$ for $E$ epochs.
3. **Gradient Upload** — Each node uploads $\Delta M_i$ (model update, not data).
4. **Secure Aggregation** — Server aggregates: $M_{t+1} = M_t - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta M_i$
5. **Repeat** until convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Privacy Caveat:** Even gradients can leak data (gradient inversion attacks). Hence FL is *paired* with Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC), Differential Privacy, or HE in production CPS.

---

### Pillar 2: Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

HE enables computation directly on encrypted data. The result, when decrypted, matches the result of the same computation on plaintext.

**Three variants critical to CPS:**
- **PHE (Partially HE):** Supports specific operations (e.g., Paillier supports addition) — fast but limited.
- **SHE (Somewhat HE):** Supports both addition and multiplication, but limited depth — used in many IoT pilots.
- **FHE (Fully HE):** Supports arbitrary computation on ciphertext — *the holy grail*, but computationally heavy (1000×–10000× overhead).

**Why this matters for CPS:**
- Cloud-based CPS analytics (e.g., smart grid load forecasting) can run on encrypted smart meter data.
- Hospital CPS can outsource diagnostic ML inference without revealing patient data.

**Standard schemes:**
- **BFV / BGV** — integer arithmetic.
- **CKKS** — approximate real-number arithmetic (ideal for ML/AI inference on CPS sensor data).
- **TFHE** — Boolean gate-level, fast bootstrapping.

---

### Pillar 3: Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)

ZKP allows a *prover* to convince a *verifier* that a statement is true **without revealing any information beyond the validity of the statement itself**.

**Two families:**
- **Interactive ZKPs** — prover and verifier exchange messages (e.g., Schnorr identification).
- **Non-Interactive ZKPs (NIZKP)** — single message proof; foundation of **zk-SNARKs** and **zk-STARKs**.

**CPS applications:**
- Autonomous vehicle proves it has valid insurance + license *without revealing identity*.
- Smart meter proves its reading is within a valid range *without revealing the exact value*.
- Industrial CPS proves compliance with safety regulations *without exposing trade secrets*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **zk-SNARKs** = Succinct Non-interactive ARgument of Knowledge — small proofs, trusted setup required.
> - **zk-STARKs** = Scalable Transparent ARgument of Knowledge — larger proofs, no trusted setup, quantum-resistant.

---

### Pillar 4: Differential Privacy (DP)

DP provides a **mathematically rigorous** guarantee that the inclusion or exclusion of any single individual's data does not significantly affect the output of a computation.

**Formal definition:** A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **ε-DP** if for all datasets $D, D'$ differing in exactly one record, and for all outputs $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \leq e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

**CPS applications:**
- Aggregated smart grid consumption data released for research.
- Wearable health statistics published for public health studies.
- Vehicular trajectory datasets shared for urban planning.

**Noise mechanisms:**
- **Laplace Mechanism** — add $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$ noise (for numeric queries).
- **Gaussian Mechanism** — add $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ noise (for $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP).
- **Exponential Mechanism** — for non-numeric outputs.

---

### Pillar 5: Blockchain & Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)

DLT offers **immutable, auditable, decentralized** records — natively suited for privacy-preserving CPS data trails.

**CPS-specific use cases:**
- **Supply chain CPS** — provenance tracking of pharmaceutical cold-chain data.
- **Energy trading** — peer-to-peer energy transactions in smart grids without central utility.
- **Medical CPS** — tamper-proof audit logs of who accessed what patient data, when.

**Privacy-enhancing DLT patterns:**
- **Public chain + ZKP** — verify transactions without revealing payload.
- **Private/Permissioned chain** — Hyperledger Fabric, Corda for enterprise CPS.
- **Hash-based commitments** — anchor off-chain encrypted data to chain for integrity proofs.

---

### Pillar 6: Confidential Computing & TEEs

**Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)** are hardware-isolated regions (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV, ARM TrustZone, Apple Secure Enclave) where code and data are protected from the host OS, hypervisor, and physical attackers.

**CPS deployment pattern:**
- Edge gateways in industrial CPS run sensitive aggregation inside TEEs.
- Autonomous vehicles use TEEs to protect ML model weights from extraction.

**Combined with attestation:** Remote parties can cryptographically verify that a specific binary is running inside a genuine TEE on a known hardware platform.

---

### Pillar 7: Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) & Decentralized Identifiers (DID)

SSI flips the identity paradigm: **users own and control their identifiers and credentials**, not central authorities.

**Building blocks:**
- **DID (Decentralized Identifier)** — globally unique, resolvable, cryptographically verifiable.
- **Verifiable Credentials (VCs)** — tamper-proof, cryptographically signed claims.
- **Verifiable Data Registry** — blockchain or DHT for DID resolution.

**CPS applications:**
- Citizens of a smart city hold VCs for parking permits, library access, transit passes — all without a central citizen database.
- Patients own their medical records; hospitals request access on-demand, with patient consent logged immutably.

---

### Pillar 8: AI-Driven Privacy & Privacy-Enhancing Technologies (PETs)

The frontier: **using AI to defend privacy** while also recognizing AI as a *new threat vector*.

**Two directions:**
1. **Privacy-Preserving AI** — AI training/inference that doesn't expose data (FL + DP + HE).
2. **Privacy-Threat Detection AI** — ML models that detect anomalous data flows, inference attacks, re-identification attempts in real-time.

**Emerging subfields:**
- **Membership Inference Attack (MIA) defense** — detecting whether a record was part of training data.
- **Model Inversion defense** — preventing reconstruction of training samples from model outputs.
- **Adversarial Privacy** — using adversarial training to make models robust against privacy attacks.

---

## 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet — Comparative Analysis of Future Privacy Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Privacy Guarantee | Computational Overhead | CPS Suitability | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Federated Learning** | Data locality, no raw data movement | Low–Medium | **High** (distributed sensors) | Gradient leakage, communication cost |
| **Homomorphic Encryption** | Computation on ciphertext | **Very High** (1000×–10000×) | Medium (cloud-assisted CPS) | Bootstrapping cost, ciphertext expansion |
| **Zero-Knowledge Proofs** | Provable non-disclosure | Medium (zk-SNARKs) | **High** (verification scenarios) | Trusted setup (SNARKs), proof size |
| **Differential Privacy** | Mathematical ε-bounded leakage | Low | **High** (statistical release) | Utility loss, ε-budget management |
| **Blockchain / DLT** | Immutable audit, decentralization | Medium (consensus) | Medium (cross-organizational) | Scalability, public-chain privacy |
| **TEEs / Confidential Computing** | Hardware-rooted isolation | Low | **High** (edge gateways) | Hardware trust assumption, side-channels |
| **SSI / DIDs** | User-controlled identity | Low | **High** (citizen-facing CPS) | Adoption, key recovery, revocation |
| **AI-Driven PETs** | Adaptive, threat-responsive | Variable | **Emerging** | Adversarial arms race |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Privacy Trend Application |
|---|---|
| **Smart Healthcare CPS** | FL + DP for multi-hospital diagnostic models; SSI for patient records. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | ZKP for license/insurance verification; TEE for in-vehicle model protection. |
| **Smart Grids** | HE for encrypted billing; blockchain for peer-to-peer energy trading. |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | FL for predictive maintenance without exposing factory data. |
| **Smart Cities** | SSI for citizen services; DP for urban mobility data release. |
| **Defence CPS** | Quantum-resistant cryptography; TEE-based secure command chains. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Analytical Matrix — Formal Frameworks & Derivations

## 3.1 Formal Privacy Frameworks

### Framework 3.1.1 — Differential Privacy (DP) Bound Derivation

The **Laplace Mechanism** adds noise drawn from a zero-mean Laplace distribution with scale parameter $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$, where $\Delta f$ is the **sensitivity** of the query function $f$.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity Definition:**

The $\ell_1$-sensitivity of a function $f: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^k$ is:

$$\Delta f = \max_{D, D': \|D - D'\|_1 = 1} \| f(D) - f(D') \|_1$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Distribution PDF:**

A random variable $X$ follows the Laplace distribution with scale $b$:

$$f_X(x) = \frac{1}{2b} \exp\left(-\frac{|x|}{b}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Applying the DP Definition:**

Let $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \eta$, where $\eta \sim \text{Lap}(b)$ and $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$.

For any two neighboring datasets $D, D'$ and any output $y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) = y]}{\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') = y]} &= \frac{\Pr[f(D) + \eta = y]}{\Pr[f(D') + \eta = y]} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\Pr[\eta = y - f(D)]}{\Pr[\eta = y - f(D')]} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\frac{1}{2b}\exp\left(-\frac{|y - f(D)|}{b}\right)}{\frac{1}{2b}\exp\left(-\frac{|y - f(D')|}{b}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left(\frac{|y - f(D')| - |y - f(D)|}{b}\right) \\[6pt]
&\leq \exp\left(\frac{|f(D) - f(D')|}{b}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \exp\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) = \exp(\varepsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ratio is bounded by $e^{\varepsilon}$, satisfying the **ε-DP** guarantee.

> [!NOTE]
> The **triangle inequality** in step 5 ($|y - f(D')| - |y - f(D)| \leq |f(D) - f(D')|$) is the key mathematical step.

---

### Framework 3.1.2 — Federated Learning Convergence Bound

A simplified convergence analysis for FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017) with $N$ clients and $T$ communication rounds:

**Assumptions:**
- Each local loss function $F_i$ is $L$-smooth and $\mu$-strongly convex.
- Local steps per round: $E$.
- Learning rate: $\eta$.

**Convergence bound (informal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[F(\bar{w}_T) - F(w^*)] \leq \frac{1}{\mu} \left[ \frac{L}{\eta T + \mu^{-1}} \left( F(w_0) - F(w^*) + \frac{L \eta^2 \sigma^2}{2} \right) \right. \\[6pt]
\left. + \eta L \left( \frac{\sigma^2}{N} + 6 L \Gamma + 8 (E-1)^2 G^2 \right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $\sigma^2$ — variance of local gradients (data heterogeneity).
- $\Gamma$ — *client drift* due to non-IID data distributions.
- $G$ — bound on gradient norm.
- $w^*$ — global optimum.

**Privacy implication:** $\sigma^2$ can be *intentionally inflated* by adding DP noise — this degrades convergence but buys privacy. The **privacy-utility tradeoff** is explicit and tunable.

---

### Framework 3.1.3 — Homomorphic Encryption Correctness

For a **Paillier cryptosystem** (additively homomorphic), given public key $(n, g)$ and plaintexts $m_1, m_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Enc}(m_1) \cdot \text{Enc}(m_2) \mod n^2 &= g^{m_1} r_1^n \cdot g^{m_2} r_2^n \mod n^2 \\[6pt]
&= g^{m_1 + m_2} (r_1 r_2)^n \mod n^2 \\[6pt]
&= \text{Enc}(m_1 + m_2 \mod n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves **additive homomorphism**: $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) = E(m_1 + m_2)$.

**For CKKS (approximate HE used in CPS ML):**

$$E(m_1) \oplus E(m_2) = E(m_1 + m_2), \quad E(m_1) \otimes E(m_2) = E(m_1 \cdot m_2)$$

with bounded approximation error (managed via rescaling and bootstrapping).

---

### Framework 3.1.4 — Zero-Knowledge Proof Soundness & Completeness

A ZKP system $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{V})$ for language $\mathcal{L}$ with relation $R$ must satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Completeness:} \quad & \forall (x, w) \in R: \Pr[\langle \mathcal{P}(x, w), \mathcal{V}(x) \rangle = 1] = 1 \\[6pt]
\textbf{Soundness:} \quad & \forall x \notin \mathcal{L}, \forall \mathcal{P}^*: \Pr[\langle \mathcal{P}^*(x), \mathcal{V}(x) \rangle = 1] \leq \varepsilon_s \\[6pt]
\textbf{Zero-Knowledge:} \quad & \forall \mathcal{V}^*: \exists \mathcal{S}: \text{View}_{\mathcal{V}^*}(\mathcal{P}) \approx_c \mathcal{S}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\varepsilon_s$ is the **soundness error** and $\approx_c$ denotes **computational indistinguishability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **zk-SNARKs**, soundness is *computational* (relies on cryptographic assumptions), while for **zk-STARKs**, soundness is *information-theoretic* — hence quantum-resistant.

---

## 3.2 The Privacy–Utility–Performance Trilemma

A foundational concept in future CPS privacy is the **PUP Trilemma**: a system can simultaneously achieve at most two of:

1. **Strong Privacy** (low ε, encrypted computation).
2. **High Utility** (accurate models, useful outputs).
3. **High Performance** (low latency, low compute cost).

| Pillar | Privacy | Utility | Performance | Dominant Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FL** | Medium–High | High | Medium | Gradient leakage vs. accuracy |
| **HE (FHE)** | Very High | High | **Low** | Compute cost vs. data confidentiality |
| **DP** | High (tunable) | Medium | High | Noise magnitude vs. accuracy |
| **ZKP** | Very High | N/A (proof-only) | Medium | Setup cost vs. succinctness |
| **TEE** | High | High | High | Hardware trust vs. side-channel exposure |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT743 Module 4]**

### Question 1
**Q: Differentiate between Federated Learning and traditional centralized Machine Learning from a privacy perspective. List two CPS scenarios where FL offers clear privacy advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Centralized ML | Federated Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Location** | All raw data aggregated at central server | Raw data stays at edge device |
| **Privacy Risk** | Centralized honeypot — single breach exposes all | Distributed — no single point of mass exposure |
| **Computation** | Heavy on server, light on device | Light on server, moderate on edge |
| **Attack Surface** | Large (server + transmission) | Smaller (only gradients transmitted) |

**Two CPS scenarios:**
1. **Smart wearable health monitoring** — Heart-rate, ECG, SpO₂ data from millions of users remain on-device; only model updates are shared, preventing large-scale patient data leakage.
2. **Autonomous vehicle fleet learning** — Driving patterns, road conditions, and obstacle encounters are trained locally in each vehicle ECU; only model deltas are aggregated, protecting driver behavior and location privacy.

*(3 marks — KTU evaluation expects: 1 mark for tabular distinction, 1 mark per valid scenario with privacy reasoning.)*

---

### Question 2
**Q: What is Differential Privacy? Define the privacy parameter ε and state what a smaller ε implies.**

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Privacy (DP):** A mathematical framework that quantifies the privacy guarantee of a randomized algorithm by bounding how much its output can change when a single individual's record is added or removed from the dataset.

**Privacy parameter ε (epsilon):** The privacy budget — a non-negative real number controlling the strength of the privacy guarantee.

- **Smaller ε** → **Stronger privacy** (output barely changes with any single record).
- **Larger ε** → **Weaker privacy** (output may reveal individual contributions).

**Industry standard values:**
- **ε ≤ 1** — strong privacy (used by Apple, Google).
- **ε ≤ 3** — moderate privacy.
- **ε > 5** — weak privacy (often inadequate for sensitive CPS data).

**Example in CPS:** A smart grid releasing aggregated household consumption data with ε = 0.5 ensures that whether or not a specific household was included, the released statistics change negligibly — protecting individual consumption patterns.

*(3 marks — KTU evaluation expects: 1 mark definition, 1 mark ε definition, 1 mark implication of small ε with example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT743 Module 4, Choice A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** in the context of CPS privacy. Discuss its three variants (PHE, SHE, FHE) with suitable CPS use-cases for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the architecture of **Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)** and explain how Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs) and Verifiable Credentials (VCs) can enhance privacy in a smart-city CPS deployment.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** Homomorphic Encryption is a cryptographic technique that enables computation on ciphertext such that decrypting the result yields the same output as performing the computation on the original plaintext.

**Mathematical property:** For an encryption scheme $E$ and operations $\oplus, \otimes$ on plaintext and ciphertext:

$$E(m_1) \oplus E(m_2) = E(m_1 + m_2), \quad E(m_1) \otimes E(m_2) = E(m_1 \times m_2)$$

**Three Variants:**

**1. Partially Homomorphic Encryption (PHE):**
- Supports *unlimited* operations of *one type* (either addition or multiplication).
- Examples: **Paillier** (additive), **RSA** (multiplicative — multiplicatively homomorphic).
- Computational cost: Low.
- **CPS use case:** Smart grid — encrypted meter readings can be summed across households to compute total neighborhood consumption without decrypting individual values.

**2. Somewhat Homomorphic Encryption (SHE):**
- Supports *limited* number of both addition and multiplication operations.
- Limited by *multiplicative depth* before noise overwhelms the ciphertext.
- Examples: **BGV**, **BFV** schemes.
- **CPS use case:** Encrypted voting in autonomous vehicle coordination — vehicles submit encrypted preferences; the aggregator computes a ranking without learning individual votes.

**3. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE):**
- Supports *arbitrary* computation on ciphertext.
- Uses *bootstrapping* to refresh noise — computationally expensive (1000×–10000× overhead vs. plaintext).
- Schemes: **TFHE**, **CKKS** (approximate arithmetic, ideal for ML).
- **CPS use case:** Cloud-based ML diagnostic inference on encrypted patient CPS data — hospital sends encrypted CT/MRI data to cloud; cloud returns encrypted diagnosis; only the hospital can decrypt.

**Privacy utility for CPS:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HE transforms the trust model: CPS data processors (cloud, third-party analytics) become **computationally incapable** of accessing plaintext, eliminating insider threats and mass-surveillance risk. The data is **cryptographically sealed** during processing.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI)** is a paradigm where individuals or CPS entities own and control their digital identities without relying on a central authority. Three pillars:

**1. Decentralized Identifiers (DIDs):**
- Globally unique identifiers of the form `did:method:unique-string`.
- Resolved via a **Verifiable Data Registry** (blockchain, distributed hash table).
- Each DID has an associated **DID Document** containing public keys and service endpoints.
- **No central registrar** — the user generates and controls the key pair.

**2. Verifiable Credentials (VCs):**
- Cryptographically signed, tamper-evident claims issued by trusted entities.
- Example: A hospital issues `VC: { "subject": "did:patient:123", "claim": "blood type: O+", "issuer": "did:hospital:abc" }`.
- The VC is stored in the **user's digital wallet** (mobile app, edge device).

**3. Verifiable Presentations (VPs):**
- When a CPS service needs verification, the user presents a *subset* of credentials, often with **selective disclosure** (ZKP-backed) to reveal only required attributes.

**Smart City CPS Deployment:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| Citizen mobile wallet | Holds DIDs, VCs (driving license, residency, library card) |
| City department issuer | Signs VCs (e.g., parking permit VC) |
| Blockchain / DLT | DID registry, VC revocation registry |
| CPS service (parking gate, library kiosk) | Verifies VC presentation on-demand |

**Privacy advantages:**
- **Minimal disclosure** — citizen proves "I have a valid parking permit" without revealing license plate, name, or address.
- **No central citizen database** — eliminates mass-breach risk.
- **Revocable yet private** — issuer revokes a VC; verifier checks status via ZKP without learning other credentials.
- **User-controlled consent** — every presentation is cryptographically logged; users can audit who accessed their data.

> [!NOTE]
> SSI aligns naturally with **GDPR's data minimization principle** and **right to be forgotten** — because no central database exists to be forgotten from; credentials are issued once, stored with the user, and revoked by the issuer when needed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT743 Module 4, Choice B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Privacy–Utility–Performance (PUP) Trilemma** in CPS privacy. Illustrate with examples how Federated Learning, Homomorphic Encryption, and Differential Privacy each navigate this trilemma differently.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)** in CPS privacy. Compare hardware-based (TEE) and cryptographic-based (HE/DP) privacy approaches with respect to trust assumptions, performance, and attack surface.

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**The PUP Trilemma:**

> *A CPS privacy mechanism can optimize at most two of the following: strong Privacy, high Utility, high Performance — the third is necessarily compromised.*

$$
\text{Privacy} + \text{Utility} + \text{Performance} \leq 2 \quad \text{(in their fully-optimized forms)}
$$

**Federated Learning — favors Utility + Performance, accepts moderate Privacy:**

- **Utility:** Model accuracy preserved (often within 1–3% of centralized baseline).
- **Performance:** Low compute overhead per client; communication bottleneck.
- **Privacy:** *Moderate* — raw data never leaves device, but **gradients can leak** (gradient inversion attacks by Zhu et al., 2019).
- **CPS example:** Smart speaker federated keyword recognition — high accuracy, real-time, but raw audio can be partially reconstructed from gradients.

**Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) — favors Privacy + Utility, sacrifices Performance:**

- **Privacy:** *Very high* — data never decrypted during computation.
- **Utility:** Exact (or near-exact, CKKS) computational results.
- **Performance:** *Low* — 1000×–10000× compute overhead; ciphertext expansion (1 MB ciphertext for 1 KB plaintext in some schemes).
- **CPS example:** Encrypted genomic analysis — strong privacy, accurate results, but 10-second query instead of 1-millisecond.

**Differential Privacy — favors Privacy + Performance, sacrifices Utility:**

- **Privacy:** *Mathematically rigorous* (ε-bounded leakage).
- **Performance:** Negligible overhead (just noise addition).
- **Utility:** *Moderate* — accuracy degrades with smaller ε; statistical queries remain valid, individual predictions lose fidelity.
- **CPS example:** Smart grid aggregated load release — ε = 0.5 adds 15% relative error to small-cell statistics, but real-time and private.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Mechanism | Privacy | Utility | Performance | Trade-off Accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FL** | Medium | High | High | Gradients can leak |
| **FHE** | Very High | High | **Low** | 1000×+ compute overhead |
| **DP** | High (tunable) | Medium | High | Utility loss via noise |

**Conclusion:** No single mechanism solves all three — modern CPS deployments **layer multiple PETs** (e.g., FL + DP + TEE) to triangulate optimum.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Trusted Execution Environment (TEE):** A hardware-isolated region within a processor that protects code and data from the host OS, hypervisor, BIOS, and physical attackers. TEEs use **memory encryption** and **attestation** to provide confidentiality and integrity guarantees.

**Major TEE implementations:**

| TEE | Vendor | Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Intel SGX** | Intel | Servers, cloud CPS edge |
| **AMD SEV / SEV-SNP** | AMD | Virtualized cloud CPS |
| **ARM TrustZone** | ARM | Embedded CPS, IoT, mobile |
| **Apple Secure Enclave** | Apple | Consumer CPS (iPhone, Watch) |
| **RISC-V Keystone** | Open-source | Research CPS |

**Role in CPS Privacy:**

1. **In-vehicle privacy** — Autonomous vehicle ECUs run ML inference inside TEE; model weights and sensor data are protected from extraction.
2. **Smart meter aggregation** — Smart meter gateways aggregate neighborhood consumption inside TEE; utility company sees only aggregate, not individual readings.
3. **Industrial CPS** — Factory-floor predictive maintenance models protected inside TEE from corporate espionage.
4. **Healthcare CPS** — Patient monitoring data analyzed in TEE-enclaves on hospital servers; insider IT staff cannot read raw data.

**TEE Attestation Flow:**
1. Enclave generates **attestation quote** signed by hardware key.
2. Remote verifier validates quote against vendor's root certificate.
3. Verifier confirms: genuine TEE + correct code + secure state.
4. Verifier releases decryption key to enclave only.

**Comparison: Hardware (TEE) vs. Cryptographic (HE/DP) Approaches:**

| Aspect | TEE (Hardware) | HE / DP (Cryptographic) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Assumption** | Hardware vendor (Intel, AMD) is honest | Mathematical hardness assumptions (e.g., LWE for HE) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (5–30% overhead) | High (HE) to low (DP) |
| **Attack Surface** | Side-channel attacks (Spectre, Foreshadow, cache timing) | Cryptanalysis, parameter misconfiguration |
| **Verifiability** | Remote attestation (cryptographic) | Mathematical proofs, ε-bounds |
| **Quantum Resistance** | Generally yes (hardware-agnostic) | Lattice-based HE is quantum-resistant; DP is data-agnostic |
| **Failure Mode** | Hardware vulnerability (catastrophic) | Mathematical break (gradual) |
| **CPS Suitability** | Edge gateways, vehicles, wearables | Cloud analytics, statistical release |

**Hybrid Approach (Best Practice):**
- **TEE + DP:** Process encrypted data inside TEE, add DP noise before release.
- **TEE + HE:** Use TEE to accelerate HE bootstrapping; HE to protect TEE-to-TEE communication.
- **TEE + FL:** Use TEE to protect FL gradient aggregation server.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **TEE with HSM** (Hardware Security Module). **TEE** is a *general-purpose execution region*; **HSM** is a *dedicated crypto accelerator* with no general code execution.
> - Do **not** claim TEEs provide "absolute security" — always mention **side-channel attacks** as a real and ongoing research concern.
> - For DP, always specify which mechanism (Laplace vs. Gaussian) and **just the ε value** — vague answers lose marks.
> - For ZKP, distinguishing **zk-SNARK vs. zk-STARK** (trusted setup vs. transparency) is a high-value differentiator in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

- ✅ **Federated Learning (FL):** Data stays at edge; only model gradients shared. Vulnerable to gradient inversion attacks; *combine with DP or SMPC*.
- ✅ **Homomorphic Encryption (HE):** Computation on ciphertext. **PHE** (one operation unlimited), **SHE** (limited depth), **FHE** (arbitrary, costly). **CKKS** = ideal for CPS ML.
- ✅ **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP):** Prove validity without disclosure. **zk-SNARKs** = small proofs + trusted setup; **zk-STARKs** = larger + transparent + quantum-resistant.
- ✅ **Differential Privacy (DP):** Mathematically bounded leakage via ε-budget. **Smaller ε = stronger privacy**. Use **Laplace** mechanism for numeric, **Exponential** for categorical.
- ✅ **Blockchain / DLT:** Immutable audit trails; combine with ZKP for payload privacy. Hyperledger Fabric for enterprise CPS.
- ✅ **TEEs / Confidential Computing:** Hardware isolation (Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone, AMD SEV). Vulnerable to side-channels — never claim absolute security.
- ✅ **Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI):** User-controlled identity via **DIDs** + **Verifiable Credentials**. Aligns with GDPR data minimization.
- ✅ **AI-Driven PETs:** Two-way street — AI for privacy AND privacy threats from AI. Membership Inference Attacks, Model Inversion are real and active.
- ✅ **PUP Trilemma:** Privacy + Utility + Performance cannot all be simultaneously maximized — *pick two*.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth:** Modern CPS requires *layered* privacy — FL + DP + TEE + Blockchain is the emerging blueprint.
- ✅ **Quantum-Resistance:** Post-quantum cryptography (lattice-based, hash-based) is critical for *future-proofing* CPS privacy.
- ✅ **Regulatory Awareness:** GDPR, CCPA, India's DPDP Act 2023 — all emphasize purpose limitation, data minimization, consent, and right to erasure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> Module 4 questions often appear in **Part A (3 marks)** as *short comparison* or *definition* questions, and in **Part B (14 marks)** as *case-study-based* questions requiring you to **select and justify a privacy mechanism** for a given CPS scenario. Always frame your answer as: **threat → mechanism → trade-off → justification**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Real-World Applications: Analysis of Privacy-Preserving CPS in Various Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT743 - PRIVACY IN CPS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Real-World Applications: Analysis of Privacy-Preserving CPS in Various Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Privacy-Preserving Cyber-Physical System (PP-CPS)** is an integrated computational and physical process control system in which sensing, communication, computation, and actuation occur over a feedback loop, while simultaneously enforcing cryptographic, statistical, and architectural controls that prevent inference, reconstruction, or leakage of sensitive information about the system's users, operators, or physical state. In the KTU 2024 PECPT743 syllabus, *privacy preservation* in CPS is defined as the **co-design** of (i) the control plane, (ii) the data plane, and (iii) the threat model, such that no adversary — internal or external, passive or active — can compromise confidentiality of sensor streams, user identity, location trajectory, behavioural profile, or actuation intent beyond a mathematically quantified privacy budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A CPS is *not* considered "privacy-preserving" simply because it uses TLS/HTTPS. The privacy guarantee must be a **first-class design objective** — quantified by a privacy budget ($\varepsilon$, $k$, or $l$), and verified under an explicit adversary model (honest-but-curious, malicious, covert, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Vending Machine"

Imagine a smart vending machine in a hospital corridor that automatically restocks insulin, gauze, and saline based on real-time patient ward consumption.

- **Naïve CPS Design (Privacy-Leaky):** The vending machine broadcasts *every single dispensing event* — including the patient ID, drug name, timestamp, and ward number — to a central cloud, in clear-text JSON. A janitor with a Wi-Fi sniffer learns which patient is taking which medication. This is the classic *side-channel leakage through telemetry*.
- **Privacy-Preserving CPS Design:** The machine performs **on-device aggregation** (federated analytics), broadcasts only *k-anonymous counts* (e.g., "Ward 3B consumed 14 insulin units between 14:00–15:00"), and authenticates itself using an **attribute-based credential** (e.g., "Nurse of Internal Medicine"). No single transaction leaks an individual patient's medication profile, yet the hospital's supply chain still gets the data it needs to restock.

The hospital vending machine is a microcosm of **every** PP-CPS: healthcare, smart grid, transportation, Industry 4.0, and smart cities all share this exact pattern — *useful aggregate control signal + minimal individual exposure*.

## 1.3 Why This Module Matters (Industry Pull)

| Industry Sector | Cyber-Physical Asset | Sensitive Data at Risk | Real-World Stakes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Wearables, infusion pumps, ICU monitors | PHI, biometrics, drug regimen | HIPAA / DPDP Act fines, life-safety |
| **Smart Grid** | Smart meters, SCADA, DERs | Occupancy ( NILM ), billing | Energy-theft profiling, surveillance |
| **Transportation** | V2X, autonomous fleets, ETC | Location trajectory, driving style | Stalking, insurance discrimination |
| **Industry 4.0** | PLCs, robotics, CNC, MES | Trade secrets, process know-how | Industrial espionage |
| **Smart Buildings** | HVAC, occupancy sensors, cameras | Comportment, presence | Domestic-abuse victim tracking |
| **Defence / Drones** | UAV swarms, tactical networks | Soldier location, mission plans | Operational security, lives |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standardization Bodies Used as Anchors in Case Studies:**
> - **NIST SP 800-183** — Networks of Things (NoT) reference architecture.
> - **NIST IR 8259** — IoT cybersecurity baseline.
> - **ISO/IEC 27400:2022** — IoT security & privacy guidelines.
> - **IEC 62443** — Industrial automation and control systems (IACS) security.
> - **ETSI EN 303 645** — Consumer IoT cyber security.
> - **IEEE 7000-series** — Ethically aligned design (e.g., 7002 for data privacy).

## 1.4 The Five Privacy Threat Surfaces in any CPS

1. **Sensor-layer leakage** — raw acoustic, video, vibration, or electromagnetic emissions.
2. **Communication-layer leakage** — packet headers (timing, size, destination), traffic-analysis attacks.
3. **Edge/Fog-layer leakage** — model inversion from on-device ML weights.
4. **Cloud-layer leakage** — aggregation reveals individual records, insider threats, subpoenas.
5. **Actuation-layer leakage** — actuators' physical effects (heat, sound, light) form a covert side channel.

Every case study in this module is mapped against at least one of these five surfaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Privacy-Utility-Regulation Trilemma

Every privacy-preserving CPS design must balance three competing forces, often visualized as a triangle:

- **Utility (U):** The control loop must remain stable, accurate, and timely. A privacy mechanism that adds **5 seconds of latency** to a lane-keeping controller is unacceptable.
- **Privacy (P):** Quantified by a formal budget (e.g., $\varepsilon$ in differential privacy, $k$ in $k$-anonymity).
- **Compliance (C):** Adherence to legal frameworks (GDPR Art. 25 "Privacy by Design", HIPAA Security Rule, India's DPDP Act 2023).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Mnemonic — "U-P-C":** A typical 14-mark question asks you to *analyse a given CPS case study along U-P-C axes and recommend a layered privacy architecture*. Always structure your answer as: **(1) Threat surface → (2) Adversary model → (3) Privacy budget choice → (4) Cryptographic primitive → (5) Compliance mapping.**

## 2.2 The Six Canonical Privacy-Preserving Primitives (used in case studies)

### 2.2.1 Differential Privacy (DP)

A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies **$\varepsilon$-differential privacy** if for all neighbouring datasets $D, D'$ differing in one record, and for all output sets $S$:

$$\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D) \in S] \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon} \cdot \Pr[\mathcal{M}(D') \in S]$$

- **Smaller $\varepsilon$ = stronger privacy.** Industry rule-of-thumb: $\varepsilon \le 1$ for "strong" privacy, $\varepsilon \le 3$ for "moderate", $\varepsilon \le 10$ for "weak/utility-biased".
- **Sensitivity** $\Delta f$ of a query $f$ is the maximum change in output when one record changes. For counting queries, $\Delta f = 1$.
- **Laplace mechanism:** add noise drawn from $\text{Lap}(\Delta f / \varepsilon)$.
- **Gaussian mechanism:** add noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ where $\sigma \ge \Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$ for $(\varepsilon, \delta)$-DP.

### 2.2.2 k-Anonymity & l-Diversity

- A dataset is **k-anonymous** if every record is indistinguishable from at least $k-1$ others on the quasi-identifier (QI) attributes.
- **l-Diversity** strengthens this by requiring each QI-group to contain at least $l$ "well-represented" sensitive values — preventing the *homogeneity attack*.
- **t-Closeness** further demands the distribution of sensitive values in any QI-group be within $t$ of the global distribution (Earth Mover's Distance).

### 2.2.3 Homomorphic Encryption (HE)

- **Partially HE (Paillier):** supports addition on ciphertexts.
- **Somewhat HE (BGV, BFV):** supports bounded-depth addition & multiplication.
- **Fully HE (CKKS, TFHE):** arbitrary computation on ciphertexts. CKKS is the de-facto choice for *real-valued CPS sensor streams* because it natively supports floating-point approximate arithmetic.

### 2.2.4 Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)

- Enables $n$ parties to jointly compute a function $f(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ such that each learns only the output, never the others' inputs.
- **Yao's Garbled Circuits** — boolean circuits, good for small logic.
- **Shamir Secret Sharing** — arithmetic circuits, good for aggregating sensor readings.
- **Common in V2X and federated grid balancing.**

### 2.2.5 Federated Learning (FL) / Split Learning

- Model travels to the data, not data to the model.
- Vulnerable to **gradient inversion attacks** → hence often combined with DP-SGD (Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent) or HE-aggregation.
- **Standard DP-SGD noise scale:** $\sigma \ge \frac{\text{clipping norm } C \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln(1.25/\delta)}}{n \cdot \varepsilon}$ where $n$ is the number of participating clients per round.

### 2.2.6 Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKP)

- A prover convinces a verifier of a statement without revealing the witness.
- **zk-SNARKs / zk-STARKs** are used in CPS for *compliance attestation* — e.g., a smart meter proves its reading lies within a permitted band without revealing the actual value, satisfying both the utility operator and the privacy regulator.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single source of truth — copy this table verbatim into your exam preparation sheet.**

| # | Privacy Metric / Equation | Formula | Typical Domain / Unit | Used In (Case Study) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon$-DP bound | $\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D)\in S] \le e^{\varepsilon}\,\Pr[\mathcal{M}(D')\in S]$ | $\varepsilon > 0$, dimensionless | Smart meter aggregation, wearable health telemetry |
| 2 | Laplace noise scale | $b = \Delta f / \varepsilon$ | b in same units as $f$ | NILM privacy, smart-home occupancy |
| 3 | Gaussian noise scale ( $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP ) | $\sigma \ge \Delta f \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / \varepsilon$ | $\sigma$ in units of $f$ | Federated learning, FL of CPS sensor models |
| 4 | DP-SGD noise multiplier (per-round) | $\sigma \ge C \sqrt{2\ln(1.25/\delta)} / (n\,\varepsilon)$ | $C$ = gradient clip norm, $n$ = clients | V2X perception model training |
| 5 | k-Anonymity check | $\vert$ QI-group of any record $\vert \;\ge\; k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Smart-card transit logs, EHR releases |
| 6 | Composition (sequential k queries) | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \varepsilon_i$ | additive | Streaming CPS analytics dashboards |
| 7 | Advanced composition (T reports) | $\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \approx \sqrt{2T \ln(1/\delta')}\cdot\varepsilon + T\varepsilon(e^{\varepsilon}-1)/2$ | with $\delta' = T\delta$ | Year-long building-energy studies |
| 8 | HE ciphertext expansion (Paillier) | $\text{size} = 2 \cdot \vert n \vert$ bits | $\vert n\vert \ge 2048$ | Smart-meter billing |
| 9 | CKKS rescale cost | level drops by 1 per mult | integer | Real-time control loops (encrypted MPC) |
| 10 | SMPC comm. cost (Shamir) | $O(n^2)$ messages per multiplication | messages | Multi-aggregator smart grid |
| 11 | Mutual Information leakage | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \vert Y)$ | bits | Sensor-side channel quantification |
| 12 | k-anonymity via generalization height | $h \ge \lceil \log_k \vert D \vert \rceil$ | integer | Census-style CPS datasets |
| 13 | DP under subsampling (Poisson) | $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \le \ln(1 + q(e^{\varepsilon}-1))$ | $q$ = sampling rate | FL with client dropouts |
| 14 | EMD for t-closeness | $t \ge \text{EMD}(P_g, P_{\text{global}})$ | real $\ge 0$ | Anonymized trajectory datasets |
| 15 | Mix-network anonymity (entropy) | $H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits | Onion-routing CPS telemetry |

## 2.4 How These Equations Are Used in Engineering Fields

- **Smart Grid** uses Eq. 2 (Laplace) on hourly meter readings so the utility learns *aggregate demand* but not *whether you watched a movie tonight* (NILM attack defence).
- **Healthcare wearables** use Eq. 4 (DP-SGD) to train a population-level arrhythmia detector without exporting raw ECG to the cloud.
- **V2X** uses Eq. 10 (SMPC) for *platooning coordination* — trucks compute a joint lane-change plan without revealing individual trajectories.
- **Industry 4.0** uses Eq. 8 (Paillier) so an MES (Manufacturing Execution System) can sum encrypted cycle-times across suppliers for *capacity planning* without leaking per-supplier trade secrets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the unit "dimensionless" for $\varepsilon$?** Because $\varepsilon$ is a *ratio of probabilities*, not a physical quantity. Treat it like dB — a logarithmic privacy meter where 0 dB is no privacy and $-\infty$ dB is perfect secrecy.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 PECPT743 module-4 case-study paper most commonly tests **derivation of the DP noise scale** and **composition of privacy budgets across a multi-stage CPS pipeline**. We provide an exhaustive walk-through below — every algebraic step is shown, with a textual explanation row.

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Laplace Mechanism Scale is $\Delta f / \varepsilon$

**Problem Statement.** Given a numeric query $f : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}$ with sensitivity $\Delta f$, design a randomized mechanism $\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Noise}$ that satisfies $\varepsilon$-DP.

**Step 1 — Define neighbouring datasets.**
Let $D$ and $D'$ be two datasets that differ in exactly one record. By the definition of sensitivity:

$$
\Delta f \;=\; \max_{D \sim D'} \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Assume the noise follows a zero-mean Laplace distribution.** Its probability density function is:

$$
\text{PDF}(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2b} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\vert x \vert}{b}\right)
$$

where $b > 0$ is the *scale parameter* (not rate). The corresponding ratio of likelihoods for any two outputs $r$ produced by $D$ and $D'$ is:

$$
\frac{\text{PDF}(r - f(D))}{\text{PDF}(r - f(D'))} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\vert r - f(D') \vert - \vert r - f(D) \vert}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the triangle inequality.** For any $r$:

$$
\vert r - f(D') \vert - \vert r - f(D) \vert \;\le\; \vert f(D) - f(D') \vert \;\le\; \Delta f
$$

**Step 4 — Upper-bound the likelihood ratio.** Substituting:

$$
\frac{\text{PDF}(r - f(D))}{\text{PDF}(r - f(D'))} \;\le\; \exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Enforce the $\varepsilon$-DP definition.** We require this ratio to be at most $e^{\varepsilon}$ for all $r$:

$$
\exp\!\left(\frac{\Delta f}{b}\right) \;\le\; e^{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $b$.** Taking natural log of both sides:

$$
\frac{\Delta f}{b} \;\le\; \varepsilon \quad\Longrightarrow\quad b \;\ge\; \frac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Conclusion.** The minimum sufficient scale of Laplace noise to achieve $\varepsilon$-DP for a query of sensitivity $\Delta f$ is:

$$
\boxed{\,b \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta f}{\varepsilon}\,}
$$

**Interpretation.** If you halve your privacy budget $\varepsilon$ (stronger privacy), you must **double the noise scale** — i.e., utility degrades linearly in $1/\varepsilon$. This is the central *privacy–utility trade-off curve* of CPS telemetry.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Case Studies to Primitives

The following matrix is the **single most important exam instrument** for this module. KTU examiners expect you to be able to populate it for any unseen CPS case study.

| Industry Case Study | Threat Surface (from §1.4) | Adversary Model | Selected PP-Primitive | Privacy Budget / Parameter | Compliance Anchor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart-meter NILM defence | Sensor + cloud | Honest-but-curious utility | DP with Laplace | $\varepsilon = 0.5$ per day | GDPR Art. 25 |
| ICU wearable FL model training | Edge + cloud | Malicious aggregator | DP-SGD | $\varepsilon = 1.0$, $\delta = 10^{-5}$ | HIPAA §164.312 |
| V2X platooning | Communication | Covert external | SMPC (Shamir) | $t$-of-$n$ threshold $t = 2$ | UNECE WP.29 R155 |
| Pharma MES supplier aggregation | Cloud | Honest-but-curious competitor | Paillier HE | 2048-bit modulus | FDA 21 CFR Part 11 |
| Smart-building HVAC occupancy | Sensor | Co-resident snooper | k-Anonymity + DP | $k = 10$, $\varepsilon = 2$ | ISO/IEC 27400 |
| Defence UAV swarm | All five | Nation-state active | zk-SNARK attestation | soundness error $\le 2^{-128}$ | NIST FIPS 140-3 |
| Smart-card transit (Delhi Metro) | Communication | Internal revenue officer | Mix network + ZKP | anonymity set $\ge 500$ | DPDP Act 2023 |
| Industrial pump anomaly FL | Edge + cloud | Malicious insider | FL + Trusted Exec Env (Intel SGX) | attestation per round | IEC 62443-3-3 |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Privacy Budget Composition in a Multi-Stage CPS Pipeline

**Scenario.** A smart-building CPS runs three sequential analytics stages on occupant sensor data each hour:
1. Stage A: Count of occupants (DP, $\varepsilon_A = 0.3$).
2. Stage B: Average $\text{CO}_2$ level (DP, $\varepsilon_B = 0.4$).
3. Stage C: Peak occupancy time (DP, $\varepsilon_C = 0.5$).

**Step 1 — Identify that the stages operate on the *same* underlying dataset $D$**, so we must compose privacy budgets.

**Step 2 — Apply the basic composition theorem** (Dwork & Roth, *The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy*):

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i} \varepsilon_i
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.3 + 0.4 + 0.5 \;=\; 1.2
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** A cumulative $\varepsilon = 1.2$ is in the *strong-privacy* regime. The building can safely run all three analytics every hour without breaching the organization's published privacy budget of $\varepsilon_{\text{org}} = 1.0$ per day, *if* the daily cycle uses only 12 such hours — *but* a 24-hour run would push the daily budget to $\varepsilon_{\text{daily}} = 1.2 \times 24 / \text{rounds}$ which is too aggressive.

**Step 5 — Mitigation.** Use **advanced composition** (Eq. 7 in the cheat sheet) with smaller per-stage $\varepsilon$, or **subsample** the data (Poisson sampling) to *amplify* the privacy guarantee via Eq. 13.

This kind of multi-stage analysis is the bread-and-butter of module-4 case-study questions.
